REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION.definition: A supervised depository institution or regulated lender that originates, holds, and manages commercial real estate (CRE) exposures and is expected to maintain risk management and capital commensurate with CRE concentration risk.
examples: A bank approved as an SBA 7(a) Lender that must remit SBA guaranty fees and ongoing service fees to maintain SBA guaranty coverage.

business_rules.rule_name: Short-term SBA 7(a) upfront fee must be paid within 10 business days of SBA loan number assignment or guaranty is cancelled
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) short-term loans (maturity of 12 months or less), the 7(a) Lender must pay the SBA Upfront Fee via www.pay.gov within 10 business days from the date the SBA Loan Number is assigned. If SBA does not receive the fee within 10 business days after issuance of the SBA Loan Number, SBA will cancel the guaranty. For EWCP loans re-issued after 12 months, each re-issuance is treated as a new loan and another Upfront Fee is due; SBA earns the short-term Upfront Fee when the SBA loan number is issued. The Lender may only charge the Upfront Fee to the Borrower after the Lender has paid the Upfront Fee.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) the loan is an SBA 7(a) short-term loan with maturity <= 12 months, and (b) an SBA Loan Number has been assigned/issued.
business_rules.consequences: If the Upfront Fee is not received by SBA within 10 business days after SBA Loan Number issuance, SBA cancels the guaranty for the loan. If the lender charges the borrower before the lender has paid the upfront fee, this is a program compliance violation that can result in SBA findings and potential jeopardy to guaranty enforceability.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 10-business-day deadline. EWCP re-issuance after 12 months explicitly requires a new upfront fee each time it is re-issued.

source_reference.section_id: When the Upfront Fee Must be Paid (13 CFR § 120.220(b)) > Short-term loans (maturities of 12 months or less)
source_reference.source_text: Short-term loans (maturities of 12 months or less): The 7(a) Lender must pay the Upfront Fee through www.pay.gov within 10 business days from the date the SBA Loan Number is assigned. If the fee is not received within 10 business days after issuance of the SBA Loan Number, SBA will cancel the guaranty. For EWCP loans re-issued after 12 months, each time the loan is re-issued it is a new loan, and another Upfront Fee is due.

examples: A 9-month EWCP line is assigned an SBA loan number on July 2. The lender must pay the upfront fee through pay.gov no later than 10 business days after July 2; otherwise SBA cancels the guaranty.
An EWCP loan initially issued for 12 months is re-issued for another 12-month period after 12 months have passed. The re-issuance is a new loan; the lender owes a new short-term upfront fee.
A lender attempts to collect the upfront fee from the borrower at closing before paying SBA. This violates the requirement that the lender may only charge after paying the upfront fee.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty cancelled for nonpayment; SBA program compliance finding and potential denial/repair of guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: Long-term SBA 7(a) upfront fee must be paid within 90 days of approval and due date cannot be waived or extended
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans with maturities in excess of 12 months, the 7(a) Lender must pay the SBA Upfront Fee to SBA within 90 days of the date of loan approval. If the Upfront Fee is not paid within 90 days, the guaranty will be cancelled. Issuance of an SBA loan number is the lender’s notification that an Upfront Fee is due and payable within 90 days; SBA may or may not send reminders, and lack of notice does not waive the lender’s obligation. The obligation to pay SBA is not contingent on the borrower paying the lender. The due date for upfront fee payment may not be waived or extended even if the disbursement period is extended.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the SBA 7(a) loan maturity is > 12 months and the loan has been approved (SBA approval date established).
business_rules.consequences: If the Upfront Fee is not paid within 90 days of approval, SBA cancels the guaranty. A lender cannot rely on SBA reminders or borrower payment status as justification for late payment.
business_rules.exceptions: None; the SOP explicitly states the due date may not be waived or extended even if the disbursement period is extended.

source_reference.section_id: When the Upfront Fee Must be Paid > Loans with maturities in excess of 12 months; Notification of Fee Due; Due date may not be waived
source_reference.source_text: The 7(a) Lender must pay the Upfront Fee to SBA within 90 days of the date of loan approval. If the Upfront Fee is not paid within 90 days, the guaranty will be cancelled... When SBA issues an SBA loan number, this is the Lender’s notification that an Upfront Fee is due and payable within 90 days... the obligation to pay the Upfront Fee to SBA is not contingent upon the Borrower having paid the fee to the 7(a) Lender. THE DUE DATE FOR UPFRONT FEE PAYMENT MAY NOT BE WAIVED OR EXTENDED EVEN IF THE DISBURSEMENT PERIOD IS EXTENDED.

examples: A 10-year SBA 7(a) loan is approved on August 1. The lender must pay the upfront fee to SBA by October 30 (90 days), regardless of whether the borrower has reimbursed the lender.
SBA does not send a nonpayment reminder. The lender is still obligated to pay by the 90-day deadline; the guaranty is cancelled if the deadline is missed.
The lender obtains an SBA disbursement period extension. The upfront fee due date is still not extendable and remains 90 days from approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty cancelled for nonpayment; SBA program compliance finding and potential denial/repair of guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: Additional SBA 7(a) upfront fee for a loan increase must be paid within 30 days of increase approval or total guaranty is cancelled
business_rules.description: When an SBA 7(a) loan is increased, additional appropriations are committed and an additional SBA Upfront Fee is due. The additional upfront fee must be calculated using the fee rules in effect at the time the loan was originally approved, and equals: (Upfront fee that would have been charged if the increase were part of the original loan amount) minus (Upfront fee already remitted). The additional upfront fee associated with the increase must be paid electronically within 30 days from the date the increase was approved, or the total loan guaranty will be cancelled. On loans initially disbursed, the upfront fee for any SBA-approved increase must be paid to SBA even if the increase is later cancelled.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA approves an increase to an existing SBA 7(a) loan amount (increase approval date exists).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to pay the additional upfront fee within 30 days of the increase approval results in cancellation of the total loan guaranty. Lender remains obligated to pay for SBA-approved increases after initial disbursement even if the increase is later cancelled.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Additional Upfront Fee for Loan Increases
source_reference.source_text: When a 7(a) loan is increased... an additional Upfront Fee is due... the amount of the additional Upfront Fee due for an increase will equal what the Upfront Fee would have been if the increase was part of the original loan amount, less the amount of the Upfront Fee... The additional Upfront Fee associated with the increase must be paid electronically within 30 days from the date the increase was approved, or the total loan guaranty will be cancelled. On loans that have been initially disbursed... must be paid to SBA, whether or not the increase is subsequently cancelled.

examples: A $400,000 SBA 7(a) loan is increased by $100,000. The lender recalculates the upfront fee as if the original amount were $500,000, subtracts the fee already remitted, and pays the difference within 30 days of the increase approval date.
The lender receives SBA approval for a $50,000 increase but does not remit the additional upfront fee until day 45 after approval. SBA cancels the total guaranty.
An increase is SBA-approved after initial disbursement but later the borrower decides not to proceed. The lender must still pay SBA the additional upfront fee for the approved increase.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty cancelled for nonpayment; SBA program compliance finding and potential denial/repair of guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: Extending a short-term SBA 7(a) loan beyond 12 months requires an additional upfront fee paid within 30 days of the lender’s agreement to extend
business_rules.description: When the maturity of a short-term SBA 7(a) loan is extended beyond 12 months, an additional SBA Upfront Fee is due. The additional fee must be paid electronically within 30 days from the date the 7(a) Lender agrees to the extension, or the total loan guaranty will be cancelled. The 7(a) Lender may charge the additional fee to the Borrower only after the lender has notified SBA that the maturity has been extended and has paid the additional upfront fee. No additional upfront fees are owed for loans extended beyond their original maturity date when SBA determines the extension is to effect collection and no new funds are disbursed, regardless of the original maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) an SBA 7(a) short-term loan originally has maturity <= 12 months, and (b) the lender agrees to extend maturity beyond 12 months.
business_rules.consequences: If the additional fee is not paid within 30 days from the date the lender agrees to the extension, SBA cancels the total guaranty. Charging the borrower before notifying SBA and paying the additional fee is not permitted.
business_rules.exceptions: No additional upfront fee is owed if SBA determines the extension is to effect collection and no new funds are disbursed (regardless of original maturity).

source_reference.section_id: Additional Upfront Fee for Extensions of Short-Term Loans
source_reference.source_text: When the maturity of a short -term 7(a) loan is extended beyond 12 months, an additional Upfront F ee is due. The 7(a) Lender may email FSC.Servicing@sba.gov for assistance. The additional fee must be paid electronically within 3 0 days from the date the 7(a) L ender agrees to the extension, or the total

examples: A 12-month SBA 7(a) short-term loan is extended to 24 months on September 15 (lender agreement date). The lender must pay the additional upfront fee by October 15 (30 days) or the guaranty is cancelled.
A lender extends a short-term loan beyond 12 months and immediately bills the borrower the additional fee before paying SBA. This violates the timing requirement for charging the borrower.
A loan is extended past maturity solely to facilitate collection, with no new disbursement, and SBA determines the extension is to effect collection. No additional upfront fee is owed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty cancelled for nonpayment; SBA program compliance finding and potential denial/repair of guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lender annual service fee is paid monthly on SBA Form 1502 and cannot be charged to the borrower
business_rules.description: The SBA 7(a) Lender must pay SBA a Lender’s Annual Service Fee (SBA on-going guaranty fee). The fee is based on the outstanding principal balance of the guaranteed portion of the loan at the time of SBA loan approval, and SBA specifies the amount each fiscal year via an Information Notice for loans approved during that year. The fee cannot be charged to the borrower. When multiple 7(a) loans are made within 90 days of each other, the annual service fee is set for each loan on a standalone basis (not aggregated). The lender pays this fee on a monthly basis with submission of SBA Form 1502, and SBA may charge a late fee if the on-going guaranty fee is not paid timely. The fee will be listed in the E-Tran Terms and Conditions and it is the lender’s responsibility to ensure the correct fee is included.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) loans that have an SBA guaranty and require on-going guaranty fee remittances, during servicing/monthly reporting via SBA Form 1502.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to pay timely, SBA may assess a late fee. Charging the borrower for this annual service fee is prohibited. Incorrect fee configuration in E-Tran Terms and Conditions is a lender-responsibility compliance defect.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. (EWCP payment options are referenced elsewhere but do not negate the monthly Form 1502 remittance obligation.)

source_reference.section_id: 7(a) Lender’s Annual Service Fee (13 CFR § 120.220(f))
source_reference.source_text: 120.220(f) ). The 7(a) Lender must pay SBA a Lender’s Annual Service Fee. The fee is based on the outstanding principal balance of the guaranteed portion of the loan at the time of SBA Loan approval . SBA specifies the amount of the

examples: A lender services 50 SBA 7(a) loans. Each month the lender submits SBA Form 1502 and remits the annual service fee amounts; late remittance may result in SBA late fees.
Two SBA 7(a) loans are approved for the same borrower within 60 days. The annual service fee is set for each loan independently, not combined.
A lender adds the annual service fee to the borrower’s periodic payment. This violates the rule that the annual service fee cannot be charged to the borrower.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA late fees; SBA program compliance finding and potential servicing/guaranty purchase impacts

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP ongoing fee annual option invoice timing within 60 days and payment due within 30 days with mandatory monthly SBA Form 1502 reporting
business_rules.description: For EWCP loans approved after September 27, 2010, the lender may choose to pay the EWCP Ongoing Fee monthly (with SBA Form 1502) or annually (by invoice). Regardless of payment option, the lender must submit SBA Form 1502 reports monthly for all 7(a) loans, including EWCP loans. Under the annual option, SBA’s Denver Finance Center (DFC) will send an EWCP Ongoing Fee invoice within 60 days of each 12-month interval on the EWCP loan (and after maturity as applicable), and payment is due within 30 days of the date of the invoice.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA EWCP loans approved after 2010-09-27; applies during servicing across the life of each EWCP loan; specifically when selecting payment option and when invoices are issued.
business_rules.consequences: If lender fails to submit monthly Form 1502 reports and/or fails to pay invoiced EWCP Ongoing Fees (including any interest and penalties), SBA may suspend or revoke lender’s eligibility to participate in SBA’s 7(a) program or limit the lender’s delegated authority.
business_rules.exceptions: Monthly submission of Form 1502 is required regardless of EWCP ongoing fee payment option. Invoice cadence varies by maturity: 12 months or less (invoice after maturity); >12 and <=24 months (invoice at 12 months and after maturity); >24 months (invoice at 12 months, 24 months, and after maturity).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: EWCP > Payment Options for EWCP Ongoing Guaranty Fee
source_reference.source_text: Regardless of which payment option is chosen, the Lender must continue to submit, on a monthly basis, the Lender’s SBA Form 1502 Report on all 7(a) loans... The DFC will mail the Lender an EWCP Ongoing Fee invoice... within 60 days of each 12-month interval on the EWCP loan and payment will be due within 30 days of the date of the invoice... A Lender’s failure to pay any of the fees... may result in SBA’s decision to suspend or revoke a lender’s eligibility to participate in SBA’s 7(a) program or to limit a lender’s delegated authority.

examples: An EWCP loan with 10-month maturity: lender selects annual option; DFC sends one invoice after maturity; lender must pay within 30 days of invoice date and must have submitted monthly Form 1502 reports throughout.
An EWCP loan with 18-month maturity: lender selects annual option; receives an invoice at 12 months after closing and again after maturity; each invoice must be paid within 30 days.
Lender fails to pay an EWCP ongoing fee invoice for 45 days; SBA may impose program sanctions including limiting delegated authority.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension or revocation of SBA 7(a) program eligibility; limitation of delegated authority

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Form 159 must be completed for each paid agent with itemization required when aggregate compensation exceeds $2,500
business_rules.description: For SBA transactions involving an Agent (other than an SBA-approved professional services contractor performing duties under an SBA-approved professional services contract), if the Agent is paid by the Applicant or CDC, SBA Form 159 must be completed in accordance with the form instructions and signed by the Applicant, the Agent, and the CDC. Separate SBA Forms 159 must be completed for each Agent who provides services to the Applicant. If aggregate compensation for all fees provided by the same Agent exceeds $2,500, the compensation must be itemized; fees are aggregated to establish the $2,500 threshold for itemization when an Agent charges in connection with multiple applications. Failure of an Agent to fully complete and execute SBA Form 159 may result in suspension or revocation of the Agent’s privilege to conduct business with SBA under 13 CFR Part 103. Do not report fees paid for the Third Party Lender’s loan on SBA Form 159.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 and other SBA applications when an Agent is involved and paid by Applicant or CDC, and SBA Form 159 is required by program rules; requires completion/signature and itemization threshold rules.
business_rules.consequences: If Form 159 is missing, incomplete, not properly signed, or lacks required itemization over $2,500 aggregated compensation, the submission is noncompliant and may delay processing and create program compliance findings. Agent noncompliance may lead to suspension or revocation of ability to conduct business with SBA under 13 CFR Part 103.
business_rules.exceptions: Do not report fees paid for the Third Party Lender’s loan on SBA Form 159. Certain parties are not considered Agents for this agreement and are not required to complete SBA Form 159 (e.g., Applicant’s accountant for normal-course financial statement preparation not related to the loan application; state-certified/licensed appraiser employed by the CDC for SBA loan collateral appraisal).

source_reference.section_id: Disclosure of Fees – SBA Form 159
source_reference.source_text: For Agents other than professional services contractors... if the Agent is paid by an Applicant or CDC, an SBA Form 159 must be completed... signed by the Applicant, the Agent, and the CDC... Separate SBA Forms 159 must be completed for each Agent... Failure... may result in suspension or revocation... If the aggregate compensation for all fees provided by the same Agent exceeds $2,500, the compensation must be itemized... Fees are aggregated to establish the $2,500 threshold for itemization.

examples: Required: Borrower pays a loan packager $3,000 for packaging; Form 159 must be signed by Applicant, Agent, and CDC, and the $3,000 must be itemized because it exceeds $2,500.

Required: Two agents are paid ($2,000 broker and $1,000 packager). Separate Form 159s must be completed for each agent; broker form does not require itemization if the broker’s aggregate compensation is ≤ $2,500.
Not compliant: Agent refuses to sign Form 159; CDC submits without full execution—may result in SBA action against the Agent and processing issues.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Suspension or revocation of Agent’s privilege to conduct business with SBA; program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum SBA 7(a) guaranteed amount per business is 3,750,000 dollars including affiliates and revolving lines
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) lending, the maximum outstanding dollar amount of SBA’s guaranty to any one business (including affiliates) must not exceed $3,750,000, unless the loan is approved under a program that specifically permits higher amounts. When calculating maximum guaranty availability, the lender must include the approved loan amount and any existing 7(a) or 504 loans, including revolving lines of credit. SBA treats an Eligible Passive Company (EPC) and Operating Company (OC) as “one business” for this purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans (including CAPLines references) at origination/approval when determining SBA guaranty availability for a borrower and its affiliates, including EPC/OC structures, and including revolving lines of credit.
business_rules.consequences: If the calculated SBA guaranteed amount would exceed $3,750,000, the lender must reduce the guaranteed portion and/or structure/sequence financing so the cap is not breached; otherwise SBA guaranty may be denied, repaired, or not purchased.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception only when a specific SBA program explicitly permits higher guaranteed amounts; otherwise none.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1: Standard 7(a) Loans (Loans Greater than $350,000) – Maximum guaranty amount
source_reference.source_text: When calculating the maximum guaranty percentage available to a Borrower and its affiliates, the Lender must include the approved loan amount and any existing 7(a) or 504 loans, including revolving lines of credit. The SBA’s guaranty is also known as the “SBA share” or “guaranteed portion.” The maximum guaranty amount is $3,750,000.

examples: Borrower has an existing SBA 7(a) guaranteed portion of $2,900,000 and requests a new 7(a) loan that would add $1,000,000 of SBA guaranteed portion; lender must reduce the guaranty so total guaranteed portion does not exceed $3,750,000.
EPC and OC are structured with separate entities; lender must treat them as one business and aggregate existing 7(a)/504 exposures and the new loan’s approved amount when calculating the cap.
Borrower has a $500,000 SBA revolving line approved but drawn $100,000; lender must include the approved $500,000 line amount when calculating guaranty availability.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; purchase denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum SBA 7(a) guaranty percentage is 85 percent at 150,000 dollars or less and 75 percent above 150,000 dollars
business_rules.description: SBA 7(a) maximum guaranty percentage is tiered by loan size: 85% for loans of $150,000 or less; and 75% for loans over $150,000 (subject to the $3,750,000 maximum guaranty amount).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loan approvals when determining SBA guaranteed portion percentage for the loan.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender requests or closes with a guaranty percentage above the allowable tier, SBA guaranty may be reduced, denied, or repaired; lender must adjust guaranteed portion to the applicable tier.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt (aside from overall $3,750,000 cap and any program-specific higher authorization).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1: Standard 7(a) Loans – Maximum guaranty percentage
source_reference.source_text: The maximum guaranty percentage is: i. 85% for loans of $150,000 or less ii. 75% for loans over $150,000

examples: A $120,000 7(a) loan may be structured up to an 85% SBA guaranty (subject to other SBA rules).
A $600,000 7(a) loan may be structured up to a 75% SBA guaranty (not 85%).
A $151,000 7(a) loan must use the 75% maximum tier because it is over $150,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty reduction/repair; purchase denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple SBA 7(a) loans approved within 90 days must be combined and capped at 75 percent guaranty if combined exceeds 150,000 dollars
business_rules.description: For multiple 7(a) loans approved within 90 days of each other, the gross dollar amounts must be combined to determine the applicable maximum guaranty percentage. If the combined gross amount exceeds $150,000, then the percentage of guaranty on the combined loans must not be more than 75%, subject to the $3,750,000 maximum guaranty amount. This can require reducing the guaranty percentage on a subsequent loan to keep the combined guaranteed portion at or below 75% of combined principal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a borrower (and/or affiliates as applicable) has multiple SBA 7(a) loans approved within a 90-day window.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must reduce guaranty percentage on one or more loans so the combined guaranty percentage does not exceed 75% when combined gross principal exceeds $150,000; failure risks SBA guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; still subject to overall $3,750,000 maximum guaranty amount.

source_reference.section_id: 13 CFR § 120.210 – Maximum guaranty percentage for multiple 7(a) loans
source_reference.source_text: For multiple 7(a) loans approved within 90 days of each other, the gross dollar amounts of the loans are combined. If the combined gross amount exceeds $150,000, then the percentage of guaranty on the combined loans must not be more than 75 percent (subject to the $3,750,000 limit).

examples: Loan A: $140,000 at 85% approved; Loan B: $50,000 approved 60 days later. Combined $190,000 > $150,000 so combined guaranty must be ≤ 75%; lender reduces Loan B guaranty percentage (example in SOP reduces to 47%) to keep combined SBA share ≤ $142,500.
Loan A: $100,000 and Loan B: $45,000 approved within 90 days. Combined $145,000 ≤ $150,000; each may remain eligible for the 85% tier (subject to other rules).
Loan A: $200,000 approved; Loan B: $25,000 approved 10 days later. Combined $225,000; lender must ensure combined guaranty percent ≤ 75%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/reduction; purchase denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lender service provider must have SBA-reviewed written agreement and lender must not pass LSP fees to applicant or use SBA proceeds
business_rules.description: A 7(a) lender may contract with a third-party Lender Service Provider (LSP) to assist with lender functions, but must retain day-to-day responsibility for evaluating, processing, closing, disbursing, servicing, liquidating, and litigating its SBA portfolio. If an agent meets the definition of an LSP, a formal written LSP agreement is required and must be submitted to SBA for review. An LSP may only receive compensation from the 7(a) lender for services under an SBA-reviewed LSP agreement; such charges must not be passed to the applicant or paid out of SBA-guaranteed loan proceeds. An LSP agreement may not grant the LSP power of attorney to act on behalf of the lender. If the LSP handles borrower payments, funds must be held in an account in the lender’s name and not commingled across lenders, and the LSP may not net its fee out of borrower payments.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a 7(a) lender uses a third party to perform lender functions in originating, disbursing, servicing, liquidating, or litigating SBA loans and the third party meets SBA’s definition of LSP.
business_rules.consequences: Noncompliance can result in SBA review findings, required remediation, potential enforcement actions in lender oversight, and possible impacts to SBA program participation. Fees improperly charged to applicants or paid from SBA proceeds are prohibited and may require reimbursement/cure.
business_rules.exceptions: Services performed by an LSP under an SBA-reviewed LSP agreement are not reported on SBA Form 159; other agents not covered by an SBA-reviewed LSP agreement require SBA Form 159 (not fully detailed in this rule).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4, Lender Service Provider and Agreements (Pages 71-73)
source_reference.source_text:
A Lender may contract with a third party (Lender Service Provider (LSP)) to assist the Lender
with one or more of these functions. However, the Lender itself, not the LSP, must
be able to demonstrate that it exercises day -to-day responsibility... If an Agent meets the definition of an LSP, a formal agreement between the
Agent and Lender is required and must be reviewed by SBA... An LSP may only receive compensation from the 7(a) Lender... Such charges must not be
passed on to the Applicant or paid out of the SBA -guaranteed loan proceeds... An LSP Agreement may not grant the LSP power of attorney

examples: Bank hires a fintech vendor to underwrite and interact with applicants to obtain financial info → vendor meets LSP definition; bank must have written LSP agreement submitted to SBA for review and cannot pass LSP fees to applicant.
Servicing vendor collects borrower payments into an account titled in the vendor’s name → noncompliant; account must be in lender’s name and not commingle lenders’ funds.
LSP contract includes a power of attorney clause allowing vendor to act on lender’s behalf → prohibited; must remove clause to comply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight findings; potential suspension/restriction of program privileges; required reimbursement of improper charges

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7 a lender packaging fees capped at 2,500 flat or 5 percent up to 150,000 and 3 percent over 150,000 with 30,000 maximum
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, a lender may charge the applicant a flat fee up to $2,500 per loan without documenting the service performed, but must disclose amounts charged in E-Tran. For fee amounts in excess of $2,500, the lender must complete SBA Form 159 in accordance with SOP guidance. For fees based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed what the lender charges on a percentage basis for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans or the following (whichever is less): 5% for loans $150,000 or less; 3% for loans in excess of $150,000; and the maximum fee on a percentage basis is $30,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) lender fees charged to applicants in association with a 7(a) loan (SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4: Ethics, Fees, and Agents; 13 CFR § 120.221).
business_rules.consequences: If fees exceed allowed caps, are not properly disclosed in E-Tran, or SBA Form 159 is not completed when required, SBA may review the fees and require the lender to refund any fee considered unreasonable, and may cite the lender in oversight activities.
business_rules.exceptions: Flat fee up to $2,500 does not require documentation of service performed; fees exceeding $2,500 require SBA Form 159. The cap applies to the fee charged by the lender to the applicant in association with the 7(a) loan.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4: Ethics, Fees, and Agents — Packaging fee limits and Form 159
source_reference.source_text: SBA permits the Lender to charge the Applicant a flat fee of up to $2,500 per loan without documenting the service performed. The Lender must disclose any amounts charged to the Applicant by the Lender in E-Tran. v. For fee amounts in excess of $2,500, the Lender must complete an SBA Form 159 ... b) For fees charged based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed ... whichever is less: i) 5% for loans $150,000 or less; ii) 3% for loans in excess of $150,000; iii) the maximum fee ... is $30,000.

examples: Compliant: $100,000 SBA 7(a) loan charged 5% packaging fee = $5,000 (≤ 5% cap) with Form 159 completed (fee > $2,500).
Noncompliant: $500,000 SBA 7(a) loan charged 4% packaging fee ($20,000) when lender charges 2% on similarly-sized non-SBA loans; the allowed fee is the lesser amount (2%).
Noncompliant: Lender charges $2,500 flat fee but fails to disclose the amount in E-Tran.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee refund requirement; SBA oversight criticism; potential recovery/repair at guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: SBA lender must not split a loan to charge additional packaging fees and must treat approvals over 90 days apart as separate projects
business_rules.description: An SBA lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging an additional fee to the applicant. If there is a legitimate business need to split a request into two loans (e.g., term loan and line of credit), the lender may only charge one fee within maximums based on the combined loan amounts. SBA does not intend to restrict charging a new fee if the applicant subsequently applies for a new loan for a different project or purpose; loans approved more than 90 days apart are considered to be for a different project or purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender structures an SBA 7(a) credit request into multiple facilities and charges packaging/loan fees to the applicant.
business_rules.consequences: If SBA determines the lender split the loan to increase fees, fees are impermissible and may be subject to refund/reimbursement and lender oversight findings; excessive or unreasonable fees may be required to be refunded.
business_rules.exceptions: Permitted to charge one fee based on combined amounts when split is for legitimate business need; permitted to charge a new fee when a later loan is for a different project/purpose, defined as loans approved more than 90 days apart.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4 — Prohibition on loan splitting for additional fees
source_reference.source_text: The Lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging an additional fee to the Applicant. Even if there is a legitimate business need for the Applicant’s loan request to be split into two loans (e.g., a term loan and a line of credit), the Lender may only charge the Applicant one fee within the maximums set forth above, based on the combined loan amounts. ... (Loans approved more than 90 days apart are considered to be for a different project or purpose.)

examples: Noncompliant: Lender splits a $400,000 request into two $200,000 loans to charge two separate percentage-based fees.
Compliant: Lender structures a term loan plus a line of credit for legitimate reasons but charges one fee calculated on the combined total exposure.
Compliant: Applicant returns 120 days after initial approval for a different project; lender charges a new fee for the new loan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee refund requirement; SBA lender oversight criticism; possible recovery at guaranty purchase

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7 a late payment fee capped at 5 percent when borrower is more than 10 days delinquent
business_rules.description: For SBA-guaranteed loans, lenders may charge the borrower a late payment fee that does not exceed 5% of the regular loan payment, and only when the borrower is more than 10 days delinquent on its regularly scheduled payment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA-guaranteed loan servicing where the borrower makes a regularly scheduled payment more than 10 days late.
business_rules.consequences: Charging a late fee greater than 5% of the regular payment or charging it before the borrower is more than 10 days delinquent violates SBA fee limitations and may result in required refunds and SBA oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4 — Late Payment Fee (13 CFR § 120.221(d))
source_reference.source_text: for the Lender’s similarly -sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans . b) For fees charged based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed what the Lender charges on a percentage

examples: Compliant: Regular payment is $4,000; borrower pays 15 days late; late fee charged is $200 (5%).
Noncompliant: Regular payment is $2,500; borrower is 8 days late; lender charges a late fee anyway.
Noncompliant: Regular payment is $3,000; borrower is 20 days late; lender charges $250 (> 5% cap of $150).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee refund requirement; SBA lender oversight criticism

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express and 7(a) Small program limits require 350,000 dollars per project and 500,000 dollars SBA Express aggregate cap with maximum SBA guaranty exposure 3,750,000 dollars
business_rules.description: Lenders must enforce SBA program maximums: (1) 7(a) Small loans have a maximum loan amount of $350,000 per project, including other 7(a) loans made within 90 days. (2) The maximum aggregate SBA Express loan amount is $500,000 (gross) inclusive of all outstanding SBA Express, Community Advantage Pilot, Community Express, and Patriot Express loans the Applicant and its Affiliates may have (7(a) Small loans are not included in this SBA Express aggregate limit). (3) Maximum outstanding SBA guaranty to any one business (including affiliates) must not exceed $3,750,000. (4) Maximum guaranty percentages: 7(a) Small loans—85% for loans of $150,000 or less and 75% for loans over $150,000; SBA Express loans—50%. Lenders must determine whether the Applicant has affiliates and apply maximums as if all affiliates are one business.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small and SBA Express originations/renewals where lender is determining eligibility under SBA maximum loan amount and maximum guaranty exposure limits, including affiliate aggregation.
business_rules.consequences: If lender exceeds SBA loan size/aggregate caps or guaranty exposure limits (including by failing to aggregate affiliates), SBA may reduce/deny guaranty or require corrective action; lender may be criticized in SBA oversight reviews.
business_rules.exceptions: 7(a) Small loans are not included in the $500,000 SBA Express aggregate limit (but they do count toward the $3,750,000 maximum outstanding SBA guaranty exposure).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Maximum loan amounts and guaranty limits/percentages
source_reference.source_text: 7(a) Small loans have a maximum loan amount of $350,000 per project (including other 7(a) loans made within 90 days). The maximum aggregate SBA Express loan amount is $500,000... inclusive of all outstanding SBA Express... loans the Applicant and its Affiliates may have... The maximum dollar amount outstanding of SBA’s guaranty... must not exceed $3,750,000... The maximum guaranty percentage is... 85%... 75%... For SBA Express loans: 50%.

examples: Applicant requests a $400,000 7(a) Small loan for one project: not eligible because max is $350,000 per project (including other 7(a) loans within 90 days).
Applicant has $450,000 outstanding SBA Express and applies for $100,000 SBA Express: aggregate would be $550,000 including affiliates—must be declined or resized to comply with $500,000 cap.
Borrower and affiliates already have $3,700,000 guaranteed outstanding. New proposed guaranteed portion is $100,000: would exceed $3,750,000 maximum outstanding SBA guaranty and is not permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/reduction; SBA lender oversight enforcement; potential civil remedies under SBA lender agreement

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 closing packages must be submitted electronically by SBA counsel deadline and late packages roll to next debenture sale month
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 debenture closings, the CDC (as the submitting institution) must electronically submit closing packages by the deadline established by SBA counsel. Late closing packages will not be accepted for the current month and will be held over for the next month’s debenture sale.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 debenture closing packages submitted to SBA counsel for debenture sale processing.
business_rules.consequences: If submitted after the SBA counsel deadline, the closing package is not accepted for the current month and is held over for the next month’s debenture sale, potentially delaying funding.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Closing and Post-Closing Activities > CDC’s Responsibilities
source_reference.source_text: Electronically submit closing packages by the deadline established by SBA counsel. No late closing packages will be accepted. SBA counsel will hold late packages over for the next month’s debenture sale.

examples: CDC submits the closing package 1 day after SBA counsel’s deadline for the June sale; SBA counsel holds the package for the July debenture sale.
CDC meets the deadline for the May sale; the package is processed for May with no timing deferral.
CDC submits timely but missing items; SBA counsel may require changes, but the timeliness rule is still satisfied because submission met the deadline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Debenture sale delay (package held over) and potential SBA counsel processing hold for missed deadlines

business_rules.rule_name: CDC must issue no adverse change opinion within 14 business days of SLPC file-ship request using financial statements dated within 120 days of CSA cut-off
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 debenture closings, each CDC must issue a written opinion that, to the best of its knowledge, there has been no unremedied substantial adverse change in the Applicant’s (or Operating Company’s) ability to repay the 504 loan since application submission to SBA. The opinion must be made within 14 business days prior to the CDC’s request to SLPC to ship the file and must be supported by financial statements dated no earlier than 120 calendar days from the published CSA Cut-Off Date for the applicable Debenture Funding Date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans proceeding to debenture closing; timing tied to (1) published CSA Cut-Off Date for the applicable Debenture Funding Date and (2) CDC request to SLPC to ship file.
business_rules.consequences: If SBA disagrees with the determination of no unremedied substantial adverse change, the debenture will not close until SBA is satisfied any adverse change has been remedied. If timing/financial statement currency requirements are not met, the CDC must refresh documentation/opinion per SBA process.
business_rules.exceptions: Process differences: for all 504 loans except ALP and PCLP, CDC provides finding and financial statements to SLPC; ALP and PCLP submit closing package to SBA counsel and retain the finding and financial statements in their files.

source_reference.section_id: Closing and Post-Closing Activities > CDC’s Responsibilities
source_reference.source_text: Each CDC must issue a written opinion based upon financial statements current within 120 calendar days from the published Central Servicing Agent (CSA) Cut-Off Date for the applicable Debenture Funding Date that to the best of its knowledge there has been no unremedied substantial adverse change... This CDC opinion must be made within 14 business days prior to its request to SLPC to ship the file

examples: CSA cut-off date is July 15; CDC uses financials dated April 20 (86 days old) and issues the opinion within 14 business days before requesting SLPC ship file—compliant.
CDC uses financials dated February 1 (over 120 days from the CSA cut-off date) to support its opinion—noncompliant; CDC must obtain acceptable financial statements and re-issue the opinion.
SBA disputes CDC’s no-adverse-change conclusion due to new delinquency; debenture closing is delayed until SBA is satisfied the adverse change is remedied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Debenture closing blocked until SBA satisfied adverse change remedied; potential SBA oversight criticism for defective closing file support

business_rules.rule_name: New no adverse change opinion required if debenture closing not consummated in the month after opinion and supporting financials would be over 120 days old at file request
business_rules.description: If the debenture closing is not consummated in the month following the CDC’s opinion of no unremedied substantial adverse change, the CDC must prepare a new opinion and follow the same process when the financial statements supporting the opinion will be more than 120 days old when the CDC requests the file from SLPC for closing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CDC previously issued an opinion of no unremedied substantial adverse change but the debenture closing is delayed beyond the next month; triggered if the supporting financial statements exceed the 120-day age limit at the time of requesting the file from SLPC.
business_rules.consequences: CDC must refresh the opinion and supporting financial statements under the same process; failure risks inability to proceed with closing and SBA disagreement/closing delay.
business_rules.exceptions: If supporting financial statements remain no more than 120 days old at time of SLPC file request, a new opinion is not required solely due to the delay (per example in SOP).

source_reference.section_id: Closing and Post-Closing Activities > CDC’s Responsibilities
source_reference.source_text: files. If the debenture closing is not consummated in the month following the CDC’s opinion of no unremedied substantial adverse change, the CDC must prepare a new opinion and follow the same process described above if the financial statement supporting the opinion will be more than 120 days old when the

examples: CDC opinion approved in April; closing delayed to June; financials are still within 120 days old at time CDC requests file—no new opinion required.
CDC opinion approved in April; closing delayed to July; supporting financials will be >120 days old when CDC requests file—CDC must issue a new opinion with refreshed financials.
CDC issues new opinion but still uses stale financials; SBA can require further correction and closing may be delayed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Debenture closing delay / SBA counsel hold pending refreshed opinion and acceptable financial statement support

business_rules.rule_name: CDC must forward original collateral file documents listed on SBA Form 2286 to CLSC within 30 days after debenture sale
business_rules.description: After the debenture sale, the CDC must forward the original of all documents listed on SBA Form 2286 (the 504 Debenture Closing Checklist, which serves as the original collateral listing) to the CLSC within 30 days after the debenture sale. The CDC must forward the collateral file containing all original documents listed on the Checklist and maintain the collateral file in a manner acceptable to SBA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans after debenture sale completion; the 30-day timeframe runs from the debenture sale date.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to timely forward original collateral documents can result in SBA servicing/oversight findings and may delay post-closing processing or create enforceability/collateral perfection risk.
business_rules.exceptions: If all originals are not received by 30 days after debenture sale date, CDC must send the documents it does have and send additional documents when received (text indicates continued obligation even when incomplete).

source_reference.section_id: Closing and Post-Closing Activities > CDC’s Responsibilities
source_reference.source_text: Forward the original of all documents listed on SBA Form 2286, “504 Debenture Closing Checklist,” (Checklist) (which serves as the original collateral listing) to the CLSC within 30 days after the debenture sale.

examples: Debenture sale date is August 10; CDC forwards original collateral file documents to CLSC by September 9—compliant.
CDC misses the 30-day deadline and cannot evidence delivery to CLSC—noncompliant and subject to SBA post-closing findings.
CDC lacks one original lien release by day 30; CDC forwards all received originals by day 30 and sends the remaining document upon receipt—compliant with exception handling.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA post-closing deficiency finding; potential servicing restrictions or corrective action directives

business_rules.rule_name: CDC may not unilaterally approve borrower ownership changes for 12 months after final disbursement of SBA 504 loan
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 servicing, CDCs may not unilaterally approve any adjustment to or change in the ownership of a Borrower, including a change in percentage of ownership, for 12 months after final disbursement on any loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any SBA 504 loan after final disbursement through the following 12 months; includes any ownership adjustment/change or percentage ownership change.
business_rules.consequences: CDC must not approve such changes independently; changes require SBA servicing channel involvement (e.g., request changes by contacting SBA CLSC). Unilateral approval within 12 months constitutes noncompliance and may be cited in SBA oversight or servicing reviews.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section C, Ch 2: 504 E-Tran Terms and Conditions through Disbursement > Post-Disbursement Issues
source_reference.source_text: CDCs may not unilaterally approve any adjustment to or change in the ownership of a Borrower, including a change in percentage of ownership, for 12 months after final disbursement on any loan.

examples: At month 6 after final disbursement, borrower requests to add a new 30% equity partner; CDC cannot approve unilaterally and must route through SBA servicing process.
At month 13 after final disbursement, borrower requests a minor ownership percentage change; the 12-month unilateral restriction no longer applies (other SBA servicing requirements may still apply).
CDC approves a transfer at month 3 without SBA involvement—noncompliant; subject to SBA servicing action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA servicing/oversight criticism; potential corrective action and restrictions on delegated authority

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) piggyback structures prohibited when SBA loan is junior or unsecured relative to non-SBA loan approved within 90 days
business_rules.description: For SBA-guaranteed 7(a) loans, the lender must not structure credit as a prohibited “piggyback” arrangement that creates a preference in favor of the lender. Piggyback financing exists when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single borrower at or about the same time (defined as approvals within 90 days), financing the same or similar purpose, and the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured with a junior lien position or no lien position on the collateral securing the non-guaranteed loan(s). The lender must avoid taking actions that establish a preference in favor of the lender under 13 CFR § 120.411.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION originates/holds an SBA-guaranteed 7(a) loan and also makes (or another lender makes) a non-SBA loan to the same borrower at or about the same time for the same or similar purpose.
business_rules.consequences: If structured as a prohibited piggyback (junior/no lien for SBA loan relative to non-SBA loan collateral), the loan structure is not permitted under SBA Loan Program Requirements; may result in SBA denial of guaranty purchase, repair/denial of liability, or program eligibility findings in review/oversight.
business_rules.exceptions: Not considered piggyback: (i) both SBA-guaranteed and non-SBA loans are for working capital and the non-SBA loan is secured only by working/trading assets; (ii) shared lien position (pari passu) is not piggyback when the maturity of the non-SBA loan is not shorter than the maturity of the SBA-guaranteed loan.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards > Collateral > Adequacy of Collateral
source_reference.source_text: A Lender may not take any action in connection with an SBA-guaranteed loan that establishes a preference in favor of the Lender (13 CFR § 120.411). The Lender must not have a 7(a) loan in a “piggyback” structure. Piggyback financing occurs when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single Borrower at or about the same time... SBA considers “at or about the same time” to mean loans approved within 90 days of each other.

examples: A bank approves a conventional term loan secured by a first mortgage on the property on January 1 and an SBA 7(a) loan for the same acquisition on February 15 secured only by a second mortgage: prohibited piggyback (within 90 days; junior SBA lien).

A lender provides an SBA 7(a) working capital term loan and a separate conventional revolving line secured only by A/R and inventory during the same month: not piggyback per SBA exception for working capital with trading assets collateral.
Two lenders each take a pari passu first lien on the same collateral securing both an SBA 7(a) loan and a non-SBA loan, and the non-SBA maturity is not shorter than the SBA maturity: not piggyback.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial and program eligibility findings; regulatory criticism in SBA lender review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Form 159 must be completed to disclose Agent fees for SBA 504 loans
business_rules.description: If an Agent or Agents charge an Applicant fees in connection with obtaining a 504 loan, the fees must be disclosed to SBA by completing SBA Form 159, in accordance with the SOP’s referenced process requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans when any Agent charges any fee to the Applicant related to obtaining the 504 loan.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to disclose Agent fees on SBA Form 159 creates SBA program non-compliance exposure and may result in SBA review, corrective action, and potential processing delays or escalations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Fees an Agent May Charge an Applicant for Packaging and Other Services
source_reference.source_text: If an Agent or Agents charge an Applicant fees in connection with obtaining a 504 loan, the Agent(s) must disclose the fees to SBA by completing SBA Form 159 in accordance with Paragraph E.7 below.

examples: A packager charges $2,000 for business plan preparation for a 504 application: SBA Form 159 is completed to disclose the fee.
Two Agents split packaging and referral work and each charges the borrower: both fees are disclosed to SBA via SBA Form 159.
An Agent charges only after submission but before approval: the fee still must be disclosed on SBA Form 159 because it is connected with obtaining the 504 loan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program non-compliance finding; potential corrective action and heightened review.

business_rules.rule_name: Engaging SBA 504 professional services contractors without required SBA approval triggers OCRM reporting and application processing delays
business_rules.description: If a CDC engages a contractor for functions requiring prior SBA approval without obtaining approval in accordance with the required process, the non-compliance will be reported to OCRM. In addition to other appropriate actions, loan application requests sent to the SLPC by an individual employed under the unapproved contract may be delayed until the professional services contract is approved by the D/FA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CDC (as part of SBA 504 lending operations) engages a contractor for a function that requires prior SBA approval (e.g., management, marketing, packaging, processing, closing, servicing, liquidating, certain legal services, certain independent loan reviews, co-employment) but fails to obtain that approval before engagement.
business_rules.consequences: Non-compliance reported to OCRM; loan applications submitted by contractor personnel may be delayed for processing and approval until contract approval is obtained.
business_rules.exceptions: Applies to functions requiring prior approval; does not apply to functions explicitly permitted without SBA approval (e.g., accounting, most legal services not tied to liquidation/litigation, IT, independent loan review by non-CDC).

source_reference.section_id: Review and Pre-Approval
source_reference.source_text: If a CDC engages the services of a contractor without obtaining SBA’s approval in accordance with the process described below, the CDC’s non-compliance will be reported to OCRM. In addition to any other appropriate action, any loan application requests sent to the SLPC by an individual employed under the contract may be delayed for processing and approval until such time as the professional services contract is approved by the D/FA.

examples: A CDC outsources loan closing to a contractor without D/FA pre-approval: the non-compliance is reported to OCRM and SLPC submissions by that contractor may be delayed.
A CDC hires a contractor to perform packaging without submitting the contract for approval: approvals can be held until D/FA approval is obtained.
A CDC contracts for IT support (a permitted category without SBA approval): this specific consequence is not triggered because prior approval is not required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Application processing delay; referral/reporting to SBA OCRM; SBA corrective action.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) packaging fee limits include flat fee up to $2,500 and percentage caps of 5% up to $150,000 and 3% above $150,000 with $30,000 maximum and single-fee rule for split requests
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, a lender may charge reasonable packaging fees customary for similar lenders in the geographic area, but must comply with SBA fee limits and disclosures. The lender must: (i) advise the applicant in writing that the applicant is not required to obtain/pay for unwanted services; (ii) itemize in writing any fees charged by category under 13 CFR § 120.221; and (iii) disclose amounts charged in E-Tran. SBA permits a flat fee of up to $2,500 per loan without documenting the service performed. For fees exceeding $2,500, the lender must complete SBA Form 159. Hourly packaging fees must be reasonable/customary for services actually performed and consistent with the lender’s similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. If charged as a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed what the lender charges on similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans or the following, whichever is less: 5% for loans of $150,000 or less; 3% for loans in excess of $150,000; and the maximum percentage-based fee is capped at $30,000. The lender may not split a loan into two loans to charge an additional fee; even if legitimately split (e.g., term loan + line of credit), the lender may charge only one packaging fee within the maximums based on combined loan amounts. SBA may review fees at any time and the lender must refund any fee SBA deems unreasonable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loan origination whenever the lender or its Associates charge packaging fees to an Applicant or Borrower in connection with the SBA-guaranteed loan.
business_rules.consequences: If fees exceed caps, are not properly disclosed/itemized, or are effectively duplicated (including via splitting a request), SBA may require refund of unreasonable/excess fees. Noncompliance can be cited in SBA oversight and may create issues at guaranty purchase or during review.
business_rules.exceptions: A new packaging fee may be charged if the applicant later applies for a new loan for a different project/purpose; loans approved more than 90 days apart are considered to be for a different project or purpose.

source_reference.section_id: Packaging Fees (13 CFR § 120.221(a))
source_reference.source_text: SBA permits the Lender to charge the Applicant a flat fee of up to $2,500 per loan without documenting the service performed... For fees charged based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed... 5% for loans $150,000 or less; 3% for loans in excess of $150,000; the maximum fee... is $30,000. The Lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging an additional fee... the Lender may only charge the Applicant one fee... based on the combined loan amounts.

examples: Lender charges a flat packaging fee of $2,500 on a $75,000 SBA 7(a) loan and discloses it in E-Tran; no service documentation is required under the flat-fee rule.
Lender charges 6% packaging fee on a $120,000 SBA 7(a) loan ($7,200); exceeds the 5% cap and must be reduced/refunded if charged.
Applicant requests a $500,000 term loan and $200,000 line of credit for the same project; lender cannot charge two separate packaging fees and must apply caps using the combined $700,000 amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Fee refund requirement; SBA lender oversight finding; potential issues at SBA guaranty purchase review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 CDC may rely on third party lender CIP only with annual certification; otherwise CDC must verify identity
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, SBA does not expect the CDC to duplicate the Third Party Lender’s Customer Identification Program (CIP) procedures if (i) the Third Party Lender is regulated by a Federal functional regulator and (ii) the Third Party Lender submits annual certifications to the CDC that it (or its agent) will comply with the CIP requirements of 31 CFR § 1020.220 for all third party financings of 504 loans. Under those circumstances, the CDC’s CIP may state it will rely on the Third Party Lender to verify the SBA Applicant’s identity. If the Third Party Lender has not submitted the requisite annual certification, the CDC must perform its own verification of identity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans involving a CDC and a Third Party Lender providing third-party financing.
business_rules.consequences: If CDC relies on Third Party Lender CIP without the required annual certification, the CDC is noncompliant with SBA CIP expectations and must perform independent verification to cure; continued failure creates SBA review/exam findings and potential program participation consequences.
business_rules.exceptions: CDC may still choose to perform its own identity verification even if Third Party Lender has complied with 31 CFR § 1020.220.

source_reference.section_id: I. Customer Identification Programs, paragraph 2 (504 loans reliance on Third Party Lender)
source_reference.source_text: For 504 loans: SBA does not expect CDCs to duplicate the procedures of the Third Party Lender if the Third Party Lender is regulated by a Federal functional regulator ... and submits annual certifications to the CDC that it ... will comply with the CIP requirements of 31 CFR § 1020.220 ... it is acceptable to SBA if a CDC’s CIP states that the CDC will rely on the Third Party Lender to verify the identity of the SBA Applicant ... If the Third Party Lender has not submitted the requisite annual certification to the CDC, the CDC must perform its own verification of identity.

examples: Third Party Lender provides its annual CIP certification to the CDC: CDC relies on lender’s CIP and documents reliance in its CIP procedures.
Third Party Lender is regulated but fails to provide annual certification: CDC must perform its own CIP verification for the applicant before submission/closing.
CDC performs independent verification even though lender certified annually: permitted and may be used as a compensating control.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA compliance finding; potential program participation criticism

business_rules.rule_name: SBA applicant ineligibility when any owner, required guarantor, or associate is an ineligible person within six-month lookback unless fully divested before SBA loan number
business_rules.description: SBA financing is limited to businesses whose 100% direct and/or indirect owners and SBA-required guarantors (except where otherwise noted) are U.S. citizens, U.S. Nationals, or Lawful Permanent Residents (LPRs). Entity owners must be created/organized/incorporated in the U.S., its territories, or possessions, and all direct/indirect owners and guarantors must have their primary residence in the U.S., its territories, or possessions. The SBA Lender must certify in E-Tran that no direct or indirect owner or guarantor is an Ineligible Person, and no loan may be made if any direct or indirect owner is an Ineligible Person. Six-month lookback: the Applicant is ineligible if any Associate of the business commencing 6 months prior to issuance of the SBA loan number is an Ineligible Person, unless the Ineligible Person completely divests prior to issuance of the SBA loan number and severs any relationship with the Applicant (and associated EPC/OC) in any capacity, including as an employee (paid or unpaid), for the life of the loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans at underwriting/approval through SBA loan number issuance; includes direct/indirect ownership and SBA-required guarantors, and associates within the stated lookback window.
business_rules.consequences: If an Ineligible Person is present as a direct/indirect owner (or an Associate within the six-month lookback without full divestment and severance), the Applicant is ineligible and the SBA loan must not be made; failure to comply creates severe SBA eligibility and guaranty enforceability risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Text notes exceptions may exist elsewhere (e.g., limited guaranties in certain collateral situations), but the general rule prohibits any direct/indirect owner being an Ineligible Person and imposes severance for associates for the life of the loan when divestment is used to cure eligibility.

source_reference.section_id: Businesses Owned by Non-U.S. Citizens: eligibility limitation; Ineligible Person certification; six-month lookback and divestment cure
source_reference.source_text: SBA financing is limited to businesses with 100% direct and/or indirect owners and SBA-required guarantors ... that are U.S. citizens, U.S. Nationals, or who are Lawful Permanent Residents (LPRs) ... Entity owners ... must be created ... in the United States ... SBA Lenders must certify in E-Tran that no direct or indirect owner or guarantor is an Ineligible Person. No loan may be made if any direct or indirect owner is an Ineligible Person... Six-month lookback: The Applicant business is ineligible if any Associate ... commencing 6 months prior to the date of issuance of the SBA loan number is an Ineligible Person, unless the Ineligible Person completely divests ... prior to ... SBA loan number and severs any relationship ... for the life of the 7(a) or 504 loan.

examples: A 30% indirect owner is a visa holder (an Ineligible Person): the SBA loan is prohibited unless ownership is fully divested before SBA loan number issuance (and other conditions met).

A former 10% owner (an Ineligible Person) remained an unpaid advisor within the last 6 months: even if divested, eligibility is not cured unless they sever all relationships in any capacity for the life of the loan.
All owners are U.S. citizens, but an entity owner is incorporated offshore: ineligible because entity owners must be created/organized/incorporated in the U.S. or its territories/possessions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA ineligibility determination; denial/repair of guaranty; program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 LGBTQI+-owned business status is applicant-determined using two-prong test and financial institution must provide the definition when requesting the status
business_rules.description: For Regulation B Subpart B (small business lending data collection), a business is an LGBTQI+-owned business only if it satisfies both prongs: (1) one or more LGBTQI+ individuals own or control more than 50% of the business (ownership and control need not both exceed 50%); and (2) 50% or more of net profits or losses accrue to one or more LGBTQI+ individuals. When asking an applicant to provide LGBTQI+-owned business status for reporting, the financial institution must provide the definition of LGBTQI+-owned business. The financial institution is neither permitted nor required to make its own determination of the applicant’s LGBTQI+-owned business status; it must rely on the applicant’s determination (and may optionally provide additional clarifying definitions if helpful).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions under Regulation B Subpart B when collecting LGBTQI+-owned business status data from applicants (as referenced for § 1002.107(a)(18)).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution fails to provide the definition when requesting status, or if it attempts to determine/override applicant status, the institution risks 1071/Reg B compliance violations, supervisory criticism, and potential enforcement actions related to data collection practices and integrity.
business_rules.exceptions: The institution may (but is not required to) provide additional clarification using definitions of ownership, control, and accrual concepts referenced in the commentary. If asked by the applicant, the institution must provide additional clarification by referencing the definition of LGBTQI+ individual in § 1002.102(k).

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002—Official Interpretations > § 1002.102(l) LGBTQI+-owned business
source_reference.source_text: In order to be an LGBTQI+-owned business... a business must satisfy both prongs... one or more LGBTQI+ individuals must own or control more than 50 percent of the business... Second, 50 percent or more of the net profits or losses must accrue to one or more LGBTQI+ individuals... A financial institution shall provide an applicant with the definition... The financial institution is neither permitted nor required to make its own determination regarding the applicant’s LGBTQI+-owned business status.

examples: Applicant indicates LGBTQI+-owned and reports that 60% of net profits accrue to LGBTQI+ owners: institution records applicant response and does not attempt to verify beyond required data collection procedures.

Business is controlled by an LGBTQI+ individual but LGBTQI+ individuals own 0% equity; if 50%+ net profits/losses accrue to one or more LGBTQI+ individuals, it can qualify under prongs; institution provides the definition and relies on applicant determination.
Institution’s loan officer believes the applicant does not qualify and changes the response: prohibited—financial institution must not make its own determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism; potential supervisory or enforcement action for 1071/Reg B data collection noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express and Export Express tax verification must be completed post-disbursement when immediate disbursement is requested, or guaranty may be repaired or denied
business_rules.description: For SBA Express and Export Express, a lender may close and disburse immediately if disbursement is requested by the borrower, but the lender must follow up and verify business financial data with IRS tax data and document the loan file accordingly. If, after disbursement, the lender is unable to obtain the tax transcript and, when applicable, reconcile the tax transcript with the applicant’s financial data, SBA’s guaranty may be subject to repair or denial. Additionally, if the lender uses business financial information to determine creditworthiness, it must follow the IRS tax verification process; if it does not use business financial information, it must still obtain tax transcripts to verify returns were filed and to determine size, but reconciliation is not required.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Express and Export Express loans when (a) disbursement is requested immediately, and/or (b) the lender uses business financial information for creditworthiness determination, and/or (c) the lender does not use business financial information and must still obtain transcripts for filing/size verification.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender cannot obtain required transcripts and, when applicable, reconcile them after disbursement, SBA guaranty may be subject to repair or denial; lender file must evidence follow-up verification and documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: If lender does not use business financial information to determine creditworthiness, reconciliation of transcripts is not required (but transcripts are still required for filing verification and size determination).

source_reference.section_id: SBA Express and Export Express Programs
source_reference.source_text: SBA Express and Export Express Programs: If the Lender uses business financial information to determine the creditworthiness of an SBA Loan, the Lender must follow the IRS tax verification process set out in this section. If the Lender does not use business financial information to determine creditworthiness... Lender must obtain IRS tax transcripts... but reconciliation of the tax transcripts is not required. SBA Express and Export Express Lenders are authorized to close and disburse a loan immediately... however... If... unable to obtain the tax transcript and, when applicable, reconcile... the guaranty may be subject to repair or denial.

examples: Export Express lender disburses at borrower request on closing day; lender later fails to obtain IRS transcripts—SBA guaranty may be repaired or denied.
Export Express lender uses applicant’s internally prepared financial statements to approve the loan—lender must complete IRS tax verification process described in the section.

Export Express lender uses only a non-financial, model-based approach for creditworthiness—lender must still obtain transcripts to confirm returns filed and determine size, but does not have to reconcile financials.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair or denial; regulatory criticism during SBA lender oversight

business_rules.rule_name: ECOA and Fair Housing Act prohibit discrimination or discouragement in any aspect of a credit transaction based on prohibited factors
business_rules.description: Under the ECOA, it is unlawful for a lender to discriminate on a prohibited basis in any aspect of a credit transaction. Under ECOA and the Fair Housing Act, it is unlawful to discriminate in a residential real-estate-related transaction. Prohibited conduct includes: failing to provide information or services or providing different information or services; discouraging or selectively encouraging applicants; refusing to extend credit or using different standards; varying terms (amount, interest rate, duration, type); using different standards to evaluate collateral; treating borrowers differently in servicing or default remedies; or using different standards for pooling/packaging in the secondary market. Lenders also may not express a preference based on prohibited factors.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all credit transactions and residential real-estate-related transactions covered by ECOA and/or FHAct, including marketing, application intake, underwriting, pricing, collateral evaluation, servicing, collections, and secondary market activities.
business_rules.consequences: Potential finding of discrimination in examinations; enforcement actions can include supervisory actions and civil money penalties; requirement to remediate impacted applicants/borrowers and adjust policies/procedures.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt (separate ECOA/FHAct provisions may permit certain age-related considerations and special purpose credit programs, but not addressed in this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Overview of Fair Lending Laws and Regulations; Types of Lending Discrimination
source_reference.source_text: Under the ECOA, it is unlawful for a lender to discriminate on a prohibited basis in any aspect of a credit transaction... a lender may not, because of a prohibited factor... Fail to provide information or services... Discourage or selectively encourage applicants... Refuse to extend credit or use different standards... Vary the terms of credit offered... Use different standards to evaluate collateral... Treat a borrower differently in servicing... Use different standards for pooling or packaging...

examples: A lender quotes higher interest rates to otherwise similarly qualified applicants because of national origin—prohibited disparate treatment.
A lender provides application assistance to non-minority applicants but denies minority applicants without allowing clarification—potential disparate treatment.

A lender policy sets a minimum loan amount that disproportionately excludes minority applicants; absent business necessity and lack of less discriminatory alternative, this can present disparate impact risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory enforcement action and potential civil money penalty; required corrective action plan

business_rules.rule_name: ALP Express 504 loans must be designated at submission and may not exceed 500,000 dollars and have no loan loss reserve requirement
business_rules.description: An ALP Express Loan is a 504 loan in an amount not more than $500,000 made by an ALP CDC and designated by the ALP CDC as an ALP Express Loan during submission. SBA will not review the credit decision made by the ALP CDC for an ALP Express Loan, but the loan remains subject to final approval by SLPC as to eligibility. There is no loan loss reserve requirement for loans made under ALP Express delegated authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans processed by an ALP CDC using ALP Express authority; triggered when the CDC chooses ALP Express processing and the 504 loan amount is ≤ $500,000 and properly designated during submission.
business_rules.consequences: If loan amount exceeds $500,000 or loan is not properly designated during submission, it cannot be processed as ALP Express. Although SBA will not review the credit decision, SLPC eligibility approval still applies. No loan loss reserve requirement applies when properly processed under ALP Express authority.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt for the $500,000 maximum; SLPC retains eligibility approval authority.

source_reference.section_id: Processing Methods – ALP Express
source_reference.source_text: ALP Express: ALP CDCs in good standing may underwrite, approve, close, and service ALP Express Loans. An ALP Express Loan is a 504 loan in an amount that is not more than $500,000 which has been made by an ALP CDC and designated by the ALP CDC as an ALP Express Loan during submission. SBA will not review the credit decision... However, the ALP Express Loan is subject to final approval... as to eligibility... There is no loan loss reserve requirement...

examples: CDC submits a 504 loan for $450,000 and designates it as ALP Express at submission: SBA will not review credit decision; SLPC still performs eligibility approval; no loan loss reserve is required.
CDC attempts to submit $750,000 as ALP Express: not permitted because amount exceeds $500,000; must use another processing method.
CDC forgets to designate the loan as ALP Express during submission: the loan is not treated as ALP Express even if $500,000 or less.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing/eligibility rejection and program compliance findings if mis-designated or over limit
