business_rules.rule_name: Franchise Directory listing is required before processing SBA applications and non-delegated lenders cannot submit while delegated lenders cannot approve if not listed
business_rules.description: Before processing an SBA loan application involving a franchise relationship, SBA lenders must check the SBA Franchise Directory. If the applicant operates under multiple agreements (multiple product lines), the lender must ensure all agreements that meet the FTC definition of a franchise are on the Directory; if any are not on the Directory, the application cannot proceed. If one of the applicant’s brands/agreements has been determined by SBA to be ineligible for SBA financial assistance, the loan cannot be processed regardless of FTC franchise status. For applicants operating under a single non-critical agreement (representing 50% or less of revenues), the lender must check the Directory to ensure that if the agreement meets the FTC definition of a franchise it is on the Directory; if it meets FTC franchise definition but is not on the Directory, the application cannot proceed. Additionally, for non-delegated loans SBA lenders cannot submit the application to SBA, and for delegated loans SBA lenders cannot approve the loan under delegated authority when the brand listing requirement is not satisfied.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during SBA loan intake and eligibility determination for applicants operating under franchise or franchise-like agreements (FTC definition), including multiple agreements and single non-critical agreements.
business_rules.consequences: If required franchise agreement(s) are not listed on the SBA Franchise Directory, the application cannot proceed; for non-delegated processing the lender cannot submit to SBA; for delegated processing the lender cannot approve under delegated authority. Proceeding despite a missing listing risks SBA ineligibility and delegated lender bears risk of incorrect determination in delegated cases.
business_rules.exceptions: If the agreement is not listed and the lender determines it does not meet FTC franchise definition, lender must determine brand eligibility before proceeding; SBA makes final determination for non-delegated applications and will review delegated determinations during oversight/purchase/504 pre-closing.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements — SBA Franchise Directory requirements and exceptions
source_reference.source_text: For non-delegated loans, SBA Lenders cannot submit the application to SBA, and for delegated loans, SBA Lenders cannot approve the loan under delegated authority... When an Applicant operates under multiple agreements... SBA Lenders first must check the Directory to ensure all of Applicant’s agreements... are on the Directory. If any... are not on the Directory, the application cannot proceed... If the Applicant is operating under a single agreement... 50% or less of the Applicant’s revenues... If the agreement meets the FTC definition of a franchise, but is not on the Directory, the application cannot proceed.

examples: Applicant operates under two franchise-like agreements, both meeting FTC franchise definition; one is not listed on the Directory. Lender must stop processing; delegated lender cannot approve and non-delegated lender cannot submit.

Applicant has a truck-rental agreement representing 10% of revenues. If it meets FTC franchise definition and is not on the Directory, the application cannot proceed; if lender determines it does not meet FTC franchise definition, lender must document brand eligibility determination before proceeding.
SBA has determined one of the applicant’s brands is ineligible for SBA financial assistance. The loan cannot be processed even if other agreements are listed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection; denial/repair at guaranty purchase; delegated lender bears risk for incorrect determination

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 covered credit transaction screening must exclude true leases under the UCC definition regardless of product name
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 (Regulation B, Subpart B), a financial institution must treat a transaction as excluded (not a covered credit transaction) when it is a lease meeting the UCC-based definition: a transfer from one business to another of the right to possession and use of goods for a term, primarily for business or commercial (including agricultural) purposes, in return for consideration. A lease does not include a sale (including sale on approval or sale or return) or any transaction that results in retention or creation of a security interest. The product name used by the financial institution is not determinative; classification must follow substance of the transaction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution determines whether an application/transaction is a covered credit transaction for 12 CFR part 1002, Subpart B (CFPB Section 1071 small business lending data collection and reporting).
business_rules.consequences: If the transaction is a lease as defined, the institution must not treat it as a covered credit transaction for Subpart B data collection/reporting purposes. If the institution misclassifies a lease as covered credit (or misclassifies a credit transaction as a lease to evade coverage), it risks examination criticism, data integrity findings, and potential enforcement for inaccurate reporting or evasion.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. The rule clarifies that a sale or security interest transaction is not a lease for this purpose.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.104(b) comment 104(b)-2 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final Rule  ... comment 104(b)-2 provides that the term covered credit transaction does not cover leases, and that a lease, for purposes of subpart B, is a transfer from one business to another of the right to possession and use of goods for a term, and for primarily business or commercial (including agricultural) purposes, in return for consideration. It further states that a lease does not include a sale... or a transaction resulting in the retention or creation of a security interest. In addition, comment 104(b)-2 clarifies that the name used by the financial institution for a product is not determinative...

examples: An equipment lessor offers a 36-month business equipment lease where the lessee pays for use and returns the equipment at term end with no ownership transfer: exclude from Section 1071 covered credit transactions.

A transaction labeled a “lease” requires payments substantially equivalent to the asset value and includes a $1 purchase option at end, effectively creating a security interest/sale: do not treat as an excluded lease; evaluate as a covered credit transaction if otherwise within scope.

A lender rebrands a term loan product as “lease financing” but documentation creates a security interest in the goods: product name is irrelevant; treat based on substance (covered credit transaction if otherwise in scope).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; potential CFPB enforcement for inaccurate or evasive Section 1071 data practices

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 business applicant must meet SBA business concern definition including for-profit status and joint venture foreign participation cap of 49%
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071, the institution must treat an applicant as a “business” only if it meets the SBA “business concern or concern” definition in 13 CFR 121.105, including that it is organized for profit, has a place of business in the United States, and operates primarily within the United States or makes a significant contribution to the U.S. economy. A business concern may take various legal forms (including trust, sole proprietorship, partnership, LLC, corporation, joint venture, or cooperative). If the business form is a joint venture, foreign business entity participation in the joint venture must not exceed 49%. Not-for-profit applicants do not meet the “organized for profit” element and therefore are outside the scope of Section 1071 data collection/reporting as non-business applicants.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether an applicant is a “business” for purposes of CFPB Section 1071 (Regulation B, Subpart B) data collection and reporting requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the applicant is not a “business concern” (e.g., not-for-profit), the institution must not collect/report Section 1071 data for that application as a covered small business credit application. If the institution incorrectly treats an ineligible applicant as a business (or excludes an eligible business), it risks inaccurate reporting and examination findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the rule relies on SBA definitions and interpretations.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(a) Business (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final Rule  ... define the term business as having the same meaning as the term “business concern or concern” in 13 CFR 121.105. ... includes elements such as being “a business entity organized for profit” that has “a place of business located in the United States” and “operates primarily within the United States or ... makes a significant contribution to the U.S. economy.” ... may take ... legal forms, including a trust... joint venture ... except that where the form is a joint venture there can be no more than 49 percent participation by foreign business entities... financial institutions are not required to collect and report data for not-for-profit applicants, because they are not “organized for profit”...

examples: A 501(c)(3) nonprofit community organization applies for a line of credit: exclude from Section 1071 data collection because it is not organized for profit.
A Delaware LLC with a U.S. office and operations primarily in the U.S. applies for credit: treat as a business applicant for Section 1071 scoping.
A joint venture applying for credit has 55% foreign business entity participation: it fails the SBA business concern condition; treat as not meeting the 13 CFR 121.105 definition as described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; potential resubmission/correction requirements for inaccurate Section 1071 reporting

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 trust applicants require trustee to be reported as principal owner while trust entities are not principal owners
business_rules.description: For Section 1071 principal owner data collection/reporting, only individuals who are direct owners are considered principal owners. Therefore, when a small business applicant’s ownership interests or assets are owned by a trust (an entity), the trust is not a principal owner and the institution does not collect/report principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex (or number of principal owners) based solely on the trust entity. If the applicant itself is a trust, then a trustee is considered the principal owner of the trust for reporting purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution is collecting/reporting principal owner information for a covered application under 12 CFR part 1002, Subpart B, and the applicant is a trust or is owned by a trust.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution incorrectly treats a trust entity as a principal owner, it may improperly collect/report demographic data or misstate principal owner counts. If the institution fails to identify a trustee as principal owner when the applicant is a trust, it risks missing required principal owner reporting fields.
business_rules.exceptions: Only trusts organized for profit meet the definition of “business concern” and can be within scope for Section 1071; non-profit trusts are excluded under the business definition.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(a) Business (trust treatment discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Treatment under the final rule differs... When a financial institution seeks to extend credit to a small business applicant whose ownership interests or assets are owned by a trust... only individuals who are direct owners are considered principal owners... entities such as trusts would not be principal owners and thus the financial institution would not need to collect or report principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex... And... if the applicant for a covered credit transaction is a trust, a trustee is considered the principal owner of the trust for reporting purposes.

examples: Applicant is an operating LLC whose 100% membership interest is held by a family trust: do not treat the trust entity as a principal owner; do not collect/report principal owner demographics on the trust itself.
Applicant is a for-profit trust applying for business credit: treat the trustee as the principal owner for Section 1071 principal owner reporting.
Applicant is a non-profit trust: it is not a business concern (not organized for profit) and falls outside Section 1071 reporting scope.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; data resubmission/correction requirements for erroneous principal owner reporting

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 small business eligibility must use 5 million dollars gross annual revenue threshold
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071, the Bureau’s small business definition uses a gross annual revenue threshold of $5,000,000 for determining whether an applicant is a small business for purposes of the rule’s data collection and reporting requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution must determine whether an applicant is a “small business” for Section 1071 (Regulation B, Subpart B) reporting scope.
business_rules.consequences: If the applicant’s gross annual revenue is at or below $5,000,000 (subject to any permitted methodology such as considering affiliate revenue as applicable under the rule), the application is within the small business definition for Section 1071 scoping, and the institution must proceed with required data collection/reporting for covered credit transactions. Misapplication can cause under-reporting or over-reporting and related supervisory findings.
business_rules.exceptions: The $5,000,000 threshold is subject to inflation adjustment under § 1002.106(b)(2).

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(2) Inflation Adjustment (context for $5 million threshold)
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau believes that its $5 million gross annual revenue threshold strikes an appropriate balance between covering the applications of most businesses... while minimizing the number of businesses above the SBA’s size standards whose applications will be reported to the Bureau...

examples: Applicant reports 2024 gross annual revenue of $3.2MM: treat as a small business under the $5MM threshold (subject to other coverage criteria).
Applicant reports 2024 gross annual revenue of $6.0MM: treat as not a small business for Section 1071 coverage (unless a future adjusted threshold applies).

Applicant reports $4.9MM gross annual revenue but affiliate revenue pushes above $5.0MM under the institution’s permitted methodology: treat as not small, and do not collect/report Subpart B data for that application.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; reporting remediation including corrections/resubmissions

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 five-year inflation adjustment for small business revenue threshold uses CPI-U with 500 thousand dollar rounding and January 1 effective date
business_rules.description: Every five years after January 1, 2025, the Section 1071 gross annual revenue threshold in § 1002.106(b)(1) must be adjusted based on changes to the CPI-U (U.S. city average, all items, not seasonally adjusted) as published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Each adjustment is rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000. The CPI-U base value is the January 2025 CPI-U; it is compared to the CPI-U in January 2030 and every five years thereafter. If rounding results in no change to the threshold, no adjustment occurs. If an adjustment is to take effect, it becomes effective on January 1 of the following calendar year. If CPI-U publication ceases or becomes unavailable/unusable, the Bureau will substitute another reliable U.S. Government cost-of-living indicator.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Section 1071 small business gross annual revenue threshold maintenance and operational updates by financial institutions and vendors implementing Subpart B scope logic.
business_rules.consequences: Institutions must update their “small business” threshold value and scoping logic on the effective date (January 1 of the year following an adjustment determination) and retain a defensible calculation/audit trail. Failure to update may cause systematic over/under-reporting.
business_rules.exceptions: If rounding to the nearest $500,000 produces no change, there is no adjustment. If CPI-U is unavailable/unusable, substitute another reliable U.S. Government cost-of-living indicator.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(2) and comments 106(b)(2)-1 and -2 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: finalizing new § 1002.106(b)(2) to provide that every five years after January 1, 2025, the gross annual revenue threshold... shall adjust based changes to the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers... (CPI-U). Any such adjustment will be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000... take effect... on January 1 of the following calendar year... base... is the January 2025 CPI-U... compared to... January 2030 and every five years thereafter... if publication of the CPI-U ceases... the Bureau shall substitute another reliable cost of living indicator...

examples: In 2030, the Bureau calculates an adjusted threshold based on CPI-U change from January 2025 to January 2030 and announces a new threshold rounded to the nearest $500,000; institution updates scoping threshold effective January 1, 2031.
In 2030, CPI-U change produces a calculated threshold change of $200,000, which rounds to $0 at the nearest $500,000; no threshold adjustment occurs and the institution keeps the existing threshold.

If CPI-U is discontinued in the future, the Bureau substitutes another U.S. Government cost-of-living indicator; the institution must switch to that indicator for the five-year adjustment calculation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; reporting remediation for systematic scoping errors

business_rules.rule_name: Report each covered credit transaction request as a separate covered application when requested at the same time under CFPB 1071
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 small business lending data collection, when an applicant requests two or more covered credit transactions at the same time (e.g., a term loan and a line of credit), the institution must report each request as a separate covered application. If the applicant is requesting only one covered credit transaction but is undecided between products (e.g., term loan OR line of credit), the institution reports it as a single covered application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (i) the request meets the definition of a covered application under 12 CFR part 1002 subpart B, and (ii) the applicant makes a request for two or more covered credit transactions at the same time.
business_rules.consequences: If condition is met: create and submit separate covered application records (one per covered credit transaction requested). If not followed: misreporting/underreporting risk in 1071 data submission, potential supervisory findings and required resubmission/correction.
business_rules.exceptions: If the applicant requests a single covered credit transaction but has not decided on the product type, the request is reported as a single covered application (not multiple).

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to § 1002.103(a), comment 103(a)-5
source_reference.source_text: Assuming the requirements of a covered application are met, if an applicant makes a request for two or more covered credit transactions at the same time, the financial institution reports each request as a separate covered application. For example, if an applicant is seeking both a term loan and a line of credit and requests them both on the same application form, the financial institution reports the requests as two separate covered applications... If, on the other hand, the applicant is only requesting a single covered credit transaction, but has not decided on which particular product, the financial institution reports the request as a single covered application.

examples: A business submits one form requesting a $250,000 term loan and a $75,000 revolving line of credit; the institution records and reports 2 covered applications.
A business says it wants either a term loan or a line of credit (one product, undecided); the institution reports 1 covered application.
A business requests a term loan and a line of credit in the same meeting and provides sufficient application information for both; the institution reports 2 covered applications.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism; required resubmission/correction of 1071 data; potential civil money penalties depending on severity and governance.

business_rules.rule_name: Do not treat solicitations or firm offers of credit as covered applications unless the business requests the credit under CFPB 1071
business_rules.description: Under CFPB 1071 subpart B, solicitations, firm offers of credit, or other evaluations initiated by the financial institution are not covered applications because the business has not made a request for credit. If the business responds and seeks to obtain the credit offered (and covered-application requirements are otherwise met), the business’s request constitutes a covered application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to institution-initiated offers or solicitations (e.g., firm offer of credit sent to a business) where the business has not yet requested credit.
business_rules.consequences: If institution-initiated only and no business request: do not open/report a covered application record. If the business seeks the offered credit: treat the business request as a covered application and collect/report required data.
business_rules.exceptions: None, other than the business subsequently requesting credit (which converts the interaction into a covered application if other criteria are met).

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to § 1002.103(a), comment 103(a)-4
source_reference.source_text: For purposes of subpart B of this part, the term covered application does not include solicitations, firm offers of credit, or other evaluations initiated by the financial institution because in these situations the business has not made a request for credit... if a financial institution sends a firm offer of credit to a business for a $10,000 line of credit, and the business does not respond, it is not a covered application... if the business seeks to obtain the credit offered... the business’s request constitutes a covered application.

examples: Bank mails a firm offer for a $10,000 LOC; the business does not respond; no covered application is reported.
Bank emails a pre-approved offer; the business replies requesting the LOC and provides required information; the bank treats it as a covered application.
Bank runs a marketing campaign and pre-screens businesses; until a business requests credit, these contacts are not covered applications.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism for over- or under-reporting covered applications; data integrity remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Exclude inquiries, prequalification, and most renewals from covered applications unless additional credit is requested under CFPB 1071
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 subpart B, the covered application definition does not include inquiries and prequalification requests. It also does not include reevaluation, extension, or renewal requests on an existing business credit account unless the request seeks additional credit amounts.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating whether an interaction must be treated as a covered application for 1071 data collection and reporting.
business_rules.consequences: If the interaction is an inquiry, prequalification, or a renewal/extension/reevaluation without an additional credit amount request: do not treat as a covered application (no 1071 covered application record required). If additional credit amount is requested: treat as a covered application (per § 1002.103(b) cross-reference).
business_rules.exceptions: Renewal/extension/reevaluation that seeks additional credit amounts must be treated as a covered application (see § 1002.103(b)).

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to § 1002.103(a), comment 103(a)-3
source_reference.source_text: application for purposes of subpart B of this part. See also comm ent 103(b) -4. 5. Requests for multiple covered credit transactions at one time . Assuming the requirements of a covered

examples: Borrower asks what rates are available for a line of credit but does not request credit; this is an inquiry and not a covered application.
Existing borrower renews an existing line at the same limit; this renewal is not a covered application for 1071.
Existing borrower requests renewal plus an increase from $100,000 to $150,000; treat as a covered application because additional credit is requested.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism for incorrect scoping of covered applications; potential data resubmission.

business_rules.rule_name: Tier 3 covered financial institutions generally have 33 months from CFPB 1071 rule issuance to comply
business_rules.description: In the CFPB’s section-by-section analysis for the CFPB 1071 final rule, the Bureau states it believes the majority of covered financial institutions will report data with Tier 3 and thus will have 33 months to comply with the rule from its issuance (reflecting a tiered compliance approach rather than a single three-year compliance period).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an institution is assigned to the Tier 3 compliance tier under the CFPB 1071 final rule’s tiered compliance structure.
business_rules.consequences: If Tier 3: compliance program, systems, and procedures must be operational by the Tier 3 compliance date (described here as 33 months from issuance). Missing the compliance date increases regulatory risk (supervisory findings; potential penalties depending on the enforcement posture and any grace periods described elsewhere in the rulemaking).
business_rules.exceptions: This excerpt does not specify (i) the exact issuance date, (ii) the specific Tier 3 compliance date, or (iii) whether subsequent CFPB amendments alter the timeline. Use the codified text of § 1002.114 and CFPB implementation guidance to finalize dates.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.114(a)/(b) analysis, Tiered compliance discussion (Tier 3 timeline)
source_reference.source_text: In any case, the Bureau believes that the majority of covered financial institutions will report data with Tier 3, and thus will have 33 months to comply with the rule from its issuance.

examples: A Tier 3 lender begins system build immediately after issuance and targets go-live by month 30 to allow testing before the 33-month deadline.
A Tier 3 lender delays vendor selection until month 25 and misses implementation; this creates late-compliance risk beyond the 33-month window.
A Tier 3 lender uses interim manual collection methods for the first months after the compliance date; regulators may still treat this as noncompliance if required workflows are not implemented.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism; potential civil money penalties for persistent noncompliance depending on CFPB enforcement posture and any applicable grace periods/safe harbors in the final rule.

business_rules.rule_name: Submit request for reconsideration of declined non-delegated SBA international trade application within 6 months and include current financials if after 120 days
business_rules.description: For non-delegated SBA international trade (IT) applications declined by LGPC, the lender may request reconsideration if it believes the reasons for decline have been overcome. The reconsideration request must be submitted to LGPC within 6 months of the date of decline. Any reconsideration request submitted more than 120 days after the date of decline must include current financial statements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (i) SBA IT loan application was processed non-delegated through LGPC and (ii) LGPC declined the application and the lender seeks reconsideration.
business_rules.consequences: If reconsideration is submitted within 6 months and includes required content: LGPC will consider the request. If submitted after 6 months: lender is outside the stated window, creating a high likelihood the reconsideration will not be accepted/considered. If submitted after 120 days without current financial statements: request is incomplete/noncompliant with SOP requirements and may be declined or delayed.
business_rules.exceptions: If a first reconsideration is declined, a second reconsideration may be requested from the D/FA or designee (decision final), following the SOP-described submission pathway via LGPC and including additional information addressing decline reasons.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Reconsideration of declined non-delegated IT applications (13 CFR § 120.193)
source_reference.source_text: If the Lender believes the reason(s) for decline have been overcome, a request for reconsideration may be submitted along with a detailed written explanation... Lender must submit a request for reconsideration to the LGPC within 6 months of the date of decline. Any request submitted more than 120 days after the date of decline must include current financial statements.

examples: LGPC declines on January 1; lender submits reconsideration on May 15 (within 6 months, >120 days) and includes current financial statements; request meets the SOP timing/content requirement.
LGPC declines on January 1; lender submits reconsideration on July 10 (>6 months); request is untimely under SOP and may not be considered.
LGPC declines on January 1; lender submits reconsideration on May 20 (>120 days) without current financials; request is incomplete and risks rejection/deferral.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty processing delay or denial; program compliance finding during SBA review.

business_rules.rule_name: Disburse SBA 504 loans within 48 months of approval and debt refinance without expansion within 9 months unless extended up to 15 months
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, the loan must be disbursed within 48 months from the date of approval, and SBA will automatically cancel undisbursed dollars after the approved timeframe. For Debt Refinance without Expansion, the loan must be disbursed within 9 months from the date of approval. SBA may grant an extension for Debt Refinance without Expansion not to exceed 15 months. A CDC may request an exception to policy to extend the disbursement period, subject to SBA approvals described in the guidance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 program loans using E-Tran Terms and Conditions through disbursement; includes special rule for Debt Refinance without Expansion.
business_rules.consequences: If not fully disbursed within the required period: SBA will automatically cancel undisbursed dollars. If extension is needed: CDC must request exception to policy and provide reasons and documentation of adjusted sources/uses for increased project costs. Failure to meet requirements may result in loss of authorized undisbursed funds and program compliance issues.
business_rules.exceptions: Debt Refinance without Expansion may receive an extension not to exceed 15 months. Other extensions require exception-to-policy approval path as described (including funding authority concurrence).

source_reference.section_id: Disbursement Period, Interest Rates and Loan Maturity (Disbursement Period)
source_reference.source_text: Disbursement Period: The loan must be disbursed within 48 months from the date of approval. SBA will track the approved time frame for loans and automatically cancel undisbursed dollars. For Debt Refinance without Expansion, the loan must be disbursed within 9 months from the date of approval... A CDC may request an exception to policy to extend the disbursement period... For Debt Refinance without Expansion, SBA may grant an extension not to exceed 15 months.

examples: A 504 construction project is approved on April 1, 2026; all disbursements must be completed by April 1, 2030 (48 months) or undisbursed dollars risk automatic cancellation.
A 504 Debt Refinance without Expansion is approved on April 1, 2026; it must be disbursed by January 1, 2027 (9 months) unless SBA grants an extension up to July 1, 2027 (15 months).
A CDC anticipates delays and requests an exception-to-policy extension with updated sources/uses; without approval, undisbursed funds may be cancelled.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Automatic cancellation of undisbursed SBA-authorized dollars; SBA program compliance findings.

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 final rule sets an activity-based coverage threshold of 100 originated loans and excludes Paycheck Protection Program lending from 2022–2023 coverage determination
business_rules.description: For CFPB section 1071 small business lending data collection and reporting coverage, the Bureau adopted a 100-loan activity threshold. A financial institution’s initial determination of whether it is covered (and its compliance date tier) is based on 2022 and 2023 originations; lending under the SBA Paycheck Protection Program ended May 31, 2021 and therefore does not factor into the institution’s coverage determination based on 2022–2023 originations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a financial institution is evaluating whether it is a covered financial institution for CFPB 1071 reporting and which compliance tier applies, using its 2022 and 2023 originations.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution meets the activity-based threshold (100 loans) based on the applicable origination lookback used for determining coverage, it must implement 1071 data collection, firewall/recordkeeping controls, compilation, and reporting obligations by its applicable compliance date tier; PPP lending volumes from 2020–May 2021 must not be counted for this 2022–2023-based determination.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule excerpt does not specify the full counting methodology (e.g., whether the 100-loan threshold must be met in one year or multiple years); institutions should confirm the precise threshold mechanics in final § 1002.114(b) and related commentary.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.106 Business and Small Business
source_reference.source_text: Because financial institutions’ initial determinations of whether they are covered under this final rule, and if so into which compliance date tier they fall, will be based on 2022 and 2023 originations (see final § 1002.114(b)), institutions’ Paycheck Protection Program lending activity will not factor into whether a given financial institution qualifies as a covered financial institution because such lending ceased in May 2021. ... After consideration of the comments, the Bureau believes that a 100-loan activity threshold is more appropriate.

examples: A community bank originated many PPP loans in 2020 and early 2021 but originated fewer small business covered credit transactions in 2022–2023; it must not include PPP volumes when determining 1071 coverage based on 2022–2023 originations.

A credit union evaluating 1071 coverage counts covered originations in 2022 and 2023 under the final rule’s methodology and compares to the 100-loan activity threshold; PPP loans are excluded because they ended May 31, 2021.

A lender that did not historically do small business lending but did PPP in 2020 cannot use PPP activity to justify being covered or not covered for 1071; the determination is based on 2022–2023 originations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB/agency enforcement for ECOA/Reg B violations, including supervisory findings and potential civil money penalties

business_rules.rule_name: Minority-owned business status under CFPB 1071 requires both more than 50 percent minority ownership or control and more than 50 percent accrual of net profits or losses
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 data collection, a business is a minority-owned business only if BOTH prongs are satisfied: (A) one or more minority individuals hold more than 50% of ownership or control; AND (B) more than 50% of net profits or losses accrue to one or more minority individuals. Minority individuals include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Black or African American, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, or Hispanic or Latino individuals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution requests and records applicant-provided minority-owned business status for final § 1002.107(a)(18).
business_rules.consequences: If either prong is not satisfied, the applicant is not reported as a minority-owned business for 1071 purposes (even if it meets one prong). Data collection scripts/forms must reflect the two-prong standard to reduce misclassification risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None in the cited excerpt. Applicants may be allowed to self-determine status; verification requirements are not imposed in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.102(m) Minority-Owned Business
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.102(m) defines a minority-owned business as a business for which one or more American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Black or African American, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, or Hispanic or Latino individuals hold more than 50 percent of its ownership or control and for which more than 50 percent of the net profits or losses accrue to one or more such individuals. ... a business must satisfy both prongs of the definition

examples: A company is 60% owned by Hispanic or Latino individuals, but only 40% of net profits accrue to those individuals due to a profit allocation agreement; it is not a minority-owned business under § 1002.102(m).

A business has a minority individual who controls the business (management control) even though minority individuals hold only 45% ownership, and 60% of net profits accrue to minority individuals; it meets the control prong and the net profits/losses prong and is minority-owned.
A business is 55% owned by Asian individuals and 55% of net losses accrue to those individuals (loss allocation); it meets both prongs and is minority-owned.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and required remediation for inaccurate 1071 data collection/reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Trustee must be treated as the owner of a trust when tracing ownership for CFPB 1071 minority-owned business determinations
business_rules.description: When determining ownership for CFPB 1071 minority-owned business status, ownership may need to be traced through corporate or indirect ownership structures. Where applicable, the trustee is considered the owner of a trust for purposes of the ownership prong in the minority-owned business definition.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an applicant business has trust ownership in its ownership chain and the institution is collecting/recording minority-owned business status under § 1002.107(a)(18) using § 1002.102(m) and its commentary.
business_rules.consequences: Ownership tracing logic and data collection procedures must include trust structures and must treat trustees as owners, which may affect whether the business meets the 'more than 50 percent ownership or control' prong.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final comment 102(m)-4 (ownership tracing; trustee ownership)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 102(m)-4 ... provides examples of ownership and clarifies that, where applicable, ownership needs to be traced through corporate or other indirect ownership structures. ... the Bureau believes the trustee would be considered an owner for purposes of this definition. Final comment 102(m)-4 also clarifies ... that a trustee is considered the owner of a trust.

examples: Applicant is owned 60% by a trust; the trustee is an individual who is Black or African American. For ownership tracing, the trustee is treated as the owner of the trust interest.
Applicant’s ownership is held through a holding company partially owned by a trust; the institution traces indirect ownership and assigns trust ownership based on trustee ownership status.
Applicant has two trusts each owning 30%; trustees are minority individuals; the institution counts these toward the minority ownership/control prong when appropriate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and required resubmission/correction of 1071 data for definitional misapplication

business_rules.rule_name: SBA delegated PLP or SBA Express lenders must use delegated processing except same-institution debt refinance cannot be processed as SBA Express
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) origination processing, lenders with delegated PLP or SBA Express authority must process all loan applications using their delegated authority, except they may submit applications via E‑Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures only when the loan will refinance the lender’s same-institution debt. SBA Express loans may not be processed under non-delegated procedures; therefore, a refinance of same institution debt may not be processed as an SBA Express loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution holds SBA delegated PLP or SBA Express authority and is determining whether to process a 7(a) application under delegated versus non-delegated procedures.
business_rules.consequences: If the transaction is a refinance of the lender’s own debt, the lender may submit under non-delegated procedures (but must not label/process it as SBA Express). For all other transactions, failure to process using delegated authority may be treated as a program noncompliance and may affect SBA guaranty enforceability or trigger corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: Lenders that do not have delegated PLP or SBA Express authority must submit applications to SBA via E‑Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty
source_reference.source_text: Lenders with delegated PLP or SBA Express authority: May only submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for approval under non-delegated procedures when the loan will refinance the Lender’s same institution debt. SBA Express loans may not be processed under non-delegated procedures; therefore, refinance of same institution debt may not be processed as an SBA Express loan. Except for the above, must process all loan applications using their delegated authority.

examples: A PLP lender refinancing its own existing term loan to the borrower submits the application for non-delegated SBA approval via E‑Tran; it does not process the loan as SBA Express.
An SBA Express lender originating a new working capital loan (not a refinance of the lender’s debt) must process using delegated authority; it may not choose non-delegated processing.
A non-delegated lender submits its 7(a) application to SBA via E‑Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and SBA program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA E-Tran submission must list 100 percent direct and indirect ownership and must combine married spouses and minor children ownership percentages
business_rules.description: When submitting SBA 7(a) applications through E‑Tran, the lender must list 100% of the applicant’s total direct and indirect ownership. In addition, the ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined for reporting purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loan submission to SBA via E‑Tran.
business_rules.consequences: Incomplete ownership listing (less than 100% total ownership) or failure to combine spousal/minor child ownership may result in SBA processing delays, data integrity issues, or SBA program compliance findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: E‑Tran submission ownership requirements
source_reference.source_text: Lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the Applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined.

examples: Applicant has three owners at 40%, 35%, and 25%; lender must list all owners and ensure total equals 100% in E‑Tran.
Husband owns 30% and wife owns 25%; lender combines and reports 55% ownership for the married spouses per SBA requirement.
Parent owns 60% and a minor child owns 10% through a gift; ownership is combined and still the full 100% ownership must be accounted for in E‑Tran.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays and SBA program compliance findings; potential guaranty repair exposure for material inaccuracies

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) Small loan files must include owner financial statements dated within 120 days for all owners of 20 percent or more and proposed guarantors excluding supplemental guarantors
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Small loans, the lender must obtain and retain in the loan file an owner financial statement (business or personal, as applicable) that is signed and dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for (i) all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and (ii) proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when submitting a 7(a) Small loan application to SBA and assembling/maintaining the lender’s loan file documentation.
business_rules.consequences: If owner financial statements are older than 120 days or missing for required owners/guarantors, the application may be incomplete, may face SBA processing delays, and may create SBA program compliance exceptions affecting guaranty support.
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental Guarantors are excluded from the owner financial statement requirement in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Loan file contents for 7(a) Small loans
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statement (business or personal, as applicable) signed and dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors.

examples: A borrower has three owners at 25%, 25%, and 50%; the lender must obtain owner financial statements for all three, dated within 120 days of submission.

A guarantor is required by the lender; the guarantor must provide a signed and dated financial statement within 120 days, unless the guarantor is solely a Supplemental Guarantor as defined by SBA program rules.
An owner’s financial statement signed 150 days before SBA submission must be refreshed (re-signed/updated) to be within the 120-day window.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial for material documentation deficiencies

business_rules.rule_name: SBA employee counts must include all full-time and part-time workers without converting to FTEs and must exclude 1099 contractors
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) application data (SBA Form 1919 information), the number of existing employees at the time of application must be calculated under 13 CFR § 121.106 by counting all individuals employed on a full-time, part-time, or other basis (i.e., do not convert to full-time equivalents). ‘Employees’ does not include contractors reported via IRS Form 1099.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when collecting or reporting SBA Form 1919 employee counts and job creation/retention information as part of the lender’s SBA loan application process.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender converts to FTEs or includes 1099 contractors in employee counts, the application data may be inaccurate, potentially triggering SBA review questions, processing delays, or program compliance findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Employee counting methodology for SBA Form 1919
source_reference.source_text: Number of existing employees at the time of application is calculated in accordance with 13 CFR § 121.106, which states in part that SBA counts all individuals employed on a full-time, part-time, or other basis (i.e., jobs are not converted to full-time equivalents). ... “Employees” does not include contractors reported via IRS Form 1099.

examples: A business has 10 full-time and 6 part-time employees; the lender reports 16 employees (not an FTE-adjusted number).
A borrower uses 5 independent contractors paid on 1099s and has 8 W-2 employees; the lender reports 8 employees, excluding the contractors.
A seasonal business employs workers ‘on an other basis’; the lender includes them as individuals employed under § 121.106 rather than applying an FTE conversion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance findings and potential data correction requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Non-delegated EWCP lenders must submit guaranty applications to SBA in E-Tran for SBA approval
business_rules.description: If the lender does not have delegated PLP-EWCP authority, the lender must submit EWCP applications to SBA via E-Tran for SBA approval using non-delegated procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when processing an EWCP (Export Working Capital Program) application for SBA guaranty and the lender does not have delegated PLP-EWCP authority.
business_rules.consequences: The lender must use E-Tran submission under non-delegated procedures; failure to follow the required submission channel can result in SBA not approving/processing the application as submitted and increased risk of guaranty denial or lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: Lenders that do not have delegated PLP-EWCP authority: Must submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures.

examples: A lender without PLP-EWCP delegation receives an EWCP application for a $1,000,000 export line of credit; it must submit the guaranty request in E-Tran under non-delegated procedures.
A lender without delegated PLP-EWCP authority cannot treat the EWCP request as delegated; it must route the application through SBA approval in E-Tran.
A non-delegated lender that attempts to approve internally and only later inform SBA is non-compliant with the required submission procedure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/processing refusal; SBA lender oversight finding or corrective action

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated PLP-EWCP lenders may use non-delegated submission only to refinance same-institution debt and otherwise must use delegated authority
business_rules.description: A lender with delegated PLP-EWCP authority may submit an EWCP application to SBA via E-Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures only when the EWCP loan will refinance debt owed to the lender’s same institution. Except for that refinance scenario, the lender must process EWCP applications using its delegated authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender has delegated PLP-EWCP authority and is determining whether to process the EWCP request under delegated authority or submit under non-delegated procedures.
business_rules.consequences: If the transaction is refinancing the lender’s same-institution debt, the lender may submit via E-Tran under non-delegated procedures. If not, the lender must process under delegated authority; processing under the wrong method increases risk of SBA rejection, post-approval repair, or lender oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: The only stated exception permitting non-delegated submission by a delegated PLP-EWCP lender is when the loan refinances the lender’s same institution debt.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: Lenders with delegated PLP-EWCP authority: May only submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for approval under non-delegated procedures when the loan will refinance the Lender’s same institution debt. Except for the above, must process all loan applications using their delegated authority.

examples: A PLP-EWCP delegated lender refinances its own existing working capital line with a new EWCP line; it may submit under non-delegated procedures in E-Tran for SBA approval.
A PLP-EWCP delegated lender originates a new EWCP facility to finance export orders with no refinance of its own debt; it must process using delegated authority (not non-delegated).
A delegated lender refinances a competitor bank’s note; this is not “same institution debt,” so the lender must process under delegated authority.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection/repair; SBA lender oversight finding or corrective action

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated SBA lenders must use the delegated-loan-applications email only for specific delegated-application questions and not for general policy questions
business_rules.description: The SBA email account 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov is restricted to lenders with delegated authority to ask questions about a specific loan application; it must not be used for general policy questions, which must instead be sent to 7aQuestions@sba.gov.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA lender with delegated authority is communicating with SBA by email about 7(a) delegated processing.
business_rules.consequences: If misrouted, inquiries may be delayed or not addressed as intended; use of the wrong channel may contribute to processing delays and potential compliance findings during SBA lender oversight if it results in improper handling of exceptions/approvals.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: The 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov email account is only for Lenders with delegated authority that have questions about a specific loan application and is not to be used for general policy questions, which should be sent to 7aQuestions@sba.gov.

examples: A delegated lender asks whether a specific borrower’s principals meet an eligibility nuance for a pending EWCP file; it may email 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov with a summary of facts.
A lender wants clarification on SOP interpretation unrelated to a specific file; it must email 7aQuestions@sba.gov rather than 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov.
A lender sends a general policy question to 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov and receives no timely response, delaying closing; this violates the stated channel restriction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; processing delays that can affect guaranty issuance

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP lender eligibility to submit depends on executed SBA Form 750 version and SBA Form 750EX or SBA Form 2310 status
business_rules.description: SBA Form 750 (Loan Guaranty Agreement (Deferred Participation)) was updated July 1, 2019. A lender with an earlier executed SBA Form 750 may submit EWCP applications only if it also has an executed SBA Form 750EX. If a lender has an earlier SBA Form 750 but no executed SBA Form 750EX, the lender must execute the July 1, 2019 version of SBA Form 750 to submit an EWCP application. All lenders with an executed July 1, 2019 version of SBA Form 750 are eligible to submit EWCP applications. Lenders with an executed SBA Form 2310 (PLP EWCP supplemental guaranty agreement) must process EWCP applications under delegated authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender seeks to submit an EWCP guaranty application and must confirm appropriate guaranty agreement forms are executed (SBA Form 750, 750EX, and/or 2310) and select the correct processing authority (delegated vs non-delegated).
business_rules.consequences: If required executed forms are not in place, the lender is not eligible to submit the EWCP application for guaranty until the required form(s) are executed. If SBA Form 2310 is executed, the lender must process under delegated authority.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the conditional allowance for earlier SBA Form 750 when SBA Form 750EX is also executed.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: SBA Form 750, “Loan Guaranty Agreement (Deferred Participation,” was updated as of July 1, 2019. Lenders that executed an earlier version of SBA Form 750, and that also have an executed SBA Form 750EX, are permitted to submit applications for an EWCP guaranty... If a Lender has an earlier version of the SBA Form 750, but no executed SBA Form 750EX, the Lender must execute a July 1, 2019, version of the SBA Form 750...

examples: Lender A has an executed SBA Form 750 dated 2018 and also has an executed SBA Form 750EX; it may submit EWCP guaranty applications.
Lender B has an executed SBA Form 750 dated 2018 but does not have SBA Form 750EX; it must execute the July 1, 2019 SBA Form 750 before submitting an EWCP application.
Lender C has executed SBA Form 2310; it must process EWCP applications under delegated authority (not non-delegated).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty application rejected or not eligible for submission; SBA lender oversight finding

business_rules.rule_name: Non-delegated EWCP submissions through LGPC require E-Tran submission of the guaranty application and all attachments and SBA makes final eligibility and creditworthiness decision
business_rules.description: For EWCP loans submitted using the non-delegated process through the 7(a) Loan Guaranty Processing Center (LGPC), the lender must submit the application for guaranty and all attachments via E-Tran to SBA. SBA makes the final determination of applicant eligibility and creditworthiness, including uses of proceeds, collateral adequacy, loan structure, and equity injection, and will approve or decline the loan in E-Tran.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any EWCP loan processed under non-delegated procedures through the LGPC.
business_rules.consequences: The lender must submit the full E-Tran package (guaranty application plus all attachments). SBA—not the lender—makes the final approval/decline determination for eligibility and creditworthiness factors in the non-delegated process.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: For all loans submitted using the non-delegated process through the LGPC, the Lender must submit applications for guaranty and all attachments via E-Tran to SBA. SBA will make the final determination as to the eligibility and creditworthiness of the Applicant, including approving the uses of proceeds, the adequacy of the collateral being pledged, the structure of the loan and any equity injection...

examples: A lender sends an EWCP non-delegated request to LGPC but omits a required attachment in E-Tran; the submission is incomplete and may be delayed or declined.
In a non-delegated EWCP request, SBA determines the proposed collateral is inadequate and declines the loan in E-Tran even if the lender would have approved it internally.
SBA requires an equity injection for a non-delegated EWCP request; the lender must proceed based on SBA’s terms and conditions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA approval denial/decline in E-Tran; SBA lender oversight finding for incomplete/incorrect submission

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP owners at 20% or more and proposed guarantors must provide financial statements dated within 120 days and applicant interim financials must be within 120 days of SBA submission
business_rules.description: For EWCP guaranty submissions, the lender must obtain owner financial statements (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and proposed guarantors (except Supplemental Guarantors). In addition, interim financial statements for the applicant and any affiliates must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when preparing an EWCP application for SBA submission and determining required financial documentation and freshness requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the financial statements are older than 120 days at submission, the lender must obtain updated statements before submission/processing to satisfy SBA documentation requirements; failure increases risk of SBA processing delay/decline and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental Guarantors are excepted from the owner/proposed guarantor financial statement requirement as stated.

source_reference.section_id: Non-Delegated EWCP requirements / documentation list
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statements (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors... Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.

examples: An EWCP applicant has a 25% owner whose personal financial statement is 150 days old on the planned submission date; the lender must obtain an updated statement dated within 120 days.
An EWCP applicant’s interim financials are dated 5 months before submission; the lender must collect interim financials dated within 120 days before submitting to SBA.
A proposed Supplemental Guarantor is identified; the lender does not need to collect the Supplemental Guarantor’s financial statement under this specific requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delay/decline; SBA lender oversight finding for incomplete documentation

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 small business determination uses 5 million dollars or less in gross annual revenue for preceding fiscal year
business_rules.description: For purposes of Regulation B Subpart B (CFPB Section 1071 small business lending data collection), a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less, notwithstanding SBA industry size standards. The definition also incorporates the Small Business Act’s “small business concern” meaning (15 U.S.C. 632(a) implemented at 13 CFR 121.101 through 121.107), but Subpart B applies the $5 million gross annual revenue threshold as the operative small business standard for coverage.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the financial institution determines whether an applicant is a “small business” for Section 1071/Regulation B Subpart B coverage and data collection/reporting.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution misclassifies applicants due to not applying the $5 million threshold, it risks inaccurate 1071 coverage determinations and data collection/reporting errors, which may result in supervisory findings, mandated corrective action, and potential enforcement risk for violations of implementing regulations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; rule states it applies notwithstanding 13 CFR 121.201 SBA size standards.

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.106(b)(1) Small business definition (Section-by-Section Analysis)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.106(b)(1) further states that, notwithstanding the size standards set forth in 13 CFR 121.201, for purposes of subpart B, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue, as defined in final § 1002.107(a)(14), for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less.

examples: Applicant’s preceding fiscal-year gross annual revenue is $4.2MM; institution must treat as a small business for 1071 purposes (subject to other coverage criteria).
Applicant revenue is $6.0MM; institution must treat as not a small business for 1071 purposes even if the applicant might meet some SBA employee-based size standard.
Applicant is a subsidiary; institution must still apply the $5MM preceding fiscal-year gross annual revenue standard for Subpart B small business determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; potential public enforcement action for inaccurate 1071 data collection/reporting and related Reg B violations

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 covered financial institution threshold is 100 covered credit transactions in each of the prior two calendar years
business_rules.description: A financial institution is a covered financial institution for CFPB Section 1071 (Reg B Subpart B—Small Business Lending Data Collection) only if it originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years. If the institution does not meet the 100-in-each-year threshold, it is an exempt institution for that period and is not required to collect/report Section 1071 data.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether the institution must collect and report Section 1071 small business lending data for a given calendar year; evaluate the number of originated covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years.
business_rules.consequences: If threshold met: institution must comply with Section 1071 data collection, compilation, and reporting requirements (including building/maintaining systems, procedures, and records). If threshold not met: institution is not required to collect/report Section 1071 data for that period (subject to other applicable laws).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; other coverage exclusions (e.g., product scope, HMDA-reportable exclusions) are addressed in other sections not included in this batch.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.105(b) Covered financial institution
source_reference.source_text: Covered financial institution means a financial institution that originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years.

examples: Bank A originated 120 covered credit transactions in 2024 and 105 in 2025; it is a covered financial institution for 2026 and must collect/report Section 1071 data.
Lender B originated 150 covered credit transactions in 2024 but only 95 in 2025; it is not a covered financial institution for 2026 under the 100-in-each-year test.
Credit Union C originated 100 covered credit transactions in both 2024 and 2025; it is covered for 2026.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory enforcement for failure to collect/report required Section 1071 data; supervisory criticism and potential civil money penalties

business_rules.rule_name: Small business threshold for Section 1071 is gross annual revenue of $5 million or less with CPI adjustment every 5 years rounded to $500,000
business_rules.description: For purposes of CFPB Section 1071 small business lending data collection, a business qualifies as a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less. The $5 million gross annual revenue threshold must be adjusted every 5 years after January 1, 2025, based on changes to the CPI-U (U.S. city average, all items, not seasonally adjusted). Any adjustment must be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000 and, if it takes effect, becomes effective on January 1 of the following calendar year.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether an applicant/business is a small business for Section 1071 coverage of a covered application/covered credit transaction; revenue is the preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue.
business_rules.consequences: If gross annual revenue is at or below the applicable threshold (initially $5,000,000): treat as small business for Section 1071 and apply covered-application data collection/compilation. If above: the application/transaction may fall outside Section 1071 small business scope (subject to other coverage criteria).
business_rules.exceptions: Notwithstanding SBA size standards in 13 CFR 121.201, this $5 million gross annual revenue test governs for this subpart; CPI adjustment details apply only every 5 years after 2025-01-01.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(1)-(2) Small business definition; Inflation adjustment
source_reference.source_text: for purposes of this subpart, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue... for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less. (2) Inflation adjustment. Every 5 years after January 1, 2025, the gross annual revenue threshold... shall adjust based on changes to the Consumer Price Index... Any adjustment... shall be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000... If an adjustment is to take effect... on January 1 of the following calendar year.

examples: Applicant reports $4.2 million gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year; treat applicant as a small business for Section 1071 purposes.

Applicant reports $5.3 million gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year; treat applicant as not a small business for Section 1071 purposes (even if it might meet certain SBA size standards).
If CPI adjustment yields an updated threshold of $5.26 million, the threshold would be rounded to the nearest $500,000 (e.g., $5.5 million) and would take effect January 1 of the next calendar year.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and data integrity violations if institution misclassifies small business status and fails to collect/report required Section 1071 data

business_rules.rule_name: SBA lenders must review third-party management agreements unless included in franchise disclosure documents for a Franchise Directory brand
business_rules.description: SBA lenders must review management agreements to determine whether they make the applicant an ineligible passive business. This review is required regardless of whether processing is delegated or non-delegated, unless the management agreement is part of the franchise disclosure documents (FDD) for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA lender receives an application and the borrower has a management agreement with a third party; triggers at underwriting during eligibility determination for SBA 7(a) and/or 504 loans.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to review the agreement, the lender may fail to identify ineligible passive businesses and risk SBA guaranty denial/repair upon oversight or purchase. If reviewed and found to grant sole discretion or otherwise create passive business status, the loan must be declined or restructured for eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: Review requirement does not apply when the management agreement is part of the FDD for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Management agreement review requirement
source_reference.source_text: Regardless whether processing the loan under delegated or non-delegated procedures, unless the management agreement is part of the franchise disclosure documents (FDD) for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory, SBA Lenders must review these agreements to determine whether the agreement makes the Applicant an ineligible passive business.

examples: Delegated lender processes a 7(a) request for a business with third-party operator agreement not in FDD: lender must review for passive business status.
Non-delegated lender receives an agreement embedded in FDD for a Franchise Directory brand: the 'must review' requirement in this clause does not apply (other due diligence may still be prudent).
Lender omits review and later SBA purchase review finds the agreement grants sole discretion to manager: guaranty repair/denial risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; lender oversight criticism

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB small business lending rule requires data collection by applicable tier compliance date and submission obligations by June 1 of following year
business_rules.description: Under CFPB Regulation B small business lending data collection (Section 1071), a covered financial institution must begin compiling data under §1002.107, comply with firewall requirements of §1002.108, and begin maintaining records under §1002.111 by its applicable compliance date. The institution must comply with §1002.110(c) and (d) no later than June 1 of the year after the applicable compliance date. For partial-year initial collection windows: Tier 1 institutions (compliance date October 1, 2024) must collect data October 1–December 31, 2024 and comply with §§1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2025; Tier 2 institutions (compliance date April 1, 2025) must collect data April 1–December 31, 2025 and comply with §§1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2026.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions subject to CFPB small business data collection rule; triggers based on the institution’s assigned compliance date tier and the date of covered applications.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution does not begin data compilation/firewall/recordkeeping by the compliance date or fails to meet June 1 obligations, it risks supervisory findings and CFPB enforcement (including civil money penalties) for reporting/recordkeeping violations.
business_rules.exceptions: If an institution is not a covered financial institution at the time of the compliance date, it is not required to comply for that period (see rule examples and eligibility determination outside this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002—Official Interpretations > Section 1002.114 > 114(b) Compliance date
source_reference.source_text: The applicable compliance date ... is the date by which the covered financial institution must begin to compile data ... comply with the firewall requirements ... and begin to maintain records ... In addition, the covered financial institution must comply with § 1002.110(c) and (d) no later than June 1 of the year after the applicable compliance date. ... required to collect data ... from October 1, 2024 to December 31, 2024 ... comply with §§ 1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2025.

examples: Tier 2 institution: starts collecting required 1071 data on April 1, 2025; ensures submission/related obligations for 2025 collection are met by June 1, 2026.
Tier 1 institution: fails to apply firewall procedures starting October 1, 2024 for covered applications; risk of CFPB violation findings.
Institution collects data for April–Dec 2025 but misses June 1, 2026 deadline for the specified obligations: reporting noncompliance exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB enforcement action; civil money penalties; supervisory findings requiring remediation

business_rules.rule_name: Trade credit is excluded from CFPB 1071 covered credit transactions and does not include third-party financial institution financing of supplier goods
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 small business lending data collection, trade credit is excluded from covered credit transactions. Trade credit is defined as a financing arrangement where a business acquires goods or services from another business without making immediate payment in full. An extension of business credit by a financial institution other than the supplier to finance those items is not trade credit. Credit extended by the supplying business is not trade credit for subpart B where the supplier intends to sell or transfer its rights as a creditor to a third party, such as a financial institution.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when classifying a transaction as a covered credit transaction versus excluded trade credit for Regulation B Subpart B (1071) data collection.
business_rules.consequences: If misclassified as trade credit when it is actually a covered credit transaction (e.g., a bank loan financing inventory), the institution risks underreporting and regulatory violations. If correctly excluded, no 1071 collection/reporting is required for that transaction (though ECOA/Reg B baseline may still apply).
business_rules.exceptions: Trade credit exclusion is limited to the business providing the goods or services being financed; affiliates/facilitators that provide financing are not covered by the exclusion (as described in the final rule narrative).

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.104(b)(1) Trade Credit > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.104(b)(1) defines trade credit as a financing arrangement wherein a business acquires goods or services from another business without making immediate payment in full ... an extension of business credit by a financial institution other than the supplier ... is not trade credit, and ... credit extended by a business providing goods or services ... is not trade credit ... where the supplying business intends to sell or transfer its rights as a creditor to a third party, such as a financial institution.

examples: A supplier allows a customer to buy inventory now and pay net-60: excluded trade credit (no 1071 data required for this transaction).
A bank provides a line of credit to a retailer to purchase inventory from a supplier: not trade credit; treat as covered credit transaction if other coverage criteria met.
A supplier extends credit but immediately plans to sell the receivable to a bank: not trade credit for subpart B; may become reportable depending on structure and coverage.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for data inaccuracies; potential civil money penalties for pattern/practice

business_rules.rule_name: SBA E-Tran submission must list 100% direct and indirect ownership and must combine married spouses and minor children ownership percentages
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty submissions, the lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined when reporting ownership.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when preparing and submitting an SBA CAPLines application for guaranty in E-Tran (delegated PLP or non-delegated) and completing required E-Tran data fields related to ownership.
