business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to report 100% ownership or fails to combine married spouses/minor children ownership as required, the submission may be incomplete/incorrect, resulting in SBA processing delays, file deficiencies, or post-approval/lender oversight findings and potential guaranty repair exposure depending on impact.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > E-Tran ownership reporting
source_reference.source_text: Lender must submit to E-Tran all terms and conditions required by E-Tran data fields. Lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the Applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined.

examples: Applicant ownership: Spouse A 30% and Spouse B 30%—lender combines and reports 60% for the married spouses ownership grouping in E-Tran (while still documenting ownership detail in file).
Applicant has four owners totaling 90% due to missing a 10% indirect owner; lender must identify and report the missing indirect ownership so E-Tran ownership totals 100%.
Minor child holds a 5% interest and parent holds 55%; lender combines minor child ownership with the parent/spouse grouping as required for reporting.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair exposure depending on impact

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines require owner financial statements dated within 120 days for owners of 20% or more and proposed guarantors unless lender uses a credit score to evaluate owner financials
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty applications, the lender must obtain an owner financial statement dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors. If the lender uses a credit score to evaluate the owner financials, then the lender does not need to obtain owner financial statements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines applications for guaranty (file requirements). Owner threshold: 20% or more ownership. Timing: financial statement dated within 120 days of submission to SBA.
business_rules.consequences: Missing or stale owner financial statements (when required) creates an SBA documentation deficiency that can result in processing delays, adverse findings in SBA oversight, and guaranty repair exposure if it constitutes noncompliance with program requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If the lender uses a credit score to evaluate owner financials, owner financial statements are not required per the cited text. Supplemental Guarantors are excluded from the owner financial statement requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > Owner Financial Statement requirement
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statement (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors. Lenders may use SBA Form 413 or their own equivalent form... If the Lender uses a credit score to evaluate the owner financials, then the Lender does not need to obtain owner financial statements.

examples: Two owners each hold 25%: lender obtains personal financial statements dated within 120 days of SBA submission for both owners (including spouse/minor child assets if applicable).
Owner holds 19% and is not a proposed guarantor: owner financial statement not required by the 20% threshold (but may be required by lender policy).
Lender uses credit scores to evaluate owner financials: lender may omit owner financial statements, but must document credit score usage consistent with SBA requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines require three years business financial statements or tax returns plus interim financial statements dated within 120 days and include affiliates
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty applications, the lender must obtain tax transcripts (per cross-referenced SOP section) and either business financial statements or tax returns for the last 3 years and interim financial statements for the applicant and any affiliates. Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA. The file must include year-end balance sheets for the last 3 years (with detailed debt schedule), year-end profit and loss statements for the last 3 years, and interim balance sheet (with detailed debt schedule) and interim profit and loss statement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines applications for guaranty submission. Timeframes: last 3 years year-end financials; interim financials dated within 120 days prior to submission; coverage includes applicant and any affiliates.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to obtain and retain required financial documentation and affiliate financial information may constitute SBA program noncompliance, create processing delays, increase risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial, and lead to adverse lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (requirement is framed as required documentation for the guaranty request).

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > Financial statements/tax returns and interim statements timing
source_reference.source_text: Tax transcripts... and either business financial statements or tax returns for the last 3 years and interim financial statements for the Applicant and any affiliates. Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA... Year End Balance Sheet for the last 3 years, including detailed debt schedule; Year End Profit & Loss Statements for the last 3 years; Interim Balance Sheet, including a detailed debt schedule; and interim Profit & Loss Statement.

examples: Lender collects FY2022–FY2024 year-end balance sheets (with debt schedules) and P&Ls plus an interim statement dated 60 days before submission; meets the timing requirement.
Interim financials are dated 150 days prior to SBA submission; lender must obtain updated interim statements within 120 days to comply.
Applicant has an operating affiliate: lender must also obtain the affiliate’s interim and prior 3-year statements as required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 permits reliance on applicant-provided data without verification subject to Regulation B Section 1002.107(b)
business_rules.description: Under CFPB’s final rule implementing ECOA section 1071, the Bureau states that financial institutions are permitted to rely on applicant-provided data and need not verify the data, subject to the requirements of Regulation B § 1002.107(b) and its official interpretation (comment 107(b)-1). The Bureau declined to create an additional general safe harbor for reliance on applicant-provided data because the final rule already permits reliance without verification under the specified provisions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions collecting/reporting 1071 data points when the institution receives applicant-provided data (self-reported) and is determining whether verification is required for submission.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution verifies data for its own business purposes, it must still comply with § 1002.107(b) requirements governing how data is collected/reported; failure to follow the rule may create data quality issues and expose the institution to CFPB supervisory findings or enforcement risk. The absence of a broad safe harbor means institutions should not assume immunity for inaccurate data beyond permitted reliance provisions.
business_rules.exceptions: Reliance without verification is 'subject to the requirements of § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1' (i.e., not unconditional). The cited text does not enumerate all constraints; implementers must consult § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1 for specific limitations.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.112(c) Safe Harbors > Final Rule (discussion of reliance and no verification)
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau again notes that financial institutions may rely upon applicant-provided data and need not verify such data, subject to the requirements of § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1... The Bureau believes that final § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1 strikes a reasonable balance between data quality and cost and effort incurred by lenders.

examples: Applicant reports gross annual revenue of $900,000; institution may rely on applicant-provided value without verifying, provided it follows § 1002.107(b) rules.
Institution collects applicant-reported number of workers; it may accept the applicant’s response without independent verification (subject to § 1002.107(b)).
Institution verifies a revenue figure internally for credit decisioning; it cannot assume a blanket safe harbor from 1071 accuracy obligations—must follow § 1002.107(b) requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism; potential ECOA/Reg B enforcement action for 1071 data rule violations

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 does not require precise worker counts and requires exact time in business only if the institution already obtains it
business_rules.description: In the CFPB 1071 final rule discussion of safe harbors, the Bureau states that a safe harbor for number of workers is unnecessary because § 1002.107(a)(16) permits reporting by selecting ranges rather than requiring a precise number of workers. The Bureau also states a safe harbor for time in business is unnecessary because § 1002.107(a)(17) does not require reporting of exact years in business unless the financial institution already obtains that information for its own purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions compiling and reporting 1071 data points for (a)(16) number of workers and (a)(17) time in business, particularly when determining the level of precision required for reporting.
business_rules.consequences: Institutions should implement data collection to allow selection of worker ranges rather than attempting to validate a precise headcount. Institutions are not required to report exact years in business unless they already obtain that exact information; over-collection can create operational burden and potential data inconsistency if not governed under § 1002.107 requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If the institution already obtains exact years in business for its own purposes, then exact years may be required to be reported per § 1002.107(a)(17) as described in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.112(c) Safe Harbors > Final Rule (workers and time-in-business precision)
source_reference.source_text: final § 1002.107(a)(16) does not require the reporting of a precise numbers of workers, but rather permits the selection of ranges of numbers. Similarly, the Bureau believes that a safe harbor for the time in business data point is not necessary because final § 1002.107(a)(17) does not require the reporting of exact years in business for this data point unless the financial institution already obtains that information for its own purposes.

examples: Applicant states it employs 23 workers; institution reports using the appropriate worker range selection rather than storing/reporting '23' as a required precise value.
Institution does not track exact years in business and only collects a general 'time in business' category; it is not required to report exact years under § 1002.107(a)(17) per the cited description.
Institution already captures exact years in business in its underwriting system; it must be prepared to report the exact value for § 1002.107(a)(17) (subject to full rule requirements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism for misreporting or improper data precision handling under 1071

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple covered credit transaction requests at one time must be reported as separate covered applications unless only one transaction is requested
business_rules.description: If an applicant requests two or more covered credit transactions at one time, the financial institution must report each request as a separate covered application. If the applicant is only requesting a single covered credit transaction but has not decided which particular product, the institution must report the request as a single covered application (i.e., not multiple covered applications solely because multiple products could satisfy the one requested transaction).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications under 12 CFR 1002.103(a) and reporting obligations under subpart B (1071) when multiple products/transactions are requested simultaneously.
business_rules.consequences: Incorrectly combining multiple transaction requests into one covered application, or incorrectly splitting a single request into multiple covered applications, can cause inaccurate application counts, demand measures, and fair lending analytics and may result in resubmission requirements or supervisory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond the clarified scenario where a single covered credit transaction is requested but product selection is undecided (report as one).

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-5 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: if an applicant makes a request for two or more covered credit transactions at one time, the financial institution reports each request as a separate covered application. The Bureau believes this approach furthers the purposes of section 1071 by better capturing demand for credit, including demand for different covered credit transactions

examples: Borrower requests at the same time (i) a closed-end CRE term loan and (ii) an open-end revolving line: report two covered applications.
Borrower requests one financing need and is deciding between a term loan or a line to meet that same single need: report one covered application.

Borrower submits one request for two separate lines of credit intended to be two distinct accounts: report separately consistent with comment 103(a)-7 framework (multiple lines reported based on institution procedures).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination and potential enforcement action for inaccurate 1071 reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Duplicate covered applications may be treated as a single covered application only if the institution would treat them as duplicates under its own procedures
business_rules.description: A financial institution may treat two or more duplicate covered applications as a single covered application for purposes of subpart B reporting, but only if, for credit decisioning purposes, the institution would also treat one or more of those applications as a duplicate under its own established procedures. This is a conditional permission, not a requirement, and is constrained by the institution’s internal duplication procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when more than one covered application is received that the institution identifies as duplicates based on its procedures.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution consolidates applications that it would not treat as duplicates in its own procedures, reporting may be deemed inaccurate; if it fails to consolidate true duplicates and over-reports demand, data quality and analytics may be distorted.
business_rules.exceptions: Only applicable to “true duplicates” that the institution would otherwise treat as duplicates under its own procedures.

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-8 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: The Bureau is adopting new comment 103(a)-8 to address reporting of duplicate covered applications. Under new comment 103(a)-8, a financial institution may treat two or more duplicate covered applications as a single covered application for purposes of subpart B, so long as for purposes of determining whether to extend credit, the financial institution would also treat one or more of the applications as a duplicate under its procedures.

examples: Applicant submits the same request twice due to system error; bank policy treats the second as a duplicate and does not re-underwrite: bank may report as one covered application.
Applicant submits a second application with a materially different amount or product and bank policy treats it as a new request: bank must not consolidate as a duplicate for 1071 reporting.
Applicant submits via broker and directly; bank procedures flag as duplicate and merge into one underwriting file: bank may report as one.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for inaccurate application counts and duplication handling in 1071 reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Application must be reported as covered only if covered product is requested prior to final action taken
business_rules.description: If an applicant initially requests a product that is not a covered credit transaction but, prior to final action taken, decides to seek a product that is a covered credit transaction, the application becomes a covered application and must be reported under § 1002.109. Conversely, if an applicant initially requests a covered credit transaction but, prior to final action taken, decides to seek a product that is not a covered credit transaction, the application is not a covered application and must not be reported.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the application process up to the point of final action taken when the requested product type changes between covered and non-covered credit transaction status.
business_rules.consequences: Reporting an application that becomes non-covered (or failing to report one that becomes covered prior to final action) leads to register inaccuracies and potential supervisory findings for incorrect inclusion/exclusion logic.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the rule hinges on the timing ‘prior to final action taken.’

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-9 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 103(a)-9 provides that if an applicant initially requests a product that is not a covered credit transaction, but prior to final action taken decides to seek instead a product that is a covered credit transaction, the application is a covered application and must be reported pursuant to final § 1002.109. However, if an applicant initially requests a product that is a covered credit transaction, but prior to final action taken decides instead to seek a product that is not a covered credit transaction, the application is not a covered application and thus is not reported.

examples: Applicant starts with an inquiry that becomes a non-covered product application; before final action, applicant switches to a covered term loan: report the application as covered.
Applicant applies for a covered open-end line; before final action, applicant switches to a non-covered product: do not report it as a covered application.

Applicant applies for a covered product, receives a counteroffer for a non-covered product, and accepts the non-covered product prior to final action: do not report (consistent with the covered/non-covered outcome prior to final action).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential enforcement action for incorrect covered-application scoping in 1071 reporting

business_rules.rule_name: When multiple non-affiliate business co-applicants apply, report subpart B data for only one designated small business using consistent procedures
business_rules.description: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application from multiple businesses that are not affiliates, the institution must compile, maintain, and report data under §§ 1002.107 through 1002.109 for only a single applicant that is a small business. The institution must establish consistent procedures for designating which small business is reported (e.g., first small business listed on the application form).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications with multiple business co-applicants who are not affiliates (as defined in § 1002.102(a)).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to consistently designate a single small business (or attempting to report multiple co-applicants without an approved method) can create duplication, inconsistent reporting, and potential supervisory criticism for unreliable data.
business_rules.exceptions: If co-applicants are affiliates, this commentary does not apply; affiliate handling is governed by other provisions and institution procedures consistent with the rule.

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-10 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 103(a)-10 provides that if a covered financial institution receives a covered application from multiple businesses who are not affiliates ... it shall compile, maintain, and report data pursuant to final §§ 1002.107 through 1002.109 for only a single applicant that is a small business ... A covered financial institution shall establish consistent procedures for designating a single small business for purposes of collecting and reporting data

examples: Two unrelated small businesses jointly apply for one line of credit: report data for only one designated small business under the institution’s consistent procedure.
Joint venture application lists two non-affiliate small business members as co-applicants: report for only one designated small business (e.g., first listed).
If the co-applicants are affiliates under the rule’s affiliate definition, the institution does not use this one-of-many designation approach and follows affiliate treatment rules instead.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for inconsistent co-applicant handling and 1071 reporting integrity issues

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 coverage begins when a lender has at least 100 small business covered credit originations in each of the prior two calendar years
business_rules.description: A financial institution becomes a covered financial institution for CFPB Section 1071 data collection and reporting when it originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years. When this two-year threshold is met for calendar years (Y-2) and (Y-1), the institution’s compliance obligation begins on January 1 of year Y.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution did not qualify for a specified earlier tiered compliance date but later originates enough covered credit transactions to small businesses to meet the general coverage test. Determine small business originations using the Section 1071 small business definition (gross annual revenue of $5 million or less).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution meets the 100-origination threshold in each of the two preceding calendar years, it must begin Section 1071 data collection and reporting as of January 1 of the next calendar year (its initial compliance date). If it does not meet the threshold, it is not yet required to comply under the general coverage test.
business_rules.exceptions: Tier 1/Tier 2/Tier 3 compliance schedules under § 1002.114(b)(1)-(3) may set an earlier initial compliance date for lenders that meet higher origination thresholds; this rule addresses the cross-referenced general coverage path described in § 1002.114(b)(4) via § 1002.105(b) as illustrated by the commentary examples.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, Section 1002.114(b)(4) examples (vi)-(vii)
source_reference.source_text: Because Financial Institution F originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of 2024 and 2025, it is a covered financial institution under § 1002.105(b) and is required to comply with the rule beginning January 1, 2026... Because Financial Institution G originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of 2026 and 2027... is required to comply with the rule beginning January 1, 2028.

examples: Institution originates 120 covered credit transactions to small businesses in 2024 and 105 in 2025 → compliance begins 2026-01-01.
Institution originates 99 in 2024 and 150 in 2025 → does not meet threshold for 2026-01-01; reassess after 2026 results.
Institution originates 130 in 2026 and 140 in 2027 → compliance begins 2028-01-01.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding and potential enforcement action for failure to collect/report required Section 1071 data by the initial compliance date

business_rules.rule_name: Optional early collection in the 12 months before initial Section 1071 compliance date requires only limited rule components
business_rules.description: If a financial institution voluntarily collects Section 1071 information on covered applications during the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date, it need comply only with the specified limited requirements: § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), § 1002.108, and § 1002.111(b) and (c) for the information it collects during that early-collection period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies only if the institution chooses to collect covered-application information as permitted in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date under § 1002.114(b)(1), (2), or (3).
business_rules.consequences: During this 12-month pre-compliance collection period, the institution is not required to comply with the remaining provisions of § 1002.107 (other than (a)(18) and (19)), § 1002.109, § 1002.110, § 1002.111(a), or § 1002.114. This limits operational obligations to the listed provisions for early-collected data.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule does not require early collection; it applies only when the institution elects to collect. After the initial compliance date, the institution must comply with the full set of applicable requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(1)
source_reference.source_text: a financial institution that chooses to collect information... in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date... need comply only with the requirements set out in §§ 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), 1002.108, and 1002.111(b) and (c)... need not comply with... § 1002.107 (other than §§ 1002.107(a)(18) and (19)), 1002.109, 1002.110, 1002.111(a), or 1002.114.

examples: A Tier 2 institution starts collecting applicant demographic responses 6 months early → it must follow only the cited limited provisions for that early-collected data, not full LAR reporting rules yet.
A Tier 1 institution performs a 9-month pilot of certain data fields → the pilot is governed only by §§ 1002.107(a)(18)-(19), 1002.108, and 1002.111(b)-(c).
An institution does not do any early collection → this rule is not applicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding if institution collects early but fails to follow the limited applicable provisions (e.g., firewall or handling requirements referenced by the cited sections)

business_rules.rule_name: No Section 1071 collection or reporting required for applications received before initial compliance date even if action occurs after
business_rules.description: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application before its initial compliance date, but takes final action on or after the initial compliance date, it is not required to collect Section 1071 data for that application under § 1002.107 and is not required to report it under § 1002.109.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications received prior to the institution’s initial compliance date specified in § 1002.114(b). Final action must occur on or after the compliance date.
business_rules.consequences: The institution may exclude the application from Section 1071 data collection and from submission to the Bureau, even though the decision occurs after the compliance date.
business_rules.exceptions: If the application is received on or after the initial compliance date, full data collection and reporting requirements apply. This transition relief is limited to the timing scenario described.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(2)
source_reference.source_text: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application... prior to its initial compliance date... but takes final action on or after that date, the financial institution is not required to collect data... pursuant to § 1002.107 nor to report... pursuant to § 1002.109. For example... compliance date of October 1, 2024... receives an application on September 15, 2024... final action... October 5, 2024... not required to collect... nor to report...

examples: Compliance date 2024-10-01; application received 2024-09-15; final action 2024-10-05 → not reportable under Section 1071.
Compliance date 2026-01-01; application received 2025-12-30; final action 2026-01-10 → not reportable under Section 1071.
Compliance date 2026-01-01; application received 2026-01-02; final action 2026-01-10 → must be collected and reported.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for improper inclusion/exclusion logic leading to inaccurate LAR submissions

business_rules.rule_name: Readily accessible small business status exists when lender can determine whether gross annual revenue is 5 million dollars or less from ordinary-course data
business_rules.description: A financial institution has readily accessible information needed to determine whether its covered credit originations were to small businesses if, in the ordinary course of business, it collects precise gross annual revenue, collects information sufficient to determine whether an applicant had gross annual revenues of $5 million or less, or collects and reports similar data to Federal or State agencies under other legal requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether the institution meets origination thresholds for coverage/compliance timing and must determine which originations were to small businesses as defined in § 1002.106 (gross annual revenue $5 million or less).
business_rules.consequences: If information is readily accessible, the institution is expected to use that information (rather than estimation shortcuts intended for institutions without accessible data) to determine small business status for originations when calculating applicability thresholds.
business_rules.exceptions: This definition is illustrative (“if, for instance”) and does not limit other fact patterns that may qualify as readily accessible; institutions should document their basis for concluding data is readily accessible.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(3)
source_reference.source_text: . A financial institution does not ha ve readily accessible the information needed to determine whether its originations of covered credit transactions were for small businesses as defined in § 1002.106 if it did not in the ordinary course of business collect either precise or approximate information

examples: Bank captures exact gross annual revenue for every business borrower in its LOS → it has readily accessible information for threshold calculations.
Nonbank lender stores a field that flags whether applicant revenue is ≤ $5,000,000 → readily accessible for small business determination.
Credit union reports comparable revenue metrics to a state program and retains those records in a searchable system → readily accessible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for unsupported applicability determinations and resulting failure to comply on time

business_rules.rule_name: Reasonable estimation of small business originations may annualize one quarter of self-reported revenue data by multiplying by four for both 2022 and 2023
business_rules.description: When a financial institution lacks readily accessible information to determine small business status for originations, it may use reasonable, documented estimation methods. One permitted example methodology is: during October 1 through December 31, 2023, ask every applicant (prior to closing approved transactions) to self-report whether preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue was $5 million or less; determine small business originations in that quarter; annualize by quadrupling the quarter’s small business originations; and apply the annualized figure to both calendar years 2022 and 2023 for purposes of determining compliance tier/date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies for estimating 2022 and 2023 small business originations when the institution does not have readily accessible revenue information. The method requires collection of self-reported revenue status in the period October 1 through December 31, 2023, and annualization by multiplying by 4.
business_rules.consequences: If used, the resulting annualized originations count is used to determine the institution’s tier and initial compliance date. The institution must maintain written documentation supporting the estimation methodology and results.
business_rules.exceptions: Other reasonable and documented methods are allowed, including assuming every covered credit transaction to a business customer in 2022 and 2023 was to a small business. The listed methods are non-exclusive.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(5)(i)-(iii)
source_reference.source_text: reasonable methods... include... asking every applicant... to self-report whether it had gross annual revenue... of $5 million or less, during the period October 1 through December 31, 2023... may annualize... by quadrupling... and apply the annualized number... to both calendar years 2022 and 2023... may comply... by assuming that every covered credit transaction... in... 2022 and 2023 is to a small business... or... another methodology... reasonable and documented in writing.

examples: Institution originates 750 small business covered credit transactions (based on self-report) from 2023-10-01 to 2023-12-31 → annualized estimate is 3,000 (750×4) applied to 2022 and 2023 for tiering.
Institution lacks revenue data for 2022/2023 and documents a method using internal customer segmentation to estimate small business share → allowed if reasonable and documented.
Institution chooses the conservative assumption that all business originations in 2022 and 2023 were to small businesses → uses that count for applicability/tiering.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism if estimation method is not documented in writing or is unreasonable, causing incorrect compliance date determination

business_rules.rule_name: Factoring is excluded from Section 1071 covered credit transactions when it is an accounts receivable purchase with no right to defer payment
business_rules.description: For Regulation B Section 1071 purposes, a factoring agreement is not a covered credit transaction when the provider of funds does not grant the recipient the right to defer payment (i.e., the provider seeks payment directly from a third party on a legally enforceable claim for payment for goods supplied or services rendered but not yet paid in full). Such factoring is treated as a purchase of accounts receivable and is not credit under ECOA/Regulation B for 1071 coverage.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution offers or administers a transaction described as factoring, i.e., an accounts receivable purchase transaction between businesses involving purchase/transfer/sale of a legally enforceable claim for payment for goods or services already supplied or rendered, for which payment in full has not yet been made, and the transaction does not grant the applicant the right to defer payment of a debt or incur debt and defer payment.
business_rules.consequences: If the arrangement meets the final comment 104(b)-1 description of factoring (and does not include an extension of business credit), the institution must treat the transaction as excluded from Section 1071 covered credit transactions and should not collect/report it as a covered credit transaction under 12 CFR part 1002 subpart B.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to (i) credit incident to a factoring arrangement (see related rule BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_35_002) or (ii) arrangements that do not involve goods/services already supplied or rendered (those are not factoring under the final description and may be covered).

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.104 Factoring > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: For the reasons set forth herein, the Bureau is finalizing comment 104(b)-1 largely as proposed and is not covering factoring under the rule. The Bureau believes that... a factoring agreement... is not credit under ECOA because the provider of the funds does not grant the recipient the right to defer payment. Instead, the provider of funds seeks payment directly from a third party on a legally enforceable claim for payment for goods... or services... but for which payment in full has not yet been made.

examples: A lender purchases a small business’s existing invoice receivables for delivered goods, and the factor collects directly from the buyer; the seller has no right to defer payment of any debt to the factor. Result: excluded as factoring (not a covered credit transaction for 1071).

A provider advances funds against already-issued invoices and solely collects from the account debtor; no repayment obligation with deferral right is granted to the small business. Result: treat as excluded factoring.
A transaction is labeled “factoring,” but the receivables relate to future services not yet rendered. Result: not factoring under the final description; analyze as potentially covered credit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and potential ECOA/Regulation B enforcement action (including civil money penalties) for misclassification and incorrect Section 1071 reporting/omissions

business_rules.rule_name: Credit incident to factoring or revolving factoring-like transactions must be reported as other sales-based financing under Section 1071
business_rules.description: Even though factoring (as described in comment 104(b)-1) is excluded, an extension of business credit incident to a factoring arrangement is a covered credit transaction and must be reported as an “Other sales-based financing transaction” under § 1002.107(a)(5). Additionally, where a purported factoring arrangement involves multiple revolving transactions such that the transaction between the recipient and provider is not complete at the time of the sale, the transaction constitutes credit and should be reported as an “Other sales-based financing transaction.” For such transactions, reporting includes (as applicable) the difference between the amount advanced and the amount to be repaid and estimated loan term in certain circumstances.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution offers (i) a factoring-related arrangement that includes a separate extension of business credit incident to the factoring, or (ii) multiple revolving transactions where the arrangement is not complete at the time of sale such that credit is extended (right to incur debt and defer payment).
business_rules.consequences: The institution must treat the credit component as a covered credit transaction and report it as “Other sales-based financing transaction” per § 1002.107(a)(5), and must report required term/cost fields for merchant cash advances or other sales-based financing as described (e.g., difference between amount advanced and amount to be repaid; estimated term where applicable).
business_rules.exceptions: Pure accounts receivable purchase transactions that do not include an extension of credit remain excluded (see BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_35_001).

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.104 Factoring > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: Comment 104(b)-1 thus clarifies that an extension of business credit incident to a factoring arrangement is a covered credit transaction and that a financial institution shall report such a transaction as an “Other sales-based financing transaction” under § 1002.107(a)(5)... to the extent that a purported factoring arrangement involves multiple revolving transactions such that the transaction... is not complete at the time of the sale, that transaction constitutes credit, and the Bureau would expect such a transaction to be reported as an “Other sales-based financing transaction”.

examples: A factor purchases invoices but also provides a revolving advance line allowing the business to incur debt and defer payment. Result: the credit incident must be reported as “Other sales-based financing transaction.”
A provider offers a ‘factoring’ product with ongoing revolving draws and true-up payments; the transaction is not complete at sale. Result: treat as credit and report as other sales-based financing.
A one-time purchase of an existing receivable with no additional advance/credit features. Result: excluded and not reportable as covered credit.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and potential ECOA/Regulation B enforcement action (including civil money penalties) for failing to report covered credit incident to factoring

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 census tract must be collected and reported using a three-level address hierarchy and a basis indicator
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 reportable data, the institution must collect and report the census tract for the location where the proceeds of the covered credit transaction applied for/originated will be principally applied. If that location is unknown, the institution must report the census tract of the applicant’s main office or headquarters; if that is also unknown, the institution must report the census tract of another address or location associated with the applicant. In addition to the census tract, the institution must indicate which of the three address/location types the census tract is based on.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications/covered credit transactions subject to final 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(13) where the institution is required to compile and report the census tract data point.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution does not collect/report the required census tract and basis indicator, the Section 1071 submission will be incomplete/incorrect, creating regulatory reporting violations and potential supervisory findings and resubmission requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: Only where the proceeds location is unknown may the institution use the applicant’s main office/HQ tract; only where both are unknown may the institution use another applicant-associated address/location tract. No exception stated for omitting the basis indicator when census tract is reported.

source_reference.section_id: VIII.B.6.xi; final § 1002.107(a)(13) discussion
source_reference.source_text: Proposed § 1002.107(a)(13) would have required financial institutions to collect and report the census tract containing the address or location where the proceeds of the credit applied for or originated will be or would have been principally applied; or if this information is unknown, the tract containing the address or location of the main office or headquarters of the applicant; or if this information is also unknown, the tract containing another address or location associated with the applicant. In addition to reporting the census tract, the financial institution would have been required to indicate which one of these three types of addresses or locations the census tract is based on.

examples: Origination where proceeds will be used at a new retail site: report census tract for the new retail site location and set tract_basis_indicator = proceeds_principal_location.
Applicant will not identify where proceeds will be used: report census tract for applicant headquarters and set tract_basis_indicator = applicant_hq_or_main_office.

Both proceeds location and HQ address are unknown at time of reporting: report census tract for another applicant-associated address (e.g., mailing address) and set tract_basis_indicator = other_applicant_address.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding requiring data resubmission and potential public enforcement exposure for inaccurate Section 1071 reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Firewall exception notice must disclose that decisioning personnel may access protected demographic responses including LGBTQI plus status
business_rules.description: To use the firewall exception permitting one or more employees/officers involved in making determinations concerning a covered application to access protected demographic information, the institution must provide a notice to each affected applicant. The notice must inform the applicant that one or more decisioning employees/officers may have access to the applicant’s responses regarding whether the applicant is a minority-owned, women-owned, or LGBTQI+-owned business, and responses regarding the ethnicity, race, and sex of the applicant’s principal owners.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution determines that one or more employees/officers involved in making determinations concerning the covered application should have access to protected demographic information under final § 1002.108(c) and seeks to qualify for the exception by providing the notice required under final § 1002.108(d).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution fails to provide the required notice, it does not satisfy the notice condition for the exception and risks violation of the firewall requirements, resulting in supervisory criticism and remediation orders (policy/process changes, re-training, and monitoring).
business_rules.exceptions: The institution may provide the notice more broadly than required (e.g., to all covered credit transaction applicants), but at minimum must provide it to applicants whose responses will be accessed.

source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.108(d) states that in order to satisfy the exception set forth in final § 1002.108(c), a financial institution shall provide a notice to each applicant whose responses to inquiries for protected demographic information will be accessed, informing the applicant that one or more employees or officers involved in making determinations concerning the covered application may have access to the applicant’s responses to the financial institution’s inquiries regarding whether the applicant is a minority-owned business, a women-owned business, or an LGBTQI+-owned business, and regarding the ethnicity, race, and sex of the applicant’s principal owners.

examples: Bank allows underwriter to view principal owners’ race/ethnicity to resolve an application data mismatch: bank must present the § 1002.108(d) notice to that applicant before the protected-demographic inquiries are made.

Bank configures its loan origination system so that certain credit analysts can view women-owned business status: bank must provide the notice to every applicant whose responses could be accessed by those analysts.
Bank chooses to provide the notice to all applicants for a specific small business product even if only some files will be accessed; this is permitted and still satisfies the rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and mandated corrective action for firewall control failures

business_rules.rule_name: Firewall exception notice must be delivered before protected demographic inquiries and alongside required nondiscrimination notices
business_rules.description: When providing the notice required to qualify for the firewall exception, the institution must generally deliver the notice prior to asking whether the applicant is minority-owned, women-owned, or LGBTQI+-owned and prior to asking for any principal owner’s ethnicity, race, or sex. The notice must be provided when making the protected demographic inquiries under § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19) and together with the notices required pursuant to § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any covered application where the institution will rely on the § 1002.108(c) exception and therefore must comply with § 1002.108(d) timing and delivery requirements.
business_rules.consequences: Late delivery (after inquiries) or failure to bundle with required notices can cause noncompliance with § 1002.108(d), jeopardizing the exception and exposing the institution to supervisory findings and required remediation (process redesign, system controls, and training).
business_rules.exceptions: No explicit timing exception stated in the provided excerpt; institutions may provide the notice more broadly than required (e.g., to all applicants), but timing must still be satisfied for affected applicants.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary 108(d)-3; timing and delivery
source_reference.source_text: The financial institution shall provide the notice required by final § 1002.108(d) when making the inquiries required under final § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19) and together with the notices required pursuant to § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19). ... Generally, the financial institution must provide the notice required by § 1002.108(d) prior to asking the applicant if it is a minority-owned, women-owned, or LGBTQI+-owned business and prior to asking for a principal owner’s ethnicity, race, or sex.

examples: Online application flow displays the firewall exception notice on the same screen immediately before the demographic questions; compliant.
Call center agent asks a principal owner’s race before delivering the firewall notice; noncompliant because notice was not provided prior to inquiry.
Bank emails the notice after demographic questions were completed in a prior session; noncompliant because timing requirement was not met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and mandated corrective action for failure to meet notice timing requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 time in business must be reported as collected or otherwise obtained regardless of underwriting reliance
business_rules.description: For the Section 1071 time-in-business data point, the institution must report the time the applicant has been in business as collected or otherwise obtained, regardless of whether the institution relied on that information in underwriting or credit decisioning.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications subject to final § 1002.107(a)(17) where the time-in-business field is required as part of the institution’s compilation of reportable data.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to report time in business as collected/obtained (including where it was not used in underwriting) creates inaccurate Section 1071 data submission risk and potential supervisory findings and resubmission requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt indicates flexibility in methods based on institution procedures, but does not provide an exception that allows omission solely because the institution did not rely on the information in underwriting.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule; § 1002.107(a)(17) time in business
source_reference.source_text: The final rule requires financial institutions to report time in business as collected or otherwise obtained. Although this requirement is similar to reporting the time in business relied on, the new language makes clear that the financial institution reports the time in business collected or obtained regardless of whether it relied on that information in underwriting the application.

examples: Lender does not use time in business for underwriting a credit card product but asks it on the application: lender must still report the collected time in business.
Underwriter uses owner industry experience instead of business age to decide: if the institution obtained business age from applicant financials, it must report that time in business anyway.
Applicant provides time in business during the application intake; lender later disregards it: the reported value must still reflect what was collected/obtained.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding requiring correction/resubmission of Section 1071 data

business_rules.rule_name: Principal owners ethnicity, race, and sex must be requested with a right to refuse and must be reported as provided, declined, or not provided
business_rules.description: For each covered application, the financial institution must ask the applicant to provide the ethnicity, race, and sex of the applicant’s principal owners. The institution must permit the applicant to refuse (decline) to answer and must inform the applicant that it is not required to provide the information. The institution must report either (1) substantive responses, (2) that the applicant declined (e.g., selected “I do not wish to provide this information” or similar), or (3) that the information was “not provided by applicant,” as applicable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution is processing a covered application subject to 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(19). Applies regardless of whether the applicant declines or fails to answer the inquiry about number of principal owners under 1002.107(a)(20).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution fails to request, allow refusal, provide the required notice, or accurately report the applicant’s responses/declination/non-response, the submission may be inaccurate/incomplete and expose the institution to CFPB supervisory findings and potential enforcement for violations of Regulation B/section 1071 reporting requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text. The institution may combine questions with 1002.107(a)(18) and (a)(20) (see related rule).

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to 1002.107(a)(19) Ethnicity, race, and sex of principal owners; comment 1
source_reference.source_text: A financial institution must ask an applicant to provide its principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex. The financial institution must permit an applicant to refuse (i.e., decline) to answer the financial institution’s inquiry and must inform the applicant that it is not required to provide the information... The financial institution must report the applicant’s substantive responses... that the applicant declined... or its failure to respond... (“not provided by applicant”), as applicable.

examples: A borrower applies for a $900,000 owner-occupied CRE term loan. The lender asks for principal owners’ ethnicity/race/sex; the borrower selects “I do not wish to provide.” The lender reports “applicant declined” for each field.

An applicant provides the principal owner’s race but leaves ethnicity and sex blank. The lender reports the race as provided and reports the remaining fields as declined or not provided, consistent with how the form captured the omission.
A loan officer takes an application by phone and the caller refuses to answer the demographic questions. The lender records the refusal as “declined” and includes it in the 1071 dataset.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism, required remediation and resubmission of data, and potential civil money penalties for Regulation B/section 1071 violations

business_rules.rule_name: Partial principal owner demographic responses must be reported as provided plus declined or not provided for the remainder
business_rules.description: If an applicant provides some, but not all, requested information about the ethnicity, race, and sex of a principal owner, the institution must report the information that was provided and must report that the applicant declined to provide or did not provide (as applicable) the remainder of the information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications where demographic data is requested under 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(19) and the applicant provides incomplete demographic responses for any principal owner.
business_rules.consequences: Reporting only the provided fields without coding the remainder as declined/not provided results in incomplete or inaccurate 1071 reporting and may require correction/resubmission and trigger supervisory findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to 1002.107(a)(19) Ethnicity, race, and sex of principal owners; comment 1
source_reference.source_text: If an applicant provides some, but not all, of the requested information about the ethnicity, race, and sex of a principal owner, the financial institution reports the information that was provided by the applicant and reports that the applicant declined to provide or did not provide (as applicable) the remainder of the information.

examples: Applicant provides 'Female' for sex but leaves race and ethnicity blank on the form. The lender reports sex='Female' and codes race/ethnicity as declined or not provided, as appropriate.
Applicant provides race categories but selects 'I do not wish to provide' for ethnicity. The lender reports the race responses and codes ethnicity as declined.
Telephone application: applicant answers race but refuses to answer sex. The lender reports the race response and codes sex as declined.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and required correction/resubmission of inaccurate 1071 data

business_rules.rule_name: Definition of principal owner must be provided when requesting principal owners ethnicity, race, and sex
business_rules.description: When requesting a principal owner’s ethnicity, race, and sex, the institution must provide the applicant with the definition of “principal owner” as set forth in 12 CFR 1002.102(o). Providing the definition as set forth in the sample data collection form in appendix E satisfies this requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the institution requests principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex under 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(19).
business_rules.consequences: If the definition is not provided, the demographic request process is noncompliant, creating risk of supervisory findings and potential enforcement for 1071 procedural violations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. The institution may satisfy the requirement via appendix E sample form language.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to 1002.107(a)(19); comment 2
source_reference.source_text: When requesting a principal owner’s ethnicity, race, and sex, the financial institution must also provide the applicant with the definition of the term “principal owner” as set forth in § 1002.102(o). The financial institution satisfies this requirement if it provides the definition of principal owner as set forth in the sample data collection form in appendix E.

examples: Online application: the demographic page includes a tooltip defining 'principal owner' per appendix E; requirement satisfied.
Paper form: lender includes the 'principal owner' definition text directly above the ethnicity/race/sex questions; requirement satisfied.
Telephone application: script includes the principal owner definition before asking demographic questions; requirement satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and mandated process remediation

business_rules.rule_name: Non-discrimination and purpose notices must be provided when requesting principal owners ethnicity, race, and sex
business_rules.description: When requesting a principal owner’s ethnicity, race, and sex, the institution must inform the applicant that it cannot discriminate on the basis of a principal owner’s ethnicity, race, or sex/gender, or on whether the applicant provides the information. The institution must also inform the applicant that Federal law requires it to ask for the information to help ensure fair treatment and that communities’ small business credit needs are being fulfilled. The institution may combine these notices with similar notices for business status questions if requested at the same time or on the same form.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications when the institution requests principal owners’ ethnicity/race/sex under 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(19).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to provide required notices creates noncompliance risk and may lead to supervisory findings, required remediation, and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Notices may be combined with notices required for 1002.107(a)(18) if information is requested together.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to 1002.107(a)(19); comment 4
source_reference.source_text: 4. Notices. When requesting a principal owner ’s ethnicity, race, and sex from an applicant, a financial institution must info rm the applicant that the financial institution cannot discriminate on the basis of a principal owner ’s ethnicity, race, or sex/gender, or on whether the applicant provides the

examples: Electronic data collection form includes the required notice text above the ethnicity/race/sex questions; compliant.
Branch staff asks demographic questions but does not provide the notice about non-discrimination and purpose; noncompliant and requires remediation/training.
Lender uses one combined notice covering both business status questions and principal owner demographic questions on the same form; compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and mandated corrective action (forms/scripts updates)

business_rules.rule_name: Small business lending application register must be submitted annually by June 1 with certification and weekend extension to Monday
business_rules.description: On or before June 1 following the calendar year for which data are compiled and maintained under 12 CFR 1002.107, a covered financial institution must submit its small business lending application register in the Bureau-prescribed format. An authorized representative with knowledge of the data must certify accuracy and completeness. If June 1 falls on a Saturday or Sunday, the submission is timely if submitted on the next succeeding Monday.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions required to report small business lending data under 12 CFR 1002.109(a)(1). Reporting period is the calendar year.
business_rules.consequences: Late, uncertified, or incomplete submissions can lead to supervisory findings, required resubmission, and potential enforcement exposure for failure to comply with reporting obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: Weekend rule: when June 1 is Saturday or Sunday, timely if submitted on the next Monday.

source_reference.section_id: Final 1002.109(a)(1)(i)-(iii) Annual reporting
source_reference.source_text: on or before June 1 foll owing the calendar year for which data are co mpiled and maintained as required by § 1002.107, a covered financial institution shall submit its small business lending application register in the

examples: For 2026 data, the institution submits the register on May 20, 2027; submission is timely and must be certified by an authorized representative.
June 1, 2029 falls on a Sunday; the institution submits on Monday June 2, 2029; submission is considered timely.
Institution submits on June 5 without certification; submission is noncompliant and may require resubmission with certification.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism, corrective action plan, potential civil money penalties for reporting failures

business_rules.rule_name: Merchant cash advances must be treated as covered credit transactions for CFPB Section 1071 reporting
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 (Regulation B small business data collection), the definition of “covered credit transaction” includes merchant cash advances. A covered credit transaction is an extension of business credit that is not an excluded transaction under § 1002.104(b), and final comment 104(a)-1 reiterates that covered credit transactions include merchant cash advances. The Bureau distinguishes merchant cash advances from factoring and states they should not be excluded as factoring.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to financial institutions subject to CFPB 1071 that originate or purchase business-purpose credit, when determining whether a merchant cash advance (or similar sales-based financing product) is a covered credit transaction requiring data collection and reporting.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution offers merchant cash advances and fails to treat them as covered credit transactions, it may under-collect/under-report required 1071 data, creating fair lending and regulatory compliance risk and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Only transactions that meet an explicit exclusion under § 1002.104(b) are excluded; merchant cash advances are not excluded as factoring per the discussion in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule – § 1002.104(a) covered credit transactions include merchant cash advances
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.104(a) defines the term “covered credit transaction” as an extension of business credit that is not an excluded transaction under § 1002.104(b). Final comment 104(a)-1 reiterates that the term “covered credit transaction” includes merchant cash advances... Nor does the Bureau believe that merchant cash advances should be excluded from the rule as a species of factoring...

examples: Institution provides a $50,000 merchant cash advance repaid via a percentage of daily card receipts over 6 months → treat as a covered credit transaction for 1071 data collection/reporting (unless another exclusion applies).
Institution classifies all MCAs as ‘factoring’ and excludes them from 1071 reporting → noncompliant per the rule’s interpretation; requires remediation and potential resubmission of data.
Institution offers sales-based financing with repayment over 3–12 months similar to MCA arrangements → should be evaluated as credit and likely covered unless specifically excluded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; public enforcement action; civil money penalty; mandated data correction/resubmission

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 submission must include the institution’s RSSD ID if applicable and parent name plus LEI or RSSD if available without requiring a new LEI
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 reporting, the financial institution must report its RSSD ID number if applicable. Institutions that do not have RSSD IDs (typically nondepository institutions) are not required to obtain one and should report “not applicable” in that field. The institution must also provide certain information on parent entities, if applicable, including the immediate parent and top-holding parent names, and the parent LEI (if available) and parent RSSD ID (if available). Parent entities are not required to obtain an LEI if they do not already have one.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to financial institutions reporting small business lending data to the CFPB under § 1002.109, for fields related to institutional identifiers and organizational structure.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution omits an applicable RSSD ID, fails to provide parent entity information when applicable, or incorrectly attempts to force creation of an LEI when it is not required, the submission may be incomplete or inaccurate, increasing supervisory and enforcement risk and triggering resubmission/correction obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: If the institution does not have an RSSD ID, it is not required to obtain one and should report “not applicable.” If a parent entity does not already have an LEI, it is not required to obtain one for reporting purposes.

source_reference.section_id: Paragraph 109(b)(7) and 109(b)(8) – RSSD ID and parent entity identifiers
source_reference.source_text: Proposed § 1002.109(b)(7) would have required a financial institution to report its RSSD ID number, if applicable... Financial institutions that do not have RSSD IDs... would not have been required to obtain them, and would report “not applicable” in that field... Proposed § 1002.109(b)(8) would have required... the name, the LEI (if available), and the RSSD ID (if available) of the financial institution’s immediate parent entity and... top-holding parent... no parent entity would be required to obtain an LEI if it did not already have one.

examples: FDIC-insured bank reports 1071 data → must include its RSSD ID (it has one) and must report parent holding company name and parent RSSD ID if available; parent LEI only if it already exists.
Nondepository lender subject to 1071 has no RSSD ID → reports “not applicable” and is not required to obtain an RSSD ID.

Institution’s immediate parent has no LEI → institution reports the parent name and leaves parent LEI blank/‘not available’ as allowed; it must not require the parent to obtain an LEI solely for reporting.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; required data correction; potential enforcement and civil money penalties for inaccurate reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Merchant cash advances are covered credit transactions unless excluded under Regulation B Section 1002.104(b)
business_rules.description: For CFPB small business lending rule purposes (ECOA section 1071 / Regulation B), an institution must treat merchant cash advances as included within “covered credit transactions” (i.e., business credit) unless the transaction qualifies as an excluded transaction under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution extends business credit in the form of a merchant cash advance and the transaction is not otherwise excluded by Regulation B § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.consequences: If treated as covered, the institution must collect, maintain, and report required section 1071 data points for the covered application/covered credit transaction (as applicable). Failure to classify correctly may lead to inaccurate or incomplete 1071 reporting and regulatory criticism or enforcement under ECOA/Reg B.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply if the transaction is an excluded transaction under Regulation B § 1002.104(b).

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.104 > Merchant Cash Advances > Background and Proposed Rule
source_reference.source_text: proposed § 1002.104(a) would have defined the term “covered credit transaction” as an extension of business credit that is not an excluded transaction under proposed § 1002.104(b), and proposed comment 104(a)-1 would have reiterated that the term “covered credit transaction” includes all business credit (including loans, lines of credit, credit cards, and merchant cash advances) unless otherwise excluded under § 1002.104(b).

examples: A fintech provides a $50,000 merchant cash advance repaid via a holdback percentage from daily card receipts; unless excluded under § 1002.104(b), it must be treated as a covered credit transaction for 1071 purposes.
A bank offers a merchant cash advance product through a subsidiary; if the extension is business credit and not excluded, the institution must include it in its 1071 compliance universe.
A lender re-labels a merchant cash advance as a “sale of receivables” but it functions as business credit; it remains a covered credit transaction unless excluded under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding for inaccurate 1071 coverage determination; potential ECOA/Reg B enforcement action requiring remediation and resubmission/correction of reported data

business_rules.rule_name: Sales-based financing is treated as credit under Regulation B and is covered unless excluded
business_rules.description: An institution must treat sales-based financing transactions (including merchant cash advances and similar revenue-based repayment products) as covered by the Regulation B definition of “credit” and therefore within the scope of covered credit transactions for 1071 purposes unless excluded under § 1002.104(b). The CFPB indicates it did not specifically define sales-based financing because these products are covered by the definition of “credit.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution extends funds to a business where repayment is based on anticipated sales, revenue, or invoices (sales-based financing), and the transaction is not excluded under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.consequences: Transactions must be included in 1071 scoping for data collection/reporting if not excluded. Misclassification as non-credit (or outside scope) can create missing or incorrect 1071 reporting and supervisory issues.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply where a specific exclusion under § 1002.104(b) applies to the transaction.

source_reference.section_id: Footnote 359 within Merchant Cash Advances discussion
source_reference.source_text: As stated below, the Bureau is not specifically defining sales-based financing in the rule because the Bureau believes these products are covered by the definition of “credit” in final § 1002.102(i). New York and California laws have recently sought to define sales-based financing.

examples: A lender advances funds to a restaurant and collects repayment as a variable percentage of daily revenue; it should be scoped as credit and treated as covered unless excluded under § 1002.104(b).
A provider uses a fixed daily withdrawal with a reconciliation (true-up) to align payments to revenue; it remains sales-based financing covered as credit unless excluded.
A lender purchases a portion of future invoices with payments varying by invoice volume; if structured as credit under Reg B’s definition, it should be included in 1071 coverage analysis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for under-reporting covered credit transactions; potential ECOA/Reg B enforcement action requiring corrective action plan

business_rules.rule_name: Agricultural-purpose credit is not categorically excluded from covered credit transactions under Regulation B Section 1002.104
business_rules.description: An institution must not treat agricultural-purpose credit as categorically excluded from the CFPB’s section 1071 covered credit transaction definition. Agricultural-purpose credit is generally covered by the broad ECOA/Regulation B definition of credit, and the CFPB did not finalize an exclusion for agricultural credit under § 1002.104.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to business credit used for agricultural purposes (including credit to agricultural businesses) where the transaction otherwise meets the rule’s definition of covered credit transaction and is not excluded under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.consequences: Agricultural-purpose covered applications/transactions must be included in the institution’s 1071 data collection and reporting program if not excluded. If incorrectly excluded, the institution may under-report and face supervisory findings or required corrections.
business_rules.exceptions: If a specific exclusion under § 1002.104(b) applies to the particular transaction, that transaction may still be excluded; this rule only rejects a categorical agricultural-purpose carve-out.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.104 > Agricultural-Purpose Credit > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau is not defining a “covered credit transaction” in final § 1002.104 in a way that would exclude agricultural credit from the final rule. Credit used for agricultural purposes is generally covered by the broad definition of credit under ECOA.

examples: A bank originates a line of credit to a small farm to purchase seed and equipment; if not excluded under § 1002.104(b), it is within 1071 scoping.
A Farm Credit lender makes a business-purpose agricultural loan to an independently owned ranching operation; it cannot be excluded solely because it is agricultural-purpose credit.
A credit union renews operating credit for a farming enterprise; the renewal must be evaluated for 1071 coverage like other business credit, not exempted by purpose alone.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding for incorrect product/purpose scoping; required remediation and expanded 1071 data reporting population

business_rules.rule_name: Business credit products not explicitly listed are still covered credit transactions unless excluded under Regulation B Section 1002.104(b)
business_rules.description: An institution must not rely on a finite product list to determine 1071 coverage. Products discussed in the rule commentary are not exhaustive; other types of business credit still constitute covered credit transactions unless excluded by § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any business credit product offered or originated/held by the institution that is not specifically described in the rule’s discussion but meets the definition of business credit/covered credit transaction and is not excluded under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.consequences: The institution must evaluate all business credit products for 1071 coverage and include those not excluded in data collection/reporting. Failure can cause systematic under-reporting and exam criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Transactions explicitly excluded by § 1002.104(b) remain excluded.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.104 > Agricultural-Purpose Credit > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: the products discussed in this rule do not constitute an exhaustive list of covered credit transactions; other types of business credit not specifically described in the rule and its associated commentary nevertheless constitute covered credit transactions unless excluded by final § 1002.104(b).

examples: A lender offers a specialized bridge product for small businesses that is not called out in commentary; it still must be assessed for 1071 coverage unless excluded under § 1002.104(b).
A CRE lender provides a short-term working capital facility secured by business assets; if it is business credit and not excluded, it must be included even if not listed in examples.
A lender pilots a new revenue-sharing finance product; it must be analyzed under § 1002.104 and included unless excluded under § 1002.104(b).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for incomplete coverage inventory and under-reporting; required product scoping remediation

business_rules.rule_name: Gross annual revenue must reflect the business’s entire gross annual revenue for the last fiscal year and align with small business status determination
business_rules.description: For 1071 reporting under final § 1002.107(a)(14), the institution must collect and report the applicant’s entire gross annual revenue for the business’s last fiscal year (not merely the revenue figure relied on in the credit decision).
