The institution should avoid using different gross annual revenue figures for determining small business status versus reporting the gross annual revenue data point, because discrepancies increase compliance risk.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications for covered credit transactions where the institution is required to collect/report 1071 data, including the gross annual revenue data point under § 1002.107(a)(14).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution reports a different revenue figure than that used for small business status determination, it may create reportable data inconsistencies and heightened compliance risk. Inaccurate reporting may require correction and may result in supervisory findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; collection may rely on applicant-provided revenue without verification (see related rule on verification).

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.107(a)(14) Gross Annual Revenue > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: the statutory language requiring the collection and reporting of “the gross annual revenue of the business in the last fiscal year” ... using the small business’s entire gross annual revenue ... The Bureau believes it important to ensure that the gross annual revenue figure used to determine small business status is the same total figure as the gross annual revenue reported for the data point.

examples: An institution screens an applicant as a small business using a $5,000,000-or-less revenue question; it should collect and report the specific entire gross annual revenue amount for the last fiscal year using the same total-revenue concept.

A lender underwrites using only a business line’s segment revenue, but the applicant provides total company gross annual revenue; for 1071, the lender reports the applicant’s entire gross annual revenue figure for the last fiscal year.
A borrower provides $3.2 million in gross annual revenue for the last fiscal year; the institution reports $3.2 million as the 1071 gross annual revenue data point.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding for inaccurate 1071 data; potential requirement to correct and resubmit data and enhance procedures

business_rules.rule_name: Gross annual revenue verification is not required, but verified revenue may be reported when retained in the institution’s systems
business_rules.description: For 1071 gross annual revenue reporting, the institution is not required to verify applicant-provided gross annual revenue information to comply with § 1002.107(a)(14). However, if the institution verifies gross annual revenue and retains the verified amount in its systems, it may report the verified number and is not required to maintain both the applicant-stated and verified numbers.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when collecting/reporting gross annual revenue under § 1002.107(a)(14), including situations where the institution verifies revenue during underwriting or onboarding.
business_rules.consequences: The institution may streamline system storage by retaining/reporting the verified amount. If the institution incorrectly assumes verification is required and denies/withdraws an application due to inability to verify, it may create process friction and potential compliance and operational risks; if it incorrectly reports unverified or conflicting figures, it may lead to reporting inaccuracies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.107(a)(14) Gross Annual Revenue > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: comment 107(a)(14)-1 already clarifies that a financial institution need not verify gross annual revenue information provided by the applicant to comply with final § 1002.107(a)(14) ... the financial institution may report the verified number retained in its system and is not required to maintain both numbers in its system.

examples: A borrower states $4.0 million in gross annual revenue; the bank does not verify revenue for this product and reports $4.0 million as provided by applicant.

During underwriting, a lender obtains tax returns and updates its system to $3.8 million verified gross annual revenue; it may report $3.8 million and does not need to store the earlier $4.0 million stated value.

A lender partially verifies revenue but does not update the system of record; it may continue to rely on and report the applicant’s statement without needing to report partially verified information (consistent with the discussion that verification is not required).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for inconsistent revenue reporting and inadequate procedures; potential requirement to enhance controls and correct data

business_rules.rule_name: If gross annual revenue cannot be collected or determined, report the value as not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined
business_rules.description: The institution must maintain procedures reasonably designed to collect applicant-provided data, including gross annual revenue. If, despite those procedures, the institution is unable to collect or determine a specific gross annual revenue amount, it must report the gross annual revenue as “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications requiring reporting of gross annual revenue under § 1002.107(a)(14), especially when the application is denied or withdrawn early and gross annual revenue cannot be collected or determined.
business_rules.consequences: The institution must use the prescribed reporting value instead of leaving the field blank or inventing a proxy. Failure to do so may result in 1071 data validity issues, edit failures, resubmission requirements, and supervisory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > § 1002.107(a)(14) Gross Annual Revenue > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: pursuant to final § 1002.107(c), a financial institution shall maintain procedures reasonably designed to collect applicant-provided data, including the gross annual revenue of the applicant. ... if a financial institution is nonetheless unable to collect or determine the specific gross annual revenue of the applicant, the financial institution reports that the gross annual revenue is “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.”

examples: An applicant withdraws a request for a $250,000 working capital line before submitting any financials and refuses to provide revenue; the institution reports gross annual revenue as “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.”

A lender receives an incomplete application through a broker and cannot determine the last fiscal year revenue; after applying its procedures, it reports the prescribed “not provided... undetermined” value.
A borrower provides conflicting revenue figures and the lender cannot determine a specific amount before denial; it reports “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.”
business_rules.enforcement_action: Data integrity finding and requirement to remediate procedures; potential resubmission/correction of 1071 data

business_rules.rule_name: Formerly covered Section 1071 reporters may collect protected demographic information for up to five calendar years after last required reporting year if they follow Subpart B safeguards
business_rules.description: A creditor that was required to report small business lending data under final 12 CFR 1002.109 for any of the preceding five calendar years, but is not currently a covered financial institution under final 12 CFR 1002.105(b), may collect protected demographic information under Subpart B for covered applications from small businesses (final 12 CFR 1002.103 and 1002.106(b)) if it complies with Subpart B requirements applicable to covered financial institutions (including final 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), 1002.108, 1002.111, and 1002.112). Such creditor is permitted, but not required, to report collected data if it complies with reporting requirements in final 12 CFR 1002.109 and 1002.110.
business_rules.conditions: Creditor is not currently covered under 1002.105(b) but had a reporting obligation under 1002.109 in at least one of the prior five calendar years; applies to covered applications from small businesses when collecting protected demographic information pursuant to Subpart B.
business_rules.consequences: If the creditor collects protected demographic information under this permission, it must implement the Subpart B collection safeguards and procedures referenced; failure to comply may create Regulation B/1071 compliance risk and supervisory findings. Reporting of the collected data is optional, but if the creditor reports, it must comply with 1002.109 and 1002.110 reporting rules.
business_rules.exceptions: Only applies to creditors meeting the 'preceding five calendar years' prior reporting condition; otherwise see other provisions (e.g., 1002.5(a)(4)(viii) or (ix)).

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule > Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(vii)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(vii) provides that a creditor that was required to report small business lending data pursuant to final § 1002.109 for any of the preceding five calendar years but is not currently a covered financial institution under final § 1002.105(b) may collect protected demographic information pursuant to subpart B... if it complies with the requirements of subpart B... Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(vii) also states that such a creditor is permitted, but not required, to report data...

examples: Creditor reported 1071 data in 2024 but falls below thresholds in 2026; in 2027 it may continue collecting protected demographic information for covered applications if it follows Subpart B safeguards, because 2024 is within the preceding five calendar years.
Creditor chooses to collect protected demographic information under 1002.5(a)(4)(vii) but does not implement required Subpart B procedures—this creates 1071/Reg B compliance risk.
Creditor collects under 1002.5(a)(4)(vii) and voluntarily submits data to CFPB; it must follow 1002.109 and 1002.110 requirements for that submission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; required remediation; potential civil money penalty for pattern/practice violations

business_rules.rule_name: Creditor exceeding the Section 1071 loan-volume threshold in year one may collect protected demographic information in year two if Subpart B requirements are met
business_rules.description: A creditor that exceeded the loan-volume threshold in the first year of the two-year threshold period in final 12 CFR 1002.105(b) may, in the second year, collect protected demographic information under Subpart B for covered applications from small businesses (final 12 CFR 1002.103 and 1002.106(b)), provided it complies with the Subpart B requirements applicable to covered financial institutions (including final 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), 1002.108, 1002.111, and 1002.112). The creditor is permitted, but not required, to report data collected under this provision if it complies with final 12 CFR 1002.109 and 1002.110.
business_rules.conditions: Creditor exceeded the 1002.105(b) loan-volume threshold in the first year of the two-year threshold period; applies in the second year for covered applications from small businesses.
business_rules.consequences: Permits early collection ahead of the reporting obligation to operationalize compliance systems. If the creditor collects, it must comply with Subpart B requirements; voluntary reporting is allowed only if reporting requirements are met.
business_rules.exceptions: Applies only to the specific 'exceeded threshold in first year' circumstance described.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule > Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(viii)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(viii) provides a creditor that exceeded the loan-volume threshold in the first year of the two-year threshold period provided in final § 1002.105(b) may, in the second year, collect protected demographic information pursuant to subpart B for covered applications... if it complies with the requirements of subpart B... Final § 1002.5(a)(4)(viii) also states that such a creditor is permitted, but not required, to report data...

examples: Creditor crosses the 1002.105(b) threshold in 2026 (year one) and expects coverage; it may collect protected demographic information for covered applications received in 2027 (year two) to prepare.
Creditor collects protected demographic information in year two but does not follow 1002.108/1002.111 firewall/procedural requirements—creates 1071 compliance risk.
Creditor collects in year two and voluntarily submits data; it must meet 1002.109 and 1002.110 reporting requirements for that submission.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; required remediation; potential civil money penalty for noncompliant collection/reporting practices

business_rules.rule_name: Regulation B adverse action notice must follow consumer-like timing and content when business applicant gross revenues are $1 million or less in preceding fiscal year
business_rules.description: For business credit applicants with gross revenues of $1,000,000 or less in the preceding fiscal year, Regulation B requires the creditor to provide an adverse action notification that is generally similar to that provided to a consumer in both substance and timing. This threshold drives creditor compliance processes for small business lending and requires obtaining gross annual revenue early enough to apply the correct notice requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating adverse action notice requirements for business credit applicants; threshold is gross revenues ≤ $1,000,000 in the applicant’s preceding fiscal year (Reg B).
business_rules.consequences: If the creditor fails to provide the consumer-like adverse action notification (timing and substance) when the applicant’s preceding-fiscal-year gross revenues are ≤$1,000,000, the creditor may violate Regulation B and face supervisory findings and potential enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. (Other Reg B exceptions/nuances may apply outside the provided text.)

source_reference.section_id: Background > Market considerations (Reg B notice threshold citation)
source_reference.source_text: retail/small business lenders routinely collect applicants’ gross annual revenue information because notification requirements under existing Regulation B vary for business credit applicants depending on whether they “had gross revenues of $1 million or less in [their] preceding fiscal year.” For a business applicant with gross annual revenues of $1 million or less, a creditor must provide a notification following an adverse action, such as a credit denial, that is generally similar to that provided to a consumer in both substance and timing.

examples: Applicant’s prior fiscal year gross revenues are $900,000 and the application is denied—creditor must issue the consumer-like adverse action notice (timing/substance).
Applicant’s prior fiscal year gross revenues are $1,200,000 and the application is denied—this specific $1MM consumer-like notice requirement does not apply (other Reg B requirements still apply).
Revenue is not captured, and creditor issues a non-consumer-like notice for an applicant later determined to be $1MM or less—potential Reg B violation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; fair lending/compliance program criticism; potential civil money penalty for Reg B violations

CRE_LOAN.definition: A commercial real estate loan whose primary repayment source is cash flow from real estate and whose risk profile is sensitive to general CRE market conditions, as scoped by the guidance.
examples: An SBA 7(a) loan used to acquire owner-occupied commercial real estate where SBA guaranty fees and SBA interest rate caps apply.

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum SBA 7(a) upfront fee is calculated on guaranteed portion with tiered percentages by gross loan size and maturity
business_rules.description: The SBA 7(a) Upfront Fee percentage is determined by total (gross) loan size and maturity, but the fee amount is calculated on the guaranteed portion of the loan (not the total loan amount). Maximum upfront fee percentages: (1) Loans of $150,000 or less with maturity > 12 months: 2% of guaranteed portion (lender authorized to retain 25% of the fee); (2) $150,001 to $700,000 with maturity > 12 months: 3% of guaranteed portion; (3) $700,001 to $5,000,000 with maturity > 12 months: 3.5% of guaranteed portion up to $1,000,000 PLUS 3.75% of guaranteed portion over $1,000,000; (4) Short-term loans up to $5,000,000 with maturity <= 12 months: 0.25% of guaranteed portion. SBA may announce temporary changes for a fiscal year via Information Notice. For SBA Express only, the upfront fee is waived for qualifying veteran-related ownership/control categories described in the SOP.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans where an SBA guaranty applies and an upfront fee is due; determine gross loan size, maturity category, and guaranteed portion amount.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must compute/remit the upfront fee consistent with the tier and on the guaranteed portion. Overcharging borrower or miscalculating fee can create SBA compliance defects and potentially jeopardize guaranty purchase/ability to maintain guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA may temporarily change fees for a specific fiscal year via Information Notice. SBA Express upfront fee is waived for specified veteran/service-related ownership/control categories.

source_reference.section_id: Calculation of the Upfront Fee > MAXIMUM UPFRONT FEE CHART (13 CFR § 120.220) and SBA Express waiver note
source_reference.source_text: The total loan amount determines the percentage... the Upfront Fee is based on the guaranteed portion... Loans of $150,000 or less (Maturity more than 12 months)... 2% of guaranteed portion... $150,001 to $700,000... 3%... $700,001 to $5,000,000... 3.5%... PLUS 3.75%... Short-Term Loans... 0.25% of the guaranteed portion... For SBA Express only: The Upfront Fee is waived for loans to businesses owned and controlled by a veteran...

examples: A $100,000 loan with an 85% guaranty has a guaranteed portion of $85,000. Upfront fee tier is 2%; fee = $1,700, and the lender may retain 25% ($425) as permitted.
A $5,000,000 loan with 75% guaranty has $3,750,000 guaranteed portion; upfront fee = 3.5% of first $1,000,000 ($35,000) plus 3.75% of remaining $2,750,000 ($103,125) = $138,125.
A $2,000,000 short-term (<=12 months) loan with 50% guaranty: guaranteed portion $1,000,000; upfront fee = 0.25% of $1,000,000 = $2,500.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; potential repair/denial of guaranty purchase for fee/terms noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: If SBA 7(a) loan proceeds will pay the upfront fee, E-Tran Terms and Conditions must include a use of proceeds category and first disbursement cannot be primarily for the fee
business_rules.description: The borrower may use SBA 7(a) loan proceeds to pay the SBA Upfront Fee; however, the first disbursement may not be made primarily for the purpose of paying the Upfront Fee. If the borrower plans to use loan proceeds to pay the Upfront Fee, the E-Tran Terms and Conditions must include a Use of Proceeds category for this purpose. If an escrow closing is used, the 7(a) Lender may charge the borrower the Upfront Fee only when all loan funds have been disbursed to the borrower from the escrow account.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) loan is SBA 7(a), and (b) borrower intends to fund upfront fee from loan proceeds and/or escrow closing structure is used.
business_rules.consequences: If E-Tran does not reflect a Use of Proceeds category for the upfront fee when proceeds are used, this is an SBA documentation/terms defect. If first disbursement is primarily to pay the upfront fee, this is a disbursement-use violation and may trigger SBA findings and jeopardize guaranty purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Escrow closing timing rule: charge only when all loan funds have been disbursed from escrow.

source_reference.section_id: SBA Guaranty Fee (Upfront Fee) > Borrower use of proceeds; E-Tran Terms and Conditions; escrow closing note
source_reference.source_text: The Borrower may use loan proceeds to pay the Upfront Fee; however, the first disbursement may not be made primarily for the purpose of paying the Upfront Fee. If the Borrower plans to use loan proceeds to pay the Upfront Fee, the E-Tran Terms and Conditions must include a Use of Proceeds category for this purpose. Note: When an escrow closing is used, the 7(a) Lender may charge the Borrower the Upfront Fee only when all loan funds have been disbursed...

examples: A borrower wants the loan to cover the SBA upfront fee. The lender adds an explicit upfront-fee Use of Proceeds line in E-Tran and ensures the first disbursement is mainly for project costs, not the fee.
A lender disburses $10,000 solely to pay the upfront fee as the first disbursement. This violates the rule that the first disbursement may not be primarily for paying the upfront fee.
An escrow closing is used. The lender waits to charge the borrower the upfront fee until the escrow agent has disbursed all loan funds to the borrower.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; potential repair/denial of guaranty purchase due to disbursement/documentation defects

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) upfront fee refund is only available before closing and initial disbursement and depends on maturity
business_rules.description: Upfront fee refunds are limited as follows (13 CFR § 120.220(c)): The upfront fee is based on the amount approved by SBA prior to the loan being closed and initially disbursed. To reduce the upfront fee due to a decreased approved amount, SBA must approve the decrease prior to closing and initial disbursement. Full refund: Allowed only for loans with maturity > 12 months when the loan has not been closed and initially disbursed and the lender requests cancellation of the guaranty via E-Tran; once initially disbursed, no refund is permitted. For loans with maturity <= 12 months, once the SBA loan number has been issued, no refund will be given. Partial refund: If SBA approves cancellation of a portion of the loan prior to closing and initial disbursement, SBA adjusts the upfront fee and refunds the excess if already paid; if the loan has been closed and initially disbursed, no refund is permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender seeks a refund or reduction of SBA upfront fee due to cancellation/decrease of approved loan amount; consider loan maturity and whether the loan has been closed and initially disbursed.
business_rules.consequences: If the loan has been initially disbursed, the lender is not eligible for a refund. If the lender wants a reduced upfront fee due to decreased approved amount, it must obtain SBA approval prior to closing/initial disbursement and process adjustment through E-Tran.
business_rules.exceptions: Short-term loans (<= 12 months): once SBA loan number is issued, no refund is available (even if not disbursed).

source_reference.section_id: Upfront Fee Refunds (13 CFR § 120.220(c))
source_reference.source_text: Upfront Fee Refunds ( 13 CFR § 120.220(c) ). The Upfront F ee is based on the amount that SBA has approved prior to the loan being closed and initially disbursed. Any reque st by the 7(a) Lender to decrease the approved amount must be approved by

examples: A 7-year SBA 7(a) loan is approved but the borrower withdraws before closing and no disbursement occurs. The lender cancels the guaranty via E-Tran and may receive a full upfront fee refund.
A 10-month SBA short-term loan is assigned an SBA loan number but is never closed. No upfront fee refund is permitted once the SBA loan number has been issued.
Before closing, SBA approves reducing the approved loan amount from $1,000,000 to $900,000. SBA adjusts the upfront fee and refunds the overpaid amount if the lender already remitted the higher fee.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; financial loss from nonrefundable fees due to failure to cancel/adjust before disbursement

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum variable interest rate spreads for SBA 7(a) Small loans range from base plus 3.0% to base plus 6.5% depending on loan amount
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Small variable-rate loans, the allowable spread above the base rate depends on the loan amount (13 CFR § 120.214): (a) $50,000 and less: base rate + 6.5%; (b) $50,001 up to and including $250,000: base rate + 6.0%; (c) $250,001 up to and including $350,000: base rate + 4.5%; (d) $350,001 and higher: base rate + 3.0%. Acceptable base rates for 7(a) Small are the Prime Rate or the SBA Optional Peg Rate. Base rates are rounded to two decimal places, with .004 rounded down to .00 and .005 rounded up to .01. The spread identified in the Note may not be changed during the life of the loan without the borrower’s written agreement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small loans with a variable interest rate; lender must identify base rate and ensure spread does not exceed SBA maximum for the loan amount.
business_rules.consequences: If the spread exceeds the permitted maximum, the loan is noncompliant with SBA interest rate requirements and may be subject to SBA findings and potential guaranty repair/denial. Changing spread without borrower written agreement violates SBA requirements and may create enforceability/compliance issues.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA Express variable-rate loans may use other base rates used on similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans, but the interest rate may not exceed the maximum allowable 7(a) interest rate (separate SBA Express provisions).

source_reference.section_id: Base Rate, Allowable Spread, and Allowable Variance (13 CFR § 120.214) and General Policy on Interest Rates
source_reference.source_text: For variable interest rate loans... the spread above the base rate as identified in the Note may not be changed during the life of the loan without the written agreement of the Borrower... For 7(a) Small loans... two acceptable base rates: (a) The Prime Rate; or (b) The SBA Optional Peg Rate... Base Rates will be rounded to two decimal places with .004 being rounded down to .00 and .005 being rounded up to .01... allowable spread is based on the loan amount... $50,000 and less: Base rate + 6.5%; $50,001... + 6%; $250,001... + 4.5%; $350,001 and higher: Base rate + 3%.

examples: A $45,000 7(a) Small variable-rate loan using Prime must not exceed Prime + 6.5% spread. If Prime is 8.50%, the note rate cap at origination is 15.00%.
A $300,000 7(a) Small variable-rate loan must not exceed base + 4.5%. If Optional Peg Rate is 5.12%, maximum note rate at origination is 9.62%.
A lender tries to change the spread from +4.5% to +5.0% mid-term without the borrower’s written agreement. This violates the rule that spread may not change without written agreement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; potential repair/denial of guaranty purchase due to interest rate noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express maximum variable interest rate is Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate plus 4.5% for loans $250,001 to $350,000 and plus 3.0% for loans $350,001 and greater
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Export Express loans with variable interest rates, the note rate must not exceed the applicable SBA maximum variable rate cap based on loan size: (1) for loans $250,001 up to and including $350,000, the rate cannot exceed Prime or the SBA Optional Peg Rate + 4.5%; (2) for loans $350,001 and greater, the rate cannot exceed Prime or the SBA Optional Peg Rate + 3.0%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan product is SBA 7(a) Export Express and the interest rate type is variable, at origination and whenever validating ongoing compliance with program caps.
business_rules.consequences: If the proposed or charged variable rate exceeds the applicable cap, the loan is not compliant with SBA interest rate requirements; lender must reduce pricing to within cap and correct the Note/E-Tran terms. Noncompliance may result in SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Fixed-rate Export Express loans follow SBA’s separately published maximum allowable fixed rate (Federal Register / SBA Wiki page).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: Export Express — Product Interest Rate (Page 238)
source_reference.source_text: Export Express Loans  Interest Rate  The maximum allowable fixed rate is available on SBA’s Wiki page. Maximum variable rates are: Loans $250,001 up to and including $350,000  Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 4.5%  Loans $350,001 and greater  Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 3.0%

examples: Loan amount $300,000 with Prime 8.50%: maximum allowed note rate is 8.50% + 4.50% = 13.00%; a proposed 13.25% violates the cap.
Loan amount $500,000 with Optional Peg Rate 6.10%: maximum allowed note rate is 6.10% + 3.00% = 9.10%; a proposed 8.95% complies.
Loan amount $350,000 (exactly): treated in the $250,001–$350,000 bracket; maximum is base + 4.5%.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA review finding; required pricing correction

business_rules.rule_name: Pre-disbursement interest rate change after SBA approval requires borrower written consent separate from closing and LGPC notice or E-Tran Servicing update
business_rules.description: After SBA loan approval and prior to first disbursement, the lender may change the initial Note rate (including base rate, spread, or switching fixed-to-variable or variable-to-fixed) only if the new rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of the loan application. The lender must obtain the borrower’s written consent to the interest rate change separate and apart from executing the loan documents, and must either notify the LGPC of the change or make the change through E‑Tran Servicing (program text shown for International Trade; parallel language appears in other 7(a) products).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when: (1) SBA approval has occurred; (2) no disbursement has occurred yet; and (3) lender proposes changes to initial interest rate terms (base, spread, fixed/variable selection).
business_rules.consequences: Without separate borrower written consent and required LGPC/E‑Tran action, the change is not permitted for SBA program compliance; lender must revert terms or re-document and complete required notice/update. Noncompliance may create SBA servicing/guaranty enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; must still comply with SBA maximums applicable at time of loan application.

source_reference.section_id: Post-Approval Changes to the Interest Rate — Pre-Disbursement Changes
source_reference.source_text: After loan approval and prior to first disbursement, the Lender may change the initial Note rate, including changing the base rate, the spread over the base rate, or change from a fixed rate to a variable rate, or from a variable rate to a fixed rate, provided the new interest rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of the loan application. The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written consent to the change in the interest rate (separate and apart from executing the loan documents) and must either notify the LGPC of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing.

examples: Approved as variable Prime + 3.0%; before first disbursement borrower requests fixed. Lender may switch only if fixed rate is ≤ SBA maximum at time of application and borrower signs separate consent; lender updates via E‑Tran Servicing or notifies LGPC.

Before disbursement lender wants to increase spread from +2.75% to +3.25%; permitted only if new initial note rate remains within max at application date and borrower provides separate written consent.
Before disbursement lender changes base rate from Prime to Optional Peg Rate; requires separate borrower consent and LGPC/E‑Tran update.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA servicing finding; potential guaranty repair/denial; required corrective documentation and system update

business_rules.rule_name: Post-disbursement base rate or spread change on SBA variable rate loan requires borrower written agreement and CLSC notice or E-Tran Servicing update
business_rules.description: After an SBA loan is disbursed, for a variable rate loan the lender may change the base rate or the spread over the base rate only if the new base rate or spread is based on a method permitted when the loan was approved and is consistent with the interest rate regulations at the time the loan was approved. The lender must obtain the borrower’s written agreement and must either notify the CLSC by email to FSC.Servicing@sba.gov of the change or make the change through E‑Tran Servicing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA variable rate loans after disbursement when lender proposes to change base rate selection or spread methodology/amount.
business_rules.consequences: If borrower agreement is missing or if method/regulatory consistency is not met, the change is noncompliant and must be reversed or corrected; failure may lead to SBA servicing actions and guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; further guidance referenced to SOP 50 57.

source_reference.section_id: Post-Approval Changes to the Interest Rate — Post-Disbursement Changes
source_reference.source_text: After the loan is disbursed, on a variable rate loan, the Lender may change the base rate or the spread over the base rate as long as the new base rate or spread is based on a method permitted when the loan was approved and is consistent with the interest rate regulations at the time the loan was approved. The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written agreement and must either notify the CLSC by email to FSC.Servicing@sba.gov of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing.

examples: After disbursement, lender proposes changing base from Prime to Optional Peg Rate; permitted only if that base rate method was permitted at approval and borrower signs written agreement; lender emails FSC.Servicing@sba.gov or updates E‑Tran.
After disbursement, lender increases spread by 0.50% without written borrower agreement; violates rule and requires reversal/correction.
After disbursement, lender changes spread based on a method not permitted at approval; violates rule even if borrower agrees.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA servicing finding; potential guaranty repair/denial; required borrower agreement and servicing notice/update

business_rules.rule_name: SBA variable rate adjustment frequency must be stated in the Note, may start the month after disbursement, and may not adjust more frequently than monthly without borrower consent to change the adjustment period
business_rules.description: For SBA variable rate loans: (1) the first interest rate adjustment may occur on the first calendar day of the month following initial disbursement using the base rate in effect on the first business day of the month; lenders may delay the initial adjustment period (example up to 5 years). (2) The lender must specify in the Note the frequency of interest rate adjustment. (3) The adjustment period identified in the Note may not be changed without the borrower’s written consent. (4) Subsequent adjustments may occur no more frequently than monthly and set the rate on the first calendar day of the adjustment period using the base rate in effect on the first business day of that period; the rate changes on the first calendar day even if the base rate is not known until the second business day.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) variable rate loans (including International Trade and CAPLines text shown) when establishing Note terms and when performing any servicing change to adjustment mechanics.
business_rules.consequences: If the Note omits adjustment frequency or if adjustments occur more frequently than monthly, or if the adjustment period is changed without borrower written consent, the loan is noncompliant and requires correction; may trigger SBA servicing findings and guaranty repair risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Export Express lenders may use intervals used on similarly-sized non-SBA loans, but must still specify the frequency in the Note and follow SBA’s stated mechanics where applicable in program guidance.

source_reference.section_id: Frequency of Interest Rate Adjustment (Pages 291-292)
source_reference.source_text: The first adjustment may occur on the first calendar day of the month following initial disbursement, using the base rate in effect on the first business day of the month. Lenders may delay the initial adjustment period... The Lender must specify in the Note the frequency at which the interest rate adjustment will occur. (a) This adjustment period as identified in the Note may not be changed without the written consent of the Borrower. (b) Subsequent adjustments may occur no more frequently than monthly.

examples: Loan disbursed July 15; first adjustment may be August 1 using base rate from August’s first business day; if lender wants the rate fixed for 3 years first, Note must specify delayed initial adjustment period.
Lender adjusts rate weekly after disbursement; violates 'no more frequently than monthly' requirement and requires remediation.
Lender wants to change quarterly adjustment period to monthly; must obtain borrower written consent before change.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA servicing finding; required Note correction/consent; potential guaranty repair/denial

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Note must state fixed rate explicitly and must include six specified disclosures for variable rate loans
business_rules.description: SBA Note documentation requirements: (1) For fixed rate loans, the lender must state the specific interest rate in the Note. (2) For variable rate loans, the Note must include: (a) identification of the base rate; (b) the publication where the base rate appears regularly; (c) the percentage spread (including any changes to spread if applicable); (d) the initial interest rate from disbursement to first adjustment; (e) the date or timing of the first rate adjustment; and (f) the frequency of rate adjustment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when drafting and executing the Note for either fixed or variable interest rate structures.
business_rules.consequences: If required interest rate terms are missing from the Note, documentation is deficient; lender must cure by amendment/correction consistent with SBA requirements. Documentation defects may result in SBA review findings and potential guaranty repair/denial if not corrected.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Interest Rate Requirements for an SBA Note
source_reference.source_text: For fixed rate loans, the Lender must state the specific interest rate in the Note. For variable rate loans, the Lender must include the following information in the Note: Identification of the rate being used as the base rate; The publication in which the designated base rate appears regularly... The percentage spread... The initial interest rate of the loan... The date or timing of the first rate adjustment; and The frequency of rate adjustment.

examples: Variable rate Note that states 'Prime + 3.00%' but omits the timing of first adjustment; violates documentation requirement and must be amended.
Fixed rate Note that states only 'fixed rate per SBA maximum' without a specific numeric percentage; violates requirement to state the specific rate.
Variable Note that identifies Optional Peg Rate but fails to state the publication (Federal Register); violates documentation requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA documentation exception; required cure; potential guaranty repair/denial if uncorrected

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) loans must not include balloon payments and fixed-rate payments must fully amortize by maturity
business_rules.description: SBA 7(a) amortization requirements: SBA does not allow balloon payments. A fixed interest rate loan must use a payment that will fully amortize the loan by the maturity date. Variable rate loans are typically re-amortized every time the interest rate is adjusted to ensure full amortization by the maturity date. Amortization schedules may be adjusted to meet the cash flow needs of the business, but must still comply with the no-balloon and full amortization requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when setting repayment terms at origination and when modifying repayment schedules.
business_rules.consequences: If a balloon payment structure is present or fixed-rate payment schedule does not fully amortize by maturity, the loan terms are not SBA-compliant and must be restructured; SBA review may cite deficiency with potential guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Amortization (13 CFR § 120.214(e)) (Page 293)
source_reference.source_text: Amortization: (13 CFR § 120.214(e) ): Lender should use an amortization schedule that is appropriate for the type of loan. SBA does not allow balloon payments. A fixed interest rate loan must use a payment that will fully amortize the loan by the maturity date. Typically, variable rate loans are re-amortized every time the interest rate is adjusted to ensure full amortization by the maturity date.

examples: A 10-year SBA 7(a) fixed-rate loan with payments based on a 20-year amortization and a balloon due at year 10 violates the no-balloon rule.
A variable-rate SBA loan re-amortizes after each annual rate reset so the remaining term still fully amortizes by maturity; complies with typical SBA expectation.
A lender proposes interest-only payments for the entire term with principal due at maturity; violates the no-balloon requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; required term restructuring; adverse SBA review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 Third Party Lender must be in place at application and evidenced by LOI, term sheet, or commitment letter in the application package
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, the Third Party Lender must be in place at the time of application and must be evidenced by a letter of intent/term sheet or commitment letter included in the application package, outlining the terms and conditions of the Interim and/or Third Party Loan so SBA can evaluate the 504 application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 project applications where a Third Party Loan is part of the capital stack.
business_rules.consequences: If Third Party Lender evidence is missing from the application package, the application is incomplete/insufficient for SBA evaluation and may be delayed or declined until documentation is provided.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Third Party Loan 13 CFR § 120.920 — Evidence at Application
source_reference.source_text: The Third Party Lender must be in place at the time of application and must be evidenced by a letter of intent/term sheet or commitment letter included in the application package outlining the terms and conditions of the Interim and/or Third Party Loan to enable SBA to evaluate the 504 application.

examples: CDC submits a 504 package without a bank commitment letter for the first mortgage; SBA evaluation cannot proceed until LOI/term sheet/commitment is included.
Application includes a signed term sheet describing loan amount, rate type, maturity, and collateral; satisfies evidence requirement.
Application includes only an email 'we intend to lend' with no terms/conditions; does not meet LOI/term sheet/commitment letter standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Application processing delay/deficiency; SBA/CDC submission exception

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 Third Party Loan must be at least net debenture proceeds and must be at least 50% of project costs for new businesses or limited or single purpose projects
business_rules.description: SBA 504 Third Party Loan sizing requirements: the Third Party Loan must be at least as much as the net debenture proceeds. Additionally, the Third Party Loan must total at least 50% of project costs if (a) the Borrower (or Operating Company if the Borrower is an Eligible Passive Company) has operated for 2 years or less, or (b) the project is for the acquisition, construction, conversion, or expansion of a limited or single purpose asset.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 financings involving a Third Party Loan and a CDC/SBA debenture, during structuring and underwriting prior to application submission and closing.
business_rules.consequences: If Third Party Loan sizing fails these minimums, the project structure is not compliant with SBA 504 requirements and must be restructured (e.g., increase Third Party Loan or adjust debenture/equity) before SBA processing/approval.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Separate rules also apply to federal source limits (e.g., no more than 50% of eligible project costs from federal sources), but that is not part of this rule’s sizing minimums.

source_reference.section_id: Third Party Loan 13 CFR § 120.920 — Minimum sizing requirements
source_reference.source_text: The Third Party Loan must be at least as much as the net debenture proceeds. However, the Third Party Loan must total at least 50% of the Project costs if the Borrower (or Operating Company if the Borrower is an Eligible Passive Company) has operated for 2 years or less or the Project is for the acquisition, construction, conversion or expansion of a limited or single purpose asset.

examples: Project costs $1,000,000; net debenture proceeds $380,000; Third Party Loan $350,000 violates 'at least as much as net debenture proceeds' (must be ≥ $380,000).
Startup operated 18 months with project costs $900,000; Third Party Loan must be ≥ 50% ($450,000). A proposed $400,000 first mortgage is noncompliant.
Established business 5 years, non-limited purpose asset: Third Party Loan must still be ≥ net debenture proceeds, but the special 50% rule may not be triggered by age/asset type.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Application denial/deficiency; required restructuring; SBA 504 eligibility noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 Third Party Lender documents must not allow demand before maturity through cross-default or deem-at-risk provisions unless the loan is in default
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Third Party Loans, the Third Party Lender’s note and loan documents must not include cross-default, “deem-at-risk,” or other provisions that allow the Third Party Lender to make demand prior to maturity unless the loan is in default.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 Third Party Loan documentation drafting/review before closing and during any modification of Third Party Loan documents.
business_rules.consequences: If prohibited demand-trigger provisions exist, documents must be revised to remove/limit them; otherwise the Third Party Loan terms are not SBA 504-compliant and may jeopardize SBA/CDC participation.
business_rules.exceptions: Demand prior to maturity is permitted if the Third Party Loan is in default.

source_reference.section_id: Third Party Loan — Prohibited demand provisions
source_reference.source_text: The Third Party Lender’s note and loan documents must not have any cross-default, “deem-at-risk,” or any other provisions which allow the Third Party Lender to make demand prior to maturity unless the loan is in default.

examples: Third Party Loan note states lender may accelerate if borrower defaults on an unrelated equipment lease (cross-default) even when this loan is current; violates rule and must be amended.
Third Party Loan includes a 'deem at risk' clause allowing acceleration based on lender discretion without payment default; violates rule.
Third Party Loan allows acceleration upon payment default on the Third Party Loan itself; permitted under the exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC closing condition failure; required document remediation; potential ineligibility finding

business_rules.rule_name: No collateral required for 7(a) Small loans of 50,000 dollars or less
business_rules.description: For 7(a) Small loans with a loan amount of $50,000 or less, the lender is not required to take collateral.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is a 7(a) Small loan and the total loan amount is less than or equal to $50,000.
business_rules.consequences: Loan may be originated without taking collateral; lender still may take collateral if desired under its own credit practices (not required by SBA for this threshold).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.ii
source_reference.source_text: For loans of $50,000 or less, Lenders are not required to take collateral.

examples: A 7(a) Small loan for $35,000 working capital may be approved without any lien on business assets.
A 7(a) Small loan for $50,000 to purchase equipment does not require collateral under SBA minimum requirements (lender may still choose to take a lien).
A 7(a) Small loan for $50,001 is outside this rule and must follow the >$50,000 collateral identification and minimum requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program eligibility finding or guaranty repair/denial risk if lender misapplies collateral requirements on loans above the threshold

business_rules.rule_name: Debt refinancing with 7(a) Small proceeds must keep at least the same collateral and lien priority as refinanced debt
business_rules.description: When 7(a) Small loan proceeds will be used to refinance existing debt, the new 7(a) loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt. If the refinanced debt is over-collateralized under SBA collateral requirements and the 7(a) loan will remain fully secured, the lender may release excess collateral. Substitute collateral may be offered if it has comparable value and useful life and is acceptable to SBA or a PLP lender under PLP authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan purpose includes refinancing existing debt using 7(a) Small loan proceeds.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must structure collateral package and lien position to be at least as strong as the refinanced obligation; any release of collateral requires the loan to remain fully secured under SBA requirements; any substitute collateral must be comparable and acceptable per SBA/PLP.
business_rules.exceptions: Release of excess collateral is permitted only when (i) prior debt is over-collateralized under SBA requirements and (ii) the 7(a) loan remains fully secured. Substitute collateral permitted only if comparable value/useful life and acceptable to SBA or PLP lender processing under PLP authority.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.iii
source_reference.source_text: When loan proceeds from a 7(a) Small Loan will be used to refinance existing debt, the loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt that is being refinanced... the Lender may approve the release of excess collateral. Substitute collateral may be offered providing it is of comparable value and useful life and is determined to be acceptable by SBA or a PLP Lender

examples: Refinancing a prior bank term loan secured by a first lien on equipment: the 7(a) Small refinance must also have at least a first lien on that equipment (or equivalent lien priority/collateral).

Prior debt was secured by three properties but SBA fully secured test is met with two properties after refinance; lender may release the third property as excess collateral if the 7(a) loan remains fully secured.
Borrower offers substitute equipment collateral with comparable value and useful life; PLP lender may accept substitution if acceptable under PLP authority and documented.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk due to noncompliant lien priority/collateral in refinance transactions

business_rules.rule_name: Loans over 50,000 dollars must take first lien on assets financed, with vehicle lien required only if vehicle value exceeds 20,000 dollars at SBA loan number assignment
business_rules.description: For loans over $50,000, lenders must use commercially reasonable and prudent practices to identify collateral at least as thorough as for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. At minimum, the lender must take a first lien on assets financed with loan proceeds. SBA does not require a lien on vehicles that already have a lien or unless the vehicle value is greater than $20,000 at the time the SBA loan number is assigned. The lender must document the source and dollar amount of the vehicle valuation in the credit memorandum.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (i) loan amount > $50,000 and (ii) SBA 7(a) Small loan collateral minimum requirements apply; also applies specifically when loan proceeds finance vehicles and lien decision is being made.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must perfect a first lien on financed assets except as allowed; if a financed vehicle value is > $20,000 at SBA loan number assignment, lender must place a lien (subject to stated vehicle exception language); and must document vehicle valuation source and amount in the credit memorandum.
business_rules.exceptions: Vehicle lien is not required if the vehicle already has a lien OR if vehicle value is not greater than $20,000 at SBA loan number assignment, per the cited text. (Lender may still choose to take a lien under its policies.)

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.iv(a)(i)
source_reference.source_text: For loans over $50,000... at a minimum: Lender must take a first lien on assets financed with loan proceeds, with the following exceptions: SBA does not require the Lender to place a lien on vehicles that already have a lien or unless the value of the vehicle... is greater than $20,000 at the time the SBA loan number is assigned... Lender must document the source and dollar amount of the vehicle valuation in the credit memorandum

examples: A $150,000 7(a) Small equipment purchase: lender must take a first lien on the equipment financed.

A $120,000 7(a) Small loan finances a vehicle valued at $18,000 at SBA loan number assignment: SBA does not require a vehicle lien under this vehicle threshold; lender documents valuation source and amount.
A $120,000 7(a) Small loan finances a truck valued at $35,000 at SBA loan number assignment: lender must place a lien and document valuation source and amount in the credit memo.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk due to failure to meet SBA minimum collateral/lien documentation requirements

business_rules.rule_name: Subordinate lien is acceptable for improvement financing only when existing debt is ineligible for 7(a) refinance or is on reasonable terms and must be documented in the credit memorandum
business_rules.description: When 7(a) loan proceeds will be used to improve assets, SBA permits a subordinate lien position for the 7(a) loan if either (i) the existing debt is ineligible to be refinanced with a 7(a) loan, or (ii) there is existing debt on reasonable terms (example given: existing building purchase loan plus new 7(a) improvement loan). In these cases, the lender must document this fact in the credit memorandum.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan proceeds are used to improve assets and an existing lien/debt will remain in place on the improved asset(s).
business_rules.consequences: Lender may take a subordinate position consistent with SBA requirements ONLY if the stated eligibility/reasonable-terms condition is satisfied AND is documented in the credit memo.
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond the two stated conditions permitting subordination; otherwise, standard first-lien expectations on financed assets apply.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.iv(a)(ii)
source_reference.source_text: When loan proceeds will be used to improve assets, a subordinate position is acceptable for the 7(a) loan if the existing debt is ineligible to be refinanced with a 7(a) loan, or if there is existing debt on reasonable terms... in which case the Lender must document this fact in its credit memorandum.

examples: Borrower has an existing conventional mortgage used to purchase a building; new 7(a) loan funds renovations: SBA allows 7(a) loan to be subordinate if lender documents existing debt terms and rationale in credit memo.
Existing lien is on ineligible-to-refinance debt; lender takes subordinate lien for improvements and documents in the memo: compliant.
Lender takes subordinate lien for improvements but fails to document why existing debt cannot/should not be refinanced: noncompliant documentation under SOP.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk due to insufficient file documentation supporting allowed subordinate lien position

business_rules.rule_name: When 50 percent or more of proceeds are working capital the lender must lien all business fixed assets until fully secured and need not lien real estate with less than 25 percent equity
business_rules.description: If 50% or more of loan proceeds will be used for working capital, the lender must take a lien on all of the applicant business’s fixed assets (including real estate) up to the point the loan is fully secured. SBA defines “fully secured” as taking security interests in all available fixed assets of the applicant business with combined net book value (as adjusted by SBA haircuts) up to the loan amount. For 7(a) loans, fixed assets mean real estate (land and structures) and machinery/equipment owned by the business or an Eligible Passive Company (EPC). The lender is not required to take a lien against the applicant business’s real estate when the equity is less than 25% of fair market value, and may limit the lien taken against real estate to the amount necessary to ensure the loan is fully secured.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the working capital use of proceeds is 50% or more of total loan proceeds.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must perfect liens on all available fixed assets (subject to the real estate equity exception) until the adjusted collateral value reaches the loan amount; lender may cap the real estate lien amount to what is needed to meet fully secured status.
business_rules.exceptions: Real estate lien not required when equity is < 25% of fair market value; lender may limit real estate lien to amount needed for fully secured status.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.iv(b)
source_reference.source_text: When 50% or more of loan proceeds will be used for working capital, Lender must take a lien on all of the Applicant business’s fixed assets, including real estate, up to the point that the loan is fully secured... Lender is not required to take a lien against the Applicant business’s real estate when the equity is less than 25% of the fair market value.

examples: Loan proceeds are 70% working capital and 30% equipment: lender must lien all fixed assets (equipment and any available real estate) until fully secured, except real estate with <25% equity need not be liened.
Borrower’s business owns a building with only 10% equity: lender is not required to take a lien on that real estate to satisfy the fixed-asset lien rule.
Proceeds are 40% working capital and 60% equipment purchase: this specific 50% working capital trigger does not apply; lender follows other minimum collateral rules.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk due to failure to lien required fixed assets for working capital-heavy transactions

business_rules.rule_name: Fully secured collateral value haircuts for 7(a) fixed assets and trading assets including 85 percent improved real estate and 50 percent unimproved real estate
business_rules.description: For purposes of determining whether a 7(a) loan is “fully secured,” SBA limits the collateral values that can be counted. New machinery and equipment (excluding furniture/fixtures) may be valued at no more than 75% of price minus prior liens. Used/existing machinery and equipment (excluding furniture/fixtures) may be valued at no more than 50% of net book value OR 80% with an orderly liquidation appraisal minus prior liens. Improved real estate may be valued at no more than 85% of market value; unimproved real estate at 50% of market value (value determined per SBA appraisal requirements). Furniture and fixtures may be valued at no more than 10% of net book value or appraised value. If lender takes a security interest in trading assets (A/R, inventory), no more than 10% of current book value may be used in the fully secured calculation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when lender is calculating SBA “fully secured” status for 7(a) collateral sufficiency, including working-capital-triggered collateral requirements and collateral release decisions.
business_rules.consequences: Collateral valuation used in SBA fully secured calculation must apply the stated percentage caps; overstatement can result in incorrect fully secured determination and potential SBA guaranty enforcement issues.
business_rules.exceptions: For used/existing equipment: lender may use up to 80% only with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal; otherwise 50% of net book value applies.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.a.iv(b)(i)-(v)
source_reference.source_text: New machinery and equipment... may be valued at no more than 75% of price... Used or existing machinery and equipment... no more than 50% of Net Book Value or 80% with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal... Improved real estate can be valued at no more than 85% and unimproved real estate... 50% of the market value... Furniture and Fixtures... no more than 10%... trading assets... no more than 10% of current book value

examples: New equipment purchased for $100,000 with a $10,000 prior lien: maximum counted value is 75% × $100,000 − $10,000 = $65,000.
Used equipment with net book value $80,000 and no OLV appraisal: maximum counted value is 50% × $80,000 = $40,000; with an OLV appraisal the cap could be 80% of appraised OLV (minus prior liens).
Improved real estate market value $1,000,000: maximum counted value is 85% × $1,000,000 = $850,000; unimproved land market value $1,000,000: maximum counted value is $500,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk due to improper collateral valuation in SBA fully secured determination

business_rules.rule_name: 7(a) loan request must not be declined solely for inadequate collateral and SBA guaranty cannot substitute for available collateral
business_rules.description: SBA policy states a 7(a) loan request is not to be declined solely due to inadequate collateral because SBA-guaranteed loans often serve applicants that show repayment ability but lack full collateral coverage. However, SBA does not permit its guaranty to be used as a substitute for available collateral; lenders must still take available collateral consistent with SBA requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA-guaranteed loan requests where collateral coverage is less than the loan amount but repayment ability is otherwise demonstrated.
business_rules.consequences: Credit decisioning must not automatically reject requests solely because collateral is inadequate; lender must still obtain available collateral and follow SBA collateral minimum requirements and prudent practices.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; SBA still requires available collateral not be ignored.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.v(a)
source_reference.source_text: A loan request is not to be declined solely on the basis of inadequate collateral... one of the primary reasons Lenders use the SBA-guaranteed program is for those Applicants that demonstrate repayment ability but lack adequate collateral... However, SBA does not permit its guaranty to be a substitute for available collateral.

examples: Borrower qualifies on cash flow but collateral shortfall exists: lender may proceed under SBA rather than decline solely for collateral, while still taking all available collateral per SBA rules.

Lender attempts to book a working-capital-heavy SBA loan without taking liens on available fixed assets because “SBA guaranty covers it”: prohibited; guaranty is not a substitute for available collateral.

Credit memo states decline reason only “insufficient collateral” for SBA-eligible applicant with demonstrated repayment ability: inconsistent with SBA policy (other credit weaknesses may still justify decline).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; examiner criticism; potential guaranty purchase dispute if collateral requirements ignored

business_rules.rule_name: Piggyback financing prohibited for SBA guaranteed loans when SBA loan is junior or unsecured relative to non SBA loan approved within 90 days
business_rules.description: A lender may not take actions that establish a preference in favor of the lender (13 CFR § 120.411) and must not structure a 7(a) loan as a piggyback. Piggyback financing exists when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single borrower at or about the same time for the same or similar purpose, and the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured with a junior lien position or no lien on collateral securing the non-SBA loan(s). SBA defines 'at or about the same time' as loans approved within 90 days of each other. SBA does not consider as piggyback (i) both loans for working capital where the non-SBA loan is secured only by working/trading assets, or (ii) pari passu shared lien if the non-SBA loan maturity is not shorter than the SBA loan maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when borrower has multiple loans (SBA-guaranteed and non-SBA) approved within 90 days for the same or similar purpose and secured by related collateral.
business_rules.consequences: If structure meets piggyback definition, lender must restructure (e.g., lien positions/collateral) to avoid prohibited preference; otherwise, SBA program compliance risk including guaranty enforcement issues.
business_rules.exceptions: Not piggyback when (a) both loans are working capital and non-SBA loan is secured only by working/trading assets; and (b) pari passu shared lien is permitted when non-SBA maturity is not shorter than SBA-guaranteed maturity.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2, 3.v(b)-(c)
source_reference.source_text: establishes a preference in favor of the Lender ( 13 CFR § 120.411 ). The Lender must not have a 7(a) loan in a “piggyback” structure. i) Piggyback financing occurs when one o r more lenders provide more than one loan to a single Borrower at or about the

examples: Bank makes a conventional $300,000 equipment loan secured by equipment and, within 30 days, an SBA loan for the same equipment purpose but takes only a junior lien: this is piggyback and prohibited.
SBA working capital term loan plus a separate non-SBA working capital line secured only by A/R and inventory: SBA states this scenario is not piggyback.
SBA loan and non-SBA loan share a pari passu lien on real estate; non-SBA maturity is equal to or longer than SBA maturity: not piggyback under the cited rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk and regulatory criticism for prohibited preference/piggyback structure

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum variable interest rate spreads for 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans are tiered from 6.5 percent to 3.0 percent over Prime or Optional Peg Rate
business_rules.description: For 7(a) Small and SBA Express variable rate loans, the maximum allowable note rate is the base rate (Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate) plus an allowable spread based on loan size: (1) $50,000 or less: +6.5%; (2) $50,001 up to and including $250,000: +6.0%; (3) $250,001 up to and including $350,000: +4.5%; and (4) SBA Express loans $350,001 up to and including $500,000: +3.0%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans with variable interest rates when pricing the initial note rate and ensuring spread compliance for the life of the loan.
business_rules.consequences: Initial note rate must not exceed the maximum; pricing must be adjusted downward if proposed spread exceeds tier cap; continued compliance required because the maximum spread above base cannot exceed SBA’s stated maximum.
business_rules.exceptions: Fixed-rate maximums are published separately; this rule addresses variable-rate caps. SBA Express may use other base rates for servicing (per separate text) but the interest rate throughout the term may not exceed the maximum allowable 7(a) interest rate.

source_reference.section_id: SBA QUICK REFERENCE CHART: Maximum Interest Rates Allowed; Base Rate, Allowable Spread (13 CFR § 120.214)
source_reference.source_text: Maximum variable rates are: Loans $50,000 or less: Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 6.5%... Loans $50,001 up to and including $250,000: ... + 6.0%... Loans $250,001 up to and including $350,000: ... + 4.5%... SBA Express Loans $350,001 up to and including $500,000: ... + 3.0%.

examples: A $200,000 7(a) Small variable-rate loan priced at Prime + 6.25%: not permitted; must be reduced to Prime + 6.0% or less.
A $300,000 7(a) Small variable-rate loan priced at Optional Peg Rate + 4.50%: permitted (at the cap).
An SBA Express $450,000 variable-rate loan priced at Prime + 3.25%: not permitted; must be Prime + 3.0% or less.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty repair/denial risk and required corrective action for charging impermissible interest rates under SBA rules

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express non financial default provisions require borrower written agreement at closing and cannot be sole basis for guaranty purchase request
business_rules.description: Non-financial default provisions are permitted for SBA Express only if they are substantive, agreed to by the borrower in writing at loan closing, and consistent with provisions used on the lender’s similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. A lender may not request purchase of the guaranty solely due to violation of a non-financial default provision (13 CFR § 120.520). Additionally, the note must establish a maturity date even if the instrument has demand features (e.g., a line of credit payable on demand under conditions but no later than a certain date).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Express loans where lender includes non-financial default terms in the loan agreement/note (events of default not based on payment failure).
business_rules.consequences: If provisions are not substantive, not consistent with lender’s non-SBA practice, or lack borrower written agreement at closing, provisions are not permitted under SBA Express terms; lender also cannot base SBA guaranty purchase request solely on such a violation; note must include a maturity date to comply with SBA Express requirement.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.section_id: SBA Express Non-Financial Default Provisions
source_reference.source_text: Non-financial default provisions must be substantive and must be agreed to by the Borrower in writing at loan closing; The provisions must be consistent with those used by the Lender on its similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans; A lender may not request purchase of the guaranty solely based on a violation of a non-financial default provision (see 13 CFR § 120.520); and A maturity date must be established in the note.

examples: SBA Express line of credit includes covenant that borrower must maintain insurance; borrower signs written agreement at closing and lender uses same provision on comparable non-SBA lines: permitted; but lender cannot seek SBA purchase solely because insurance lapsed if payments are current.
SBA Express loan agreement includes non-financial default clause not used in lender’s non-SBA portfolio and borrower did not separately agree at closing: not permitted under SBA Express rule.
Demand line states payable on demand but also includes 'in no case later than 06/30/2030' maturity date: satisfies the maturity date requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Guaranty purchase denial risk if lender requests purchase solely on non-financial default violation; SBA servicing/enforcement dispute risk for noncompliant default terms

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum maturity 10 years for Working Capital, Contract, and Seasonal SBA CAPLines with 120-month total revolving period cap
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) CAPLines identified as Working Capital, Contract, or Seasonal CAPLines, the maximum maturity is 10 years. If initially booked with maturity less than 10 years, the CAPLine may be renewed as an extension of maturity (not a new loan) provided the total revolving repayment period does not exceed 120 months (10 years). The loan number remains the same for renewals treated as extensions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan product is SBA 7(a) CAPLines AND CAPLine type is Working Capital OR Contract OR Seasonal; applies at origination and each renewal/extension request.
business_rules.consequences: If proposed maturity exceeds 10 years OR total revolving repayment period would exceed 120 months, the CAPLine structure is non-compliant with SBA requirements and must be restructured (e.g., reduce term) or is ineligible for SBA guaranty support under the described terms.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text. Builders CAPLines have different maturity requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities > Working Capital, Contract, and Seasonal CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: The maximum maturity on a Working Capital, Contract, or Seasonal CAPLine is 10 years. Any CAPLine with a maturity of less than 10 years can be renewed as long as the total revolving repayment period does not exceed 120 months. The renewal is an extension of maturity (not a new loan). Thus, the loan number remains the same.

examples: A Seasonal CAPLine is booked at 24 months and renewed 3 times; total revolving period would be 120 months. This is permitted if total does not exceed 120 months and each renewal is an extension.
A Contract CAPLine is requested at 11-year maturity. This violates the 10-year maximum and must be reduced to 10 years or less.
A Working Capital CAPLine is initially 60 months; borrower requests renewal that would bring total revolving repayment period to 132 months. Renewal is not permitted under this cap.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential denial of SBA liability on guaranty or adverse lender review findings

business_rules.rule_name: Additional SBA guaranty fee required when extending CAPLines originally 12 months or less to a term exceeding 12 months
business_rules.description: If a CAPLine’s original maturity was 12 months or less and the new maturity (after renewal/extension) exceeds 12 months, an additional SBA guaranty fee is due. The renewal is treated as an extension of maturity (not a new loan), but the fee consequence still applies when crossing the 12-month threshold.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines renewals/extensions where original maturity (at approval) was <= 12 months AND requested/approved new maturity is > 12 months.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must assess and remit the additional guaranty fee in accordance with referenced SBA guidance for extensions of short-term loans; failure may result in SBA fee delinquency and potential guaranty enforceability/servicing issues.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text. Fee details are referenced to another section not included in this batch.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities > Working Capital, Contract, and Seasonal CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: If the original maturity was for 12 months or less, and the new maturity exceeds 12 months, an additional guaranty fee will be due. See Section A, Ch. 5, Para. C.1.a.v., Additional Guaranty Fee for Extensions of Short-Term Loans.

examples: A CAPLine originally approved for 12 months is extended to 18 months at renewal; an additional guaranty fee is due.
A CAPLine originally approved for 9 months is extended to 12 months; additional guaranty fee trigger is not met because the new maturity does not exceed 12 months.
A CAPLine originally approved for 18 months is extended to 24 months; this rule does not apply because the original maturity was not 12 months or less.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA fee delinquency; potential program eligibility impact or servicing findings

business_rules.rule_name: Builders CAPLine maximum maturity is 60 months plus estimated time to complete construction or rehabilitation
business_rules.description: For SBA Builders CAPLines, maturity must not exceed 60 months plus the estimated time to complete construction or rehabilitation (per 13 CFR § 120.396).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CAPLine type is Builders CAPLine; applies at origination and any modification that extends maturity.
business_rules.consequences: If maturity exceeds 60 months plus estimated construction/rehab completion time, the loan term is non-compliant and must be reduced or restructured to meet SBA rules.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities > Builders CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: Fee for Extensions of Short-Term Loans. Builders CAPLines 13 CFR § 120.396 The loan must not exceed 60 months plus the estimated time to complete construction or rehabilitation.

examples: Estimated construction completion is 9 months; maximum permitted maturity is 60 + 9 = 69 months.
Estimated rehab completion is 3 months; a proposed 72-month maturity exceeds 63 months and is not permitted.
A Builders CAPLine initially set at 60 months with 6-month completion estimate is compliant (<= 66 months).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential denial of SBA liability on guaranty

business_rules.rule_name: CAPLines must include an exit strategy and maturity must not exceed the SBA guaranty period
business_rules.description: All SBA CAPLines must have an exit strategy, and loan maturity must not exceed the period of the SBA guaranty. Additionally, final disbursement should occur far enough in advance of maturity to allow assets acquired with proceeds to be converted back to cash and repaid by maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA CAPLines; evaluated during structuring/approval and monitored during servicing for draw timing relative to maturity.
business_rules.consequences: If no exit strategy is documented or if maturity exceeds guaranty period, the CAPLine does not meet SBA requirements.
