If final disbursement occurs too close to maturity to reasonably allow liquidation/repayment, the structure is inconsistent with SBA guidance and may be subject to SBA criticism or eligibility risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities > All CAPLines
source_reference.source_text:
All CAPLines must have an exit strategy. Final disbursement should occur far enough in advance of maturity so that a sufficient amount of time is available for the assets acquired with proceeds to be converted back to cash and final payment.

Loan maturity must not exceed the period of the guaranty.

examples: A Seasonal CAPLine includes a documented takeout plan from seasonal receivables conversion and cash collection; compliant with exit strategy requirement.
A CAPLine is structured with a 10-year maturity but the SBA guaranty period is approved for 7 years; maturity must be reduced to 7 years or less.

Final disbursement is scheduled 5 days before maturity on a CAPLine where inventory conversion typically takes 45 days; structure conflicts with the guidance to allow conversion back to cash before maturity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; adverse servicing/oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Eligible Passive Company use of proceeds limited to leased property and EPC owner buyout only after 36 months holding period
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) and 504 loans using an Eligible Passive Company (EPC)/Operating Company (OC) structure, the EPC must use loan proceeds only to (i) acquire or lease and/or improve or renovate real or personal property (including eligible refinancing) that is leased to one or more OCs for conducting the OC’s business, or (ii) finance a change of ownership between existing owners of the EPC. Change-of-ownership proceeds between existing EPC owners are only permitted when the real estate or personal property has been held by the selling owner(s) for at least 36 months. Except for that permitted EPC owner change-of-ownership, the EPC may not use proceeds to acquire a business, acquire stock, acquire intangible assets of a business, or refinance debt incurred for those purposes. Noncompliance can result in SBA denial of liability on the guaranty after default (7(a)) and potential CDC recovery claim in certain cases (504).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the borrower structure is EPC/OC under SBA 7(a) or SBA 504 and loan proceeds are directed to the EPC.
business_rules.consequences: If any EPC condition is not met and the loan defaults: (i) for 7(a) SBA may deny liability on the guaranty; (ii) for 504 SBA may pursue a CDC Recovery Claim in cases of fraud, negligence, or misrepresentation.
business_rules.exceptions: EPC may finance a change of ownership between existing EPC owners only if the property was held by selling owner(s) for at least 36 months. Change of ownership is not an eligible use for 7(a) CAPLines, EWCP, or revolving facilities (see delivery method chapters referenced in SOP).

source_reference.section_id: Eligible Passive Companies
source_reference.source_text: An Eligible Passive Company (EPC) must use loan proceeds only to acquire or lease, and/or improve or renovate, real or personal property (including eligible refinancing), that it leases to one or more Operating Companies (OCs) for conducting the OC's business, or to finance a change of ownership between the existing owners of the EPC. An EPC may only use loan proceeds to finance a change of ownership between existing owners of the EPC when the real estate or personal property has been held by the selling owner(s) for at least 36 months. With the exception of a change of ownership between existing owners of the EPC, an EPC may not use loan proceeds to acquire a business, acquire stock in a business or any intangible assets of a business, or to refinance debt that was incurred for those purposes.

examples: Permitted: EPC borrows SBA 7(a) funds to renovate a building and leases it to its OC that operates a manufacturing business; no business acquisition occurs.
Permitted: EPC uses proceeds to buy out an existing EPC owner’s interest, but only if the selling owner held the property for 36+ months.
Not permitted: EPC uses proceeds to buy the OC’s customer list (intangible asset) or to refinance a note originally used to purchase a business.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of liability on 7(a) guaranty; for 504, potential CDC Recovery Claim in cases of fraud, negligence, or misrepresentation

business_rules.rule_name: EPC lease must be executed, subordinated, match loan term, and rents assigned with rent capped to debt service plus direct expenses
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a)/504 EPC/OC structures, the EPC must lease the project property directly to the OC(s) and obtain a fully executed written lease. The lease must be subordinated to SBA’s mortgage/trust deed lien/security interest. The lease term (including renewal options exercisable solely by the OC(s)) must be at least equal to the term of the loan. The EPC must provide an assignment of all rents as collateral; an assignment of the lease is required only when needed to perfect the assignment of rents under applicable law or to enable the SBA Lender to exercise tenant rights upon default. Rent/lease payments may not exceed the amount needed to make the loan payment plus an additional amount for the EPC’s direct property-holding expenses (routine maintenance, utilities, insurance, property taxes). For 504, the definition of “loan payment to the Lender” includes payments to SBA, the Third Party Lender, and (if applicable) a lender financing the borrower contribution, and rent may not include accelerated payments on the Third Party Loan. Repayment ability analysis must consider whether OC cash flow covers the loan payment(s) plus ongoing property-holding expenses.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any SBA 7(a) or 504 loan closed with EPC/OC structure where EPC owns the collateral and leases to OC(s).
business_rules.consequences: Missing/defective lease documentation or rent structure that exceeds permitted amounts can be treated as noncompliance with EPC conditions, risking SBA guaranty purchase denial (7(a)) or CDC recovery exposure (504) after default; also may require corrective documentation before closing/funding.
business_rules.exceptions: Assignment of the lease is only required when necessary to perfect assignment of rents under applicable law or to enable lender/SBA to exercise tenant rights upon default.

source_reference.section_id: Conditions that apply to all EPCs
source_reference.source_text: The EPC must lease the project property directly to the OC(s); and Obtain a fully executed written lease; The lease must be subordinated to the SBA’s mortgage, trust deed lien, or security interest on the property... The lease must have a term, including options to renew exercisable solely by the OC(s), at least equal to the term of the loan; The EPC (as landlord) must furnish as collateral for the loan an assignment of all rents paid under the lease... The rent or lease payments cannot exceed the amount necessary to make the loan payment to the Lender and an additional amount to cover the EPC’s direct expenses of holding the property, such as routine maintenance, utility expenses, insurance, and property taxes.

examples: Compliant: Lease is signed, subordinated to SBA lien, has 25-year term matching a 25-year loan, and includes assignment of rents; rent equals monthly loan payment plus taxes/insurance.
Noncompliant: Lease renewals can be exercised by EPC (not solely by OC), creating a lease term shorter than the loan term.
Noncompliant (504): Rent includes extra amounts intended to accelerate the Third Party Loan principal beyond scheduled debt service.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of liability on 7(a) guaranty; for 504, potential CDC Recovery Claim; closing conditions imposed or funding delayed

business_rules.rule_name: Only one Eligible Passive Company is permitted per SBA transaction and tenancy in common requires all members to sign loan documents
business_rules.description: Multiple Operating Companies (OCs) that are separately owned can participate in an EPC/OC structure; however, multiple EPCs in one transaction are not permitted. A tenancy in common (TIC) is treated as one EPC (not a separate legal entity), and loan documents must be signed by all TIC members. Where an EPC owns several properties leased to multiple OCs, the same OCs (with the same ownership structure) must jointly occupy each property; it is not permissible for one OC to occupy one property and a different OC to occupy a different property within the same EPC/OC transaction structure.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 transactions structured with an EPC leasing to one or more OCs.
business_rules.consequences: If multiple EPCs are included or TIC members do not execute documents, the structure is not permitted/defective and may require restructuring prior to approval/closing; post-default may increase risk of SBA guaranty denial due to noncompliance with EPC conditions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for multiple EPCs prohibition; TIC is allowed but treated as a single EPC with required signatures.

source_reference.section_id: Tenancy in common; Multiple OCs/EPCs
source_reference.source_text: A tenancy in common is a form of legal ownership and does not create a new or separate legal entity. There may be several individuals or entities in a tenancy in common, but the tenancy in common is considered one EPC. The loan documents must be signed by all of the members of the tenancy in common. Multiple OCs that are separately owned can participate in an EPC/OC structure, however, multiple EPCs in one transaction are not permitted... in the case of multiple OCs, the same OCs (with the same ownership structure) must jointly occupy each property.

examples: Compliant: Two OCs co-borrow/guaranty and both jointly occupy each EPC-owned property; the EPC is a TIC and all TIC owners sign the loan documents.
Noncompliant: A project is structured with two separate LLC EPCs each owning a different building under the same SBA loan transaction.
Noncompliant: TIC has 4 members but only the managing member signs the note and security instruments.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligible as structured; SBA guaranty denial risk; required restructuring and re-documentation

business_rules.rule_name: Owner-occupancy and leasing limits require 51% existing building or 60% new construction with 3-year and 10-year utilization deadlines
business_rules.description: When SBA loan proceeds are used to purchase or improve real estate, or to refinance debt secured by real estate, occupancy requirements apply. For an existing building, the Applicant must occupy at least 51% of Rentable Property and may lease up to 49% to a third party. For new construction, the Applicant must occupy at least 60% of Rentable Property; may permanently lease up to 20% to a third party; and may temporarily lease an additional 20% with the intention of using some of that additional 20% within 3 years and all of it within 10 years. When the real estate is owned by an EPC, the EPC must lease 100% of Rentable Property to eligible OC(s); the OC(s) then must meet the same 51% (existing) or 60% (new construction) occupancy thresholds and subleasing limitations (49% max for existing; 20% permanent + 20% temporary for new construction with 3-year/10-year utilization deadlines). Occupancy compliance may be delayed after closing only when justified, but in no case may the small business have more than 1 year to meet occupancy requirements. The Borrower may not use loan proceeds to improve or renovate any of the Rentable Property to be subleased to a third party; for 504 loans, such improvements may not secure the Third Party Loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans involving purchase/improvement/refinance of business real estate, including EPC-owned real estate leased to OC(s).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to meet occupancy rules can render the use of proceeds ineligible and may result in denial of SBA support/closing restrictions; for 504, CDC must not submit closing package if occupancy will not be met upon funding unless advance SLPC approval is obtained.
business_rules.exceptions: Limited post-closing time allowed to meet occupancy if justified (e.g., pre-existing lease), but capped at 1 year. For 504, SLPC may approve an extension if requested in advance and in writing with explanation/supporting documentation.

source_reference.section_id: Occupancy and Leasing Requirements (13 CFR § 120.131)
source_reference.source_text: For an existing building, the Applicant must occupy 51% of the Rentable Property and may lease to a third party up to 49%; or For new construction, the Applicant must occupy 60% of the Rentable Property, may permanently lease to a third party up to 20% and temporarily lease an additional 20% with the intention of using some of the additional 20% within 3 years and all of it within 10 years. When the real estate is owned by an EPC: The EPC must lease 100% of the Rentable Property to an eligible OC(s)... In no case may the small business have more than 1 year to meet occupancy requirements. The Borrower may not use loan proceeds to improve or renovate any of the Rentable Property to be subleased to a third party.

examples: Existing building: Borrower occupies 55% and leases 45% to unrelated tenant—compliant.
New construction: Borrower occupies 60%, permanently leases 15%, temporarily leases 25%—noncompliant because temporary leasing cannot exceed 20%.
EPC/OC: EPC leases 100% to OC; OC occupies 60% at completion and temporarily subleases 20%, but must plan to use some of that 20% within 3 years and all within 10 years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Ineligibility for SBA financing; 504 closing package rejection; potential servicing/eligibility findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA prohibited uses include associate distributions, floor plan, excess land, delinquent trust taxes, relocation with one-third workforce reduction, and C-PACE on 7(a) or 504 collateral
business_rules.description: SBA loan proceeds may not be used for prohibited purposes including: (i) payments, distributions, or loans to an Associate of the Applicant, except compensation for services actually rendered at a fair and reasonable rate, or to facilitate eligible 7(a) change-of-ownership loans; (ii) floor plan financing; (iii) investments in real or personal property acquired and held primarily for future development, sale, lease, or investment (except Eligible Passive Company loans or Builders CAPLine), including purchase of excess land not for immediate project use; (iv) payment of delinquent taxes, including a prohibition on paying past-due Federal/state/local payroll taxes, sales taxes, or similar taxes required to be collected and held in trust; (v) for 504 only, relocation of the applicant out of a community if there will be a net reduction of one-third or more in the applicant’s workforce or a substantial increase in unemployment (unless an exception is justified by the CDC as provided in SOP); and (vi) at origination or as a servicing action, SBA lenders may not permit a C-PACE loan to be made on a property that has or will have a 7(a) or 504 loan. Delinquent business income taxes may be permitted only if the applicant has an approved IRS payment arrangement and is current on the arrangement payments.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) and 504 loans at origination and, for the C-PACE restriction, also in servicing.
business_rules.consequences: Prohibited use of proceeds results in SBA ineligibility and can lead to denial of guaranty purchase and/or regulatory/oversight findings; lender must restructure or decline the transaction, or reverse/deny the prohibited disbursement/servicing action.
business_rules.exceptions: Associate payments allowed only for fair compensation for services actually rendered or to facilitate eligible 7(a) change of ownership in accordance with SBA rules. Delinquent business income tax payment may be permitted with IRS-approved payment arrangement and borrower current. 504 relocation exception may be allowed if CDC justifies and benefits outweigh negative impact (per SOP). EPC and Builders CAPLine are exceptions to future-development property restriction.

source_reference.section_id: Restrictions on Uses of Proceeds (13 CFR § 120.130; 13 CFR § 120.881)
source_reference.source_text: Loan proceeds may not be used for any of the following purposes... Payments, distributions, or loans to an Associate of the Applicant... Floor plan financing; ...Investments in real or personal property acquired and held primarily for future development, sale, lease, or investment... The purchase of excess land... is not eligible... Loan proceeds must not be used to pay past-due Federal, state, or local payroll taxes, sales taxes, or similar taxes... Payment of delinquent business income taxes may be permitted if the Applicant has an approved payment arrangement with the IRS... For the 504 Loan Program only: To finance the relocation... if there will be a net reduction of one-third or more... Whether at loan origination or as a servicing action, SBA Lenders may not permit a C-PACE loan to be made on a property that has or will have a 7(a) or 504 loan.

examples: Not permitted: Borrower uses SBA proceeds to pay past-due payroll taxes withheld from employees (trust taxes).
Conditionally permitted: Borrower pays delinquent business income taxes only after documenting an IRS installment agreement and being current on payments.
Not permitted (504): Project relocates operations and results in a 40% workforce reduction in the original community without a documented, approved CDC exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty purchase denial; lender oversight findings; required corrective action or loan cancellation

business_rules.rule_name: Third Party Lender must be identified at 504 application and third party loan must meet minimum 50% rule for startups or limited-purpose assets
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, the Third Party Lender must be in place at the time of application and evidenced by a letter of intent/term sheet or commitment letter included in the application package. The Third Party Loan must be at least as much as the net debenture proceeds. Additionally, the Third Party Loan must total at least 50% of project costs if (i) the Borrower (or Operating Company if the Borrower is an EPC) has operated for 2 years or less, or (ii) the project is for the acquisition, construction, conversion, or expansion of a limited or single purpose asset. SBA 504 financing structure includes the borrower injecting at least 10% of the financing and limits federal sources to no more than 50% of eligible project costs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loan applications requiring a Third Party Loan as part of the capital stack.
business_rules.consequences: If the Third Party Lender is not identified or required Third Party Loan minimums are not met, the 504 application package is incomplete or ineligible and SBA cannot evaluate/approve the application as submitted.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the requirement that Third Party Lender be in place and evidenced at application. The 50% of project costs minimum is specifically triggered by operating history (≤2 years) or limited/single purpose asset projects.

source_reference.section_id: Third Party Loan (13 CFR § 120.920)
source_reference.source_text: The Third Party Lender must be in place at the time of application and must be evidenced by a letter of intent/term sheet or commitment letter included in the application package... The Third Party Loan must be at least as much as the net debenture proceeds. However, the Third Party Loan must total at least 50% of the Project costs if the Borrower (or Operating Company if the Borrower is an Eligible Passive Company) has operated for 2 years or less or the Project is for the acquisition, construction, conversion or expansion of a limited or single purpose asset.

examples: Startup borrower (18 months operating history): Third Party Loan must be at least 50% of total project costs, not merely equal to net debenture proceeds.
Limited-purpose asset hotel acquisition: Third Party Loan must be at least 50% of project costs even if borrower has operated for more than 2 years.
Noncompliant application: No Third Party term sheet/commitment included; application cannot be evaluated as complete.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Application ineligibility or processing deficiency; SBA/CDC cannot approve as submitted

business_rules.rule_name: Third Party Loan documents cannot include cross-default or deem-at-risk early demand provisions absent default and must subordinate swap default fees
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Third Party Loans, the Third Party Lender’s note and loan documents must not include cross-default, “deem-at-risk,” or any other provisions allowing the Third Party Lender to make demand prior to maturity unless the loan is in default. If the Third Party Lender takes Additional Collateral beyond its lien on the project property, then in liquidation (unless otherwise approved in writing by D/OFPO): (i) the Third Party Lender must exhaust reasonable collection avenues on the Additional Collateral no later than disposition of the project property; and (ii) apply Additional Collateral proceeds to the Third Party Loan balance prior to applying proceeds from the project property disposition to the Third Party Loan. For interest rate swap contracts used on Third Party Loans, any fees owed to the swap counterparty due to borrower default must be subordinated to the SBA 504 loan. CDCs must not enter intercreditor agreements other than SBA Form 2287 without prior written SBA consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 transactions involving a Third Party Loan, including cases with additional collateral, swap contracts, and intercreditor arrangements.
business_rules.consequences: Nonconforming Third Party Loan documents or unauthorized intercreditor terms can block SBA/CDC approval/closing, require document amendments, or trigger SBA noncompliance findings affecting eligibility and recoveries.
business_rules.exceptions: Additional Collateral liquidation/proceeds ordering can be modified only if otherwise approved in writing by D/OFPO. Early demand is permitted only when the loan is in default.

source_reference.section_id: Third Party Loan documentation constraints; Additional Collateral; Swap contracts; Intercreditor agreement
source_reference.source_text: The Third Party Lender’s note and loan documents must not have any cross-default, “deem-at-risk,” or any other provisions which allow the Third Party Lender to make demand prior to maturity unless the loan is in default... Additional Collateral... only if... The Third Party Lender liquidates... no later than the disposition of the Project Property, and ... applies any proceeds... prior to the application of proceeds from the disposition of the Project Property... Any fees owed the swap counterparty as a result of the default by the Borrower will be subordinated to the SBA 504 loan. CDCs must not enter into any Intercreditor agreement... other than SBA Form 2287... without the prior written consent of SBA.

examples: Noncompliant: Third Party note allows lender to accelerate if borrower defaults on unrelated debt (cross-default) even though Third Party Loan itself is current.
Compliant: Third Party Loan includes an interest rate swap; swap default fees are explicitly subordinated to the SBA 504 lien position.
Noncompliant: CDC signs a custom intercreditor agreement instead of SBA Form 2287 without prior written SBA consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Closing blocked pending document correction; SBA/CDC oversight findings; potential impairment of SBA recovery position

business_rules.rule_name: Agent packaging fee caps include $2,500 documentation threshold, tiered percent maxima, and $30,000 aggregate cap with prohibitions on flat and contingency fees
business_rules.description: An Agent may charge an SBA applicant for packaging and certain other services only if fees are reasonable and customary for services actually performed, and consistent with fees charged on similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. Packaging/other service fees may be hourly (no maximum, but must be reasonable and fully documented by rate and time) or percentage-based subject to caps: (i) 3% on loans of $50,000 or less; (ii) 2% for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25% on the portion over $1,000,000; and (iii) maximum aggregate percentage-based fee cap of $30,000. All fees over $2,500 must be supported by documentation of the service performed. SBA does not allow Agents to charge standard/flat fees charged to all applicants, contingency fees payable only if the loan is approved/closed, or fees for services not reasonably necessary. SBA may review fees at any time and Agents must refund any fee deemed unreasonable or impermissible by SBA. 7(a) lenders must review agent services/fees when there is an indication fees might be excessive or when an applicant complains; lenders should report unreasonable/impermissible fees to D/OCRM.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when any Agent (including loan packagers/brokers) charges the applicant or the lender in connection with SBA 7(a) loan applications.
business_rules.consequences: Impermissible or excessive fees require reduction/refund and may trigger SBA/OCRM oversight findings; failure to review/report can create lender compliance issues and reputational risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Hourly fees have no maximum but must be reasonable/customary and documented. Percentage caps apply when fees are charged on a percentage basis (combined maximums across multiple services).

source_reference.section_id: Fees an Agent May Charge an Applicant; Review of Agent Fees
source_reference.source_text: An Agent may charge an Applicant fees... based on an hourly rate or on a percentage of the loan amount... all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed... the fee may not exceed... 3 percent on loans of $50,000 or less; 2 percent for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25 percent on the portion over $1,000,000... The maximum fee... is $30,000. SBA does not allow an Agent to charge... A standard or flat fee... Contingency fees... or For services that are not reasonably necessary... Agents must refund any fee considered unreasonable or impermissible by SBA.

examples: Percentage-based fee compliant: $40,000 loan with 3% packaging fee ($1,200) and documented services.
Percentage-based fee noncompliant: $2,000,000 loan charged 2% on full amount ($40,000) because it exceeds the $30,000 aggregate cap and ignores the 0.25% tier over $1,000,000.
Noncompliant: Agent charges a non-refundable $5,000 flat fee to every applicant regardless of services; SBA can require refund.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA fee refund/reduction requirement; OCRM oversight findings; potential agent discipline actions

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Form 159 and E-Tran agent fee disclosures require agent identification and itemization for compensation over $2,500
business_rules.description: When an Agent (other than an LSP performing duties under an SBA-reviewed LSP Agreement) is paid by an applicant or a 7(a) lender, an SBA Form 159 must be completed per form instructions and signed by the applicant, the agent, and the 7(a) lender; separate SBA Forms 159 are required for each Agent who provides services to the applicant. The 7(a) lender must identify in E-Tran whether the lender charged any fees and the amount, and whether an Agent was involved; when an Agent was involved, the lender must provide the Agent’s name and full address details. If aggregate compensation from the same Agent exceeds $2,500, an itemization and supporting documentation must be attached to SBA Form 159. Failure to provide Agent information in E-Tran may result in an OCRM oversight finding; failure of an Agent to fully complete and execute Form 159 may result in suspension or revocation of the Agent’s privilege to do business with SBA under 13 CFR Part 103.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when an Agent participates and is compensated by applicant or lender (excluding LSPs under SBA-reviewed LSP Agreement for the duties covered).
business_rules.consequences: Incomplete E-Tran disclosures may result in OCRM oversight findings; incomplete SBA Form 159 execution can lead to agent suspension/revocation and can delay processing/closing due to missing required disclosures.
business_rules.exceptions: Agents that are LSPs performing duties under an SBA-reviewed LSP Agreement are treated differently for Form 159 requirements (per SOP).

source_reference.section_id: Disclosure of Fees – SBA Form 159
source_reference.source_text: 7(a) Lenders must identify in E-Tran whether the 7(a) Lender charged any fees and the amount of those fees, and whether an Agent was involved in any way with the transaction. When an Agent was involved... must provide the name, street address, city, state, and zip code of the Agent. Failure to do so may result in a finding by OCRM... an SBA Form 159 must be completed... and signed by the Applicant, the Agent, and the 7(a) Lender. Separate SBA Forms 159 are required for each Agent... If the aggregate compensation... exceeds $2,500, an itemization... and supporting documentation must be attached.

examples: Compliant: Two agents assist an applicant; lender completes two separate Forms 159 and attaches itemization for the agent paid $3,200; E-Tran includes both agent details and lender fee amounts.
Noncompliant: Lender indicates “Agent involved = Yes” in E-Tran but omits the agent address fields; OCRM can cite a finding.
Noncompliant: Agent refuses to sign Form 159; SBA may suspend/revoke agent privileges under 13 CFR Part 103 and lender cannot treat disclosure as complete.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCRM oversight finding; agent suspension or revocation under 13 CFR Part 103; processing delays

business_rules.rule_name: Entities with 20% or more ownership must provide unlimited full guaranty and ownership reductions remain subject for 6 months with narrow divestiture exceptions
business_rules.description: For SBA loans, all entities with direct and/or indirect ownership of 20% or more of an applicant must provide an unlimited full guaranty. If the 20%+ owner is a trust (revocable or irrevocable), the trust must guarantee with the trustee executing on behalf of the trust and providing required certifications; if the trust is revocable, the trustor also must guarantee. Any person subject to guaranty requirements 6 months prior to the loan application remains subject even if ownership is reduced below 20% (six-month lookback rule). Exceptions to the six-month lookback: (i) complete divestiture prior to application, including severance of any relationship with the applicant (and associated EPC) in any capacity (including unpaid employee) for the life of the loan; and (ii) for 7(a) partial changes of ownership, guaranty determinations are based on post-sale direct/indirect ownership percentages.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loan applications requiring guaranties from owners and ownership-affiliated entities.
business_rules.consequences: Missing required guaranties can make the application/closing noncompliant; can delay approval/closing and can create SBA guaranty enforceability and oversight issues.
business_rules.exceptions: Six-month lookback does not apply where the person completely divests and severs all relationships for the life of the loan, or for 7(a) partial changes of ownership where post-sale ownership is used to determine guarantors.

source_reference.section_id: Corporate/Other Guaranties; Reducing Ownership Interest (Six-month lookback rule)
source_reference.source_text: All entities that have direct and/or indirect ownership of 20% or more of an Applicant must provide an unlimited full guaranty... if the entity... is a trust... the trust must guarantee... if the trust is revocable, the Trustor also must guarantee... Any Person subject to the guaranty requirements 6 months prior to the date of the loan application would continue to be subject... even if... changed... to less than 20%... Exception... When that Person completely divests their interest prior to the date of application... severance of any relationship... for the life of the 7(a) or 504 loan.

examples: Compliant: An LLC owns 25% indirectly through a holding company; the LLC provides an unlimited full guaranty and provides financial statements.
Noncompliant: A revocable trust owns 30% but only the trustee signs; the trustor does not guarantee—violates revocable trust requirement.
Six-month lookback: An owner held 22% four months before application but reduced to 10% one month before application; still must guarantee unless complete divestiture and severance applies.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility/closing deficiency; guaranty enforceability risk; lender/CDC oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Tax transcript verification is required before first disbursement or 504 debenture funding request with 10-business-day follow-up and Record Not Found handling
business_rules.description: Except for SBA Express and Export Express, SBA lenders must obtain IRS tax return transcripts and reconcile applicant financial data against transcripts (7(a): prior to first disbursement; 504: prior to submitting the request to fund the debenture). Transcript lookback depends on size standard method: (i) NAICS size standard requires last 3 years for the Applicant (or Operating Company if Applicant is an EPC), unless start-up; if operating <3 years, obtain all years in operation. (ii) Alternative size standard requires last 2 years; if operating <2 years, obtain all years in operation. The requirement does not include the most recent fiscal year if fiscal year-end is within 6 months of SBA receiving the application; if an extension was filed, lender must obtain a copy of the extension and evidence of payment of estimated taxes. If IRS reports no record for the Applicant or required years, no disbursements may be made and the loan must be canceled or closing postponed until resolved (unless lender documents satisfaction of SOP’s good-faith steps). If lender does not receive IRS response/transcript within 10 business days, lender must follow up by resubmitting Form 4506-C or Form 8821 marked “Second Request.” For 3-year transcript cases, if “Record Not Found” occurs for the middle year but the other 2 years are verified and there is some evidence of refund/payment for the missing year, lender may reasonably assume a return was filed if documented in the loan file.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans (excluding SBA Express and Export Express) during underwriting/closing and before disbursement or debenture funding request.
business_rules.consequences: Absent required transcripts/reconciliation, lender must not disburse; if IRS has no record for required years, lender must cancel the loan or postpone closing until resolved (unless documented good-faith steps apply).
business_rules.exceptions: SBA Express and Export Express are excluded. Most recent fiscal year transcripts may be excluded if fiscal year-end is within 6 months of SBA receipt of application; extension requires obtaining extension copy and estimated tax payment evidence. Good-faith assumption allowed for missing middle year in 3-year request under specified conditions with documentation.

source_reference.section_id: IRS Tax Transcript/Verification of Financial Information
source_reference.source_text: Except for the SBA Express and Export Express Programs, SBA Lenders must obtain tax return transcripts and reconcile... for 7(a), prior to first disbursement... or for 504, prior to submitting the request to fund the debenture... NAICS Size Standard... for the last 3 years... Alternative Size Standard... last 2 years... If the SBA Lender does not receive a response... within 10 business days, the SBA Lender must follow up... with the notation “Second Request”... If the IRS advises that it has no record... no disbursements may be made... the loan must be canceled, or the closing must be postponed.

examples: 7(a) standard processing: Lender must obtain and reconcile 3 years of transcripts before first disbursement (unless size method dictates 2 years).
IRS delay: No transcript received within 10 business days; lender resubmits 4506-C/8821 labeled “Second Request” before proceeding.
Record Not Found middle year: Lender verifies year 1 and year 3 transcripts, obtains proof of tax payment/refund for missing year 2, documents steps, and proceeds under good-faith assumption.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Disbursement prohibition; loan cancellation or closing postponement; SBA oversight findings for noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) working capital, inventory, and intangible asset maturities must not exceed 10 years
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, the stated maturity must be the shortest appropriate term based on the use of proceeds and the Borrower’s ability to repay. When the proceeds are used for working capital, inventory, or financing intangible assets (including goodwill), the loan maturity must not exceed 10 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and use of proceeds includes working capital, inventory, or intangible assets (including goodwill).
business_rules.consequences: If the maturity exceeds 10 years for these uses, the loan terms are not SBA-compliant and the lender must reduce the maturity to 10 years or less (or restructure the use-of-proceeds allocation). Non-compliance may create SBA eligibility/guaranty risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for working capital/inventory/intangible assets.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities (13 CFR § 120.212)
source_reference.source_text: 7(a) loans must have a stated maturity. The loan term must be the shortest appropriate term based on the use of proceeds and the Borrower's ability to repay. A loan’s term must be: Working capital or inventory loans and the financing of intangible assets (including goodwill) must not exceed 10 years.

examples: Eligible: $500,000 SBA 7(a) working capital loan with a 7-year maturity.
Not eligible as structured: $250,000 SBA 7(a) loan used for goodwill financing with a 12-year maturity; must be reduced to 10 years or less.
Eligible: $150,000 inventory line structured as a term loan with a 10-year maturity (cannot exceed 10 years).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) equipment and leasehold improvement maturities generally 10 years with limited extensions to 15 years and 12 months
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, equipment, fixtures, or furniture maturities generally should not exceed 10 years. The maturity may be up to 15 years if the IRS asset class useful life supports the longer term. For a loan to finance equipment and/or leasehold improvements, the term may include an additional reasonable period needed to complete installation/completion, not to exceed 12 months. For loans for leasehold improvements (except for leasehold interests in land), maturity may not exceed 10 years plus an additional reasonable completion period, but in no case more than 12 months.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and proceeds finance equipment/fixtures/furniture and/or leasehold improvements.
business_rules.consequences: If maturity exceeds the permitted maximum based on asset class useful life and the 12-month completion cap, the loan must be restructured (shorter maturity) to remain SBA-compliant; otherwise SBA eligibility/guaranty risk exists.
business_rules.exceptions: Up to 15 years permitted only when IRS asset class useful life supports the term; the additional completion/installation period is capped at 12 months.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities (13 CFR § 120.212)
source_reference.source_text: Generally, equipment, fixtures, or furniture loans should not exceed 10 years. However, the term may be up to 15 years if the IRS asset class useful life supports the term. The term for a loan to finance equipment and/or leasehold improvements may include an additional reasonable period, not to exceed 12 months... Loans for leasehold improvements (except for leasehold interests in land) may not exceed 10 years... but in no case more than 12 months.

examples: Eligible: $400,000 SBA 7(a) loan for equipment with a 10-year maturity.
Eligible with support: $900,000 SBA 7(a) loan for equipment with a 15-year maturity when IRS asset class supports 15 years.
Not eligible as structured: Leasehold improvements loan with 10-year term plus 18 months completion period; must be reduced to 10 years plus no more than 12 months.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) real estate loan maturity must not exceed 25 years plus necessary construction or renovation period
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) real estate loans (including acquisition, rehabilitation, renovation, construction, or improvements to leasehold interests in land), the maturity must not exceed 25 years. If use of proceeds includes construction or renovation, an additional period reasonably necessary for the construction/renovation may be added to the 25-year maximum maturity.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and the use of proceeds is real estate (acquisition/rehab/renovation/construction/improvements to leasehold interests in land).
business_rules.consequences: Maturity exceeding 25 years (plus only the additional reasonable construction/renovation period when applicable) must be corrected to maintain SBA compliance; otherwise SBA eligibility/guaranty risk exists.
business_rules.exceptions: Additional time beyond 25 years is only permitted when a portion of proceeds is used for construction or renovation, and only for the period reasonably necessary for that work.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities (13 CFR § 120.212)
source_reference.source_text: Real estate loans (including acquisition, rehabilitation, renovation, construction, or improvements to leasehold interests in land) must not exceed 25 years... If the use of proceeds on a real estate loan includes construction or renovation, an additional period reasonably necessary for the construction or renovation period may be added to the 25-year maximum maturity.

examples: Eligible: SBA 7(a) owner-occupied real estate acquisition loan with a 25-year maturity.
Eligible if justified: Real estate acquisition plus renovation loan with a 25-year maturity plus a documented additional renovation period.
Not eligible as structured: Real estate acquisition-only loan with a 30-year maturity and no construction/renovation component.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) mixed purpose and change of ownership maturities may be blended with 25-year cap if 51% or more proceeds are for real estate
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) mixed purpose loans and loans for changes of ownership, the maturity may be blended based on the underlying financed purposes. If 51% or more of the use of proceeds is for real estate, the maximum maturity may be up to 25 years. For stock purchases, maturity may be based on the underlying assets/interest financed as supported by a business valuation/appraisal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and (a) proceeds include multiple purposes and/or refinancing of multiple purposes, and/or (b) proceeds are used for changes of ownership, including stock purchases.
business_rules.consequences: If the maturity is set beyond what is permitted by the blended maturity logic or exceeds 25 years where the 51% real estate test is met, the terms must be restructured; otherwise SBA eligibility/guaranty risk exists.
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond the stated 51% real estate condition and valuation-supported stock purchase approach.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Maturities (13 CFR § 120.212)
source_reference.source_text: Mixed purpose loans and loans for all types of changes of ownership... the maturity may be a blended maturity or, if 51% or more of the use of the 7(a) loan’s proceeds are for real estate, the maximum maturity may be up to 25 years. For stock purchases, the loan maturity may be based on the underlying assets/interest financed by the 7(a) loan as supported by a business valuation/appraisal.

examples: Eligible: 7(a) loan where 60% of proceeds are for real estate and 40% for working capital; maturity set at 25 years (subject to SBA rules and underwriting).
Eligible: Stock purchase financed by 7(a) where maturity is set based on the financed underlying assets and supported by a business valuation/appraisal.
Not eligible as structured: Mixed purpose loan with only 40% real estate proceeds but a 25-year maturity without blended maturity support.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) variable interest rate maximum spreads over Prime or Optional Peg Rate by loan size
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans with a variable interest rate, the allowable spread over the acceptable base rate is capped by loan amount. Acceptable base rates are the Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate. Maximum variable spreads are: (1) $50,000 or less: +6.5%; (2) $50,001 to $250,000: +6.0%; (3) $250,001 to $350,000: +4.5%; (4) $350,001 and greater: +3.0%. The initial Note rate must not exceed the SBA maximum (base + allowable spread).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan with a variable interest rate (Prime or Optional Peg base).
business_rules.consequences: If the spread exceeds the maximum for the loan size, the interest rate terms are not SBA-compliant and must be corrected before closing/servicing changes. Non-compliance may create SBA eligibility/guaranty risk and servicing remediation obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. (Fixed rate maximums are separately published; variable caps apply when fixed rate is not locked for entire term without reset.)

source_reference.section_id: Interest Rates (13 CFR §§ 120.213 & 120.214)
source_reference.source_text: Maximum variable rates are: Loans $50,000 or less Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 6.5%; Loans $50,001 up to and including $250,000... + 6.0%; Loans $250,001 up to and including $350,000... + 4.5%; Loans $350,001 and greater... + 3.0%... There are two acceptable base rates: The Prime Rate; or The SBA Optional Peg Rate.

examples: Eligible: $400,000 7(a) variable rate loan at Prime + 2.75% (≤ Prime + 3.0%).
Not eligible as structured: $200,000 7(a) variable rate loan at Prime + 6.50% (exceeds Prime + 6.0% cap for $50,001–$250,000).
Eligible: $50,000 7(a) variable rate loan at Optional Peg + 6.25% (≤ +6.5%).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) default interest rates are prohibited and lenders may not split loans to charge higher rates
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, default interest rates are not permitted. Additionally, the 7(a) lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the Applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) loans, including variable and fixed rate structures.
business_rules.consequences: If a note includes a default interest rate provision, it must be removed/changed to comply. If a lender splits a loan to obtain higher pricing, the structure violates SBA requirements and creates SBA eligibility/guaranty risk and potential program enforcement consequences.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: General Policy on Interest Rates (13 CFR §§ 120.213 & 120.214)
source_reference.source_text: for any 7(a) loan is governed by SBA’s regulations on interest rates, which preempts any provisions of a state’s constitution or law. The Lender negotiates the interest rate with the Applicant,

examples: Not permitted: A 7(a) note states that upon default the rate increases by 5.00%; this default rate provision must be removed.
Not permitted: A lender structures a $500,000 request as two loans ($350,000 and $150,000) to charge higher pricing on the smaller tranche.
Permitted: A single $500,000 loan priced within the applicable cap and without a default rate.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility risk; potential repair/denial at purchase or program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) refinancing is ineligible if it shifts potential loss to SBA and requires debt to be current for 12 months with 29-day delinquency standard
business_rules.description: SBA-guaranteed loan proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (including same institution debt) if the refinancing shifts all or part of a potential loss to SBA. The debt to be refinanced must have been current for at least the last 12 months (or for the life of the loan, whichever is less). “Current” means a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days. A loan that has matured and is not paid within 29 days after maturity is not current and is not eligible for refinancing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and use of proceeds includes refinancing of existing business debt (including same institution debt).
business_rules.consequences: If the refinancing would shift potential loss to SBA or the debt fails the current-payment history test, the refinancing use of proceeds is not eligible. The lender must remove/replace the ineligible refinance component or the loan is not eligible for SBA guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: The “current for 12 months” requirement is “12 months or the life of the loan, whichever is less.”

source_reference.section_id: Debt Refinancing (13 CFR §§ 120.140(j)(1) and 120.201)
source_reference.source_text: SBA-guaranteed loan proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (including the same institution’s debt). This includes any refinancing that will shift all or part of a potential loss from the original lender to the SBA. The debt to be refinanced must be, and must have been, current for at least the last 12 months or for the life of the loan, whichever is less. “Current” means that a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days.

examples: Not eligible: Refinancing a note that is 45 days past due (fails the 29-day definition of current).
Not eligible: Refinancing a troubled credit where the lender would otherwise sustain a loss, shifting that loss to SBA.
Not eligible: A matured loan that was not paid within 29 days of the maturity date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair exposure due to ineligible use of proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) same institution debt refinancing requires non-delegated processing and 36-month payment transcript with late-payment explanations
business_rules.description: Refinancing of Same Institution Debt (SID) may not be processed under a lender’s PLP authority and must be processed under non-delegated procedures. The lender must submit the loan via E-Tran to SBA for non-delegated processing and must: (1) include a transcript showing due dates and when payments were received for the prior 36 months (or the life of the loan, whichever is less); and (2) explain in writing any late payments and late charges during the last 36 months (late payments are any payment made beyond 29 days of the due date).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and proceeds will refinance Same Institution Debt (SID).
business_rules.consequences: If processed under PLP/delegated authority or without the required transcript and explanations, the SID refinance does not meet SBA procedural requirements and may be rejected or create guaranty purchase/repair risk.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA does not consider certain items as SID refinancing (e.g., certain interim loans within 90 days or undistributed construction loans at time of PLP number issuance; and seller debt payoff in complete change of ownership) but those exceptions must be evaluated separately.

source_reference.section_id: Refinancing Same Institution Debt (SID)
source_reference.source_text: Refinancing Same Institution Debt (SID). Refinancing of SID may not be processed under a Lender’s PLP authority... The Lender must submit the loan via E-Tran to SBA for non-delegated processing and: Include a transcript showing the due dates and when payments were received... for the prior 36 months, or the life of the loan, whichever is less; and Explain in writing any late payments and late charges... (Late payments are defined as any payment made beyond 29 days of the due date.)

examples: Required: Lender refinances its own conventional term loan with a 7(a) loan; submits via E-Tran non-delegated and attaches 36 months payment transcript and a memo explaining two 35-day late payments.
Not compliant: Lender processes an SID refinance under PLP and does not provide payment transcript; application is procedurally deficient.
Required: Loan is only 18 months old; lender provides transcript for the life of the loan (18 months) and explains any payments beyond 29 days.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection and/or guaranty purchase repair risk due to procedural noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) refinancing must provide at least 10 percent improvement to installment payment amount with special rule for escalating payments
business_rules.description: When refinancing debt with an SBA 7(a) loan, the new installment payment amount must be at least 10% less than the existing installment amount(s). If other debt is refinanced at the same time, that debt may be included in the improvement calculation. If the new note has an escalating payment structure, the new installment amount must be at least 10% less than the expected installment amount within the next 12 months.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when CRE_LOAN is an SBA 7(a) loan and use of proceeds includes refinancing eligible business debt.
business_rules.consequences: If the refinancing does not meet the 10% improvement requirement (including the 12-month look-forward for escalating payments), the refinancing request is not eligible under SBA requirements and must be restructured or removed to maintain SBA eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: The provided excerpt references that an exception applies for certain debt types (e.g., demand note or balloon payment, credit card obligations, HELOC used for business-related purposes), but the full exception text is truncated in the source excerpt and requires confirmation in the complete SOP section before automation.

source_reference.section_id: Ten Percent Improvement to Installment Payment Amount
source_reference.source_text: Ten Percent Improvement to Installment Payment Amount. When refinancing debt, the new installment payment amount must be at least 10 percent less than the existing installment amount(s). If other debt is being refinanced at the same time, such debt may be included in the installment payment improvement calculation. If the note terms include an escalating payment structure, the new installment amount must be at least 10 percent less than the expected installment amount within the next 12 months.

examples: Eligible: Existing installment is $10,000/month; new installment is $8,900/month (11% reduction).
Not eligible: Existing installment is $5,000/month; new installment is $4,700/month (6% reduction).
Escalating payment test: New note steps up to $9,500/month within 12 months; existing payment is $10,000/month. New payment is only 5% lower, so it fails the 10% requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair exposure due to ineligible refinancing structure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) maximum maturity is 10 years for working capital, inventory, and intangible assets
business_rules.description: SBA 7(a) loans must have a stated maturity and the term must be the shortest appropriate term based on use of proceeds and the borrower’s ability to repay. For working capital or inventory loans and the financing of intangible assets (including goodwill), the maturity must not exceed 10 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when use of proceeds includes working capital, inventory, or intangible assets (including goodwill).
business_rules.consequences: If structured with a maturity longer than 10 years for these purposes, the loan does not meet SBA requirements and may be subject to SBA repair/denial of guaranty or required restructuring before approval/closing.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for working capital/inventory/intangibles.

source_reference.section_id: 13 CFR § 120.212 – Loan maturities (working capital/inventory/intangibles)
source_reference.source_text: 7(a) loans must have a stated maturity. The loan term must be the shortest appropriate term based on the use of proceeds and the Borrower's ability to repay. A loan’s term must be: Working capital or inventory loans and the financing of intangible assets (including goodwill) must not exceed 10 years.

examples: A $300,000 SBA 7(a) working capital term loan is proposed with a 12-year maturity; lender must reduce maturity to 10 years or less.
A 7(a) loan that finances goodwill as part of a transaction must have a term of 10 years or less for that intangible-asset purpose component.
A loan with mixed uses where 60% is working capital and 40% equipment should use a blended maturity but cannot extend the working-capital component beyond 10 years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) maximum maturity is 25 years for real estate and up to 20 years for farm real estate-heavy uses
business_rules.description: Real estate SBA 7(a) loans (including acquisition, rehabilitation, renovation, construction, or improvements to leasehold interests in land) must not exceed 25 years, with an additional period reasonably necessary for construction or renovation allowed when those uses are included. Mixed purpose loans and certain refinances may use a blended maturity; if 51% or more of the proceeds are for real estate, maximum maturity may be up to 25 years. For loans to farm enterprises where land and structures for farming comprise 51% or more of the use of proceeds, maximum maturity is 20 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans with real estate as a use of proceeds, including mixed purpose loans, and to farm enterprise loans meeting the 51% test for land/structures.
business_rules.consequences: If maturity exceeds SBA maximums for the applicable use-of-proceeds profile, lender must shorten the term or restructure to comply; noncompliance risks SBA guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: If real estate use includes construction or renovation, an additional period reasonably necessary for the construction/renovation period may be added to the 25-year maximum maturity.

source_reference.section_id: 13 CFR § 120.212 – Loan maturities (real estate, mixed purpose, farm enterprises)
source_reference.source_text: Real estate loans (including acquisition, rehabilitation, renovation, construction, or improvements to leasehold interests in land) must not exceed 25 years... If the use of proceeds on a real estate loan includes construction or renovation, an additional period reasonably necessary for the construction or renovation period may be added to the 25-year maximum maturity... if 51% or more of the use of the 7(a) loan’s proceeds are for real estate, the maximum maturity may be up to 25 years... Where land and structures ... for farming comprise 51% or more of the use of proceeds, the maximum maturity is 20 years.

examples: A 7(a) loan for purchase of an office building (no construction) must have a maturity of 25 years or less.
A 7(a) loan for acquisition plus 9 months of renovation may have a term up to 25 years plus a reasonable additional renovation period (e.g., 9 months) if documented as necessary.
A farm enterprise loan where 60% of proceeds are for farmland/poultry houses must have a maturity of 20 years or less (not 25).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) maximum variable interest rate is Prime or Optional Peg plus 6.5, 6.0, 4.5, or 3.0 percent based on loan size
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) variable-rate loans, the maximum allowable rate is based on loan size and is limited to an acceptable base rate (Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate) plus a maximum spread. The tiered maximum spreads are: (1) Loans $50,000 or less: base + 6.5%; (2) Loans $50,001 through $250,000: base + 6.0%; (3) Loans $250,001 through $350,000: base + 4.5%; (4) Loans $350,001 and greater: base + 3.0%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans with variable interest rates; enforce at note origination and whenever rate resets are calculated to ensure the note rate does not exceed SBA maximum.
business_rules.consequences: If the note rate exceeds the applicable maximum, lender must reduce pricing to comply; noncompliance risks SBA guaranty repair/denial and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Fixed-rate loans follow SBA’s published maximum allowable fixed rate (Federal Register/FTA Wiki). If a fixed rate will not remain in effect for the entire term without adjustment/reset, variable-rate caps apply.

source_reference.section_id: SBA Quick Reference Chart – Maximum Interest Rates Allowed (Standard 7(a))
source_reference.source_text: Maximum variable rates are: Loans $50,000 or less Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 6.5% ... Loans $50,001 up to and including $250,000 Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 6.0% ... Loans $250,001 up to and including $350,000 ... + 4.5% ... Loans $350,001 and greater Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 3.0%

examples: A $40,000 SBA 7(a) variable-rate loan indexed to Prime at 8.50% may not exceed 15.00% (8.50% + 6.50%).
A $300,000 SBA 7(a) variable-rate loan indexed to Optional Peg at 7.00% may not exceed 11.50% (7.00% + 4.50%).
A $600,000 SBA 7(a) variable-rate loan indexed to Prime at 8.50% may not exceed 11.50% (8.50% + 3.00%).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; reimbursement/fee issues; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Default interest rates are prohibited on SBA 7(a) loans
business_rules.description: SBA 7(a) loan notes may not include or apply default interest rates. In a default scenario, lenders must not increase the interest rate solely due to default through a default-rate provision.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) loans at note drafting/closing and during servicing when determining default charges and interest accrual mechanics.
business_rules.consequences: If a default interest provision is included or applied, the loan may be noncompliant and subject to SBA guaranty repair/denial, required corrective action, and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: General Policy on Interest Rates (13 CFR §§ 120.213 & 120.214)
source_reference.source_text: Default interest rates are not permitted.

examples: Note proposes Prime + 3.00% with a default rate of Prime + 6.00% upon payment default; lender must remove the default-rate provision.
Servicing system automatically adds 2.00% to the interest rate when the loan becomes 30 days past due; lender must disable that function for SBA 7(a) loans.
Loan agreement uses late fees (if otherwise permitted) but does not increase the interest rate upon default; this complies with the prohibition on default interest.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum loan-to-value 90% for SBA 504 projects that refinance only qualified debt
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 refinancing projects that refinance only Qualified Debt, the maximum loan-to-value (LTV) of the Refinancing Project is 90%. LTV is evaluated based on the value of the Eligible Fixed Asset(s) securing the Qualified Debt and the total Refinancing Project structure such that the Refinancing Project LTV does not exceed 90%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the SBA 504 project is a refinancing project and the project refinances only Qualified Debt.
business_rules.consequences: If the Refinancing Project LTV exceeds 90%, the structure is not compliant and must be restructured (e.g., reduce financed amount and/or increase borrower cash contribution) before approval/closing.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 90% cap in this condition.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Loan-to-Value Limitations (Page 342)
source_reference.source_text:
Loan -to-Value Limitations
i. For projects that refinance only Qualified Debt, the maximum loan to value
of the Refinancing Project allowed is 90%.

examples: Refi project value $1,000,000; total refinanced/financed amount $900,000 → LTV 90% (permitted).
Refi project value $1,000,000; total refinanced/financed amount $925,000 → LTV 92.5% (not permitted; must restructure).
Refi project value $2,500,000; total refinanced/financed amount $2,250,000 → LTV 90% (permitted).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility/closing denial; findings in SBA review or lender/CDC oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower must add cash or acceptable fixed-asset collateral when qualified debt refinance exceeds 90% of collateral value
business_rules.description: If the amount of Qualified Debt being refinanced is more than 90% of the value of the Eligible Fixed Asset(s) securing the Qualified Debt, the Borrower must provide additional cash or other fixed asset collateral acceptable to SBA so the Refinancing Project does not exceed a 90% loan-to-value (LTV).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinancing projects when the Qualified Debt amount to be refinanced is > 90% of the value of the Eligible Fixed Asset(s) securing that Qualified Debt.
business_rules.consequences: If the borrower does not provide sufficient additional cash or SBA-acceptable fixed asset collateral, the transaction must be restructured or declined because the Refinancing Project would exceed the 90% LTV requirement.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Loan-to-Value Limitations (Page 342)
source_reference.source_text:
ii. For projects when the amount of Qualified Debt being refinanced is more
than 90 percent of the value of the Eligible Fixed Asset(s) securing the
Qualified Debt, the Borrower must provide additional cash or o ther fixed
asset collateral acceptable to SBA so as not to exceed a 90% loan to value of
the Refinancing Project.

examples: Eligible fixed assets value $1,000,000; qualified debt to be refinanced $950,000 (>90%) → borrower must add cash and/or SBA-acceptable fixed-asset collateral so the refinancing project LTV is ≤ 90%.
Eligible fixed assets value $2,000,000; qualified debt to be refinanced $1,850,000 (92.5%) → borrower adds $50,000 cash contribution; effective LTV reduces toward ≤ 90% depending on structure.
Eligible fixed assets value $1,200,000; qualified debt to be refinanced $1,050,000 (87.5%) → this rule does not trigger.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility/closing denial; findings in SBA review or lender/CDC oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum loan-to-value 90% applies to refinancing projects with eligible business expenses and project value cannot be increased by adding collateral
business_rules.description: For any SBA 504 refinancing projects that include the financing of Eligible Business Expenses, a maximum 90% LTV of the Refinancing Project applies. Additionally, the value of the Refinancing Project may not be increased by adding additional collateral for purposes of meeting the LTV calculation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA 504 refinancing project includes financing of Eligible Business Expenses as part of the refinancing project.
business_rules.consequences: If LTV exceeds 90% (and/or if the project attempts to increase project value by adding collateral to satisfy LTV), the structure is not compliant and must be restructured or declined.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Loan-to-Value Limitations (Page 342)
source_reference.source_text:
iii. For any projects that include the financing of Eligible Business Expenses, a
maximum 90% loan to value of the Refinancing Project will apply. The
value of the Refinancing Project may not be increased by adding additional
collateral.

examples: Refi project includes $75,000 eligible business expenses; project value $1,000,000; total financing $925,000 → LTV 92.5% (not permitted).
Borrower proposes to pledge additional collateral to increase the 'value of the Refinancing Project' to meet 90% LTV → not permitted (project value cannot be increased by adding collateral).
Refi project includes eligible business expenses; project value $2,000,000; total financing $1,800,000 → LTV 90% (permitted, assuming proper valuation rules).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility/closing denial; findings in SBA review or lender/CDC oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Debt Refinance Without Expansion must be fully disbursed within 9 months of SBA approval with one possible 6-month extension for good cause
business_rules.description: SBA 504 loans for Debt Refinance without Expansion must be disbursed within 9 months after loan approval. The D/FA (or designee) may approve an extension of the disbursement period for an additional 6 months for good cause.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans structured as 'Loans for Debt Refinance without Expansion.' The timeline starts at SBA loan approval date.
business_rules.consequences: If disbursement is not completed within 9 months and no approved extension exists, the loan is noncompliant and may be subject to SBA corrective action (including potential cancellation/withdrawal of approval depending on SBA processing rules).
business_rules.exceptions: One extension up to 6 months may be approved for good cause by D/FA or designee.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Other Implementation Guidelines (Page 342)
source_reference.source_text:
iii. Loans for Debt Refinance without Expansion must be disbursed within 9
months after loan approval. The D/FA or designee  may approve a request for
extension of the disbursement period  for an additional 6 months for good
cause.

examples: SBA approval dated July 1, 2025 → all disbursements must be completed by April 1, 2026 unless an extension is approved.
Disbursement not completed by month 9; CDC requests extension with justification; D/FA designee approves +6 months → new deadline month 15.
Disbursement not completed by month 9 and no extension requested/approved → noncompliance and subject to SBA action.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA corrective action; processing/closing exception findings; potential approval cancellation risk

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing appraisal not required at application but must be dated within 12 months of application approval before closing
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 refinancing, appraisals are not required at time of application. Prior to closing, the appraisal must be dated within 12 months of the date the application was approved and must otherwise comply with SBA appraisal requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinancing applications where an appraisal is required by SBA appraisal rules; timing requirements govern when the appraisal must be obtained and its permissible age relative to application approval date.
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal is older than 12 months from application approval date at the time of closing, the appraisal does not meet SBA requirements and the closing should not proceed until compliant appraisal documentation is obtained.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; other appraisal exceptions may exist elsewhere in SOP for certain collateral types.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Documentation Requirements (Page 343-344)
source_reference.source_text:
iv. Appraisal. Appraisals are not requ ired at time of application. Appraisals
dated within 12 months of the date the application was approved are required
prior to closing, and appraisals must otherwise comply with the requirements

examples: Application approved August 15, 2025; appraisal dated September 1, 2024 → older than 12 months at closing; obtain a new/updated appraisal.
Application approved August 15, 2025; appraisal dated December 1, 2024 → within 12 months; acceptable for closing (subject to other appraisal requirements).
No appraisal in file at application submission → acceptable (timing rule allows no appraisal at application), but must be obtained before closing when required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA closing deficiency; post-closing review findings and corrective action

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 debenture gross amount minimum $25,000 and maximum $5,000,000 aggregate per small business including affiliates and other SBA guaranties
business_rules.description: The SBA 504 Gross Debenture is subject to SBA minimum and maximum limits. The minimum debenture amount is $25,000. For all 504 projects except Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers, the Gross Debenture is limited to an outstanding balance of $5,000,000 maximum in the aggregate for each small business concern, including affiliates. In calculating this limit, SBA loan guaranties (committed or outstanding) received through the 504 or 7(a) (including Community Advantage) programs must be included.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 projects generally; the $5,000,000 cap applies except for Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers (which have different limits). Applies on an aggregate basis per small business concern including affiliates.
business_rules.consequences: If the requested/structured Gross Debenture would exceed the applicable maximum (or is below the $25,000 minimum), the debenture amount must be adjusted or the application is not eligible under SBA debenture limit rules.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers have different maximums described separately.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Debenture Limits (Page 351)
source_reference.source_text: the eligible administrative costs . The Gross Debenture is subject to the following dollar limits and conditions: The minimum dollar amount for a debenture is $25,000. 13 CFR § 120.930(b) For a ll 504 Projects except for Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers , the gross debenture is limited to an outstanding

examples: Borrower group has $4.8MM outstanding SBA 504 gross debentures; requests new $400k gross debenture (non-energy, non-manufacturer) → would exceed $5.0MM aggregate; not eligible unless reduced.
Applicant requests gross debenture $20,000 → below $25,000 minimum; not eligible as structured.
Applicant has $4.7MM SBA exposure comprised of 504 + 7(a) guaranties; requests new $500k 504 gross debenture (non-exempt) → total $5.2MM; must reduce/decline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing denial; post-approval eligibility exception findings

business_rules.rule_name: Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects debenture limit $5,500,000 per project and $16,500,000 aggregate per borrower group
business_rules.description: For Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects, the maximum Gross Debenture is $5,500,000 per eligible project. Additionally, the outstanding Gross Debentures issued for a small business concern (including affiliates) for Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects must not exceed $16,500,000 in the aggregate. The $5,500,000 per-project limit is not reduced by other outstanding SBA loan guaranties through other SBA loan programs, and energy projects do not reduce the $5,000,000 limit for other 504 projects.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the SBA 504 project qualifies as an Eligible Energy Public Policy Project under SOP criteria referenced in the debenture limit section.
business_rules.consequences: If the gross debenture for an energy project exceeds $5,500,000, or if the borrower group’s aggregate outstanding energy-project gross debentures exceed $16,500,000, the project is not eligible as structured and must be reduced/restructured/declined.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond category-specific treatment (not reduced by other SBA guaranties; energy projects do not reduce non-energy 504 limits).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects (Page 351-352)
source_reference.source_text: for other 504 Projects. Proje cts for Small Manufactur ers $5,500,000 maximum for each eligible project , defined as a business with its primary NAICS Code in Sectors 31, 32, and 33, and

examples: Energy project request gross debenture $5.7MM → exceeds $5.5MM per project; must reduce or restructure.
Borrower group has $16.2MM outstanding energy-project gross debentures; requests new $500k energy debenture → aggregate $16.7MM; not eligible unless reduced.
Borrower group has $5MM non-energy 504 exposure; requests $5.5MM energy debenture → allowed if energy-eligible and within $16.5MM energy aggregate (and per-project limit met).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing denial; post-approval eligibility exception findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 maturity must be 10, 20, or 25 years and third-party loan minimum term must be 7 or 10 years depending on 504 maturity
business_rules.description: SBA 504 loan maturity must be 10, 20, or 25 years based on remaining useful life of the property financed, including: maximum 25 years for real estate and minimum 10 years for machinery and equipment; if both real estate and equipment are financed, maturity is based on the asset category that constitutes the majority of loan proceeds.
