business_rules.exceptions: If the lender cannot obtain the deviation statement but obtains a new appraisal demonstrating market value meets or exceeds the original estimate, then no additional action by lender is necessary (per SBA guidance).

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Commercial Real Estate (i)(e)-(f)
source_reference.source_text: If the loan will be used to finance new construction or the substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate what the market value will be at completion of construction. (“Substantial” means rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of the application.) After construction is completed, Lender must obtain a statement... If the Lender cannot obtain such a statement, then the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission.

examples: A $2MM SBA loan includes $800k rehab on a $2MM purchase (40%): substantial rehab; appraisal must be ‘at completion’ and lender must obtain post-construction deviation statement.

Borrower finishes construction but contractor refuses to sign deviation statement: lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing (unless it gets a new appraisal meeting/exceeding the original estimate).

SBA-guaranteed funds were used during the construction period and final value is lower: lender must notify CLSC with reasons and a remedy recommendation, including FMV and liquidation values of any additional collateral.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan closing prohibition without SBA written permission; SBA lender oversight finding and potential guaranty repair/denial if closed improperly

business_rules.rule_name: Closing restriction when appraised value is less than 90% of estimated value unless SBA permission or delegated lender documentation applies
business_rules.description: If the lender requires the appraisal at closing, the loan application must include an estimated real estate value identified in the credit memorandum, and the appraisal must support the estimated value at closing. At closing, if the appraised value is 90% or more of estimated value, the lender may close but must include a written explanation in the file as to why appraisal is less than the estimate. If the appraised value is less than 90% of estimated value, the lender generally may not close without SBA’s prior written permission; the justification must explain reasons for difference and proposed remedy (e.g., additional equity/collateral) and identify fair market and liquidation values of additional collateral. Exception: PLP Lenders (Standard 7(a)) and Export Express lenders are permitted to close when appraisal is <90% but must retain a thorough written justification including risk offsets such as additional equity or collateral and may be reviewed by SBA at guaranty purchase or lender oversight.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when appraisal is obtained at closing and an estimated value was used in underwriting/credit memo; triggered by comparison of appraised value at closing to estimated value.
business_rules.consequences: If appraised value <90% and lender closes without SBA prior written permission when required (non-PLP Standard 7(a)), SBA may take adverse action (e.g., guaranty repair/denial). If appraised value >=90% but lender fails to document written explanation, file is non-compliant and subject to SBA criticism at oversight/guaranty purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: PLP Lenders (Standard 7(a)) and Export Express lenders may close when appraisal is <90% of estimated value, but must retain written justification and analysis addressing risk mitigation steps.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Commercial Real Estate (i)(k)
source_reference.source_text: If at time of closing the appraised value: i) Is 90% or more of the estimated value, the Lender may close the loan but must include a written explanation... ii) Is less than 90% of estimated value, the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission... iii) Exception for PLP Lenders: PLP Lenders are permitted to close a loan when the appraisal is less than 90% of the estimated value but the Lender must include a written justification as part of its file...

examples: Estimated value in credit memo is $1,000,000 and appraisal at closing is $920,000 (92%): lender may close but must document why appraisal is below estimate.

Estimated value is $1,000,000 and appraisal is $850,000 (85%): a non-PLP Standard 7(a) lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing and provide remedy recommendations and values for additional collateral.
An Export Express lender closes at 85% of estimated value: permitted, but lender must keep thorough justification and explain steps taken to offset the low appraisal risk (e.g., more equity).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan closing prohibition without SBA prior written permission (where required); potential SBA guaranty repair/denial and adverse lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: Existing building appraisal should be as-is and SBA collateral valuation must exclude rental income and intangible asset value
business_rules.description: When an SBA loan will be used to acquire an existing building that does not require construction, the appraiser should estimate market value on an as-is basis. If the appraiser estimates value other than as-is, the narrative must explain why as-is was not used. When valuing the collateral, the lender must not include the contributory value of any rental income or the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans secured by commercial real estate where the transaction is acquisition of an existing building without construction, and for collateral valuation practices in SBA commercial real estate appraisals generally.
business_rules.consequences: If appraisal is not as-is (without narrative justification) or if valuation includes prohibited rental income or intangible asset value, the collateral value used for underwriting may be deemed non-compliant and subject to SBA oversight findings and potential guaranty repair/denial if it affects credit decision or collateral adequacy.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Commercial Real Estate (i)(g)-(h)
source_reference.source_text: If the loan will be used to acquire an existing building that does not require construction, the appraiser should estimate market value on an as-is basis. If the appraiser estimates the value other than on an as-is basis, the narrative must include an explanation of why the as-is basis was not used. When valuing the collateral, the Lender must not include the contributory value of any rental income or the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal.

examples: SBA loan acquires an existing warehouse with no construction: appraisal should be as-is; if appraiser uses stabilized value, report must explain why as-is not used.
Appraisal includes business value attributable to in-place leases as an ‘intangible’: lender must exclude that intangible value from collateral valuation for SBA purposes.
Appraisal credits projected rental income streams as separate ‘contributory’ value beyond real estate: lender must not include that component.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial if collateral value was overstated using prohibited components

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express commercial real estate requires USPAP appraisal for close relationship transactions or when needed, otherwise an evaluation consistent with interagency guidance
business_rules.description: For SBA Export Express loans secured by commercial real property, if the loan finances a transaction involving parties with a close relationship (e.g., between existing owners or family members), or if the lender otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness, the lender must obtain an appraisal. Appraisals must comply with USPAP and be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. If an appraisal is not required under those triggers, the lender must obtain an appropriate evaluation of the commercial real estate that is consistent with safe and sound banking practices; evaluations need not be USPAP or performed by state licensed/certified appraisers, but should be consistent with interagency guidance on evaluations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Export Express loans secured by commercial real property. Trigger (appraisal required) if close relationship transaction exists or lender determines appraisal is needed for creditworthiness; otherwise (evaluation required) when appraisal not required.
business_rules.consequences: If appraisal-required conditions are met but lender only obtains an evaluation, SBA requirement is violated. If appraisal not required but lender obtains no evaluation, file is deficient. Non-compliance may be cited in SBA oversight and may affect guaranty purchase outcomes.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_ExportExpress > Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements > Commercial Real Estate (i)(a)(i)-(iii)
source_reference.source_text: For all loans secured by commercial real property: i) If the loan finances a transaction involving parties with a close relationship... or if the Lender otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness, the Lender must obtain an appraisal. ii) Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. iii) If an appraisal is not required... all Lenders must obtain an appropriate evaluation... consistent with safe and sound banking practices.

examples: Export Express loan funds purchase of a building from the borrower’s parent: appraisal is required (close relationship).
Export Express loan secured by CRE with no close relationship and lender deems appraisal unnecessary: lender must still obtain an evaluation consistent with safe and sound practices.
Export Express appraisal completed 13 months before guaranty application: cannot be relied on due to the 12-month requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial if appraisal/evaluation requirements were not met

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loans must take a first lien unless SBA approves a second lien and piggyback financing within 90 days is prohibited for second-lien justification
business_rules.description: International Trade (IT) loans must be secured by a first lien position or first mortgage on the property or equipment financed with IT loan proceeds or on other assets of the applicant. An IT loan may be secured by a second lien if SBA determines it provides adequate assurance of payment. For a second lien scenario where the first lien secures an existing loan used to acquire the collateral, SBA allows the IT loan to be second lien only if the first-lien loan was not made at or about the same time as the IT loan; SBA defines 'at or about the same time' as loans approved within 90 days of each other (piggyback financing). Lenders must document offsetting risk factors and justification (including where first-lien rate is significantly higher or maturity significantly shorter). IT loans may not be processed under PLP authority when the IT loan will not have a first lien on the assets being financed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loans. Triggered when determining required lien position and when considering second-lien collateral structure.
business_rules.consequences: If IT loan does not take a required first lien and SBA has not determined a second lien provides adequate assurance, the structure is non-compliant and may result in SBA denial/repair. If first-lien loan and IT loan were approved within 90 days, second lien position is treated as piggyback and is disallowed for this justification framework. If IT loan lacks first lien, it cannot be processed under PLP authority.
business_rules.exceptions: Second lien is permissible only if SBA determines adequate assurance of repayment and the 90-day piggyback restriction is not violated, with documented offsetting factors and clear justification.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards for International Trade > Required Lien Position
source_reference.source_text: Each IT loan must be secured either by a first lien position... except that an IT loan may be secured by a second lien... if the SBA determines the second lien position provides adequate assurance... SBA considers “at or about the same time” to mean loans approved within 90 days of each other... IT loans may not be processed under a Lender’s PLP authority when the IT loan will not have a first lien on the assets being financed.

examples: IT loan finances equipment, but existing equipment loan approved 45 days earlier holds first lien: treated as piggyback; IT second lien structure fails the 90-day rule and is not eligible under this framework.

IT loan improves a building with an existing mortgage from 3 years ago: IT loan may be second lien if SBA determines adequate assurance and lender documents offsetting factors (e.g., other collateral).
A PLP lender attempts to process an IT loan under PLP while taking only a second lien on financed assets: not permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial/repair of guaranty eligibility for improper lien position; adverse SBA lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) refinance must maintain at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt
business_rules.description: When Standard 7(a) loan proceeds are used to refinance existing debt (except for trading assets), the SBA-guaranteed loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt being refinanced. If the refinanced debt is over-collateralized based on SBA collateral requirements and the SBA loan will remain fully secured, the lender may approve release of excess collateral; substitute collateral may be offered if comparable in value and useful life and acceptable to SBA or a PLP lender under PLP authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan_purpose includes refinancing existing debt using SBA Standard 7(a) proceeds; excludes refinancing of trading assets.
business_rules.consequences: If collateral and lien priority are reduced below the refinanced debt (outside permitted releases/substitutions), the loan fails SBA collateral minimum requirements, creating risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial and SBA review findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Excess collateral may be released if debt being refinanced is over-collateralized under SBA requirements and the SBA loan remains fully secured. Substitute collateral allowed if comparable value/useful life and acceptable to SBA or PLP lender under PLP authority. Trading assets are excluded from the refinance-collateral parity requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards > Collateral
source_reference.source_text: When loan proceeds will be used to refinance existing debt, except for trading assets, the loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt that is being refinanced. When the debt being refinanced is considered to be over collateralized based upon SBA collateral requirements and the SBA loan will remain fully secured, the Lender may approve the release of excess collateral. Substitute collateral may be offered providing it is of comparable value and useful life...

examples: Refinancing a conventional term loan secured by a first lien on business real estate with an SBA 7(a): the SBA 7(a) must retain at least that first-lien real estate collateral position (unless fully secured with permitted substitution/release).
Refinancing equipment debt secured by a UCC lien on equipment: the SBA refinance must take at least the same equipment collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt.
Refinancing an A/R revolver secured by inventory and receivables (trading assets): the refinance-collateral parity rule does not apply to the trading asset portion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) fully secured collateral test uses SBA valuation haircuts and limits trading assets to 10% of book value
business_rules.description: SBA considers a Standard 7(a) loan “fully secured” when the lender has taken security interests in all available fixed assets of the applicant (real estate, machinery and equipment owned by the business or an EPC) with combined value up to the loan amount, using SBA-prescribed valuation haircuts and net of prior liens. Valuation caps include: new machinery/equipment (excluding furniture/fixtures) valued at no more than 75% of price minus prior liens; used/existing machinery/equipment valued at no more than 50% of net book value, or 80% with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal, minus prior liens; improved real estate valued at no more than 85% of market value; unimproved real estate valued at no more than 50% of market value; furniture/fixtures valued at no more than 10% of net book value or appraised value. If the lender takes a security interest in trading assets, no more than 10% of current book value may be used in the fully secured calculation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Standard 7(a) loans > $350,000 where the lender evaluates whether the loan is “fully secured” under SBA collateral requirements and determines collateral coverage using fixed assets and, if pledged, trading assets.
business_rules.consequences: If valuation haircuts/caps are not applied, the lender may incorrectly conclude the loan is fully secured, leading to noncompliance with SBA collateral requirements and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial in a review.
business_rules.exceptions: Trading assets may be left unpledged at lender discretion; if pledged, only 10% of current book value counts toward fully secured. Vehicle lien requirements are addressed separately within SBA collateral requirements (see embedded threshold in SBA guidance).

source_reference.section_id: Collateral Requirements for Standard 7(a) Loans
source_reference.source_text: SBA considers a loan as “fully secured” if the Lender has taken security interests in all available fixed assets... New machinery and equipment... may be valued at no more than 75% of price minus any prior liens... Used or existing machinery and equipment... 50% of Net Book Value or 80% with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal... Improved real estate... no more than 85%... unimproved... 50% of the market value... Furniture and Fixtures... no more than 10%... If the Lender decides to take a security interest in trading assets... no more than 10% of current book value may be used...

examples: A borrower buys new equipment for $200,000 with no prior lien: for fully secured calculation, lender may count up to $150,000 (75% of price).
Used equipment with net book value $100,000 and an OLA supports $120,000: lender may count up to $96,000 (80% of OLA) less any prior liens; without OLA, only $50,000 (50% of NBV).
Borrower pledges A/R and inventory with current book value $500,000: lender may count only $50,000 (10%) toward fully secured.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) vehicle liens required only if vehicle value exceeds $20,000 at SBA loan number assignment and vehicle is not already liened
business_rules.description: For Standard 7(a) collateral requirements, SBA does not require the lender to place a lien on vehicles that already have a lien. SBA also does not require a lien on a vehicle unless the vehicle value exceeds $20,000 at the time the SBA loan number is assigned by SBA. Vehicle value must be supported by an independent third party (e.g., appraisal OLV, independent vehicle valuation company/website) or the purchase price allocable to the vehicle if the 7(a) loan is used to purchase the vehicle. The lender must document whether a vehicle already had a lien, or the source and dollar amount of the vehicle valuation in the credit memorandum.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Standard 7(a) loans where the applicant owns vehicles (business or EPC) and the lender is determining required collateral liens at the time the SBA loan number is assigned.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to lien a required vehicle (> $20,000 and no existing lien) or failure to document lien/valuation source may create SBA collateral noncompliance and jeopardize SBA guaranty enforceability in review/purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: No SBA-required lien when the vehicle already has a lien, or when vehicle value is $20,000 or less at SBA loan number assignment.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral Requirements for Standard 7(a) Loans (vehicles)
source_reference.source_text: SBA does not require the Lender to place a lien on vehicles that already have a lien or unless the value of the vehicle... is greater than $20,000 at the time the SBA loan number is assigned by SBA. For the purpose of determining whether the Lender must place a lien on a vehicle, Lender must document whether the vehicle already had a lien on it, or the source and dollar amount of the vehicle valuation in the credit memorandum.

examples: Borrower owns a truck valued at $18,500 (per NADA) and no lien: SBA does not require the lender to take a lien based solely on this vehicle.
Borrower owns a van valued at $27,000 with no lien at SBA loan number assignment: lender must take a lien and document the valuation source and amount.
Borrower owns a vehicle worth $45,000 but it is already subject to a prior lender lien: SBA does not require an additional lien solely due to value.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) collateral shortfall requires liens on solely owned personal real estate of co-borrowers, owners of 20% or more, and guarantors with limits to shortfall and 150% of equity
business_rules.description: If an SBA-guaranteed 7(a) loan is not “fully secured” (collateral shortfall), the lender must take available equity in personal real estate (residential and investment property, including other commercial real estate) that is solely owned by any co-borrowers, direct and/or indirect owners of 20% or more of the applicant, and guarantors (except Supplemental Guarantors). Liens on personal real estate may be limited to the amount of the collateral shortfall. In addition, liens on personal real estate may be limited to 150% of the equity in the collateral. Trading assets may be included as necessary (counting no more than 10% of current book value for fully secured calculations).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Standard 7(a) loans where the lender determines there is a collateral shortfall under SBA’s “fully secured” definition.
business_rules.consequences: If required personal real estate liens are not taken (or are not properly limited/documented), SBA collateral requirements are not met, increasing risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial and findings in SBA oversight.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to Supplemental Guarantors. Liens may be limited to (i) the collateral shortfall amount and (ii) 150% of equity in the personal real estate collateral (as permitted). Additional exception for properties with <25% equity is addressed in a separate rule.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral shortfall
source_reference.source_text: Collateral shortfall: If there is a collateral shortfall (not “fully secured”) on the SBA-guaranteed loan the Lender: Must take available equity in the personal real estate... that is solely owned by any Co-Borrowers, direct and/or indirect owners of 20% or more of the Applicant and guarantors except Supplemental Guarantors. Liens on personal real estate may be limited to the amount of the collateral shortfall. In addition, liens on personal real estate may be limited to 150% of the equity in the collateral.

examples: A Standard 7(a) loan has a $250,000 collateral shortfall after SBA valuation haircuts: lender must take liens on solely owned personal investment real estate of a 30% owner (if available equity exists), but may cap the lien to $250,000.
A guarantor (not a Supplemental Guarantor) solely owns a residence with $100,000 equity: lender may limit the lien amount to up to $150,000 (150% of equity) if needed, subject to shortfall cap.
A 10% owner solely owns a rental property: SBA shortfall lien requirement does not apply to that owner because the threshold is 20% or more (unless the person is a co-borrower or guarantor).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) does not require real estate collateralization when equity is less than 25% of fair market value with third-party documentation
business_rules.description: SBA does not require a lender to collateralize a Standard 7(a) loan with real estate (commercial, residential, or investment properties owned by the applicant or personally by the owners) to meet the “fully secured” definition when the equity in the real estate is less than 25% of the property’s fair market value. The lender must document in the loan file the source (other than the personal financial statement) used to determine that equity is less than 25%.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating whether to take real estate collateral to satisfy SBA’s “fully secured” definition and the lender determines the real estate equity is <25% of FMV.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender does not document the third-party/source evidence for <25% equity, SBA may find collateral noncompliance in review, risking SBA guaranty repair/denial. If equity is ≥25%, the lender must follow SBA collateral shortfall/liens rules as applicable.
business_rules.exceptions: Only applies when equity is <25% of FMV and the lender documents the source other than the PFS. Does not waive other collateral requirements for available fixed assets/personal real estate when equity thresholds are met.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral shortfall (25% equity exception)
source_reference.source_text: SBA does not require a Lender to collateralize a loan with real estate... to meet the “fully secured” definition when the equity in the real estate is less than 25% of the property’s fair market value. The Lender must document in their loan file the source (other than the personal financial statement) for making the determination of less than 25% equity.

examples: Owner’s residence FMV is $400,000 with liens totaling $320,000 (equity 20%): lender is not required by SBA to take a lien solely to meet fully secured, but must document the FMV and lien sources outside the PFS.
Investment property FMV is $500,000 with liens $350,000 (equity 30%): the <25% equity exception does not apply; collateralization expectations follow SBA shortfall rules.
Applicant provides a third-party AVM or appraisal and payoff statements supporting equity at 22%: acceptable documentation source (not just the PFS).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial due to file documentation deficiency; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental indemnification provisions running with the land are ineligible SBA 7(a) collateral conditions and must be removed or waived for the Federal Government
business_rules.description: When assessing collateral for SBA 7(a) loans, environmental indemnification provisions that run with the land are not eligible and must be removed or waived as to the Federal Government. Lenders must also consider and document how deed restrictions, covenants, easements, and engineering controls may affect collateral value/marketability.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 7(a) collateral includes real estate and recorded restrictions/indemnities are present or identified during due diligence.
business_rules.consequences: If an environmental indemnification provision running with the land is not removed or waived for the Federal Government, the collateral condition is ineligible under SBA requirements, increasing risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial and legal enforceability issues.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for environmental indemnification provisions running with the land. Certain deed restrictions intended to protect health and safety may be acceptable (e.g., restrictions based on environmental concerns limiting residential/day care/school/hospital use), but the ineligible status specifically applies to environmental indemnification provisions running with the land.

source_reference.section_id: Adequacy of Collateral (restrictions affecting value/marketability)
source_reference.source_text: When assessing the adequacy of collateral, the Lender must consider the impact that covenants and other restrictions recorded against the collateral may have on its value and marketability... Environmental Indemnification Provisions that run with the land are not eligible and must be removed or waived as to the Federal Government.

examples: A property deed includes an environmental indemnity covenant binding future owners and benefiting a private third party: lender must require removal or waiver as to the Federal Government before closing/funding to avoid ineligible collateral condition.
A deed restriction prohibits residential use due to prior contamination (health/safety related): may be acceptable, but lender must still analyze and document value/marketability impact.
An engineering control requires installation of extraction wells before improvements: lender must document impact on marketability/value and address underwriting/collateral adequacy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; legal enforceability/marketability risk; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership financing requires the small business to be borrower or co-borrower and cannot be made solely to an individual
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) change of ownership transactions, an SBA-guaranteed loan cannot be made solely to an individual. The small business must be either the borrower or a co-borrower. The change of ownership must promote the sound development and/or preserve the existence of a small business.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan proceeds are used for change of ownership (stock purchase/redemption or asset purchase of all or substantially all assets while continuing operations).
business_rules.consequences: If structured as a loan solely to an individual without the small business as borrower/co-borrower, the use of proceeds/structure is not permitted for SBA guaranty, risking SBA denial of guaranty or repair/denial upon purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the prohibition on loans made solely to an individual; additional allowed structures and co-borrower requirements vary by transaction type and are addressed elsewhere in the SBA guidance.

source_reference.section_id: Change of Ownership (general requirements)
source_reference.source_text: The following requirements apply: The change of ownership must promote the sound development and/or preserve the existence of a small business; An SBA-guaranteed loan cannot be made solely to an individual. The small business must be either the Borrower or a Co-Borrower;

examples: An individual seeks an SBA 7(a) loan in their personal name to buy a business’s stock: prohibited unless the small business is borrower or co-borrower per SBA change of ownership rules.
An acquiring operating company (small business) is borrower for a 100% stock purchase: permitted structure (subject to other SBA requirements).
A personal holding company borrower with no operating small business as borrower/co-borrower: not permitted for change of ownership financing under this rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of guaranty approval or guaranty repair/denial upon purchase; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership proceeds capped at business valuation and any financed shortfall must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) change of ownership, the maximum 7(a) use of proceeds is capped at the business valuation amount. If the business valuation is lower than the sales agreement, any financed capital required to meet the shortfall (in addition to the 7(a) loan and any equity injection) must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 7(a) proceeds are used to finance a change of ownership via stock purchase/redemption or asset purchase meeting SBA definition.
business_rules.consequences: If 7(a) proceeds exceed business valuation or if additional financed capital is not subordinate when valuation is below purchase price, the transaction violates SBA requirements and may result in SBA guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the valuation cap and subordination requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Change of Ownership (valuation cap/subordination)
source_reference.source_text: The maximum 7(a) loan uses of proceeds for any change of ownership is capped at the business valuation amount. When the business valuation is lower than the sales agreement, any financed capital required to meet the shortfall (in addition to the 7(a) loan and any equity injection) must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan.

examples: Purchase price is $1,200,000 but business valuation is $1,000,000: 7(a) proceeds for the acquisition cannot exceed $1,000,000; any additional financed amount to bridge $200,000 must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan.
Purchase price equals valuation at $850,000: 7(a) proceeds may finance up to $850,000 (subject to other SBA limits) without requiring separate subordination for a valuation gap.
Valuation is $900,000 and sale agreement is $950,000 with a seller note for $50,000: the seller note must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan (in addition to any equity injection).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership prohibits seller earnouts and limits seller consulting to 12 months unless partial sale or ESOP or cooperative buys controlling interest of 51% or more
business_rules.description: In SBA 7(a) change of ownership transactions, seller earnouts are prohibited. Except as provided, the seller may not remain as an officer, director, stockholder, or employee of the business. If a short transitional period is needed, the small business may contract with the seller as a consultant for a period not to exceed 12 months (including any extensions). The seller may remain as an owner/officer/director/stockholder/employee when (i) one or more current owners sells less than their entire ownership (partial change of ownership), or (ii) the purchaser is an ESOP/equivalent trust or a cooperative acquiring a controlling interest (51% or more), including 100% acquisitions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 7(a) proceeds are used for change of ownership financing.
business_rules.consequences: If a prohibited seller earnout is included or the seller remains in a prohibited role beyond the permitted transition/exception, SBA may deem the transaction noncompliant, creating risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial and required restructuring.
business_rules.exceptions: Seller consulting is allowed for a transitional period not exceeding 12 months including extensions. Seller may remain in roles for partial change of ownership, or where ESOP/equivalent trust/cooperative acquires controlling interest (51% or more). Buyer rebates based on performance are permitted (distinct from prohibited earnouts) as they benefit the borrower.

source_reference.section_id: Change of Ownership (earnouts; seller role restrictions)
source_reference.source_text: Seller earnouts/buyer rebates: Seller earnouts are prohibited... Except as provided below, the seller may not remain as an officer, director, stockholder, or employee of the business. If a short transitional period is needed... contract with the seller as a consultant for a period not to exceed 12 months including any extensions... The seller may stay on... when: One or more of the current owners is selling less than their entire percentage...; or The purchaser is an ESOP... or a cooperative... acquiring a controlling interest (51% or more)...

examples: A purchase agreement includes an earnout payable to the seller only if revenue targets are met: prohibited earnout under SBA change of ownership rules.
Seller stays on as a consultant to train the new owner for 9 months after closing: permitted transitional consulting (≤12 months).
An ESOP purchases 60% of the employer business and the seller remains as an employee: permitted because the purchaser is an ESOP acquiring controlling interest (≥51%).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender review finding; required transaction restructuring

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express maximum loan amount $500,000 and guaranty percentage 90% up to $350,000 and 75% above $350,000 with combined-loan 90-day limitation
business_rules.description: Export Express maximum gross loan amount is $500,000. Maximum guaranty percentage is 90% for loans of $350,000 or less and 75% for loans over $350,000 up to $500,000. Multiple Export Express loans are allowed up to the $500,000 program maximum (gross), but the guaranteed amount of all Export Express loans counts toward the $3,750,000 maximum SBA exposure (guaranty outstanding) for all SBA loans to a borrower and its affiliates at any one time. If multiple Export Express loans are approved within 90 days of each other and the combined gross amount of those Export Express loans to one borrower (including affiliates) exceeds $350,000, then the maximum guaranty percentage on the second loan must be reduced so the combined guaranty is no more than 75% (and still subject to the $3,750,000 maximum guaranty amount limit). When determining maximum guaranty exposure and percentages, the lender must include the approved loan amount for a revolving line of credit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Export Express loans and lines of credit; includes evaluation of affiliates under 13 CFR § 120.151 and SBA maximum guaranty exposure limits.
business_rules.consequences: If lender exceeds the $500,000 Export Express gross maximum, applies incorrect guaranty percentage thresholds, fails to reduce guaranty on second loan in 90-day combined-loan scenario, or exceeds $3,750,000 SBA guaranty exposure to one business (including affiliates), SBA guaranty may be limited/denied and lender may face SBA review findings and guaranty purchase denials/repairs.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the $500,000 maximum, guaranty percentage thresholds, the 90-day combined-loan limitation, or the $3,750,000 maximum SBA guaranty exposure. SBA treats an EPC and an OC as “one business” for the $3,750,000 maximum guaranty exposure.

source_reference.section_id: Loan Terms and Conditions for Export Express > Maximum Loan Amount; Maximum Guaranty Amounts and Percentages
source_reference.source_text: The maximum loan amount is $500,000 (gross)... The maximum dollar amount outstanding of SBA’s guaranty to any one business (including affiliates) must not exceed $3,750,000... If multiple Export Express loans are approved within 90 days of each other, and the combined gross loan amount... exceeds $350,000, then the maximum guaranty percentage on the second loan must be reduced... no more than 75%... The maximum guaranty percentage is: 90% for loans of $350,000 or less; and 75% for loans over $350,000 up to $500,000.

examples: A borrower requests a $300,000 Export Express term loan: maximum guaranty is 90% (subject to overall SBA exposure limits including affiliates).

A lender approves two Export Express loans within 60 days: $250,000 then $200,000 (combined $450,000 > $350,000). The second loan’s guaranty percentage must be reduced so the combined guaranty is no more than 75%.

A borrower group (including affiliates) already has $3,700,000 of SBA-guaranteed exposure outstanding. A new Export Express request would increase SBA guaranty above $3,750,000: not permitted without reducing guaranteed exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty limitation/denial; SBA guaranty repair/denial upon purchase; adverse SBA lender review finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) loans over $500,000 secured by commercial real property require USPAP appraisal dated within 12 months
business_rules.description: For SBA loans greater than $500,000 secured by commercial real property, the lender must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. The appraisal must comply with USPAP and must be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. For federally regulated lenders, SBA provides no exemption under Interagency appraisal/evaluation guidance for transactions insured or guaranteed by a U.S. Government agency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) SBA loan amount > $500,000 and (2) loan is secured by commercial real property.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to obtain a compliant and timely appraisal, the lender risks SBA program non-compliance (which can affect guaranty enforceability and lead to SBA findings/repair/denial in a guaranty purchase context).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for federally regulated lenders; the text explicitly states no exemption is granted under Interagency guidance for such lenders.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements – Commercial Real Estate (i.a)
source_reference.source_text: For all loans greater than $500,000 secured by commercial real property, all Lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. For federally-regulated Lenders, no exemption is granted under the Interagency Guidance Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines for Transactions Insured or Guaranteed by a U.S. Government Agency.

examples: A $750,000 SBA CAPLines loan secured by a warehouse: lender obtains a USPAP appraisal by a state licensed/certified appraiser dated within 12 months of guaranty application.
A $600,000 SBA loan secured by a retail building: lender tries to use only an evaluation; this violates the SBA appraisal requirement.
A federally regulated lender attempts to rely on an Interagency exemption instead of getting an appraisal: SBA states no exemption is granted for this scenario.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program non-compliance; potential repair/denial risk in guaranty purchase; regulatory/SBA criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) loans of $500,000 or less secured by commercial real property require appraisal for related-party transactions otherwise evaluation
business_rules.description: For SBA loans of $500,000 or less secured by commercial real property: (1) if the transaction involves parties with a close relationship (e.g., between existing owners or family members), or if SBA or the lender concludes an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness, the lender must obtain a USPAP appraisal as required for larger loans; otherwise (2) the lender must obtain an appropriate evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices and consistent with Interagency evaluation guidance (USPAP not required and evaluator need not be state licensed/certified).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA loan amount is $500,000 or less and is secured by commercial real property. Appraisal required if close relationship transaction or lender/SBA determines appraisal needed; otherwise evaluation required.
business_rules.consequences: If lender fails to obtain the required appraisal in related-party/necessity scenarios or fails to obtain an appropriate evaluation when appraisal is not required, lender risks SBA program non-compliance and collateral valuation weaknesses.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements – Commercial Real Estate (i.b)
source_reference.source_text: b) For all loans $500,000 or less secured by commercial real property: i) If the loan finances a transaction involving part ies with a close relationship (for example, transactions between existing owners or family members), or if SBA or the Lender otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness, the Lender

examples: A $450,000 SBA loan secured by a small office building where seller and buyer are family members: appraisal required (not just an evaluation).
A $300,000 SBA loan secured by commercial property in an arm’s-length deal: lender obtains an evaluation consistent with interagency guidance (USPAP not required).
A $500,000 SBA loan with unusual property risks: lender determines an appraisal is necessary to evaluate creditworthiness; appraisal is obtained per the SBA standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program non-compliance; credit file documentation criticism; collateral valuation deficiency.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinance without expansion requires qualified debt with at least 75% fixed-asset use and 6-month seasoning and other secured debt must be current for 12 months with no payment over 30 days past due
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 permissible debt refinance without expansion, the refinanced obligation must meet SBA’s “Qualified Debt” and, if included, “Other Secured Debt” requirements. Qualified Debt must be a commercial loan where substantially all (75% or more) of proceeds of the existing debt (or of the original loan if refinanced multiple times) was used to acquire Eligible Fixed Assets and the remaining amount (25% or less) was incurred for the benefit of the small business seeking refinancing. The existing debt must be the most recent refinancing of the original loan (if refinanced). Qualified Debt must be incurred not less than 6 months prior to application, incurred for the benefit of the small business seeking refinancing, and secured by Eligible Fixed Assets for at least 6 months. The refinancing may consist of a combination of two or more loans only if each loan satisfies Qualified Debt requirements. If refinancing includes Other Secured Debt, that debt must be secured for at least 6 months prior to application by the same Eligible Fixed Assets securing the Qualified Debt, and the borrower must have been current on all payments due for not less than 12 months preceding application; “current” means no payment was more than 30 days past due under original or modified written terms (including deferments) when the modification was agreed to in writing less than one year prior to the application date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CRE loan is structured as an SBA 504 Refinancing Project that does not involve an expansion under 13 CFR § 120.882(g). Applies at application/eligibility determination and underwriting.
business_rules.consequences: If the debt to be refinanced fails Qualified Debt requirements (e.g., <75% fixed asset use, <6 months seasoning, or insufficient collateral seasoning), the project is not eligible for 504 refinance without expansion and must be declined or restructured. If Other Secured Debt fails the 12-month current requirement, it cannot be included and may render the refinancing project ineligible as structured.
business_rules.exceptions: Qualified Debt may include certain federally-guaranteed debt only under additional conditions described separately (e.g., notice and substantial benefit requirements). Liens on machinery/equipment purchased with original loan proceeds may be waived if adequate collateral protection is demonstrated (addressed in separate SBA guidance within the same section).

source_reference.section_id: Permissible Debt Refinance without Expansion, Definitions of Qualified Debt and Other Secured Debt (13 CFR § 120.882(g))
source_reference.source_text: “Qualified Debt” means a commercial loan that: ... Substantially all (75% or more) of the proceeds of the existing debt was used to acquire an Eligible Fixed Asset(s)... Was incurred not less than 6 months prior to the date of application... Has been secured by Eligible Fixed Asset(s) for at least 6 months... “Other Secured Debt”... must be secured for at least 6 months prior to the date of application by the same Eligible Fixed Asset(s)... The Borrower has been current on all payments due for not less than 12 months... “Current on all payments due” means that no payment was more than 30 days past due

examples: Existing mortgage was used 80% to buy the building and 20% for business benefit, was originated 10 months ago, and has been secured by the building for 10 months; it meets Qualified Debt seasoning and use-of-proceeds tests.

Borrower wants to refinance a line secured by the same building but had a payment 45 days late within the last 12 months; that other secured debt fails the “current” requirement and cannot be included.
Borrower seeks to refinance two separate loans; one meets the 75% fixed-asset test and one does not (only 60% fixed asset use). The project cannot include the non-qualifying loan as Qualified Debt.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan ineligibility for SBA 504 refinancing; SBA processing rejection or post-closing enforcement/repair

business_rules.rule_name: Refinancing federally guaranteed debt in a 504 refinance without expansion must provide a substantial benefit through lower installment payments and may require 10 business days’ notice to existing SBA lender
business_rules.description: When a 504 refinance without expansion includes refinancing debt subject to a federal guarantee, additional conditions apply. Existing 504 debt may be included only if both the Third Party Loan and the 504 loan are being refinanced, or the Third Party Loan has been paid in full, and the CDC has notified the existing CDC or 7(a) lender in writing (letter or email) no less than 10 business days to advise the existing loan is being refinanced. Refinancing of a federally-guaranteed loan must provide a substantial benefit to the borrower after Eligible Business Expenses, prepayment penalties, financing fees, and other financing costs are accounted for; ‘substantial benefit’ means the portion of the new installment amount attributable to the refinanced debt must be less than the existing installment amount(s). For the percentage reduction calculation, prepayment penalties, financing fees, and other financing costs must be added to the amount being refinanced, but Eligible Business Expenses must not be added. The CDC may request the D/FA (or designee) to approve an exception to the reduction requirement for good cause; PCLP CDCs may not use delegated authority to approve a loan requiring this exception. If the refinanced loan is guaranteed by a Federal agency other than SBA, the CDC must document in writing that the refinancing is permissible under the other agency’s requirements or otherwise approved by the other agency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinance without expansion where any portion of the existing debt to be refinanced is federally guaranteed (including SBA 504/7(a) or other federal agency guarantee).
business_rules.consequences: If the refinance does not reduce the installment amount attributable to the refinanced debt (as defined by SBA calculation rules) and no approved exception exists, the refinancing is not permissible under SBA rules and must be declined/restructured. Failure to provide the required 10-business-day written notice for existing SBA loans is noncompliance. Missing documentation regarding another agency’s permissibility/approval is a file/eligibility defect.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception to installment reduction requirement may be approved by D/FA or designee for good cause upon CDC request; PCLP CDCs cannot approve the exception under delegated authority.

source_reference.section_id: Qualified Debt may include federally guaranteed debt; Substantial benefit and 10 business day notice requirements
source_reference.source_text: May include debt subject to a federal guarantee... The CDC has notified the existing CDC or 7(a) Lender in writing by letter or email no less than 10 business days to advise them the existing loan is being refinanced. The refinancing of a Federally-guaranteed loan provided a substantial benefit... “Substantial benefit” will mean that the portion of the new installment amount attributable to the debt being refinanced must be less than the existing installment amount(s). In calculating the percentage reduction... prepayment penalties, financing fees, and other financing costs, must be added... but not Eligible Business Expenses.

examples: Refinancing an existing SBA-guaranteed loan: new installment attributable to the refinanced debt is $9,500/month vs. current $10,000/month (after including fees/penalties in the refinanced amount calculation); meets substantial benefit test.
CDC plans to refinance an existing 7(a) loan but sends notice to the existing lender only 5 business days before submission; fails the minimum 10 business day notice requirement.
Borrower’s new payment would be higher than existing after fees/penalties are included in the refinanced amount; CDC must seek D/FA exception for good cause or the project is not permissible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection; SBA post-closing review findings; potential ineligibility/repair impacting SBA support

business_rules.rule_name: Eligible business expenses in a 504 refinance without expansion must be operating expenses due within 18 months and must be specifically described and itemized in the application and CDC file
business_rules.description: If a borrower requests that an SBA 504 refinance without expansion include Eligible Business Expenses (EBE), EBE is limited to operating expenses incurred but unpaid prior to the 504 application date or that will become due within 18 months after the application date (e.g., salaries, rent, utilities, inventory; not capital expenditures). Business lines of credit and business credit cards may be included as EBE only if (i) loan proceeds are not used to cover personal expenses, (ii) if personal expenses occurred, the applicant identifies and deducts personal purchases from the EBE amount, (iii) the line/credit card is in the name of the small business, and (iv) the applicant and CDC certify in the loan application that the refinanced debt was incurred exclusively for EBE. The application must include a specific description and an itemization of each expense amount. The CDC must retain documentation showing EBE itemization (gross figure not acceptable), and the CDC credit memorandum must document the nature of the EBE, provide the itemization, and include CDC certification that the EBE are eligible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinance without expansion applications where EBE proceeds are included as part of the Refinancing Project.
business_rules.consequences: If EBE is not limited to eligible operating expenses within the stated timing window, or if required itemization/certification is missing, the EBE portion is ineligible and must be removed or the loan declined/restructured. Lack of required documentation in the CDC file creates SBA compliance deficiencies and can lead to post-review findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the timing and itemization requirements; personal-expense amounts must be deducted if present in lines/credit cards.

source_reference.section_id: Eligible Business Expenses definition and documentation retention requirements
source_reference.source_text: “Eligible Business Expenses (EBE)” are payments of the business... limited to the “Operating Expenses”... incurred but not paid prior to the date of the 504 application or that will become due... within 18 months after the date of application... Business Lines of credit and business credit cards may be included, if: Loan proceeds are not used to cover any personal expenses... the Applicant and the CDC certify... exclusively for EBE... If the Borrower is requesting that the refinancing include EBE, the application must include a specific description and an itemization... EBE must be itemized (a gross figure is not acceptable).

examples: Borrower includes $120,000 of unpaid rent and utilities incurred pre-application and $80,000 of inventory payments due within 12 months; provides an itemized schedule and CDC credit memo includes certification—eligible EBE.
Borrower seeks to include a business credit card balance but the statement includes $5,000 personal travel charges; borrower must identify and deduct the $5,000 from EBE refinanced amount.
Borrower requests $200,000 EBE as a single lump sum with no itemization; CDC must require itemization or remove EBE from the refinancing request.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection or post-closing finding requiring corrective action; potential ineligibility/repair for unsupported use of proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: 504 refinance without expansion requires applicant in continuous operation for 2 years and CDC must decline if ownership change causes new business classification
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 refinance without expansion, the Applicant must have been in operation for the entire 2-year period ending on the date the application is submitted, supported by financial statements submitted at application. If the business has operated for more than 2 years but there has been a change of ownership, the CDC must determine (under standards in the SBA ‘New Business’ definition in Appendix 3) whether the Applicant should be considered a New Business; if so, the application must be declined. The CDC must document the justification for its determination in its credit memorandum.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinance without expansion applications at submission and underwriting. Triggered by applicant operating history and ownership change review.
business_rules.consequences: If the applicant has not been in operation for the entire prior 2-year period, the application is not eligible and must be declined. If ownership change results in New Business classification, the CDC must decline. Failure to document the determination and justification is a compliance defect and can result in SBA findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; ownership change requires CDC determination using Appendix 3 standards.

source_reference.section_id: Operating history and ownership change/new business determination requirement
source_reference.source_text: The Applicant must have been in operation for all of the 2-year period ending on the date that the application is submitted, as evidenced by the financial statements submitted at the time of application. If the business has been in operation for more than 2 years at the time of application, and there has been a change of ownership... determine... whether the Applicant should be considered a New Business... and the application declined. The CDC must document the justification... in its credit memorandum.

examples: Company formed 18 months before application; despite strong cash flow, it fails the 2-year continuous operation requirement and must be declined for this 504 refinancing program use.
Business has operated for 10 years but was sold 4 months ago; CDC evaluates under Appendix 3 New Business standards and documents whether to treat it as New Business; if New Business, decline.
Business has operated for 5 years with partial ownership change; CDC determines it does not meet New Business criteria and documents justification in the credit memorandum.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility denial; SBA post-review finding for failure to evidence operating history or document required determination

business_rules.rule_name: Seismic building code compliance evidence required for SBA 504 new construction and building additions
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 projects involving construction of a new building or an addition to an existing building, the CDC must obtain evidence that the project complies with the National Earthquake Hazards Reduction Program (NEHRP) Recommended Provisions or a substantially equivalent building code. Acceptable evidence includes a certificate by a licensed building architect/engineer (or similar professional) or a letter from a state/local government agency confirming that an occupancy permit is required and the underlying code includes seismic standards.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 use of proceeds includes construction of a new building or an addition to an existing building.
business_rules.consequences: If evidence of NEHRP (or equivalent) compliance is not obtained/retained, the project fails a required SBA construction condition, creating eligibility/closing noncompliance risk and potential SBA enforcement (including repair/denial remedies depending on program context).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for new building/addition construction; leasehold-improvement-specific exception is addressed in a separate rule.

source_reference.section_id: Construction Loan Provisions: NEHRP evidence requirement (13 CFR § 120.174)
source_reference.source_text: In the construction of a new building or an addition to an existing building, the CDC must obtain: i. Evidence of compliance with the “National Earthquake Hazards Reduction Program Recommended Provisions for the Development of Seismic Regulations for New Building” (NEHRP), or a building code that has substantially equivalent provisions. (13 CFR § 120.174) ... Examples of evidence include a certificate issued by a licensed building architect, construction engineer or similar professional, or a letter from a state or local government agency stating that an occupancy permit is required and that the local building codes ... include the Seismic standards.

examples: A 504 project constructs a new warehouse: CDC obtains an engineer’s certificate stating the design complies with ASCE Standard 7 seismic provisions and retains it in the file.
A 504 project adds a second story to an existing office building: CDC obtains a city letter confirming the building permit is based on a code with seismic standards.
CDC closes a 504 construction project without any seismic compliance evidence: this violates SBA construction conditions and must be remediated before disbursement/closing can be supported.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA repair/denial of guaranty or servicing/closing findings; regulatory criticism in SBA review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 E-Tran terms must include NEHRP condition for construction proceeds including permanently affixed leasehold improvements
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, the E-Tran Terms and Conditions must address the NEHRP provision whenever any selected use of proceeds includes construction financing (including leasehold improvements). If leasehold improvements funded by loan proceeds will become permanently affixed to any structure on leased premises, they must comply with NEHRP. If improvements are only temporary, NEHRP compliance is not required; if the Applicant demonstrates temporariness, the CDC may request modification of the SBA E-Tran Terms and Conditions to remove the NEHRP provision. The CDC must certify the project was completed in accordance with final plans and specifications unless a minor portion is escrowed for a valid reason.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans where use of proceeds includes construction financing (including leasehold improvements).
business_rules.consequences: If E-Tran Terms and Conditions do not include required NEHRP language when applicable, or if CDC fails to obtain required certification of completion (subject to escrowed minor portion allowance), SBA closing/disbursement conditions are not met and the CDC faces SBA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Temporary leasehold improvements do not need to comply with NEHRP if the Applicant can demonstrate the improvements will be temporary; CDC may request modification to remove NEHRP provision. Certification exception: CDC need not certify full completion only when a minor portion is escrowed for a valid reason.

source_reference.section_id: Construction Loan Provisions: NEHRP in E-Tran T&C; leasehold improvement exception; certification (13 CFR § 120.891)
source_reference.source_text: The E-Tran Terms and Conditions must address the NEHRP provision when any of the use of proceeds options selected includes construction financing, including leasehold improvements. If the leasehold improvements made with loan proceeds will become permanently affixed ... then they must comply with the NEHRP. If the leasehold improvements are only temporary, they do not need to comply ... CDC may request modification ... to remove the NEHRP provision ... The CDC must certify that the Project was completed in accordance with the final plans and specifications unless a minor portion of the project has been escrowed for a valid reason. (13 CFR § 120.891)

examples: Loan funds pay for permanent HVAC and electrical buildout in a leased suite that will be affixed to the structure: E-Tran T&C must include NEHRP condition and CDC must obtain/retain compliance evidence.
Loan funds pay for temporary movable partitions and modular furniture: Applicant documents temporariness; CDC requests E-Tran T&C modification to remove NEHRP provision.
Project is substantially complete but final landscaping is escrowed for a valid reason: CDC may still certify completion subject to the minor escrowed portion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA closing/servicing finding; potential denial/repair of guaranty conditions for noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: Equipment and fixtures collateral list must include serial numbers for items valued at $5,000 or more and be reflected on UCC-1
business_rules.description: If collateral includes equipment and/or fixtures, the CDC must obtain a list of all equipment and fixtures pledged as collateral. For items with a unit value of $5,000 or more, the list must include a description and serial number (if applicable). The list should be included in the collateral description (Item 4, COLLATERAL) on the UCC-1 Financing Statement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 loan collateral includes equipment and/or fixtures.
business_rules.consequences: If the list is missing required fields (description/serial where applicable) or not properly reflected in UCC-1 collateral description, the security interest may be imperfect or inadequately documented, creating SBA compliance and loss-given-default risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Serial number requirement applies only where applicable; items under $5,000 unit value are not required by this paragraph to include serial numbers.

source_reference.section_id: Special Provisions for Personal Property, Equipment and Fixtures as Collateral
source_reference.source_text: If the collateral for the loan includes equipment and/or fixtures: The CDC must obtain a list of all equipment and fixtures that are collateral for the loan. For items with a unit value of $5,000 or more, the list must include a description of the equipment/fixture and serial number, if applicable. The list should be included in the collateral description (Item 4. COLLATERAL) on the UCC-1 Financing Statement.

examples: A 504 project pledges a CNC machine valued at $42,000: the collateral list includes make/model/description and serial number and is incorporated into the UCC-1 Item 4.
Fixtures include restaurant hood systems valued at $9,500 each: list includes descriptions and serial numbers where available, then referenced on the UCC-1.
CDC files a UCC-1 that generically states “all equipment” without the required itemization for ≥$5,000 units: documentation is deficient and must be corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA review finding; collateral documentation deficiency and potential impairment of lien perfection

business_rules.rule_name: USPAP appraisal required for SBA 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property with state-certified appraiser required above $1,000,000 value
business_rules.description: For all SBA Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property, the lender must obtain an appraisal performed by a State-licensed or State-certified appraiser, and the appraisal must comply with USPAP. The appraiser must be independent (no appearance of conflict of interest), independent of the loan production function, and not involved in approving the transaction. If the commercial property's estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified (State-licensed is insufficient). Federally-regulated lenders do not receive an exemption under the Interagency Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines for transactions insured or guaranteed by a U.S. Government agency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (i) the loan is an SBA Standard 7(a) loan and (ii) collateral includes commercial real property and (iii) the lender is originating/closing the loan (including where an appraisal is required for closing).
business_rules.consequences: If an appraisal does not meet these requirements (USPAP compliance, independence, correct license level), the loan file is not compliant with SBA collateral requirements and may be subject to SBA lender oversight findings and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Non-commercial real estate (e.g., residence) or real estate taken solely to secure a personal guaranty has no specific SBA appraisal requirements (covered in a separate SOP section).

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements > Commercial Real Estate
source_reference.source_text: For all Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property, all Lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). ... Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified.

examples: A Standard 7(a) loan secured by a warehouse estimated at $950,000: lender may use a State-licensed appraiser, but appraisal must be USPAP-compliant and independent.
A Standard 7(a) loan secured by an office building estimated at $1.3MM: lender must use a State-certified appraiser (State-licensed does not satisfy SBA requirement).

A federally regulated bank making an SBA-guaranteed CRE loan cannot claim an interagency evaluation exemption for the guaranteed transaction; it must still obtain an appraisal meeting SBA requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CRE appraisal must be dated within 12 months of the SBA guaranty application and lender may not rely on appraisals older than 12 months
business_rules.description: For SBA Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property, completed appraisals must be dated within 12 months of the SBA application for guaranty. A lender may not rely on an appraisal prepared more than 12 months prior to the date of the application under any circumstance (whether obtained during application or at closing).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property when an appraisal is used for underwriting and/or closing.
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal date is more than 12 months prior to the application date, the lender must obtain a new appraisal (or updated appraisal meeting requirements). Relying on a stale appraisal creates SBA compliance defects and potential guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for commercial real estate appraisals; non-commercial real estate may follow different SBA requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Appraisal date requirement)
source_reference.source_text: Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. ... An appraisal may be submitted as part of the loan application ... or as part of the loan closing. In no case may the Lender rely on an appraisal that was prepared more than 12 months prior to the date of the application.

examples: Application date is 2026-06-01 and appraisal date is 2025-05-15 (over 12 months old): lender must not rely on it and must obtain a new appraisal.
Application date is 2026-06-01 and appraisal date is 2025-08-01 (10 months old): appraisal satisfies the 12-month freshness requirement.
Lender ordered an appraisal at closing but it was completed 13 months before the application date: lender may not use it to close.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CRE appraisal must name the lender as client or intended user and lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or applicant
business_rules.description: For SBA Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property, the appraisal must identify the lender as the client and/or an intended user (as defined in USPAP) so that the scope of work is appropriately identified. The lender is prohibited from using an appraisal prepared for the seller or the applicant. The appraisal cost may be passed on to the applicant. Federally-regulated lenders may follow their primary regulator’s FIRREA requirements only to the extent those requirements permit otherwise regarding identification of the client/intended user.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender obtains an appraisal for an SBA Standard 7(a) loan secured by commercial real property.
business_rules.consequences: If an appraisal was prepared for the seller or applicant or fails to appropriately identify the lender as client/intended user (where required), the appraisal is not acceptable for SBA purposes and must be replaced/updated, creating SBA compliance defects and potential guaranty purchase risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Federally-regulated lenders may follow FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise regarding client/intended user identification.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Client/intended user; no seller/applicant appraisal)
source_reference.source_text: In order for the appraiser to identify the scope of work appropriately, the appraisal must identify the Lender as the client and/or an intended user of the appraisal... The Lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the Applicant. The cost may be passed on to the Applicant.

examples: Seller provides an appraisal to the lender: lender cannot use it for SBA purposes; lender must order an appraisal prepared for the lender.
Appraisal engagement letter lists the lender as the client and the SBA as an intended user: acceptable if otherwise compliant.
Borrower orders an appraisal directly and delivers it to lender: lender may not rely on it if it was prepared for the applicant rather than requested by/prepared for the lender.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Construction or substantial renovation SBA CRE appraisals must estimate market value at completion and substantial renovation means rehab costs exceed one-third of purchase price or fair market value
business_rules.description: If an SBA loan will be used to finance new construction or the substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate the market value at completion of construction. 'Substantial' renovation is defined as rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third (33.333...%) of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CRE-secured loans where proceeds finance (i) new construction or (ii) substantial renovation, as defined.
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal does not include an 'at completion' market value estimate when required, the lender must obtain a compliant appraisal; proceeding without meeting the requirement creates SBA collateral/underwriting compliance defects and potential guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Construction/substantial renovation appraisal requirements)
source_reference.source_text: If the loan will be used to finance new construction or the substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate what the market value will be at completion of construction. ("Substantial" means rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of the application.)

examples: Purchase price is $900,000 and rehab budget is $320,000 (> one-third): it is a substantial renovation; appraisal must estimate market value at completion.

FMV at application is $600,000 and rehab costs are $180,000 (= 30%): not substantial under the SBA definition; 'at completion' valuation may not be required by this SBA trigger (subject to other lender/SBA requirements).
New ground-up construction financed with SBA proceeds: appraisal must provide market value at completion, not just current land value.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Post-construction completion statement required for SBA construction or substantial renovation and loan may not close without SBA permission if statement cannot be obtained
business_rules.description: After construction is completed for an SBA-financed new construction or substantial renovation project, the lender must obtain a statement from the appraiser, general contractor, project architect, or construction management firm that the building was built with only minor deviations (if any) from the plans and specifications used for the original estimate of value. If the lender cannot obtain the statement, the lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans used to finance new construction or substantial renovation of an existing building where the appraisal estimated market value at completion based on plans/specifications.
