business_rules.consequences: If the statement is not obtained, lender must either (i) obtain SBA’s prior written permission before closing, or (ii) not close. Failure to comply risks SBA guaranty repair/denial and lender oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited requirement (other remedies for valuation differences may apply in other paragraphs).

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Post-construction statement; closing restriction)
source_reference.source_text: After construction is completed, Lender must obtain a statement from the appraiser, general contractor, project architect, or construction management firm that the building was built with only minor deviations (if any) from the plans and specifications upon which the original estimate of value was based. If the Lender cannot obtain such a statement, then the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission.

examples: Construction finished; GC provides a signed statement confirming only minor deviations from plans: lender may proceed (subject to other closing requirements).
Construction finished; no party will provide the statement: lender must not close unless SBA provides prior written permission.
Construction finished; architect provides statement describing minor deviations that do not materially affect value: requirement satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan may not close; SBA prior written permission required; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CRE loan closing restrictions when appraised value is below estimated value using 90% threshold with PLP and Export Express documentation alternatives
business_rules.description: If an SBA lender uses an estimated value in the credit memorandum and obtains the appraisal at closing, then at closing: (i) if the appraised value is 90% or more of the estimated value, the lender may close but must include a written explanation in the loan file as to why the appraisal is less than the estimated value; (ii) if the appraised value is less than 90% of the estimated value, the lender may not close without SBA’s prior written permission. Exception: PLP lenders may close even if appraised value is less than 90% but must include a written justification in the file, including analysis of why the appraisal is low and steps taken to offset SBA risk (e.g., additional equity/collateral). For Export Express, lenders are permitted to close when appraisal is less than 90% but must include a written justification subject to SBA review at guaranty purchase/oversight.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender requires the appraisal at closing and used an estimated real estate value in the application/credit memorandum as support pending appraisal; applies to SBA loans secured by commercial real estate where these SBA appraisal closing procedures are used.
business_rules.consequences: If appraised value < 90% and lender is not operating under a permitted exception (PLP/Export Express documentation alternative), lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing. Missing required explanation/justification is a file defect and increases SBA guaranty purchase risk.
business_rules.exceptions: PLP lenders and Export Express lenders may close even if appraised value < 90% of estimated value, but must retain the required written justification for SBA review.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Appraisal at closing; 90% rule; PLP exception)
source_reference.source_text: If at time of closing the appraised value: Is 90% or more of the estimated value, the Lender may close the loan but must include a written explanation... Is less than 90% of estimated value, the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission... Exception for PLP Lenders: PLP Lenders are permitted to close a loan when the appraisal is less than 90% of the estimated value but the Lender must include a written justification...

examples: Estimated value in credit memo is $1,000,000 and appraisal at closing is $920,000 (92%): lender may close but must document written explanation for the shortfall.
Estimated value is $1,000,000 and appraisal is $850,000 (85%): non-PLP lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing.
Export Express loan with estimated value $500,000 and appraisal $430,000 (86%): lender may close but must retain a written justification with mitigation steps (e.g., additional equity/collateral).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan may not close without SBA prior written permission (non-excepted lenders); SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA collateral value must exclude contributory value of rental income and exclude intangible assets from the appraisal
business_rules.description: When valuing collateral for SBA CRE-secured lending, the lender must not include (i) the contributory value of any rental income, or (ii) the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal, in determining collateral value for SBA purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property when the lender is valuing collateral using an appraisal.
business_rules.consequences: Including prohibited components (rental income contributory value or intangible asset value) may overstate collateral value and create SBA underwriting/collateral compliance defects, potentially resulting in SBA lender oversight criticism and guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate (Valuation exclusions)
source_reference.source_text: When valuing the collateral, the Lender must not include the contributory value of any rental income or the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal.

examples: Appraisal includes a business enterprise value component for brand/intangibles: lender must exclude the intangible value from SBA collateral value calculations.
Appraisal narrative provides a contributory value attributable to rental income streams: lender must not include that contributory value in SBA collateral valuation.
Lender uses only the permitted real property market value component and excludes intangibles: compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership within 36 months requires compliant real estate appraisal plus appraisal review or senior staff site visit documentation
business_rules.description: For change of ownership transactions where the business was transferred within 36 months prior to the loan application date, SBA requires (i) a business real estate appraisal that meets SBA appraisal requirements, and (ii) either an appraisal review by another appraiser selected directly by the lender or a site visit by a senior member of the lender’s staff. The lender must document the file, including the date of the site visit and a description of items reviewed on site (if the site visit option is used).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loan applications involving changes of ownership when the target business has been transferred within the 36 months preceding the application date and the transaction involves business real estate collateral.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender does not obtain the required appraisal and the review/site visit and documentation, the loan file is noncompliant with SBA change of ownership appraisal controls, increasing SBA oversight and guaranty purchase risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Additional Appraisal Requirements for all Changes of Ownership
source_reference.source_text: For businesses that have been transferred within 36 months prior to the date of the loan application, SBA requires: An appraisal of the business real estate that meets the appraisal requirements above; and Either a “review” of the appraisal by another appraiser selected directly by the Lender or a site visit by a senior member of the Lender’s staff. The Lender must document the file and include the date of the visit and a description of the items reviewed on site.

examples: Business ownership transferred 18 months ago: lender must obtain compliant real estate appraisal plus either a second-appraiser review or a senior staff site visit with written documentation.
Ownership transferred 40 months ago: this specific 36-month SBA trigger does not apply (other appraisal requirements may still apply).
Lender elects site visit instead of appraisal review: loan file must include visit date and description of items reviewed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express collateral is not required at or below $50,000 but standby letter of credit support lines over $50,000 must be at least 25% collateralized
business_rules.description: For Export Express loans of $50,000 or less, lenders are not required to take collateral. For loans over $50,000, the lender must follow its written collateral policies and procedures established for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans, except for Export Express lines of credit over $50,000 used to support the issuance of a standby letter of credit. For those standby letter of credit support lines, the line of credit must have collateral (cash, cash equivalent, or project) that will provide coverage for at least 25% of the issued standby letter of credit amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Export Express loans/lines at origination when determining collateral requirements; special rule applies to Export Express lines of credit over $50,000 used to support a standby letter of credit.
business_rules.consequences: If standby letter of credit support line lacks required minimum 25% collateral coverage, lender must obtain additional eligible collateral or restructure/decline; for >$50,000 non-standby LOC purposes, lender must adhere to its written collateral policies used for similarly-sized non-SBA loans.
business_rules.exceptions: Loans of $50,000 or less do not require collateral; for >$50,000 non-standby-LOC Export Express credit, use lender’s existing written collateral policies and procedures for similarly-sized non-SBA loans.

source_reference.section_id: c. Collateral
source_reference.source_text: For loans of $50,000 or less, Lenders are not required to take collateral. For loans over $50,000, the Lender must follow the written collateral policies and procedures... except for Export Express lines of credit over $50,000 used to support the issuance of a standby letter of credit. The line of credit must have collateral (cash, cash equivalent or project) that will provide coverage for at least 25% of the issued standby letter of credit amount.

examples: Export Express term loan of $45,000—collateral not required by SBA program rule (lender may still take collateral under its own policy if desired).
Export Express standby letter of credit support line of $200,000 must have at least $50,000 of eligible collateral coverage (25%).
Export Express line of credit of $150,000 not tied to a standby letter of credit—must follow lender’s written collateral policy for similarly-sized non-SBA loans.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; SBA guaranty vulnerability if collateral requirement not met for standby LOC support lines

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express lines of credit must be at least 70% for export purposes when used as a general export transaction line
business_rules.description: Export Express loan proceeds must be used for an export development activity. When proceeds are used to obtain a general line of credit for Export Transaction purposes, the borrower may use portions of the line for domestic purposes in the normal course of business, provided that no less than 70% of the line of credit will be used for export purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an Export Express loan is structured as a general line of credit for export transaction purposes and borrower intends some domestic use.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender cannot support that at least 70% of the line will be used for export purposes, the use of proceeds is not eligible for Export Express as described.
business_rules.exceptions: Domestic use is permitted only up to 30% when the facility is a general export transaction line of credit (other eligible uses may have different constraints).

source_reference.section_id: Eligibility and Eligible Uses of Proceeds for Export Express
source_reference.source_text: Obtaining a general line of credit for Export Transaction purposes (as a normal course of business, the Borrower may use portions of the line of credit for domestic purposes, as long as no less than 70% of the line of credit will be used for export purposes);

examples: Borrower requests $500,000 Export Express revolver and projects $375,000 for export orders and $125,000 domestic (75% export)—eligible under 70% rule.
Borrower expects only 60% of revolver usage to support exports—fails the 'no less than 70%' requirement for a general export transaction line.
Borrower uses the revolver primarily for domestic working capital with occasional export shipments—cannot be justified as a general export transaction line under this provision.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility/use-of-proceeds violation; SBA guaranty repair/denial risk in review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express collateral is not required at or below 50,000 dollars and is policy-driven above 50,000 dollars
business_rules.description: For SBA Express loans of $50,000 or less, the Lender is not required to take collateral. For SBA Express loans over $50,000, the Lender must, to the maximum extent practicable, follow its written collateral policies and procedures for similarly-sized, non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans, using commercially reasonable and prudent practices to identify collateral consistent with those procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Express loans (7(a)) at origination/closing. Triggered by requested/approved loan amount threshold at $50,000.
business_rules.consequences: If loan amount is ≤ $50,000, collateral is optional and cannot be required solely due to SBA program minimums. If loan amount is > $50,000, collateral selection and lien-taking must follow lender’s written non-SBA collateral procedures to the maximum extent practicable; failure may result in SBA compliance findings and potential jeopardy to SBA guaranty enforceability in a repair/denial scenario.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the $50,000 threshold; lender may still take collateral on loans ≤ $50,000 based on prudent practices and credit decisioning.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral for SBA Express Loans
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must use commercially reasonable and prudent practices to identify collateral that conforms to procedures at least as thorough as those used for their similarly-sized, non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. For loans of $50,000 or less, Lenders are not required to take collateral. For loans over $50,000, the Lender must, to the maximum extent practicable, follow the written collateral policies and procedures that it has established and implemented for its similarly-sized, non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans.

examples: SBA Express loan request is $35,000 for working capital: lender may close without taking any collateral and remains compliant with SBA Express minimum collateral rules.

SBA Express loan request is $75,000 secured by business assets: lender must follow its standard collateral policies for similarly-sized non-SBA commercial loans (e.g., take UCC on business assets if that is standard).
SBA Express loan request is $200,000: lender must document collateral decisions consistent with its non-SBA procedures; skipping required lien steps without justification is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and SBA review findings for failure to follow required collateral practices

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express piggyback structures are prohibited and are defined by approval within 90 days with SBA loan in junior or no-lien position
business_rules.description: A lender may not take actions that establish a preference in favor of the lender in connection with an SBA-guaranteed loan and must not structure a 7(a) loan as a piggyback. Piggyback financing occurs when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single borrower at or about the same time (SBA defines as approved within 90 days of each other), financing the same or similar purpose, where the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured with a junior lien position or no lien position on the collateral securing the non-guaranteed loan(s).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) / SBA Express when borrower has multiple loans approved within 90 days for same/similar purpose and collateral overlap exists with SBA loan taking junior or no lien while non-SBA loan takes senior lien.
business_rules.consequences: If identified as a piggyback structure, the SBA-guaranteed loan structure is impermissible and must be restructured (e.g., adjust lien positions, combine facilities, or alter terms) before closing; noncompliance can create SBA guaranty enforcement risk and program violations.
business_rules.exceptions: Not considered piggyback when: (1) both SBA-guaranteed and non-SBA-guaranteed loans are for working capital and the non-SBA loan is secured only by working/trading assets; or (2) the lender shares a lien position pari passu and the non-SBA loan maturity is not shorter than the SBA-guaranteed loan maturity.

source_reference.section_id: Adequacy of Collateral – Piggyback structure prohibition
source_reference.source_text: have a 7(a) loan in a “piggyback” structure. Piggyback financing occurs when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single Borrower at or about the same time, financing the same or similar purpose, and where the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured

examples: Borrower gets a $500,000 conventional loan secured by first lien on a building and, within 60 days, an SBA Express loan for the same project secured by a junior lien on the same building: this is a prohibited piggyback structure.

Borrower gets an SBA Express working capital loan secured by UCC on business assets and a separate non-SBA working capital line secured only by inventory/receivables: SBA states this is not piggyback.

Two lenders fund same-purpose loans approved 45 days apart with pari passu liens on the same collateral, and the non-SBA maturity is equal to or longer than the SBA maturity: not considered piggyback per the SOP.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and program violation findings

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loan change of ownership is ineligible for partner buyouts and partial acquisitions under 100%
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans used to finance a change of ownership, the following transactions are not eligible: (1) a change of ownership between existing owners of the Applicant (partner buyout), and (2) a change of ownership where the Applicant is purchasing less than 100% of the ownership of a business.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the SBA 7(a) International Trade loan use of proceeds includes financing a change of ownership.
business_rules.consequences: Loan cannot be processed/financed as an International Trade loan. If originated as IT despite ineligibility, SBA guaranty eligibility is at risk (e.g., denial at purchase and/or lender oversight findings).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: 7(a) Export Trade Finance: International Trade (Effective June 1, 2025) — change of ownership eligibility
source_reference.source_text: The following changes of ownership are not eligible for financing as an International Trade loan: A change of ownership between existing owners of the Applicant, and a change of ownership where the Applicant is purchasing less than 100% of the ownership of a business.

examples: Applicant has two 50/50 owners; one owner wants to buy out the other using an SBA IT loan. This is a change of ownership between existing owners and is not eligible as an IT loan.
Applicant proposes purchasing 80% of a target company with an SBA IT loan while the seller retains 20%. Because less than 100% is being purchased, it is not eligible as an IT loan.
Applicant proposes acquiring 100% of a business (asset or stock purchase) with IT loan proceeds; this rule does not bar eligibility based on purchase percentage.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty ineligibility/denial; regulatory criticism in lender oversight review

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loan change of ownership proceeds capped at business valuation and any shortfall financing must be subordinate
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade loans financing a change of ownership, the maximum 7(a) use of proceeds for the change of ownership is capped at the business valuation amount. If the business valuation is lower than the sales agreement, any financed capital required to meet the shortfall (in addition to the 7(a) loan and any equity injection) must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when IT loan proceeds are used for a change of ownership and purchase agreement price exceeds the business valuation amount.
business_rules.consequences: Loan structure must be adjusted so that (a) 7(a) proceeds do not exceed the valuation amount and (b) any additional financed funds are documented as subordinate to the 7(a) loan; otherwise IT financing is not compliant with SBA program requirements and guaranty eligibility is at risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: 7(a) Export Trade Finance: International Trade (Effective June 1, 2025) — change of ownership proceeds limitations
source_reference.source_text: The maximum 7(a) loan uses of proceeds for any change of ownership is capped at the business valuation amount. When the business valuation is lower than the sales agreement, any financed capital required to meet the shortfall (in addition to the 7(a) loan and any equity injection) must be subordinate to the 7(a) loan.

examples: Sales agreement is $1,200,000 but valuation is $1,000,000. The 7(a) change-of-ownership proceeds may not exceed $1,000,000; any financed $200,000 shortfall must be subordinated to the 7(a) loan.

Sales agreement equals valuation at $850,000. The 7(a) change-of-ownership proceeds may be up to $850,000 (subject to other SBA limits); no subordination requirement is triggered by a valuation shortfall.

Sales agreement is $1,000,000, valuation is $900,000, borrower contributes $150,000 equity, and seeks additional $50,000 seller note. The 7(a) portion must be capped at $900,000 for the change of ownership, and the $50,000 financed shortfall (seller note) must be subordinated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty ineligibility/repair or denial at purchase; lender oversight finding

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade change of ownership prohibits seller earnouts, restricts seller involvement, and limits transitional consulting to 12 months
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade loans financing a change of ownership: (1) seller earnouts are prohibited; (2) buyer rebates based on business performance are allowed, but if the borrower receives rebate funds, they should first be applied to pay down the 7(a) loan to a point that will not trigger a subsidy recoupment fee, with remaining funds allowed for business purposes; (3) the seller may not remain as an officer, director, stockholder, or employee of the Applicant; if a short transitional period is needed, the small business may contract with the seller as a consultant for a period not to exceed 12 months including any extensions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to IT loans where proceeds finance a change of ownership and the purchase agreement/transaction includes seller contingent compensation, seller continuing role, or consulting services.
business_rules.consequences: Transaction terms must be structured to eliminate prohibited earnouts and prohibited seller roles; consulting arrangements (if used) must be time-limited to 12 months (including extensions). Noncompliance risks IT loan eligibility and SBA guaranty enforceability at purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Buyer rebates are allowed as described; consulting is permitted only as a consultant (not officer/director/stockholder/employee) and only up to 12 months including extensions.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — seller earnouts/rebates and seller continuing involvement
source_reference.source_text: Seller earnouts are prohibited; however, buyer rebates based on business performance are allowed... it should first be applied to pay down the 7(a) loan to a point that will not trigger a subsidy recoupment fee... The seller may not remain as an officer, director, stockholder, or employee of the Applicant. If a short transitional period is needed, the small business may contract with the seller as a consultant for a period not to exceed 12 months including any extensions.

examples: Purchase agreement includes an earnout payable to the seller based on revenue targets. This is prohibited and must be removed for IT eligibility.

Seller will stay on as 'Operations Director' for 6 months. This is not allowed; instead, the seller may only be retained as a consultant and the consulting term (including extensions) must be 12 months or less.

Deal provides a performance-based buyer rebate payable back to the borrower after closing. Rebate funds received should be applied first to pay down the 7(a) loan to avoid triggering a subsidy recoupment fee; any remaining funds may be used for business purposes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty ineligibility/denial; lender oversight criticism

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loan maximum loan amount is $5,000,000 and maximum SBA guaranty is 90% up to $4,500,000 with $4,000,000 working capital guaranty cap
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans: (1) the maximum loan amount is $5,000,000; (2) the maximum guaranty amount is $4,500,000 and IT loans may receive a maximum guaranty of 90% or $4,500,000; and (3) the amount guaranteed for working capital for the IT loan combined with any other outstanding 7(a) loan for working capital cannot exceed $4,000,000. When calculating the maximum guaranty percentage available to a borrower and its affiliates, the lender must include the approved loan amount for a revolving line of credit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loans, including when the borrower has affiliates and/or other outstanding 7(a) working capital loans.
business_rules.consequences: If the requested structure exceeds these caps, the lender must reduce the loan amount/guaranty structure, reallocate uses of proceeds, or otherwise restructure to comply; otherwise SBA guaranty eligibility is at risk.
business_rules.exceptions: These IT rules are stated as exceptions to the general $3,750,000 SBA guaranty cap for most other 7(a) loans (not applicable here because the IT loan is within the exception).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — Maximum Loan Amount and Maximum Guaranty
source_reference.source_text: The maximum loan amount is $5,000,000... both International Trade and EWCP loans permit a maximum dollar amount outstanding of SBA’s guaranty of $4,500,000... IT loans may receive a maximum guaranty of 90 percent or $4,500,000... The amount guaranteed for working capital for the IT loan combined with any other outstanding 7(a) loan for working capital cannot exceed $4,000,000.

examples: IT loan request is $5,200,000. This exceeds the $5,000,000 maximum loan amount and must be reduced to $5,000,000 or less.
Borrower requests a $5,000,000 IT loan at 90% guaranty ($4,500,000). This meets both the 90% and $4,500,000 guaranty cap.

Borrower already has a 7(a) working capital loan with $3,500,000 guaranteed outstanding. Any additional working-capital guaranteed amount under the IT loan may not cause combined working-capital guaranteed amount to exceed $4,000,000 (i.e., only $500,000 additional guaranteed working capital capacity remains).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair at purchase; supervisory criticism

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) loan term must be the shortest appropriate term and working capital, inventory, and intangible asset financing must not exceed 10 years
business_rules.description: SBA 7(a) loan maturities must be the shortest appropriate term based on the borrower’s ability to repay. Working capital or inventory loans and the financing of intangible assets (including goodwill) must not exceed 10 years. Generally, equipment, fixtures, or furniture loans should not exceed 10 years; however, the term may be up to 15 years if the IRS asset class useful life supports the term. The term for a loan to finance equipment and/or leasehold improvements may include an additional reasonable period, not to exceed 12 months, when necessary to complete installation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans, including International Trade 7(a) loans, when setting maturity by use of proceeds category (working capital/inventory/intangibles/equipment/leasehold improvements).
business_rules.consequences: Loan maturity must be set within these constraints; if proposed maturity exceeds the applicable cap, lender must shorten maturity or restructure components; otherwise SBA eligibility/guaranty enforceability risk exists.
business_rules.exceptions: Equipment terms may extend up to 15 years only if supported by IRS asset class useful life; installation/leasehold improvement add-on period must not exceed 12 months.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — Loan Maturities (13 CFR § 120.212)
source_reference.source_text: an SBA 7(a) loan may be guaranteed at zero percent. c. Loan Maturities 13 CFR § 120.212 A loan’s term must be: i. The shortest appropriate term, depending upon the Borrower's ability to repay; ii. Working capital or inventory loans and the financing of intangible assets (including goodwill) must not exceed 10

examples: A 7(a) loan with 100% working capital use of proceeds is proposed at 12 years. This violates the 10-year cap and must be reduced to 10 years or less.
A 7(a) loan financing equipment is proposed at 15 years; this is permissible only if IRS asset class useful life supports a 15-year term.
A 7(a) loan finances equipment installation expected to take 18 months; the additional installation period included in the term cannot exceed 12 months, so lender must adjust term/structure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair at purchase; lender oversight criticism

business_rules.rule_name: SBA International Trade loan may not close if appraisal is below 90% of estimated value without SBA permission
business_rules.description: For SBA International Trade (IT) loans where the lender requires an appraisal at closing, the application must include an estimated real estate value identified in the lender credit memorandum along with the requirement for an appraisal supporting the estimate at closing. At closing: if the appraised value is 90% or more of the estimated value, the lender may close but must include a written explanation in the loan file explaining why the appraisal is less than the estimated value. If the appraised value is less than 90% of the estimated value, the lender may not close without SBA’s prior written permission. The lender’s justification to SBA must explain the valuation difference and recommend a remedy (e.g., additional equity or additional collateral); if additional collateral is required, the lender must identify fair market value and liquidation value of the additional collateral. PLP lender exception: PLP lenders may close when appraisal is less than 90% of estimated value but must keep written justification in the file, including risk offsets, subject to SBA review at guaranty purchase or lender review.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans when an appraisal is required at closing and an estimated value was used in underwriting/credit memorandum.
business_rules.consequences: If appraisal <90% and no SBA prior written permission (non-PLP) or no required justification (PLP), the loan closing is noncompliant with SBA SOP and may jeopardize SBA guaranty enforceability or result in SBA findings at review/guaranty purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: PLP Lender exception permits closing below 90% without prior SBA permission, but requires written justification and documented risk-mitigation steps in file.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards for International Trade (IT) — appraisal variance at closing
source_reference.source_text: If at time of closing the appraised value: Is 90% or more of the estimated value, the Lender may close the loan but must include a written explanation as to why the appraisal is less than the estimated value in the loan file; or Is less than 90% of estimated value, the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission (see exception below for PLP Lenders). The Lender’s justification to SBA must provide a sufficient understanding of the reasons for the differences in values ... such as additional equity or additional collateral.

examples: Estimated value $1,000,000; appraisal $920,000 (92%) → lender may close but must document written explanation for the $80,000 difference in the file.
Estimated value $1,000,000; appraisal $880,000 (88%) → non-PLP lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing and propose remedies (e.g., additional equity).
Estimated value $1,000,000; appraisal $850,000 (85%) → PLP lender closes but includes thorough written justification and documents added collateral with both fair market and liquidation values.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk; regulatory criticism at SBA review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership within 36 months and loan over $500,000 requires appraisal plus appraisal review or senior staff site visit
business_rules.description: For SBA change-of-ownership transactions where the business was transferred within 36 months prior to the loan application date and the loan amount exceeds $500,000: the lender must obtain (1) an appraisal of the business real estate meeting SBA appraisal requirements, and (2) either a review of the appraisal by another appraiser selected directly by the lender or a site visit by a senior member of the lender’s staff. The lender must document the loan file with the date of the site visit (if chosen) and a description of the items reviewed on site.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans involving a change of ownership when transfer occurred within the prior 36 months and loan amount > $500,000.
business_rules.consequences: If the required appraisal and either appraisal review or senior staff site visit documentation is missing, the file is noncompliant and may expose the lender to SBA findings, potential guaranty enforceability issues, or post-purchase review exceptions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Additional Appraisal Requirements for all Changes of Ownership
source_reference.source_text: Additional Appraisal Requirements for all Changes of Ownership: For businesses that have been transferred within 36 months prior to the date of the loan application and the loan amount is more than $500,000, SBA requires: An appraisal of the business real estate that meets the appraisal requirements above; and Either a review of the appraisal by another appraiser selected directly by the Lender or a site visit by a senior member of the Lender’s staff. The Lender must document the file and include the date of the visit and a description of the items reviewed on site.

examples: A business sold 18 months ago applies for a $750,000 SBA loan → lender obtains real estate appraisal and has an independent second appraiser review it; the review memo is in the file.
A business transferred 30 months ago applies for a $600,000 loan → lender documents a senior lender site visit with date and items reviewed (equipment, occupancy, condition).
A business transferred 24 months ago applies for a $550,000 loan but file has only an appraisal and no review/site visit documentation → noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk; adverse SBA review findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 project property appraisal required over $500,000 and must meet USPAP and appraiser credential standards
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans involving commercial real estate project property: a real estate appraisal is required if the estimated value of the project property is greater than $500,000. If estimated value is $500,000 or less, the CDC must still obtain an appraisal when specific risk conditions exist (e.g., equity in land owned for 2+ years contributed as borrower contribution; third-party lender OREO property; close relationship transaction; seller carryback as part of contribution; change of ownership; or SBA/CDC determines appraisal needed for creditworthiness). If an appraisal is not required, an appropriate evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices and interagency appraisal/evaluation guidance is required. Appraiser independence is required (no conflict; independent of loan production; not involved in approval). The appraiser must be state-licensed or state-certified; when estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified. The appraisal report must comply with USPAP and be dated no more than 12 months before the application date. SBA must be identified as the client or intended user (CDC may also be client/intended user); it is acceptable if the third-party lender is the client and SBA is intended user. The CDC may not use an appraisal prepared for the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans where project collateral includes commercial real estate and a CDC is obtaining/applying appraisal/evaluation requirements for the project property.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to obtain the required appraisal/evaluation or to meet independence, credentialing, USPAP, recency, and intended user requirements may result in SBA processing delays, inability to close under SBA requirements, or post-closing SBA findings affecting guaranty/participation approvals.
business_rules.exceptions: If estimated value is $500,000 or less and none of the listed triggering conditions apply, an evaluation (not USPAP-required and not necessarily performed by a state licensed/certified appraiser) is acceptable if consistent with interagency guidance. Appraisal submission/approval requirements may differ for delegated loans (not fully detailed in excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 — Appraisals (13 CFR § 120.160(b))
source_reference.source_text: SBA requires a real estate appraisal if the estimated value of the Project Property is greater than $500,000. If the estimated value of the Project Property is $500,000 or less: The CDC must obtain an appraisal under the following circumstances: ... When the project includes a change of ownership; ... If an appraisal is not required ... the CDC must obtain an appropriate evaluation ... The appraiser must be: Independent ... and Either State-licensed or State-certified with the following exception: when the Project Property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified. The “Appraisal Report” must be prepared in compliance with ... USPAP and dated no more than twelve months prior to the date of application.

examples: Estimated project property value $750,000 → CDC must obtain USPAP appraisal; appraiser must be independent and state-licensed/certified.
Estimated value $450,000 but transaction is between family members → appraisal is required due to close relationship trigger even though value is ≤$500,000.
Estimated value $1,250,000 → appraiser must be state-certified (not only state-licensed) and the appraisal date must be within 12 months of application.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing/approval limitation; potential inability to close; SBA review findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) business transferred within 36 months and loan amount over $500,000 requires real estate appraisal plus appraisal review or senior staff site visit
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans where the business has been transferred within 36 months prior to the loan application date and the loan amount is more than $500,000, SBA requires (a) an appraisal of the business real estate meeting the applicable appraisal requirements; and (b) either an appraisal review by another appraiser selected directly by the lender OR a site visit by a senior member of the lender’s staff. If the lender chooses a site visit, the lender must document the file with the date of the visit and a description of items reviewed on site.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (i) the financed business was transferred within the prior 36 months, and (ii) the SBA 7(a) loan amount is > $500,000, and (iii) business real estate is part of collateral/transaction to be appraised.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: obtain compliant appraisal plus appraisal review or senior staff site visit documentation prior to submission/approval per SBA process. If not followed: SBA credit/eligibility deficiencies, increased risk of SBA guaranty denial/repair and post-review findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, appraisal requirements for businesses transferred within 36 months
source_reference.source_text: For businesses that have been transferred within 36 months prior to the date of the loan application and the loan amount is more than $500,000, SBA requires: a) An appraisal of the business real estate...; and b) Either a review of the appraisal by another appraiser selected directly by the Lender or a site visit by a senior member of the Lender’s staff. The Lender must document the file and include the date of the visit and a description of the items reviewed on site.

examples: A business acquired 24 months ago applies for a $750,000 SBA 7(a) loan secured by business real estate; lender must obtain an appraisal and also an appraisal review or senior staff site visit documentation.

A business acquired 40 months ago applies for a $750,000 SBA 7(a) loan; the 36-month transfer lookback condition is not met, so this specific enhanced requirement does not apply (other appraisal rules may still apply).
A business acquired 12 months ago applies for a $450,000 SBA 7(a) loan; the >$500,000 threshold is not met, so this specific requirement does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; SBA program compliance finding; required corrective action.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) special purpose property change of ownership requires certified general appraiser going concern appraisal with at least four similar appraisals in last 36 months and USPAP compliance
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions involving a business operating from a Special Purpose Property, if the (amount being financed including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing) minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment is over $250,000 OR if there is a close relationship between buyer and seller, the lender must obtain an independent appraisal performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser. The appraiser must be independent of the loan production function, not involved in approval, and must not have the appearance of a conflict of interest. The appraisal must allocate separate values to land, building, equipment, and intangible assets. The Certified General Real Property Appraiser must have completed no less than four going concern appraisals of equivalent special use property within the last 36 months (as shown in qualifications). Each assignment must instruct the appraiser to conduct the appraisal in compliance with current USPAP guidelines.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership when (i) Special Purpose Property is involved, and (ii) net financed amount excluding appraised real estate/equipment is > $250,000 OR close relationship between buyer and seller exists.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: obtain the required certified general appraisal meeting independence, allocation, experience (≥4 going concern appraisals in last 36 months), and USPAP instruction requirements. Failure may cause SBA program noncompliance, jeopardize guaranty, and require remediation or decline.
business_rules.exceptions: If net financed amount is $250,000 or less AND there is no close relationship, lender may perform its own valuation unless internal policies require an independent Qualified Source valuation (per earlier provision); however, this special-purpose-property appraisal trigger is specifically stated for >$250,000 or close relationship scenarios.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Special Purpose Properties ii)(b)-(e)
source_reference.source_text: for the Applicant or the seller. The cost of the business valuation may be passed on to the Applicant. i) Non-Special Purpose Properties: (a) If the amount being financed (including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other fina ncing) minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment

examples: Net financed amount excluding appraised real estate/equipment is $400,000 and the business operates from a special purpose property; lender must obtain a certified general appraiser appraisal with the specified allocation and experience requirements.

Net financed amount is $150,000 but buyer and seller are related (close relationship) and special purpose property is involved; lender must obtain the certified general appraisal per the close-relationship trigger.
Certified general appraiser has only 2 similar going concern appraisals in last 36 months; lender must select a different appraiser or cannot meet the SOP requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; post-approval review findings; forced remediation/repurchase risk.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) construction component over $350,000 requires 100% performance and payment bonds and builder’s risk and workers’ compensation insurance before construction begins
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans with a construction component, requirements differ based on construction component size. If the construction component is more than $350,000, prior to commencement of any construction the lender must obtain evidence that the licensed contractor has furnished a 100% performance bond and a labor and materials payment bond, and that the contractor carries appropriate Builder’s Risk and Worker’s Compensation insurance. Bonds may be issued only by a corporate surety approved by the Treasury Department using an American Institute of Architects form or comparable coverage, and only the borrower may be named as obligee on the bonds. If the construction component is $350,000 or less, SBA grants a blanket waiver of the lender’s requirement of performance/payment bonds and evidence of builder’s risk/workers’ compensation insurance, provided the lender applies the same policies, procedures, and processes for offsetting construction risk as it applies to similarly-sized, non-SBA-guaranteed construction loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans with any construction component; bifurcates based on whether the construction component is (i) ≤ $350,000 or (ii) > $350,000.
business_rules.consequences: If construction component > $350,000 and required bonds/insurance evidence not obtained pre-construction: noncompliance with SBA requirements and increased risk to SBA guaranty coverage. If construction component ≤ $350,000: lender may rely on blanket waiver only if it can demonstrate it applied the same risk-offset policies as non-SBA loans; otherwise treat as noncompliant and remediate.
business_rules.exceptions: Blanket waiver applies only when construction component is $350,000 or less AND lender applies the same construction risk-offsetting policies/procedures/processes as similarly-sized non-SBA construction loans.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 5, Construction Loan Provisions (13 CFR §§ 120.200 and 120.174)
source_reference.source_text: If the construction component of the 7(a) loan is $350,000 or less, SBA has granted a blanket waiver on the Lender’s requirement of a performance bond and the labor and materials payment bond and evidence that the contractor carries appropriate Builder's Risk and Worker's Compensation Insurance when the Lender applies the same policies... If the construction component of the 7(a) loan is more than $350,000: Prior to the commencement of any construction, Lender must obtain... Evidence that the licensed contractor has furnished a l00% performance bond and labor and materials payment bond and that the contractor carries appropriate Builder’s Risk and Worker’s Compensation Insurance...

examples: Construction component is $500,000: lender must collect 100% performance bond, payment bond, and evidence of builder’s risk and workers’ comp before construction starts.
Construction component is $300,000: lender can rely on SBA blanket waiver only if it applies the same construction risk policies as comparable non-SBA loans and documents that alignment.
Construction component is $360,000: even if borrower is strong, the >$350,000 threshold triggers the bond/insurance evidence requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; program compliance finding; possible loss of guaranty coverage for affected loss.

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 guarantee type reporting must select applicable guarantee type or report no guarantee if the application ends before any guarantee is identified
business_rules.description: For CFPB Regulation B Section 1071 reporting, a financial institution must report the type(s) of guarantee(s) obtained for an originated covered credit transaction (or that would have been obtained if originated) by selecting from the listed guarantee types. If an application is denied, withdrawn, or closed for incompleteness before any guarantee has been identified, the institution must select “no guarantee.” For State versus local guarantees, the institution must choose the category based on the entity directly administering the program, not the source of funding.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered credit transactions/application records subject to 12 CFR Part 1002 (Regulation B) Section 1071 data compilation requirements for the credit type data point (guarantee subcategory).
business_rules.consequences: Incorrect guarantee-type reporting (including failing to report 'no guarantee' when applicable or misclassifying State vs local guarantees) creates Section 1071 data integrity violations and increases risk of CFPB supervisory findings and potential enforcement for inaccurate reporting.
business_rules.exceptions: If an application is denied, withdrawn, or closed for incompleteness before any guarantee is identified, 'no guarantee' must be selected (this is a conditional instruction, not an optional exception).

source_reference.section_id: Final comment 107(a)(5)-7 guarantee type instructions
source_reference.source_text: Comment 107(a)(5)-7 now states that if an application is denied, withdrawn, or closed for incompleteness before any guarantee has been identified, the financial institution selects “no guarantee.” ... Comment 107(a)(5)-7 now includes separate items for these guarantee types, and states that the financial institution chooses State government guarantee or local government guarantee, as applicable, based on the entity directly administering the program, not the source of funding.

examples: A small business loan application is withdrawn before the lender identifies whether it will obtain an SBA guarantee; the lender reports guarantee type as 'no guarantee' for the 1071 record.
A loan is originated with an SBA 7(a) guarantee; the lender selects the appropriate government guarantee type from the 1071 guarantee list (not 'no guarantee').
A city economic development agency administers a guarantee funded by a State appropriation; the lender reports it as a local government guarantee because the city is the administering entity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; potential civil money penalty and required resubmission/correction of 1071 data

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 loan term must be reported in months from origination to legal maturity or termination and may be not applicable only when no term exists
business_rules.description: Under final 12 CFR § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii), a financial institution must report the length of the loan term, in months, if applicable. Loan term is the number of months after which the legal obligation will mature or terminate and is generally measured from the date of origination and reported that way. If the credit product does not have a loan term (e.g., certain credit cards), report loan term as 'not applicable.' Products with an estimated loan term (e.g., merchant cash advances and other sales-based financing) are not treated as having no term; for those, report the loan term, if any, that the institution estimated, specified, or disclosed in processing or underwriting.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered credit transactions subject to CFPB Regulation B Section 1071 reporting requirements where the credit type data point requires reporting loan term in months if applicable.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to report loan term in months when applicable, or improper use of 'not applicable' for products that have an estimated/disclosed term, results in inaccurate 1071 reporting and increases risk of CFPB supervisory findings and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: Use 'not applicable' only for products that truly do not have a loan term (e.g., certain credit cards). Merchant cash advances and other sales-based financing with estimated/specified/disclosed term do not qualify for 'not applicable' solely because the term is estimated.

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii) and comment 107(a)(5)-8 loan term instructions
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii) requires a financial institution to report the length of the loan term, in months, if applicable... it explain[s] ... the loan term is the number of months after which the legal obligation will mature or terminate... loan term for small business credit is generally measured from the date of origination... if a credit product ... does not have a loan term, the financial institution reports loan term as “not applicable.”

examples: A small business term loan originated on 2026-01-15 matures on 2031-01-15; the lender reports a 60-month loan term.
A business credit card product has no stated maturity/termination date; the lender reports loan term as 'not applicable.'
A merchant cash advance application includes a disclosed estimated term of 8 months; the lender reports 8 months (not 'not applicable') because an estimated/disclosed term exists.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; potential civil money penalty and required remediation of 1071 reporting controls

business_rules.rule_name: SBA loan applicant with third-party management agreement is ineligible if manager has sole discretion over operations
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) and 504 lending eligibility, a business is an ineligible passive business if it has entered into a management agreement with a third party that gives the management company sole discretion over the business operations. The applicant may remain eligible only if it exercises meaningful oversight and the management company does not have sole discretion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting/eligibility review identifies a third-party management agreement covering the applicant business operations for an SBA 7(a) or 504 credit request (origination, renewal, or material modification).
business_rules.consequences: If the management agreement grants sole discretion to the management company, the applicant must be treated as an ineligible passive business and the SBA loan must be declined as ineligible (or restructured so the applicant retains meaningful oversight).
business_rules.exceptions: Applicant remains eligible if the management company does not have sole discretion AND the applicant exercises meaningful oversight meeting all enumerated oversight elements (see related rule on meaningful oversight).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses
source_reference.source_text: Businesses that have entered into a management agreement with a third party that gives the management company sole discretion over the business operations are ineligible passive businesses. However, if the management company does not have sole discretion to manage the operations of the business and the Applicant exercises meaningful oversight of the business, the Applicant is eligible.

examples: A hotel is operated under an agreement where the third-party operator can set pricing, hire/fire staff, and control bank accounts without owner approval: ineligible passive business for SBA purposes.
A restaurant uses a management firm, but the owner approves budgets and capex and controls accounts: potentially eligible (evaluate meaningful oversight elements).
A borrower proposes SBA financing but provides a contract granting the manager unilateral authority to incur expenses and sign contracts: must treat as ineligible unless contract is amended.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in SBA lender oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Meaningful oversight requires budget approval, capex or expense approval over threshold, account control, and employee oversight by applicant
business_rules.description: For SBA eligibility where a third-party management agreement exists, the applicant must demonstrate meaningful oversight. Meaningful oversight includes a management agreement that provides for the applicant to: (i) approve the annual operating budget; (ii) approve any capital expenditures or operating expenses over a significant dollar threshold; (iii) have control over the bank accounts; and (iv) have oversight over the employees operating the business, who must be employees of the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA 7(a) or 504 applicant uses a management company and eligibility depends on whether the applicant is a passive business due to operational control delegation.
business_rules.consequences: If any required meaningful oversight element is missing (e.g., manager controls accounts or employees are not applicant employees), treat applicant as ineligible passive business unless agreement is revised to satisfy all requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; lender may document alternative facts only if they still satisfy the four elements as written.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Meaningful oversight
source_reference.source_text: “Meaningful oversight” by the Applicant means involvement in the decisions made concerning the operation of the business, which include a management agreement that provides for the Applicant to do all of the following: i. Approve the annual operating budget; ii. Approve any capital expenditures or operating expenses over a significant dollar threshold; iii. Have control over the bank accounts; and iv. Have oversight over the employees operating the business (who must be employees of the Applicant).

examples: Agreement allows applicant to approve annual budget and capex over $25,000, and applicant controls operating account; employees are on applicant payroll: meets oversight elements (subject to full review).
Management company hires and pays all employees directly and applicant has no personnel authority: fails oversight requirement; applicant ineligible unless revised.
Applicant approves budget but manager can incur unlimited operating expenses without approval: fails the 'over significant dollar threshold' approval element.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in SBA lender oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Salon suites, ghost kitchens, and similar rental-by-space models are SBA-ineligible unless revenue is membership dues and space is unassigned and fully serviced
business_rules.description: Shopping centers, office suites (including salon suites), ghost kitchens, and similar business models that generate income by renting space to accommodate independent businesses are SBA-ineligible unless: (a) revenue is earned through membership dues (not rent); (b) customers do not have an assigned space they can return to and the space cannot be personalized; and (c) the business is responsible for upkeep/maintenance and supplies equipment necessary for a fully functioning office/kitchen. If the model is combined (some rent and some membership dues), the business is not eligible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) or 504 loan eligibility determinations for applicants whose business model monetizes the use of space by independent operators (origination/renewal).
business_rules.consequences: If any of the three conditions is not met, or if revenue includes both rent and membership dues, the applicant is SBA-ineligible and cannot obtain an SBA loan for any purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility only when all three membership-based conditions are met and there is no combined rent/membership model.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Shopping centers/office suites/ghost kitchens
source_reference.source_text: Shopping centers, office suites (aka salon suites), ghost kitchens, and similar business models that generate income by renting space ... are not eligible unless all of the following apply: a) The revenue is earned through membership dues (not rent); and b) The business’s customers do not have an assigned space ... and the space cannot be personalized; and c) The business is responsible for upkeep and maintenance and is supplying the equipment necessary...

examples: A ghost kitchen leases dedicated kitchen bays with lockable storage and monthly rent: ineligible.
A co-working space charges monthly membership dues, provides unassigned desks and shared equipment, and customers cannot personalize or reserve a specific office: potentially eligible.
A salon suite operator charges some stylists fixed rent and offers others a day-pass membership: combined model; ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

business_rules.rule_name: Hotels, motels, and similar transient lodging are SBA-eligible only if more than 50% of prior-year revenue is from stays of 30 days or less
business_rules.description: Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, and similar businesses are SBA-eligible only if more than 50% of prior-year revenue is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time and the business complies with zoning and other legal requirements. For a start-up business, projections must show more than 50% of revenue will be from transients staying 30 days or less. Rentals must comply with local laws and regulations, including zoning codes and HOA/condo association rules.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) or 504 loan eligibility determinations for lodging or similar rental businesses; tested at underwriting using prior-year revenue or projections for start-ups.
business_rules.consequences: If the transient revenue test is not met (>50% from ≤30-day stays) or legal compliance is not demonstrated, the applicant is ineligible for SBA financing.
business_rules.exceptions: Start-up businesses may qualify using projections rather than prior-year revenue, but must still exceed the 50% transient revenue threshold and comply with local laws.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Limited eligibility for renting/leasing > Hotels/motels
source_reference.source_text: Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, or similar types of businesses are eligible if more than 50% of the business’s revenue for the prior year is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time and the business complies with all zoning and other legal requirements. If the Applicant is a Start-Up Business, the Applicant’s projections must show that more than 50% of the business’s revenue will be derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time.

examples: A motel with 65% of prior-year revenue from nightly and weekly stays (≤30 days) and documented zoning compliance: eligible under this criterion.
An RV park where most tenants sign 6-month leases and only 20% of revenue is from short-stay guests: ineligible.
A start-up marina provides projections showing 55% revenue from transient slips rented by the week, plus evidence of zoning compliance: may be eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

business_rules.rule_name: SBA ineligibility for legal gambling when prior-year gross revenue from gambling exceeds one-third or business purpose is gambling
business_rules.description: A business is SBA-ineligible if it obtains more than one-third of its annual gross revenue for the prior year, including rental income, from legal gambling activities. If the purpose of the business is gambling (e.g., pari-mutuel racetrack or casino), it is ineligible regardless of the percentage of gross revenue derived from gambling. A business may be eligible if it obtains one-third or less of its annual gross revenue (including rental income) from certain permitted gambling-related sources such as commissions from official State lottery ticket sales under a State license or state-licensed and supervised gambling activities in states where legal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 applicant eligibility screening at underwriting; lender must evaluate prior-year revenue sources and business purpose.
business_rules.consequences: If >33.333% threshold is exceeded or business purpose is gambling, the SBA loan must be declined as ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligible if one-third or less of prior-year annual gross revenue (including rental income) is derived from: (i) commissions from official State lottery ticket sales under State license; or (ii) gambling activities licensed and supervised by a state authority where legal.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Legal gambling
source_reference.source_text: Businesses that obtain more than one-third of their annual gross revenue for the prior year, including rental income, from legal gambling activities are not eligible. If the purpose of the business is gambling, such as a pari-mutuel betting racetrack or a gambling casino, the business is not eligible, regardless of the percentage of gross revenue derived from gambling.

examples: A convenience store earns 20% of revenue from state lottery commissions and the rest from retail sales: eligible under this criterion (assuming all other requirements met).
A restaurant earns 40% of revenue from on-premise video lottery terminals: ineligible (>1/3).
A casino hotel applies for SBA financing and claims gambling is only 25% of revenue: still ineligible because the purpose of the business is gambling.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

business_rules.rule_name: SBA requires an environmental investigation for all commercial property taken as loan collateral
business_rules.description: SBA requires an Environmental Investigation of all commercial Property upon which a security interest (e.g., mortgage, deed of trust, or leasehold deed of trust) is offered as security for a loan or debenture. The depth/type of investigation varies with contamination risk, and SOP standards are minimum standards.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans when commercial property collateral is being taken via mortgage/deed of trust/leasehold deed of trust (origination and collateral substitutions).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to complete required Environmental Investigation is noncompliance with SBA environmental policies; for 7(a), failure may result in a denial of SBA guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; SOP provides different investigation types based on risk, but the minimum requirement is that an Environmental Investigation is required for all commercial property offered as security.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Environmental Policies and Procedures > Environmental Investigations
source_reference.source_text: SBA requires an Environmental Investigation of all commercial Property upon which a security interest such as a mortgage, deed of trust, or leasehold deed of trust is offered as security for a loan or debenture. The type and depth of an Environmental Investigation to be performed varies with the risks of Contamination.

examples: SBA 7(a) loan secured by a first mortgage on a retail building: Environmental Investigation required.
SBA 504 project with leasehold deed of trust interest: Environmental Investigation required.
SBA loan secured only by business assets and no commercial real property lien: this specific rule does not trigger (no commercial property offered as security).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial; regulatory criticism in lender oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental investigation reports must be dated within one year of SBA loan number and include reliance letter with work by environmental professional
business_rules.description: Environmental Investigation Reports must be dated within one year of issuance of the SBA loan number. Transaction Screens, Phase I and Phase II ESAs must be performed by an Environmental Professional and be accompanied by the Reliance Letter in Appendix 5 (even if addressed to the SBA Lender). Requests for exceptions to SBA Environmental Policies and Procedures must be directed to the Environmental Committee, regardless of processing method.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans where an Environmental Investigation is required, including delegated and non-delegated processing.
business_rules.consequences: If report date is older than one year from SBA loan number issuance, or if required reliance letter/environmental professional requirements are missing, the file is deficient and may result in SBA guaranty denial/repair or inability to proceed under non-delegated processing without correction/exception.
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions must be requested through the Environmental Committee; rule states exception requests are required regardless of processing method.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Environmental Policies and Procedures > Submission of Environmental Investigation Reports
source_reference.source_text: of any Environmental Investigation Report in the loan file. All SBA Lenders must comply with and meet the requirements of the Environmental Policies and Procedures as set forth in this SOP. For example, all Transaction Screens, Phase I and Phase II ESAs must be performed by an Environmental Professional

examples: Loan number issued 2026-05-15; Phase I dated 2025-06-01 (older than one year): must refresh or obtain exception per SOP process.
Phase I is current but no reliance letter included: documentation deficiency; obtain reliance letter before closing/guaranty eligibility.
Delegated SBA Express loan: reports need not be submitted via E-Tran, but must be retained in file and still must meet dating/professional/reliance letter requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in lender oversight

business_rules.rule_name: Section 106 review clearance must be retained or submitted depending on delegated versus non-delegated SBA processing
business_rules.description: When Section 106 review clearance is issued by local SBA counsel, SBA lenders must retain or submit the clearance based on processing method: delegated lenders must retain the clearance in the SBA loan file and provide it with any guaranty purchase request; non-delegated lenders must include the clearance with the application submitted to the SBA loan processing center.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after Section 106 review clearance is issued in connection with collateral/property review under SBA requirements for loans where this process is applicable.
business_rules.consequences: If required clearance is missing from the file or not submitted with guaranty purchase request/application as required, SBA may delay processing, request additional information, or deny/repair guaranty purchase due to documentation deficiency.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Section 106 review clearance retention/submission
source_reference.source_text: SBA Lenders processing the loan under their delegated authority must retain the clearance from local SBA counsel in the SBA Loan file. The clearance must be provided with any guaranty purchase request and may be reviewed by SBA when conducting lender oversight activities. SBA Lenders processing the loan under non-delegated procedures must include the clearance from local SBA counsel with the application for review by the SBA loan processing center.

examples: Delegated 7(a) loan: Section 106 clearance obtained but not in file at purchase: SBA may require cure before honoring guaranty purchase.
Non-delegated 504: clearance not included in application package: SBA processing center may suspend review pending receipt.
Delegated lender retains clearance and includes it with guaranty purchase request: compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA purchase denial/delay; lender oversight finding

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 energy reduction eligibility requires at least 10% reduction and excludes new businesses
business_rules.description: To qualify as an SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Project under the “reduction of existing energy consumption” category, the project must reduce existing energy consumption by at least 10%. This category cannot be used for projects involving new businesses. If the project involves construction or acquisition of a facility, the new facility must replace an existing facility, energy consumption must be compared on a square-footage basis, the applicant must demonstrate the new facility will use 10% less energy than the existing facility, and the new facility must be located in the same local area as the existing facility (e.g., same city/town/county/zip/MSA or as otherwise deemed appropriate by SBA).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan_purpose indicates SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Project eligibility is being claimed under the “reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%” pathway, including when proceeds involve construction or acquisition of a facility.
business_rules.consequences: If any required element is not met (e.g., <10% reduction, project is for a new business, not a replacement facility, not same local area, or comparison not on square footage basis), the project is not eligible under this 10% reduction category and must qualify under a different eligible public policy category or be processed as non-energy eligibility; otherwise SBA/CDC may not approve the project under this category.
business_rules.exceptions: Retrofit projects follow a separate rule (see related rule BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_31_002). SBA may deem the “local area” differently as appropriate. No exception stated for new businesses under this category.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application > Energy_10percent_reduction_504
source_reference.source_text: Projects for the reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%. This category may not be used for Projects involving new businesses. ... The new facility must replace an existing facility. ... the Applicant must be able to demonstrate that the new facility will use 10% less energy than the existing facility. The energy consumption between the two facilities must be compared for energy consumption on a square footage basis. ... The new facility must be located in the same local area (e.g., the same city, town, county, zip code, metropolitan statistical area or as otherwise deemed appropriate by SBA).

examples: Acquisition: Applicant buys a replacement facility in the same county and documents (kWh/sq ft) that the new facility will use 12% less energy than the existing facility; eligible under the 10% reduction pathway.
Construction: Applicant builds a new additional site (not replacing an existing facility); ineligible under the 10% reduction pathway even if projected to be efficient.
Acquisition: Replacement facility is in a different MSA and the applicant cannot support SBA’s acceptance as the “same local area”; fails the location condition and is ineligible under this pathway.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC eligibility rejection or requirement to re-structure the project to meet another eligibility category

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 retrofit energy projects must reduce the subject facility’s energy consumption by at least 10%
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Projects involving retrofit of an applicant’s existing facility, the retrofit must reduce the energy consumption of that facility by at least 10%. The reduction test is applied to the retrofitted facility regardless of energy usage of any other facilities the applicant may operate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 eligibility is being claimed under the retrofit portion of the “reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%” category and proceeds finance modifications/retrofits at an existing facility.
