business_rules.consequences: If in second position, lender must ensure release mechanics are feasible and that combined release amounts do not exceed 80% of completed project FMV/selling price. Noncompliance can jeopardize SBA guaranty and create collateral liquidation risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the conditional acceptance of second lien and release-clause requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Builders CAPLines
source_reference.source_text:
Builders CAPLines

SBA will accept no less than a second lien position on the property being constructed or renovated if the purpose of the first lien was to acquire the property... Lenders must not take a second lien position if the first lienholder requires that the entire loan be paid in full before any property is released... the total amount necessary to release the first and second liens may not exceed 80% of the fair market value (selling price) of the completed project.

examples: Builder CAPLine in 2nd lien: the lot-release schedule must allow parcel releases without requiring payoff of the entire 1st lien loan; otherwise, lender must not take the 2nd lien structure.
Completed home selling price is $500,000: combined payoff required to release both liens must be ≤ $400,000 (80% of $500,000).
Subdivision first-lien lender provides a release clause requiring $50,000 per lot: lender documents release terms and confirms combined lien release amount stays within the 80% cap per parcel sale.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial and elevated loss exposure due to unreleaseable collateral.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA commercial real estate collateral loans over 500,000 dollars require USPAP appraisal dated within 12 months of guaranty application
business_rules.description: For SBA loans greater than $500,000 secured by commercial real property, lenders must obtain an appraisal performed by a State licensed or certified appraiser, compliant with USPAP. SBA additionally requires completed appraisals to be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. For federally regulated lenders, the SOP states that no exemption is granted under the Interagency Guidance Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines for transactions insured or guaranteed by a U.S. Government Agency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans (including CAPLines where applicable) secured by commercial real property when the loan amount is greater than $500,000.
business_rules.consequences: If loan amount > $500,000 and secured by commercial real property, an appraisal meeting state licensing/certification and USPAP standards and dated within 12 months of the guaranty application must be in file. Missing/expired/noncompliant appraisal can result in SBA guaranty repair/denial and safety-and-soundness criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the >$500,000 threshold; additional SOP subsections address $500,000 or less scenarios.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements (Commercial Real Estate)
source_reference.source_text:
Commercial Real Estate:

For all loans greater than $500,000 secured by commercial real property, all Lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty.

examples: A $750,000 SBA loan secured by an office building: lender must obtain a USPAP appraisal by a state-licensed or state-certified appraiser dated within 12 months of the guaranty application.
A $600,000 SBA loan secured by a warehouse: using only an evaluation is not permitted under this SBA requirement.
A $1,200,000 SBA loan: appraisal must meet this rule plus the appraiser credentialing rule for >$1,000,000 estimated value (state-certified).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; examiner criticism for valuation policy noncompliance.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA commercial real estate appraiser independence required and state certified credential required when value exceeds 1,000,000 dollars
business_rules.description: The appraiser engaged for SBA commercial real estate collateral must be independent with no appearance of a conflict of interest (including being independent of the loan production function and not involved in approval). The appraiser must be state-licensed or state-certified; however, when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever an SBA appraisal is obtained for commercial real property collateral; the state-certified requirement is triggered when estimated value > $1,000,000.
business_rules.consequences: If estimated property value > $1,000,000, using only a state-licensed (not certified) appraiser violates SBA requirements and may lead to SBA guaranty repair/denial. Appraiser conflicts can invalidate the valuation for SBA purposes and create underwriting control failures.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements (Appraiser must be independent; state-certified over $1,000,000)
source_reference.source_text:
The appraiser must be:

Independent and have no appearance of a conflict of interest... independent of the loan production function, and not involved in the approval of the transaction); and

Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified.

examples: Estimated collateral value is $1,200,000: lender must use a state-certified appraiser, not merely state-licensed.
Loan officer selects an appraiser who also sits on the loan approval committee: prohibited due to lack of independence; lender must replace the appraiser.
Estimated value is $900,000: state-licensed appraiser is permissible, provided independence and other SBA requirements are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; examination criticism for appraisal independence failures.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 energy public policy compliance must be documented using annual actual energy usage and independent third-party evaluation
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Projects under the 10% reduction category (paragraph i) or renewable energy category (paragraph ii), the applicant must document compliance via an energy audit, engineering report, or other professional evaluation as deemed appropriate by SBA. The evaluation must be based on annual energy usage measured in actual energy usage units (e.g., kilowatt hours, therms, or gallons), not in dollar costs. At a minimum, the documentation must include: (a) a description of the facility or facilities; (b) current energy usage; (c) projected energy usage based on all modifications/retrofits and all equipment installations/replacements/retrofits; and (d) qualifications of the party performing the evaluation. The evaluation must be performed by an independent third party (not the applicant, interim lender, third party lender, or any of their affiliates).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 Energy Public Policy eligibility is claimed under paragraph i (10% energy reduction) or paragraph ii (renewable energy >15%) and the lender/CDC is compiling the eligibility package for SBA/CDC review.
business_rules.consequences: If documentation is missing required elements, uses dollar costs instead of actual energy usage, or is performed by a non-independent party, SBA may deem the project not properly documented for eligibility and delay, request additional information, or decline eligibility under the Energy Public Policy category.
business_rules.exceptions: Applies expressly to paragraphs i and ii. “Other professional evaluation” may be used only if deemed appropriate by SBA.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application > Energy documentation requirements
source_reference.source_text: the Applicant must document the Energy Public Policy Project’s compliance through either an energy audit, engineering report, or other professional evaluation... based on the annual energy usage... (measured in actual energy usage, e.g. kilowatt hours, therms, or gallons... not in dollar costs), and that, at a minimum, includes... a description of the facility... the current energy usage; the projected energy usage...; and the qualifications of the party performing... each of which must be performed by an independent third party (by an entity other than the Applicant, the interim lender, the Third Party Lender, or any of their respective affiliates).

examples: A licensed engineering firm provides an annual kWh baseline and projected kWh after HVAC and envelope upgrades, includes methodology and professional qualifications, and is not affiliated with borrower or lenders; meets documentation requirements.
Borrower submits a spreadsheet showing $ savings on utility bills without kWh/therms/gallons; fails the “actual energy usage” requirement and must be corrected.
Third party lender’s affiliate prepares the energy audit; fails the independent third-party requirement and must be redone by a qualifying independent party.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility denial or processing delay pending receipt of compliant third-party documentation

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines must be declined if business cash flow does not provide timely reasonable assurance of repayment regardless of collateral
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) CAPLines, the lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner consistent with prudent lending standards. The applicant’s cash flow is the primary source of repayment (and for CAPLines may include conversion of assets to cash). If the lender’s financial analysis does not demonstrate reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow, the CAPLines request must be declined, even if collateral is available or there are outside sources of repayment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines applications (all CAPLines) during underwriting/credit decision when the lender’s financial analysis indicates insufficient reasonable assurance of timely repayment from business cash flow.
business_rules.consequences: If the condition is met (no reasonable assurance from business cash flow), the loan request must be declined. Approving despite this exposes the lender to SBA guaranty denial/repair and adverse lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text. The standard notes that CAPLines cash flow may include conversion of assets to cash, but still requires reasonable assurance of timely repayment from business cash flow.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines > C. Credit Standards for all CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: A Lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner, consistent with prudent lending standards. The cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment. In CAPLines, the cash flow of the Applicant may include the conversion of assets to cash. Thus, if the Lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral available or outside sources of repayment.

examples: Applicant offers strong collateral coverage (e.g., pledged inventory), but projected operating cash flow is negative and does not support repayment; lender must decline the CAPLines request.
Seasonal CAPLine applicant proposes repayment primarily from guarantor support; business cash flow analysis does not show timely repayment capacity; lender must decline.
Applicant’s cash flow can support repayment only if a speculative outside refinancing occurs; absent reasonable assurance from business cash flow, lender must decline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; adverse SBA lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines minimum debt service coverage ratio 1.15x on historical or projected basis and 1.00x on global basis
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) CAPLines, debt service (DS) is defined as future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of the new SBA loan proceeds. Operating cash flow (OCF) is defined as EBITDA. The applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (OCF/DS) must be at least 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis, and at least 1.00 (1:1) on a global basis.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines underwriting when calculating DSCR for credit decisioning. Requires current debt schedule (including shareholder debt) to compute DS and DSCR.
business_rules.consequences: If DSCR is below 1.15 (historical/projected) or global DSCR is below 1.00, the credit fails the SBA CAPLines minimum cash flow coverage standard and should not be approved absent a documented, program-compliant resolution (e.g., restructure request, reduced loan size, or decline). Noncompliance may result in SBA guaranty repair/denial upon purchase request or lender oversight review.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines > Underwriting > Debt Service definition and DSCR thresholds
source_reference.source_text: Debt Service (DS) is defined as the future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The Applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (OCF/DS) must be equal to or greater than 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis and 1:1 on a global basis. To perform a complete analysis of debt service, it is important for a Lender to obtain a current debt schedule prepared by the Applicant, including any shareholder debt.

examples: Existing business CAPLine: OCF $230,000; DS $200,000 → DSCR 1.15x meets the minimum (assuming global DSCR is also ≥ 1.00).
Projected basis: OCF $345,000; DS $320,000 → DSCR 1.08x; fails SBA CAPLines minimum 1.15x; lender must reduce DS, increase OCF support, or decline.
Global basis: business DSCR 1.20x but global DSCR (including affiliate/other obligations) is 0.95x; fails the 1.00x global requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; adverse SBA lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines existing businesses require three most recent years of historical financial information plus an interim financial statement
business_rules.description: When underwriting SBA 7(a) CAPLines for existing businesses, the lender’s repayment ability analysis must be based on the three most recent years of historical financial information (e.g., tax returns or balance sheet with debt schedule and income statement) plus an interim financial statement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines underwriting for existing businesses (not new businesses) when preparing the financial analysis of repayment ability.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender does not obtain/analyze the required historical and interim financial information, underwriting is incomplete and may be cited as a file deficiency and may support SBA guaranty repair/denial if it results in noncompliance with SBA loan program requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines > Underwriting > Financial analysis of repayment ability
source_reference.source_text: Financial analysis of repayment ability: a) For existing businesses based on the three most recent years of historical financial information (tax returns or balance sheet with debt schedule and income statement) plus an interim financial statement. (13 CFR § 120.191)

examples: Applicant has operated for 6 years: lender must analyze the last 3 fiscal years tax returns (or balance sheet + debt schedule + income statement) and an interim statement before approving the CAPLine.
Existing business provides only 1 year of tax returns; lender must obtain the other 2 years plus an interim statement to meet SBA underwriting requirements.
Existing business has reviewed (unaudited) statements: lender may use them as historical financial information if they meet the required elements, but still must include an interim statement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; SBA guaranty repair exposure for underwriting deficiencies

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines new businesses projections must reflect positive cash flow within 2 years
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) CAPLines underwriting of new businesses, the lender’s repayment ability analysis must be based on detailed projections, including supporting assumptions, and those projections must reflect positive cash flow within 2 years.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines underwriting for new businesses when using projections to analyze repayment ability.
business_rules.consequences: If projections do not reflect positive cash flow within 2 years (or are not detailed/supported), the underwriting does not meet SBA CAPLines requirements and the lender should not approve absent program-compliant corrective action; noncompliance may result in SBA guaranty repair/denial and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines > Underwriting > Financial analysis for new businesses
source_reference.source_text: For new businesses, based on detailed projections, including the supporting assumptions which reflect positive cash flow within 2 years.

examples: Startup requests a Working Capital CAPLine and submits 24-month projections showing negative cash flow through month 24; lender must treat as not meeting the SBA requirement and should decline or restructure.
New business projections show positive cash flow starting month 18 with supportable assumptions (signed contracts, credible costs); meets the positive cash flow within 2 years requirement.
Projections show positive cash flow within 2 years but lack supporting assumptions (no basis for revenue growth); underwriting is incomplete and must be corrected before approval.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; adverse SBA lender oversight findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines require two years of detailed projections when recent historical and interim cash flow does not show sufficient debt service coverage
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) CAPLines, the lender must document historical cash flow for existing businesses that demonstrates total debt service coverage after the SBA loan. If historical cash flow from the most recent full year and interim financial statements does not show sufficient debt service coverage, the lender must obtain and analyze 2 years of detailed projections with supporting assumptions justifying reliance on projections instead of historical performance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines underwriting for existing businesses when the most recent full year historical cash flow and interim financial statements do not show sufficient debt service coverage after the SBA loan.
business_rules.consequences: If insufficient historical/interim coverage exists and the lender does not obtain/analyze 2-year detailed projections with supporting assumptions, underwriting is incomplete and may be criticized; SBA guaranty repair/denial risk increases if noncompliance affects eligibility/creditworthiness determination.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines > Underwriting > Historical cash flow vs projections requirement
source_reference.source_text: Historical cash flow for existing businesses, that demonstrates total debt service coverage after the SBA loan; if the historical cash flow from the most recent full year and the interim financial statements do not show sufficient debt service coverage, Lender must obtain from the Applicant and analyze 2 years of detailed projections including the supporting assumptions justifying relying on projections instead of historical performance.

examples: Existing business shows DSCR below required levels on the last fiscal year and interim results; lender must collect 24 months of projections and document assumptions before approval consideration.
Borrower has seasonal volatility and interim results are weak; lender obtains 2-year projections supported by signed customer orders and cost structure; meets the projection analysis requirement.
Lender approves based on collateral value without obtaining the required 2-year projections despite weak interim performance; this is a program compliance deficiency.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; SBA guaranty repair exposure for inadequate underwriting support

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Export Express must be declined if cash flow analysis does not show reasonable assurance of timely repayment regardless of collateral
business_rules.description: For SBA Export Express loans, the lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner consistent with prudent lending standards and treat the applicant’s cash flow as the primary repayment source (not expected collateral liquidation). If the lender’s financial analysis demonstrates the applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow, the lender must decline the request regardless of collateral available or outside repayment sources.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any request for financial assistance to be guaranteed by SBA under Export Express where the lender performs underwriting and cash flow analysis.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender originates despite insufficient cash flow repayment assurance (relying primarily on collateral), SBA may determine the lender failed to follow SBA Loan Program Requirements, increasing risk of repair/denial of guaranty purchase, findings in lender oversight, and heightened loss exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; the rule is framed as a must-decline requirement when repayment assurance from cash flow is not demonstrated.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards for Export Express > Cash flow as primary source; must decline if no reasonable assurance
source_reference.source_text: A Lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner, consistent with prudent lending standards. The cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment, not any expected recovery from the liquidation of collateral. Thus, if the Lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral available or outside sources of repayment.

examples: Borrower shows negative trailing cash flow and projections do not cover debt service; collateral is strong: lender must decline the Export Express request.

Borrower has adequate cash flow coverage and moderate collateral shortfall: lender may proceed (SBA guaranty is not a substitute for available collateral, but collateral inadequacy alone is not a decline basis).
Borrower offers a third-party guarantor with outside income, but business cash flow still cannot support repayment: lender must decline under this standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of guaranty purchase or denial of liability; lender oversight criticism

business_rules.rule_name: SBA EWCP maximum advance rates 90 percent on foreign receivables and 75 percent on eligible export inventory with at least monthly borrowing base certificates
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Export Working Capital Program (EWCP) asset-based revolving lines of credit, maximum advance rates are 90% on eligible foreign receivables and 75% on eligible export-related inventory located within the United States. The borrower must submit a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC) to the lender at least monthly (or more frequently if the lender customarily requires on similarly sized non-SBA loans). If the borrowing base shows the borrower is over-advanced, the lender must immediately require the borrower to make a payment to reduce the outstanding balance to within the borrowing base formula.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to EWCP asset-based revolving lines of credit where advances are made against a borrowing base of foreign receivables and/or export-related inventory. Also applies to monitoring and control requirements for borrowing base reporting and overadvance remediation.
business_rules.consequences: If advances exceed the maximum advance rates or if an overadvance is identified, the lender must take immediate corrective action (require payment) to bring the balance within the borrowing base. Failure to enforce can result in SBA guaranty risk and lender program compliance findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Control accounts 'may be required' at LGPC/PLP-EWCP lender discretion (non-delegated vs delegated), but BBC submission and overadvance cure are stated as minimum/mandatory. Separate advance-rate limitations apply to uninsured open-account receivables (e.g., 80%) in other EWCP provisions not captured in this rule.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8, Section B, Ch 4, Advances and Receivables (ii)
source_reference.source_text: On an asset-based revolving line of credit where advances are made against a borrowing base of foreign receivables and/or export-related inventory, the maximum advance rates are 90% on eligible foreign receivables and 75% on eligible export-related inventory located within the United States... At a minimum, the Borrower must submit a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC) to the Lender at least monthly... If the borrowing base shows the Borrower is over-advanced, the Lender must immediately require the Borrower to make a payment...

examples: Eligible foreign receivables total $1,000,000 and eligible export inventory totals $400,000 → maximum borrowing base = $900,000 + $300,000 = $1,200,000.
Borrower submits monthly BBC showing loan balance $1,250,000 but allowed borrowing base $1,200,000 → lender must immediately require a $50,000 payment.
Lender customarily requires weekly borrowing base reporting for similar non-SBA asset-based lines → for EWCP, lender may require weekly (more frequent than monthly) under the rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender review finding; potential repair/denial of SBA guaranty on liquidation if collateral controls are not followed

business_rules.rule_name: Automated valuation model use requires minimum confidence criteria, anti-value-shopping controls, and fallback to appraisal or alternative valuation when unreliable
business_rules.description: When using automated valuation models (AVMs) for evaluations, the institution must maintain AVM performance criteria for accuracy and reliability for the transaction/activity/geography; establish internal confidence score minimums (or similar criteria) for when each model may be used; implement controls to preclude “value shopping” when more than one AVM is used for the same property; and establish procedures to obtain an appraisal or use a different valuation method when the AVM’s value is not reliable to support the credit decision. The institution must also identify circumstances under which an AVM may not be used (e.g., post-natural-disaster conditions, model performance outside tolerances for the market/price tier, non-homogeneous properties such as atypical lot sizes/property types).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to real estate-secured transactions where the institution proposes to use an AVM as the valuation method (in whole or in part) to support a credit decision, including evaluation development under agency appraisal/evaluation guidelines.
business_rules.consequences: If confidence criteria are not met, or prohibited circumstances exist, the institution must not use the AVM as the sole valuation support and must obtain an appraisal or apply an alternate valuation method; failure to implement anti-value-shopping controls or confidence minimums increases valuation risk and may be cited as unsafe/unsound practice.
business_rules.exceptions: None specified; the institution may define the specific minimum confidence score thresholds and tolerance bands by model/market/property type in internal policy.

source_reference.section_id: Appendix C > Determining AVM Use
source_reference.source_text: Maintain AVM performance criteria for accuracy and reliability... Establish internal confidence score minimums... Implement controls to preclude “value shopping” when more than one AVM is used... Establish procedures for obtaining an appraisal or using a different valuation method... Identify circumstances under which an AVM may not be used, including: When market conditions warrant, such as during the aftermath of a natural disaster...

examples: An AVM returns a low vendor confidence score below the institution’s minimum for the property type. Result: order an appraisal or use another valuation method before approving the loan.
A lender runs three AVMs and selects the highest value. Result: prohibited by anti-value-shopping controls; the institution must implement controls to prevent selecting values opportunistically.
A property is atypical (non-homogeneous) with unusual lot size. Result: AVM use is disallowed per policy; use an appraisal or alternative method.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for unsafe and unsound valuation practices and appraisal/evaluation guideline noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: Automated valuation model validation must be independent, ongoing, and cannot rely solely on vendor representations even with value insurance or guarantees
business_rules.description: The institution must establish standards and procedures for independent and ongoing AVM monitoring and model validation to ensure results are credible. Validation may be internal or by a qualified independent third party, but validators must be independent of model development/sales functions and independent of loan production and collection processes. The institution must not rely solely on AVM vendor validation representations and must perform appropriate model validation even when AVMs are supported by value insurance/guarantees. Validation procedures must specify, at a minimum: sample size expectations, level of geographic analysis, testing frequency and re-testing criteria, performance measures, and acceptable performance ranges; AVM results should be compared to actual sales data in a specified market prior to the information being available to the model; and if multiple AVMs are used, each AVM should be validated and outcomes compared to loans valued via other processes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever AVMs are used to establish value in evaluations or credit decisions and the institution maintains AVM-based valuation programs.
business_rules.consequences: Institutions must document validation results and use audit/validation findings to update AVM policies and procedures when warranted. Failure to maintain independent validation is a safety-and-soundness control weakness and may invalidate reliance on AVM values for risk management.
business_rules.exceptions: None; insurance/guarantees do not eliminate the validation obligation.

source_reference.section_id: Appendix C > Validating AVM Results
source_reference.source_text: An institution should establish standards and procedures for independent and ongoing monitoring and model validation... An institution should not rely solely on validation representations provided by an AVM vendor... perform appropriate model validation regardless of whether it relies on AVMs that are supported by value insurance or guarantees... specify, at a minimum: Expectations for an appropriate sample size... Testing frequency...

examples: A bank accepts a vendor’s annual AVM validation report without internal testing. Result: noncompliant with guidance; bank must perform its own appropriate validation.
AVM values are insured by a third-party guarantee. Result: bank still must validate the model and understand claim process/insurer strength.
A bank uses two AVMs for different geographies but validates only one. Result: each AVM used must be validated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for deficient model risk management and unsafe and unsound valuation controls

business_rules.rule_name: ADC underwriting standards should require feasibility studies and sensitivity and risk analyses for market-variable shocks
business_rules.description: For development and construction projects and completed commercial properties, underwriting standards/policy should establish requirements for feasibility studies and sensitivity and risk analyses, including testing the sensitivity of income projections to changes in economic variables such as interest rates, vacancy rates, and operating expenses.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to acquisition, development, and construction (ADC) lending and underwriting where repayment depends on project performance, sale/lease-up, and market conditions.
business_rules.consequences: If feasibility and sensitivity analyses are not required/performed, the institution may not identify downside risks to cash flow and completion/stabilization, leading to unsafe or unsound underwriting and supervisory criticism (e.g., MRAs) and elevated loss exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: No explicit exception stated; scope should be commensurate with size and type of project or property.

source_reference.section_id: Interagency Guidelines for Real Estate Lending; ADC policies
source_reference.source_text: For development and construction projects, and completed commercial properties, the policy should establish, commensurate with the size and type of the project or property, • requirements for feasibility studies and sensitivity and risk analyses (e.g., sensitivity of income projections to changes in economic variables such as interest rates, vacancy rates, or operating expenses).

examples: Construction loan for a 200-unit multifamily: underwriting includes feasibility study plus sensitivity of NOI to +200 bps rates and +5% vacancy shock.
Retail strip center redevelopment: sensitivity analysis tests rent reductions and higher operating expenses to validate DSCR under stress.
Industrial development: feasibility analysis evaluates lease-up assumptions and downside absorption to validate takeout/refinance capacity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Matters requiring attention and examination criticism for unsafe or unsound ADC underwriting practices

business_rules.rule_name: ADC underwriting standards should set maximum loan limits as a percentage of cost and as a percentage of market value
business_rules.description: Prudent ADC lending standards should establish loan limits expressed as (i) a maximum percentage of cost (loan-to-cost, LTC) and (ii) a maximum percentage of market value (loan-to-value, LTV) to ensure the borrower contributes sufficient equity. These caps should be embedded in underwriting standards and used in credit approval and monitoring.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to ADC lending and underwriting where project costs and appraised market value are used to size credit exposure and ensure borrower equity.
business_rules.consequences: Absent defined LTC and LTV maximums, borrower equity may be insufficient, increasing default/loss severity. Supervisory criticism may cite inadequate lending policy limits and weak collateral risk controls.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt does not define numeric caps; institution must set caps consistent with its risk appetite and regulatory expectations.

source_reference.section_id: ADC policies; loan limits discussion
source_reference.source_text: Prudent policies typically establish loan limits as a maximum percentage of cost (i.e., LTC) as well as market value (i.e., LTV) to ensure that the borrower contributes sufficient equity.

examples: If project total cost is $10MM and policy LTC cap is 75%, maximum loan amount by LTC is $7.5MM even if appraised value supports more.
If appraised market value is $12MM and policy LTV cap is 70%, maximum loan amount by LTV is $8.4MM even if cost-based sizing allows $9MM.
If both LTC and LTV apply, underwriting uses the more conservative (lower) resulting maximum loan amount.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Examination criticism for inadequate real estate lending policy limits and unsafe collateral risk management

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) and 504 applicant is ineligible if the business is non-profit
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) and 504 loans, a non-profit Applicant business is ineligible under 13 CFR 120.110(a). For-profit subsidiaries of non-profits may be eligible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting an SBA 7(a) or SBA 504 loan and determining Applicant eligibility under 13 CFR 120.110.
business_rules.consequences: If the Applicant is a non-profit business, the lender must treat the Applicant as ineligible for an SBA-guaranteed loan and must not proceed with SBA loan approval/guaranty submission for any purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: For-profit subsidiaries of non-profit businesses may be eligible (must separately confirm they are for-profit and otherwise eligible).

source_reference.section_id: Types of Ineligible Businesses – Non-profit businesses (13 CFR 120.110(a))
source_reference.source_text: Non-profit businesses are ineligible (for-profit subsidiaries may be eligible). 13 CFR § 120.110(a)

examples: Applicant is a 501(c)(3) community arts nonprofit seeking an SBA 7(a) loan to renovate its building → ineligible; SBA guaranty cannot be obtained.

Applicant is a for-profit LLC subsidiary of a nonprofit hospital system applying for an SBA 504 loan to acquire equipment → not automatically ineligible under the nonprofit rule; proceed to other eligibility tests.
Applicant is a nonprofit trade association applying for an SBA 7(a) working capital line → ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism for eligibility control failure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) and 504 applicant is ineligible if primarily engaged in lending unless revenue and loan-sale timing tests are met
business_rules.description: SBA cannot guarantee a loan that provides funds to businesses primarily engaged in lending, investment, or to an otherwise eligible business engaged in financing, factoring, or investment not related or essential to the business. Prohibited lending-type businesses include banks, life insurance companies (but not independent agents), finance companies, factoring companies, investment companies, bail bond companies, and other businesses whose stock in trade is money. Limited eligibility exceptions apply with quantitative tests: (1) a pawn shop that provides financing is eligible if more than 50% of prior-year revenue was from sale of merchandise (not interest); (2) a business financing credit sales is eligible if less than 50% of revenue is from financing its sales; (3) a mortgage servicing company is eligible if it disburses loans and sells them within 14 calendar days of loan closing, and mortgage companies primarily engaged in servicing loans are eligible, but mortgage companies that make loans and hold them in portfolio are not eligible; (4) a check cashing business is eligible if it receives more than 50% of revenue from the service of cashing checks; (5) fee-based financial advisor services may be eligible if loan proceeds are not used to invest in the advisor’s own portfolio.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting SBA 7(a) or 504 loans and the Applicant’s primary business activity involves lending/financing, investment, factoring, insurance carrier activity, bail bonds, or similar money-as-inventory operations.
business_rules.consequences: If the Applicant is primarily engaged in lending/investment and does not meet a stated exception test, the Applicant is ineligible for an SBA-guaranteed loan and the lender must decline/withdraw SBA processing for the Applicant.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility may be available under the limited circumstances listed in the rule description (including the >50% or <50% prior-year revenue tests and the 14-calendar-day loan sale requirement for certain mortgage servicing companies).

source_reference.section_id: Businesses Engaged in Lending (13 CFR 120.110(b))
source_reference.source_text: SBA cannot guarantee a loan that provides funds to businesses primarily engaged in lending... This prohibits SBA Loans to: Banks; Life Insurance Companies... Finance Companies; Factoring Companies... Other businesses whose stock in trade is money... A pawn shop... is eligible if more than 50% of its revenue... was from the sale of merchandise... A business that provides financing... is eligible, provided less than 50% of its revenue is from financing its sales... A mortgage servicing company... sells them within 14 calendar days... A check cashing business is eligible if it receives more than 50% of its revenue...

examples: Applicant is a finance company originating consumer installment loans → ineligible for SBA 7(a).
Applicant is a pawn shop: prior-year revenue is 55% from merchandise sales and 45% interest/fees → eligible under the pawn shop exception (still must meet all other SBA eligibility rules).

Applicant is a mortgage company that originates loans and holds them in its portfolio for months → ineligible; does not meet the 14-calendar-day sale criterion and is not primarily a servicing company.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism for underwriting eligibility noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) and 504 applicant is ineligible as a passive business unless membership model and meaningful oversight tests are satisfied
business_rules.description: Passive businesses are ineligible under 13 CFR 120.110(c), including (i) developers/landlords that do not actively use or occupy the assets acquired or improved with loan proceeds (except Eligible Passive Companies under 13 CFR 120.111), (ii) businesses primarily engaged in subdividing and developing real property for resale on its own account, and (iii) businesses primarily engaged in owning/purchasing real estate and leasing it for any purpose. Shopping centers, office suites/salon suites, ghost kitchens, and similar models renting space to independent businesses are not eligible unless ALL criteria are met: revenue earned through membership dues (not rent); customers do not have assigned space; and the business is responsible for upkeep/maintenance and supplies the equipment necessary for a fully functioning office/kitchen. Mixed models with any rent revenue are fully ineligible. Additionally, an Applicant is ineligible if a third-party management agreement gives the management company sole discretion over business operations, unless the Applicant exercises meaningful oversight, defined as: approving the annual operating budget; approving capital expenditures or operating expenses over a significant dollar threshold; having control over bank accounts; and oversight over employees operating the business (who must be employees of the Applicant). SBA lenders must review management agreements to determine passive business status unless the agreement is part of the franchise disclosure documents for a brand on the Franchise Directory. Agreements for control (including side agreements) giving a non-guarantor owner/investor control render the business ineligible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a)/504 underwriting when the Applicant’s business model involves rental/leasing of real estate or space, or when operations are delegated to a management company under a management agreement, or when ownership/control arrangements exist with non-guarantor investors.
business_rules.consequences: If the Applicant meets an ineligible passive business condition or fails any required membership-model/meaningful-oversight criteria, the lender must treat the Applicant as ineligible for an SBA-guaranteed loan and must not proceed with SBA loan approval/guaranty submission.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligible Passive Companies under 13 CFR 120.111 may be eligible (not detailed in this excerpt). Management agreements embedded in FDDs for brands on the Franchise Directory do not require the same lender review requirement described (but eligibility still depends on control/oversight facts).

source_reference.section_id: Passive Businesses (13 CFR 120.110(c)) and management agreement oversight
source_reference.source_text: Shopping centers, office suites... ghost kitchens... are not eligible unless all of the following apply: The revenue is earned through membership dues (not rent)... customers do not have an assigned space... The business is responsible for upkeep... supplying the equipment... A business is either fully eligible or fully ineligible... combined model... is not eligible... Businesses that have entered into a management agreement... sole discretion... are ineligible... “Meaningful oversight”... Approve the annual operating budget; Approve any capital expenditures... Have control over the bank accounts; and Have oversight over the employees...

examples: Ghost kitchen collects ‘membership dues’ but assigns each member a dedicated lockable kitchen station → fails the ‘no assigned space’ criterion → ineligible for SBA 7(a).
Salon suite operator charges both fixed rent and a small membership fee → combined model → fully ineligible.

Hotel owner signs a management agreement giving a third-party operator sole discretion; owner does not control bank accounts and cannot approve budgets → ineligible passive business. If amended so owner approves budgets, controls accounts, approves major spend, and employees are owner’s employees → may be eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism for eligibility control failure

business_rules.rule_name: Hotels and similar lodging businesses are SBA-eligible only if over 50% of revenue is from transients staying 30 days or less
business_rules.description: Businesses engaged in renting or leasing may be eligible in limited circumstances. Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, and similar businesses are eligible if more than 50% of prior-year revenue is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time and the business complies with all zoning and other legal requirements. For a Start-Up Business, projections must show more than 50% of revenue will be derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less. Rentals (including through internet platforms) must comply with all local laws and regulations, including zoning codes and homeowners/condominium association rules.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting SBA 7(a) or 504 loans for lodging or transient-stay businesses (including platform-based rentals) and evaluating eligibility under passive/rental business rules.
business_rules.consequences: If the business fails the >50% transient revenue test, exceeds 30-day transient stay criterion, or is not compliant with zoning/legal requirements (including HOA/COA rules), the Applicant is ineligible for SBA-guaranteed financing.
business_rules.exceptions: Start-up businesses may use projections instead of prior-year actuals, but projections must still show >50% transient revenue and must comply with laws and association rules.

source_reference.section_id: Limited circumstances for renting/leasing – transient lodging test
source_reference.source_text: Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, or similar types of businesses are eligible if more than 50% of the business’s revenue for the prior year is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less... If the Applicant is a Start-Up Business, the Applicant’s projections must show that more than 50%... Rentals... through an internet platform... must be in compliance with all local laws and regulations, including zoning codes, and homeowners or condominium associations rules.

examples: Operating motel shows 62% of last year’s room revenue from stays of 7 nights or fewer and is properly zoned → passes transient test (still must meet all other SBA criteria).
RV park shows 40% of revenue from overnight/weekend stays and 60% from 6-month seasonal leases → fails >50% transient revenue test → ineligible.

Start-up marina has no prior-year revenue; projections show 55% of revenue from transient slips (≤30 days) and confirms local zoning/permit compliance → may be eligible under the projection-based test.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism for eligibility control failure

REAL_ESTATE_LENDING_POLICY.definition: The written policy required by real estate lending regulations that establishes appropriate limits and standards for extensions of credit secured by liens on or interests in real estate, including CRE loans.
examples: SBA lending procedures requiring Section 106 due diligence and retention of Historic Property Borrower Certification and counsel clearance

business_rules.rule_name: Historic property due diligence and Section 106 review or SBA Form 2481 certification required for SBA-financed purchase or renovation and clearance becomes void if intentions change before disbursement
business_rules.description: For all SBA loan transactions involving the purchase or renovation of a property or site (including installation of equipment), SBA lenders must conduct documented due diligence to determine whether the property is listed on or eligible for the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP). If due diligence indicates no NRHP listing/eligibility, lender documents the file and does not need to request Section 106 review (unless later receiving a screen-out notice). If due diligence indicates NRHP listing/eligibility, then: (1) for acquisitions with no intention to alter/renovate/rehabilitate/restore/demolish, lender must obtain SBA Form 2481 (Historic Property Borrower Certification) before submitting the application/requesting an SBA loan number and provide it to local SBA counsel for clearance; clearance becomes null/void and a Section 106 review is required if intentions change or alterations become required between certification execution and disbursement; (2) for any intention to modify (regardless of funding source), lender must request a Section 106 review by local SBA counsel and provide specified information, including whether modifications have commenced; if commenced, lender must instruct borrower to stop work or the loan may not be approved/disbursed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loan transactions involving purchase or renovation of a property/site. Triggered by due diligence results indicating NRHP listing/eligibility or historic significance, and by borrower intent to modify property.
business_rules.consequences: If lender fails to perform/document due diligence or fails to obtain required Form 2481 and counsel clearance (when applicable), or fails to request Section 106 review when modifications are intended, SBA may condition approval, delay closing/disbursement, or deny approval/disbursement. If modifications have commenced and borrower is not instructed to stop work, the SBA loan may not be approved and/or disbursed.
business_rules.exceptions: If due diligence yields no indication of NRHP listing/eligibility, Section 106 review not required unless lender later receives a screen out notice from SBA loan processing center. SBA may, at its discretion, issue E-Tran terms and conditions requiring historic property review clearance prior to closing/renovations under hardship circumstances.

source_reference.section_id: Historic Properties > Due diligence and certification/review requirements
source_reference.source_text: For all SBA Loan transactions involving the purchase or renovation of a property or site, all SBA Lenders must conduct due diligence to determine whether the SBA Loan transaction involves a property or site that is listed on the NRHP or has historic significance... The due diligence conducted must follow prudent lending practices and be documented in the SBA Loan file... the SBA Lender must obtain a Historic Property Borrower Certification... on SBA Form 2481... clearance will become null and void and a Section 106 review will be required if... the Borrower... changes its intentions... If... intends to alter... the SBA Lender must request a Section 106 review... If so, SBA Lender must instruct the Borrower... to stop work, otherwise the SBA Loan may not be approved and/or disbursed.

examples: Borrower uses an SBA loan to purchase a building in a historic district and certifies no renovations: lender must obtain SBA Form 2481 and local SBA counsel clearance and retain it in the file.
Between Form 2481 execution and disbursement, borrower decides to renovate the façade: prior clearance becomes void and lender must request Section 106 review before disbursement/renovations.
Lender learns renovations already started on an NRHP-eligible property: lender must instruct borrower to stop work or the loan may not be approved/disbursed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA approval/disbursement condition or denial; adverse SBA lender oversight findings for failure to comply with Section 106 process

business_rules.rule_name: Written notice to SBA 504 applicants that SBA does not require use of an Agent
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loan applications involving a CDC and/or Agent, the Applicant must be clearly informed in writing that SBA does not require the use of an Agent for packaging or referring a loan application. This disclosure is a program ethics/fee requirement intended to prevent coercive or misleading practices.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loan applications where a CDC and/or Agent is involved in packaging and/or referring the application.
business_rules.consequences: If the written disclosure is not provided, the lender/CDC risks SBA program non-compliance findings and potential corrective action, including fee disputes and refunds if combined with other fee violations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the SOP text.

source_reference.section_id: Fees an Agent May Charge an Applicant for Packaging and Other Services
source_reference.source_text: CDCs and Agents must clearly inform any Applicant in writing that the SBA does not require the use of an Agent for packaging or referring a loan application.

examples: A CDC uses a third-party loan packager for a 504 submission: the engagement letter given to the borrower includes a written statement that SBA does not require an Agent.
A broker refers a 504 Applicant: before collecting any packaging fee, the broker provides a written disclosure that Agent use is not required by SBA.
If the Applicant only deals directly with the CDC and no Agent is used, this disclosure requirement is not triggered by an Agent relationship (but may still be included as a best practice).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program non-compliance finding and required corrective action (e.g., revised disclosures; potential fee dispute remediation).

business_rules.rule_name: Maximum SBA 504 Agent percentage-based fees and documentation required over $2,500
business_rules.description: Agent fees charged to an Applicant for packaging and other services must be reasonable and customary for services actually performed. If fees are charged as a percentage of the loan amount, the combined fee for all services must not exceed: (1) 3% on loans of $50,000 or less; (2) 2% for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000; plus (3) 0.25% on the portion over $1,000,000. The aggregate maximum percentage-based fee is $30,000. If the Agent charges fees based on hourly rate, there is no maximum, but the fees must be reasonable and customary and the hourly rate and time spent must be documented. In either pricing method, all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documentation of the service performed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an Agent charges an Applicant fees in connection with obtaining a 504 loan for packaging and/or other services, whether billed hourly or as a percentage.
business_rules.consequences: Fees exceeding stated caps, lacking documentation over $2,500, or not reasonable/customary may be deemed impermissible; SBA may require refund and/or fee reduction and may review fees at any time.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; hourly fees have no numeric cap but remain subject to reasonableness/customary and documentation requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Fees an Agent May Charge an Applicant for Packaging and Other Services
source_reference.source_text: An Agent may charge an Applicant fees for packaging and other services based on an hourly rate or on a percentage of the loan amount. In either case, all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed... For fees charged... based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed... 3 percent on loans of $50,000 or less; 2 percent for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25 percent on the portion over $1,000,000... The maximum fee... in the aggregate to an Applicant on a percentage basis is $30,000.

examples: A $40,000 504 loan: maximum percentage-based Agent fee is 3% = $1,200; if the Agent charges $3,000 total, the CDC/lender should flag the overage and require reduction/refund and documentation over $2,500.
A $900,000 504 loan: maximum percentage-based combined Agent fee is 2% of $900,000 = $18,000; an Agent invoice of $22,000 violates the cap.

A $2,000,000 504 loan: maximum fee is 2% of $1,000,000 ($20,000) + 0.25% of $1,000,000 ($2,500) = $22,500; an Agent charges $29,000 percentage-based (allowed if documented and reasonable), but cannot exceed $30,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Required fee reduction/refund; SBA program non-compliance finding; potential escalations to SBA oversight.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 Agent fees may not be flat, contingent on approval, or for unnecessary services
business_rules.description: SBA does not allow an Agent to charge an Applicant: (1) a standard or flat fee charged to all Applicants; (2) contingency fees paid only if the loan is approved or closed; or (3) fees for services not reasonably necessary in connection with an application. SBA may review fees at any time and Agents must refund any fee deemed unreasonable or impermissible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any Agent fee charged to an Applicant in connection with obtaining a 504 loan.
business_rules.consequences: Impermissible fees are subject to SBA review and required refund; continued violations may lead to program integrity actions and CDC escalation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Fees an Agent May Charge an Applicant for Packaging and Other Services
source_reference.source_text: SBA does not allow an Agent to charge an Applicant: A standard or flat fee charged to all Applicants; Contingency fees (fees paid only if the loan is approved or closed); or For services that are not reasonably necessary in connection with an application. SBA may review these fees at any time. Agents must refund any fee considered unreasonable or impermissible by SBA.

examples: A broker charges every 504 Applicant a flat $5,000 'standard packaging fee' regardless of work performed: prohibited flat fee; must be refunded if charged.
An Agent agreement provides 'fee payable only upon SBA approval and closing': prohibited contingency fee.
An Agent bills for 'marketing services unrelated to the loan application' as an application fee: prohibited because not reasonably necessary.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Required refund of impermissible/unreasonable fees; SBA program non-compliance finding.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA International Trade loan variable-rate maximum spreads of 6.5%, 6.0%, 4.5%, and 3.0% by loan size and default interest prohibited
business_rules.description: For SBA International Trade (IT) loans with variable rates, the maximum allowable note rate is an acceptable base rate (Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate) plus an allowable spread determined by loan amount: (i) $50,000 and less: base + 6.5%; (ii) $50,001 up to and including $250,000: base + 6.0%; (iii) $250,001 up to and including $350,000: base + 4.5%; (iv) $350,001 and greater: base + 3.0%. Default interest rates are not permitted. The 7(a) lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loans, specifically variable interest rate structures and loan pricing at origination and throughout loan life.
business_rules.consequences: If note rate exceeds maximum base+spread for the applicable loan size, or if default interest is included, the loan is not compliant with SBA interest rate rules and may be subject to SBA repair/denial of guaranty and examination findings. If loan is split to evade caps, it violates SBA policy.
business_rules.exceptions: Fixed-rate maximums are published separately in the Federal Register and are not specified in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Interest Rates: Maximum variable rates; Default interest not permitted; Anti-splitting
source_reference.source_text: Maximum variable rates are: Loans $50,000 or less Cannot exceed Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate + 6.5% ... $50,001 up to and including $250,000 ... + 6.0% ... $250,001 up to and including $350,000 ... + 4.5% ... $350,001 and greater ... + 3.0% ... Default interest rates are not permitted. ... The 7(a) Lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the Applicant.

examples: IT loan of $40,000 variable at Prime + 7.0%: noncompliant because cap is Prime + 6.5%.
IT loan of $300,000 variable at Optional Peg + 4.5%: compliant (cap is +4.5% for $250,001–$350,000).
Lender proposes two notes of $200,000 each instead of one $400,000 note to charge higher spreads: prohibited loan splitting.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA repair/denial of guaranty; regulatory criticism in SBA review

business_rules.rule_name: SBA variable-rate note must define base rate terms and apply base-rate rounding rule .004 down and .005 up to the nearest .01
business_rules.description: For SBA variable-rate loans, the Note must define: (i) base rate and spread (Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate); (ii) frequency of change; (iii) range of fluctuation; and (iv) ceiling and floor (if any). The Prime Rate is the rate in effect on the first business day of the month as identified in a national financial newspaper or website. The Optional Peg Rate is published quarterly in the Federal Register. Base rates must be rounded to two decimal places, with .004 rounded down to .00 and .005 rounded up to .01.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA loan is structured with a variable interest rate.
business_rules.consequences: If required terms are missing from the Note, or if base-rate rounding is not applied as required, the loan documentation is noncompliant with SBA variable-rate requirements and may lead to SBA findings, repair requirements, or guaranty eligibility issues.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited paragraph.

source_reference.section_id: Variable interest rate note definitions and base-rate rounding requirements
source_reference.source_text: For loans with a variable interest rate, the following terms must be defined in the Note: i) Base Rate and spread ... There are two acceptable base rates: The Prime Rate; or The SBA Optional Peg Rate. ... Base Rates will be rounded to two decimal places with .004 being rounded down to .00 and .005 being rounded up to .01. ii) Frequency of change; iii) Range of fluctuation; and iv) Ceiling and floor (if any).

examples: Note states ‘Prime + 3.00%, adjusts monthly, no floor, 12% cap’: compliant because base/spread, change frequency, ceiling/floor are defined.
Base rate is calculated as 8.005% and rounded to 8.01% per rule: compliant rounding.
Note references ‘market rate’ without defining Prime or Optional Peg and has no adjustment frequency: noncompliant documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA documentation exception; potential repair/guaranty eligibility issue

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express appraisals must name the lender as client or intended user and may not be seller or applicant prepared
business_rules.description: For SBA Express (and 7(a) Small) commercial real estate appraisals, the appraisal must identify the lender as the client and/or an intended user as defined in USPAP, except that federally regulated lenders may follow primary regulator FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise. The lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the applicant. The appraisal must be an “Appraisal Report” prepared in compliance with USPAP. The appraisal cost may be passed on to the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender obtains/relies on an appraisal for a 7(a) Small or SBA Express loan secured by commercial real property.
business_rules.consequences: Nonconforming appraisal (wrong client/intended user, seller/applicant-prepared, not USPAP Appraisal Report) must be replaced/cured before SBA reliance; failure may lead to SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and documentation exceptions.
business_rules.exceptions: Federally regulated lenders may follow primary regulator’s FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise for client/intended user identification.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate – Client/intended user and appraisal report requirements
source_reference.source_text: In order for the appraiser to identify the scope of work appropriately, the appraisal must identify the Lender as the client and/or an intended user of the appraisal... The Lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the Applicant. The cost may be passed on to the Applicant. The appraisal must be an “Appraisal Report” prepared in compliance with USPAP.

examples: Seller provides an appraisal commissioned by the seller: lender cannot use it for SBA Express; lender must order/obtain its own compliant appraisal naming the lender as client/intended user.

Appraisal engagement letter identifies only the borrower as intended user: lender must obtain corrected appraisal identifying lender as client/intended user (subject to FIRREA allowance for federally regulated lenders).
Lender orders an Appraisal Report compliant with USPAP and charges the borrower the appraisal fee at closing: permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and documentation exception findings

business_rules.rule_name: SBA agent packaging and referral fees must be documented over 2,500 dollars and percentage-based fees are capped at 3 percent, 2 percent, and 0.25 percent with 30,000 dollar aggregate maximum
business_rules.description: Agent fees charged to an applicant for packaging and other services must be reasonable and customary for services actually performed. Fees may be hourly (no maximum, but hourly rate and time spent on each service must be documented) or percentage-based, subject to caps: 3% on loans of $50,000 or less; 2% for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25% on the portion over $1,000,000; and the maximum fee in the aggregate on a percentage basis is $30,000. In either hourly or percentage approach, all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed. SBA prohibits agents from charging a standard/flat fee to all applicants, contingency fees, or fees for unnecessary services; SBA may require refunds of unreasonable or impermissible fees.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504-related agent fees (and generally SBA ethics/fees guidance) when agents charge applicants for packaging or other services in connection with obtaining SBA financing.
business_rules.consequences: If fees exceed caps or are impermissible, SBA may deem them unreasonable or impermissible and require refund; CDCs must review fees when there is a complaint or indication fees might be excessive; excessive fee findings may require agent to reduce/refund and refrain from collecting excess.
business_rules.exceptions: Hourly fees have no maximum, but must be reasonable/customary and documented; percentage fee caps apply to combined services provided to the applicant (combined fee cannot exceed stated maximums).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4: Ethics, Fees, and Agents – Agent fees
source_reference.source_text: The fees must be reasonable and customary for the services actually performed... all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed... For fees charged... based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed... 3 percent on loans of $50,000 or less; 2 percent for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25 percent on the portion over $1,000,000... The maximum fee... is $30,000... SBA does not allow an Agent to charge... a standard or flat fee... contingency fees... or for services that are not reasonably necessary...

examples: Agent charges 3% on a $40,000 loan ($1,200): permissible if reasonable and services performed; if total agent fees exceed $2,500, the services must be documented.

Agent charges 2% on $800,000 ($16,000) plus 0.25% on $500,000 ($1,250) for a $1.3MM loan, total $17,250: within percentage caps and below $30,000 aggregate maximum; documentation required because > $2,500.
Agent charges a non-refundable $5,000 flat fee to all applicants regardless of work performed: impermissible; SBA may require refund.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA refund requirement for unreasonable/impermissible fees; CDC escalation to OCRM; processing delays and program compliance findings

business_rules.rule_name: OCC appraisal and evaluation thresholds allow evaluations up to 500,000 dollars and require state certified appraisers at 1,000,000 dollars and nonresidential 500,000 dollars
business_rules.description: Under 12 CFR 34 subpart C as summarized in the Comptroller’s Handbook, OCC-supervised banks may use evaluations in lieu of appraisals for certain transactions, including when the loan amount is $500,000 or less; when the transaction is a business loan of $1 million or less and income from sale or rental of real estate is not the primary source of repayment; and certain existing extensions of credit (no new money beyond reasonable closing costs, or new money advanced when no obvious/material market or property change threatens collateral adequacy). State-certified appraisers are required for all loans/transactions of $1 million or more and for nonresidential loans/transactions of $500,000 or more (including one-to-four family construction loans), and for complex residential loans/transactions of $500,000 or more.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to OCC-regulated banks’ real estate-related financial transactions subject to 12 CFR 34 subpart C, including CRE-secured and other real-estate related business loans.
business_rules.consequences: If a transaction meets a threshold requiring an appraisal and state-certified appraiser, the bank must obtain one; using an evaluation instead (or using an appraiser without required credential) is noncompliant and may result in supervisory criticism and remediation requirements. Where evaluations are permitted, bank must still follow safe and sound valuation practices.
business_rules.exceptions: Regulation exempts certain transactions (not fully enumerated in the excerpt), including examples such as federal government/federal agency guaranteed loans (referenced generally). OCC may require an appraisal/evaluation for safety and soundness concerns even if an exemption might otherwise apply.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisals and Evaluations
source_reference.source_text:
the appraisal regulations permit the use of evaluations in lieu of appraisals for transactions

• in which the loan amount is $500,000 or less,
• in which the transaction is a business loan of $1 million or less and income from the sale or rental of real estate is not the primary source of repayment,
• representing an existing extension of credit when there is no new money advanced...

The services of a state-certified appraiser are required for

• all loans or other transactions of $1 million or more,
• nonresidential loans... of $500,000 or more...

examples: A $450,000 noncomplex CRE-secured renewal with no material market change: an evaluation may be used instead of an appraisal if the transaction meets the regulatory conditions and bank policy supports it.
A $1,500,000 nonresidential real estate transaction: bank must obtain an appraisal performed by a state-certified appraiser.

A $800,000 business loan where repayment is primarily from operating cash flow (not real estate rents/sale): evaluation may be permitted if other conditions are met; if repayment is primarily from rents, appraisal requirements may apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; Matters Requiring Attention for appraisal program deficiencies.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 requires a real estate appraisal when estimated project property value exceeds $500,000
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, SBA requires a real estate appraisal if the estimated value of the project property is greater than $500,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 transactions involving commercial real estate project property where an estimated value is determined as part of the application/credit package.
business_rules.consequences: If an appraisal is not obtained when required, the package is incomplete for SBA 504 standards and may be delayed or rejected until a compliant appraisal is provided.
business_rules.exceptions: If estimated value is $500,000 or less, appraisal may still be required under specific circumstances (see related rule BR_REAL_ESTATE_LENDING_POLICY_COLLATERAL_31_006).

source_reference.section_id: Collateral and Appraisals > Appraisals > Commercial Real Estate
source_reference.source_text: SBA requires a real estate appraisal if the estimated value of the Project Property is greater than $500,000.

examples: Estimated project property value is $750,000; an appraisal is mandatory and must be obtained before submission/approval.
Estimated project property value is $500,001; appraisal is required because value is greater than $500,000.
Estimated project property value is $450,000; appraisal is not automatically required but may be required under special circumstances (close relationship transaction, OREO, etc.).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC processing delay or declination pending completion of required appraisal

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 requires an appraisal at $500,000 or less when land equity, OREO, close relationship, seller carryback, ownership change, or SBA/CDC necessity applies
business_rules.description: When the estimated value of SBA 504 project property is $500,000 or less, an appraisal is still required if any of the following circumstances apply: (1) equity in land owned for 2 years or more is being contributed as part of borrower’s contribution; (2) the real estate is the third party lender’s OREO property; (3) the loan finances a transaction involving parties with a close relationship (e.g., existing owners or family members); (4) the seller is carrying back a loan that is part of the borrower’s contribution; (5) the project includes a change of ownership; or (6) SBA or the CDC otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness. If an appraisal is not required under these triggers, the CDC must obtain an appropriate evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 project property with estimated value <= $500,000.
business_rules.consequences: If a trigger condition exists and an appraisal is not obtained, the collateral valuation will be noncompliant with SBA 504 standards and may cause SBA/CDC to delay/decline or require corrective action. If no trigger condition exists, failure to obtain an appropriate evaluation may also be cited as not consistent with safe and sound practices.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. SBA/CDC discretion explicitly applies to requiring an appraisal when otherwise necessary to evaluate creditworthiness.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral and Appraisals > Appraisals > If estimated value is $500,000 or less
source_reference.source_text: If the estimated value of the Project Property is $500,000 or less: The CDC must obtain an appraisal under the following circumstances: Equity in land owned for 2 years or more is being contributed... The real estate is Third Party Lender’s OREO property... transaction involving parties with a close relationship... The seller... carrying back a loan... When the project includes a change of ownership; or If SBA or the CDC otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary...

examples: Property value estimated at $400,000 and borrower contributes land held for 3 years as equity; appraisal is required despite value <= $500,000.
Property value estimated at $350,000 and seller carries back a note treated as borrower contribution; appraisal is required.
Property value estimated at $475,000 with no listed triggers; appraisal not required but an evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices must be obtained.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC processing delay or required remediation to obtain appraisal/evaluation

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 appraisal must be USPAP-compliant, dated within 12 months, performed by an independent licensed appraiser, and state-certified when value exceeds $1,000,000
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 commercial real estate appraisals: (1) the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of conflict of interest, independent of the loan production function, and not involved in approving the transaction; (2) the appraiser must be State-licensed or State-certified, and when the estimated project property value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified; (3) the appraisal report must be prepared in compliance with USPAP and dated no more than twelve months prior to the date of application; (4) the appraisal must identify SBA as the client or an intended user (CDC may be client/intended user; acceptable if third party lender is client and SBA is intended user); and (5) the CDC may not use an appraisal prepared for the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever an appraisal is required/obtained for SBA 504 project property collateral.
